title: Non-Destructive Prediction of Fruit Ripeness and Firmness Using Hyperspectral Imaging and Lightweight Machine Learning Models
summary: Post-harvest fruit quality assessment is essential for reducing food waste, yet reliable non-destructive methods typically depend on expensive hyperspectral cameras and computationally intensive deep learning models. These systems typically require GPU resources, large-scale training data, and domain expertise, limiting their feasibility for many real-world agricultural settings. This study systematically evaluates 20 classical machine learning algorithms on hyperspectral imaging data for simultaneous ripeness classification and firmness prediction across five fruit species, using cross-validated experimental design with Bayesian hyperparameter optimization. Data preprocessing strategy, particularly class balancing and spectral transformations, contributes as much to prediction accuracy as algorithm choice. Our results show that tree-based machine learning models can outperform state-of-the-art deep earning models reported in Fruit-HSNet. Moreover, the findings indicate that only three visible-range wavelengths are needed to recover over 94% of full-spectrum accuracy, demonstrating that low-cost multispectral sensors combined with lightweight machine learning models can serve as practical alternatives to expensive hyperspectral cameras and complex deep learning approaches for practical fruit quality sorting.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that lightweight machine learning models (especially tree-based) combined with only three visible-range wavelengths can match or outperform expensive hyperspectral deep learning approaches for fruit quality assessment across five species.
AI.motivation: To reduce food waste by making non-destructive fruit quality assessment more accessible, as current hyperspectral + deep learning methods are too expensive and computationally demanding for real-world agricultural settings.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of 20 classical ML algorithms on hyperspectral data for ripeness classification and firmness prediction across five fruit species, using cross-validation, Bayesian hyperparameter optimization, and analysis of preprocessing strategies (class balancing, spectral transformations).
AI.result: Tree-based ML models outperform deep learning (Fruit-HSNet); only three visible-range wavelengths recover >94% of full-spectrum accuracy; data preprocessing contributes as much to accuracy as algorithm choice.
AI.conclusion: Low-cost multispectral sensors combined with lightweight ML models are practical alternatives to expensive hyperspectral cameras and complex deep learning for fruit quality sorting in real-world settings.

title: Fixed-phase Resonance Tracking for Fast Nonlinear Resonant Ultrasound Spectroscopy
summary: Nonlinear Resonant Ultrasound Spectroscopy (NRUS) experiments that rely on repeated sampling of resonance curves are inherently sensitive to measurement protocol due to evolution of material parameters caused by fast and slow dynamic effects. We introduce a model-assisted discrete-time resonance tracking method that maintains a system at its instantaneous resonance condition without the need to acquire full frequency sweeps. Resonance is defined through a prescribed phase relation between excitation and response, and the excitation frequency is iteratively updated using a linearized frequency--phase model. The procedure allows controlled suppression of transient wave buildup using optional feedforward correction with respect to external control parameter. The method is demonstrated on NRUS and on conditioning--relaxation protocol conducted on sandstone bar, providing estimates of resonance frequency and damping. Comparison with conventional approaches shows that measurement speed and mode stability significantly influence the inferred nonlinear indicators. The proposed framework is not limited to nonlinear acoustics and can be applied to arbitrary resonant systems with slowly evolving parameters.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a model-assisted discrete-time resonance tracking method for NRUS that maintains systems at instantaneous resonance without full frequency sweeps, demonstrating improved measurement speed and stability on sandstone, with broader applicability to resonant systems with slowly evolving parameters.
AI.motivation: Traditional NRUS experiments are inherently sensitive to measurement protocol due to fast and slow dynamic effects causing material parameter evolution, requiring a method that avoids full frequency sweeps while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: A discrete-time resonance tracking method using a linearized frequency-phase model, where resonance is defined by a prescribed phase relation, with iterative frequency updates and optional feedforward correction for controlled transient suppression.
AI.result: Demonstrated on NRUS and conditioning-relaxation protocols on sandstone bars; comparison shows measurement speed and mode stability significantly influence inferred nonlinear indicators.
AI.conclusion: The framework is not limited to nonlinear acoustics and can be applied to arbitrary resonant systems with slowly evolving parameters.

title: Generalizable CT-Free PET Attenuation and Scatter Correction for Pediatric Patients
comment: 13 pages, 15 figures, 7 tables. Source code available at https://github.com/wujm7/GPCN-PET
summary: Computed tomography (CT)-based attenuation and scatter correction improves quantitative PET but adds radiation exposure that is particularly undesirable in pediatric imaging. Existing CT-free methods are commonly trained in homogeneous settings and often degrade under scanner or radiotracer shifts, which limits their clinical utility. We propose the Generalizable PET Correction Network (GPCN), a dual-domain network for domain-robust CT-free PET attenuation and scatter correction. GPCN combines a multi-band contextual refinement module, which models pediatric anatomical variability through wavelet-based multiscale decomposition and long-range spatial context modeling, with a frequency-aware spectral decoupling module, which performs coordinate-conditioned amplitude/phase refinement in the Fourier domain. By synergizing multi-band spatial contextual modeling with asymmetric frequency-spectrum decoupling, the network explicitly separates invariant topological structures from domain-specific noise, thereby achieving precise quantitative recovery of both anatomical organs and focal lesions. This design aims to separate anatomy-dominant structures from domain-sensitive spectral residuals and to improve robustness across heterogeneous imaging conditions. We train and evaluate the method on 1085 pediatric whole-body PET scans acquired with two scanners and five radiotracers. In both joint training and zero-shot cross-domain evaluation, GPCN outperforms representative baselines and maintains stable quantitative accuracy on unseen scanner-tracer combinations. The method is further supported by ablation, region-wise quantitative analysis, and downstream segmentation experiments. In our cohort, the CT component of the conventional protocol corresponded to an average effective dose of 10.8 mSv, indicating the potential clinical value of reliable CT-free correction for pediatric PET.

AI.tldr: GPCN is a dual-domain network for CT-free PET attenuation/scatter correction that uses multi-band spatial and frequency-spectral decoupling modules to achieve domain-robust performance across different scanners and radiotracers, tested on 1085 pediatric PET scans with 10.8 mSv dose reduction potential.
AI.motivation: CT-based PET correction adds radiation exposure that is particularly undesirable in pediatric imaging, while existing CT-free methods degrade under scanner or radiotracer shifts, limiting clinical utility.
AI.method: GPCN combines a multi-band contextual refinement module (wavelet-based multiscale decomposition + long-range spatial context) with a frequency-aware spectral decoupling module (coordinate-conditioned amplitude/phase refinement in Fourier domain), separating invariant anatomical structures from domain-specific spectral noise.
AI.result: Evaluated on 1085 pediatric whole-body PET scans (2 scanners, 5 radiotracers); outperforms baselines in joint training and zero-shot cross-domain settings; maintains stable accuracy on unseen scanner-tracer combinations; supported by ablation and downstream segmentation experiments.
AI.conclusion: GPCN enables reliable CT-free PET correction for pediatric imaging, avoiding an average effective dose of 10.8 mSv, showing strong generalization across heterogeneous imaging conditions.

title: Triple-Phase Sequential Fusion Network for Hepatobiliary Phase Liver MRI Synthesis
summary: Gadoxetate disodium-enhanced MRI is essential for the detection and characterization of hepatocellular carcinoma. However, acquisition of the hepatobiliary phase (HBP) requires a prolonged post-contrast delay, which reduces workflow efficiency and increases the risk of motion artifacts. In this study, we propose a Triple-Phase Sequential Fusion Network (TriPF-Net) to synthesize HBP images by leveraging the sequential information from pre-HBP sequences: while T1-weighted imaging serves as the indispensable baseline, the model adaptively integrates arterial-phase (AP) and venous-phase (VP) features when available. By modeling the tissue-specific contrast uptake and excretion dynamics across these three phases, TriPF-Net ensures robust HBP synthesis even under the stochastic absence of one or both dynamic contrast-enhanced sequences. The framework comprises an Enhanced Region-Guided Encoder and a Dynamic Feature Unification Module, optimized with a Region-Guided Sequential Fusion Loss to maintain physiological consistency. In addition, clinical variables, including age, sex, total bilirubin, and albumin, are incorporated to enhance physiological consistency. Compared with conventional methods, TriPF-Net achieved superior performance on datasets from two centers. On the internal dataset, the model achieved an MAE of 10.65, a PSNR of 23.27, and an SSIM of 0.76. On the external validation dataset, the corresponding values were 12.41, 23.11, and 0.78, respectively. This flexible solution enhances clinical workflow and lesion depiction, potentially eliminating the need for delayed HBP acquisition in HCC imaging.

AI.tldr: TriPF-Net synthesizes hepatobiliary phase MRI images from pre-HBP sequences (T1, arterial, venous) using a sequential fusion network, achieving high-quality HBP synthesis even when dynamic phases are missing, with MAE 10.65/12.41 on internal/external datasets.
AI.motivation: HBP acquisition requires a prolonged post-contrast delay, reducing workflow efficiency and increasing motion artifacts. The authors aim to eliminate the need for delayed HBP acquisition by synthesizing it from earlier sequences.
AI.method: TriPF-Net uses an Enhanced Region-Guided Encoder, a Dynamic Feature Unification Module, and Region-Guided Sequential Fusion Loss. It adaptively integrates AP and VP features with T1 baseline, and incorporates clinical variables (age, sex, bilirubin, albumin) for physiological consistency.
AI.result: On internal dataset: MAE 10.65, PSNR 23.27, SSIM 0.76. On external dataset: MAE 12.41, PSNR 23.11, SSIM 0.78. Outperforms conventional methods at both centers.
AI.conclusion: TriPF-Net offers a flexible, robust solution that enhances clinical workflow and lesion depiction, potentially eliminating the need for delayed HBP acquisition in HCC imaging.

title: A General Framework for Generative Self-supervised Learning in Non-invasive Estimation of Physiological Parameters Using Photoplethysmography
summary: Aligning physiological parameter labels with large-scale photoplethysmographic (PPG) data for deep learning is challenging and resource-intensive. While self-supervised representation learning (SSRL) can handle limited annotated data, the challenge lies in learning robust shared representations from vast unlabeled data and integrating contextual cues to learn distinctive representations. To alleviate these challenges, a generative SSRL framework TS2TC is proposed to utilize the temporal, spectrogram, and temporal-spectrogram mixed domains to explore and incorporate the unique features of PPG for universal and noninvasive physiological parameter estimation. A pretext task named Cross-Temporal Fusion Generative Anchor (CTFGA) is designed, modeling temporal dependencies and reconstructing independent segments at a coarse level to provide robust global feature extraction and local contextual representation. The framework includes sub-signals from PPG with diverse frequency scales and order derivatives reflecting hemodynamics to facilitate learning shared representations at varying semantic levels. Secondly, a cognitive-inspired dual-process transfer (DPT) strategy is formulated, consisting of prior-dependent autonomous processes and posterior observation reasoning processes, to leverage the independent and integrated advantages of shared and specific representations. TS2TC introduces a bilinear temporal-spectrogram fusion method in the mixed domain, aligning latent representations from different domains and establishing fine-grained contextual interactions across multiple sources of information. Extensive experiments on physiological parameter estimation tasks showed that the joint performance of CTFGA and DPT outperforms standard generative learning significantly. TS2TC achieved an average 2.49\% improvement in RMSE over state-of-the-art estimation methods with only 10\% training data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes TS2TC, a generative self-supervised representation learning framework that uses temporal, spectrogram, and mixed-domain representations with a Cross-Temporal Fusion Generative Anchor (CTFGA) pretext task and a dual-process transfer (DPT) strategy to estimate physiological parameters from PPG data, achieving superior performance with only 10% training data.
AI.motivation: Aligning physiological parameter labels with large-scale PPG data for deep learning is challenging and resource-intensive. Existing SSRL methods struggle to learn robust shared representations from vast unlabeled data and integrate contextual cues for distinctive representations.
AI.method: TS2TC is a generative SSRL framework using three domains (temporal, spectrogram, temporal-spectrogram mixed). It introduces CTFGA pretext task for coarse-level temporal dependency modeling, sub-signals with diverse frequency scales and order derivatives for shared representations, a cognitive-inspired dual-process transfer (DPT) strategy, and a bilinear temporal-spectrogram fusion method.
AI.result: TS2TC achieved an average 2.49% improvement in RMSE over state-of-the-art methods with only 10% training data, and the joint performance of CTFGA and DPT significantly outperforms standard generative learning.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TS2TC framework effectively addresses the challenges of limited labeled PPG data by leveraging self-supervised learning across multiple domains, with the combination of generative pretext tasks and dual-process transfer providing substantial performance gains.

title: SwarmDrive: Semantic V2V Coordination for Latency-Constrained Cooperative Autonomous Driving
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, submitted to VTC fall 2026 workshop on W9: 2nd Workshop on Shaping Future Connectivity: Emerging and Intelligent Technologies for Sustainable Vehicular Networks
summary: Cloud-hosted LLM inference for autonomous driving adds round-trip delay and depends on stable connectivity, while purely local edge models struggle under occlusion. We present SwarmDrive, a semantic Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) coordination framework in which nearby vehicles run local Small Language Models (SLMs), share compact intent distributions only when uncertainty is high, and fuse them through event-triggered consensus. We evaluate SwarmDrive in a 5-seed executable study built around one occluded intersection case, combining matched operating-point comparisons with robustness sweeps. In that setting, SwarmDrive under its 6G communication setting ("Swarm 6G") raises success from 68.9% to 94.1% over a single local SLM while reducing latency from a 510 ms cloud reference to 151.4 ms. However, an increased number of participating vehicles leads to higher communication overhead and packet loss. SwarmDrive also evaluates the impact of swarm-size, packet-loss, and entropy-threshold sweeps and shows that the cooperative gain holds across ablations and is best balanced near an active swarm size of 4 vehicles and an entropy trigger threshold of 0.65 in the current prototype. These results show that semantic edge cooperation can work under tight latency constraints in the targeted intersection case, but they are not a deployment-grade validation of a real 6G stack.

AI.tldr: SwarmDrive is a V2V coordination framework using small language models on nearby vehicles to share compact intent distributions via event-triggered consensus, improving autonomous driving success from 68.9% to 94.1% over a single local SLM with lower latency (151.4 ms vs 510 ms cloud) in an occluded intersection scenario, though communication overhead and packet loss increase with swarm size.
AI.motivation: Cloud-hosted LLM inference introduces unacceptable round-trip delays and depends on stable connectivity, while purely local edge models fail under occlusion, motivating a cooperative V2V approach that balances latency and robustness.
AI.method: Nearby vehicles run local Small Language Models (SLMs), share compact intent distributions only when uncertainty is high (event-triggered), and fuse them through consensus mechanisms under a semantic V2V coordination framework.
AI.result: SwarmDrive under 6G raises success from 68.9% to 94.1% over a single local SLM, reduces latency from 510 ms (cloud) to 151.4 ms; optimal performance at swarm size 4 and entropy threshold 0.65; cooperative gain holds across ablations but communication overhead and packet loss increase with more vehicles.
AI.conclusion: Semantic edge cooperation via V2V can work under tight latency constraints for occluded intersections, but results are not a deployment-grade validation of a real 6G stack.

title: Rejection Sampling is Optimal for Relative Entropy Coding
summary:
In relative entropy coding, a sender aims to design a stochastic code such that, on input $X \sim P_X$, the receiver can generate a sample $Y \sim P_{Y \mid X}$. It is a standard result that (1) this requires at least $I(X; Y)$ bits, (2) the lower bound is achievable within a logarithmic gap, and (3) this gap cannot be reduced in general. The necessity of the gap suggests that the mutual information is not the correct information measure to quantify the rate of relative entropy coding.
  A potential alternative emerged in the work of Flamich et al. (2025), who proved a tighter lower bound of $I_F(X \to Y)$, a quantity we call the functional information. In this paper, we show that this lower bound is tight by constructing the ring toss code, an encoding method for rejection sampling which uses at most $I_F(X \to Y) + \log e$ bits. This demonstrates that rejection sampling is optimal for relative entropy coding. Our result implies that the classical mutual information lower bound is achievable within $\log(I(X; Y) + 1) + 2.45$ bits in general and within $1.45$ bits for singular channels, which are both the tightest bounds of their kind to date. Moreover, our one-shot result also recovers Sriramu and Wagner's asymptotic results on the second-order redundancy of relative entropy codes.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the ring toss code, achieving the functional information lower bound for relative entropy coding within a constant gap of log e bits, proving rejection sampling is optimal. It provides the tightest bounds to date on the mutual information gap.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed mutual information I(X;Y) is a lower bound for relative entropy coding but has an irreducible logarithmic gap, suggesting mutual information is the wrong measure. The authors seek to establish functional information I_F(X→Y) as the correct rate measure.
AI.method: The authors construct the "ring toss code," an encoding method for rejection sampling that achieves the functional information lower bound I_F(X→Y) + log e bits, showing rejection sampling is optimal for relative entropy coding.
AI.result: Achieves I_F(X→Y) + log e bits. Classical mutual information bound improved to within log(I(X;Y)+1)+2.45 bits generally, and 1.45 bits for singular channels. Recovers second-order asymptotic results.
AI.conclusion: Functional information is the correct information measure for relative entropy coding rate, and rejection sampling is optimal. The ring toss code provides tightest known bounds.

title: HalalBench: A Multilingual OCR Benchmark for Food Packaging Ingredient Extraction
summary: No standardized benchmark exists for evaluating OCR on food packaging, despite its critical role in automated halal food verification. Existing benchmarks target documents or scene text, missing the unique challenges of ingredient labels: curved surfaces, dense multilingual text, and sub-8pt fonts. We present HalalBench, the first open multilingual benchmark for food packaging OCR, comprising 1,043 images (50 real, 993 synthetic) with 36,438 annotations in COCO format spanning 14 languages. We evaluate four engines: docTR achieves F1=0.193, ML Kit 0.180, EasyOCR 0.167, while all fail on Japanese (F1=0.000). A clustering ablation shows 36% F1 improvement from our post-processing algorithm. We validate findings through HalalLens (https://halallens.no), a production halal scanner serving 20+ countries. Dataset and code are released under open licenses.

AI.tldr: HalalBench is the first open multilingual benchmark for food packaging OCR, with 1,043 images and 36,438 annotations across 14 languages. Existing OCR engines perform poorly (best F1=0.193), with complete failure on Japanese, but a clustering post-processing algorithm yields 36% F1 improvement.
AI.motivation: No standardized benchmark exists for OCR on food packaging, which is critical for automated halal food verification. Existing benchmarks focus on documents or scene text, ignoring unique challenges like curved surfaces, dense multilingual text, and small fonts.
AI.method: Created HalalBench with 1,043 images (50 real, 993 synthetic) and 36,438 COCO-format annotations across 14 languages. Evaluated docTR, ML Kit, and EasyOCR engines, and developed a clustering-based post-processing algorithm.
AI.result: docTR achieved F1=0.193, ML Kit 0.180, EasyOCR 0.167; all engines failed on Japanese (F1=0.000). Clustering ablation showed 36% F1 improvement from the post-processing algorithm.
AI.conclusion: Food packaging OCR remains a challenging open problem, especially for non-Latin scripts. The proposed post-processing algorithm significantly improves performance. The benchmark and code are publicly released.

title: CT-Guided Spatially-varying Regularization for Voxel-Wise Deformable Whole-Body PET Registration
summary: Whole-body Positron Emission Tomography (PET) registration is essential for multi-parametric tumor characterization and assessment of metastatic disease progression. In deep learning-based deformable registration, the dense displacement field (DDF) regularizer is crucial for stabilizing optimization and preventing unrealistic deformations in large 3D volumes. A key challenge in whole-body deformable registration is anatomical heterogeneity, rigid structures (e.g., bones) should undergo stronger regularization, whereas soft tissues require more flexible deformation and weaker constraints. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective CT-guided spatially-varying regularization strategy for whole-body cross-tracer deformable PET registration. The key idea is to use the paired CT volume from the PET/CT acquisition to construct a voxel-wise regularization map for the DDF, replacing the conventional single global regularization weight. This yields anatomy-adaptive regularization strength across rigid and soft tissues. The proposed method is evaluated on a real clinical cross-tracer PET/CT dataset of 296 patients involving 18F-PSMA and 18F-FDG, showing that the proposed method achieves statistically significant improvements over weakly-supervised registration baseline in both whole-body registration performance and organ-wise alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a CT-guided spatially-varying regularization strategy for whole-body PET deformable registration, using CT volumes to create voxel-wise regularization maps that adapt to tissue type (rigid vs. soft), outperforming conventional single-weight methods on a 296-patient cross-tracer dataset.
AI.motivation: Whole-body PET registration faces anatomical heterogeneity where rigid structures (bones) and soft tissues require different regularization strengths, but existing methods use a single global regularization weight that cannot adapt to local tissue properties.
AI.method: A CT-guided spatially-varying regularization strategy that uses paired CT volumes from PET/CT acquisition to construct a voxel-wise regularization map for the dense displacement field (DDF), replacing the conventional single global regularization weight to enable anatomy-adaptive regularization.
AI.result: Evaluated on a clinical cross-tracer PET/CT dataset of 296 patients (18F-PSMA and 18F-FDG), the method achieves statistically significant improvements over weakly-supervised registration baseline in both whole-body registration performance and organ-wise alignment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed anatomy-adaptive regularization strategy effectively addresses the heterogeneity challenge in whole-body PET registration by applying stronger regularization to rigid structures and weaker constraints to soft tissues, leading to improved registration accuracy.

title: Structural Limits of Soft Fusion in Multi-Warden Covert Communication
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
summary: This paper investigates covert wireless communication with a Fusion Center (FC) that aggregates raw energy measurements from multiple Wardens via soft fusion. Extending our prior work on power-threshold randomization, we consider a stronger adversarial model in which FC randomizes both the number of active Wardens W and the detection threshold t, while Alice and a friendly Jammer jointly randomize their transmit powers under an outage constraint at Bob. We derive a closed-form expression for FC's optimal soft-fusion threshold and show that it is independent of the number of active Wardens. Thus, strategic uncertainty in the sensing infrastructure provides no meaningful detection advantage for FC under soft fusion. We further establish a robustness theorem showing that, even under arbitrary FC randomization over (W,t), Alice and Jammer can maintain outage-feasible communication at Bob while preserving covertness with high probability, provided their power ranges are sufficiently large. This reveals a structural limitation of soft fusion. A game-theoretic formulation characterizes the Nash equilibrium mixed strategies of both sides, accounting for deployment costs and detection-pressure parameters. Analytical and numerical results show that: 1) soft fusion is largely insensitive to the number of Wardens; 2) even semi-strategic finite-support geometric randomization of W performs comparably to the full game-theoretic equilibrium; and 3) the covertness-reliability tradeoff remains nearly invariant across a wide range of FC deployment costs and strategy parameters. These findings exemplify a Red Queen effect, in which FC incurs increasing operational costs for only marginal gains in detection performance, and highlight the need for alternative detection architectures.

AI.tldr: This paper studies covert wireless communication where a Fusion Center (FC) uses soft fusion of energy measurements from multiple Wardens. The FC randomizes the number of active Wardens and detection thresholds, while Alice and a friendly Jammer randomize their transmit powers under an outage constraint at Bob. The key finding is that soft fusion is fundamentally limited - the FC gains no meaningful detection advantage from strategic uncertainty, exhibits a Red Queen effect where increasing operational costs yield marginal detection gains, and alternative detection architectures are needed.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental limitations of soft fusion-based detection in covert wireless communication, specifically whether strategic randomization of sensing infrastructure (number of Wardens and detection thresholds) by a Fusion Center can significantly improve detection performance against covert transmissions.
AI.method: The authors derive closed-form expressions for the FC's optimal soft-fusion threshold, establish a robustness theorem, and formulate a game-theoretic model with Nash equilibrium analysis. They consider a scenario where FC randomizes both the number of active Wardens W and detection threshold t, while Alice and a Jammer randomize transmit powers under a reliability outage constraint at Bob.
AI.result: (1) Soft fusion is largely insensitive to the number of Wardens; (2) The FC's optimal soft-fusion threshold is independent of the number of active Wardens; (3) Even semi-strategic finite-support geometric randomization performs comparably to the full game-theoretic equilibrium; (4) The covertness-reliability tradeoff remains nearly invariant across a wide range of FC deployment costs and strategy parameters.
AI.conclusion: Soft fusion exhibits a Red Queen effect where FC incurs increasing operational costs for only marginal detection gains, revealing a structural limitation of soft fusion. The authors highlight the need for alternative detection architectures beyond soft fusion for covert wireless communication scenarios.

title: Airspeed Forward-Invariance for Unpowered Fixed-Wing Aircraft
summary: Autonomous fixed-wing flight is becoming a key capability in aerial robotics, enabling sensing, mobility, and contingency operations across both small-scale Uncrewed Aircraft Systems and large-scale Advanced Air Mobility. During unpowered operation in fixed-wing platforms, airspeed is regulated solely through potential-kinetic energy exchange, making airspeed dynamics highly sensitive to guidance commands, particularly under wind. This paper presents a viability-based airspeed protection for ground-referenced guidance in steady wind, where airspeed evolution depends explicitly on the commanded flight path angle. Leveraging Nagumo's tangency condition, we derive a closed-form, wind-dependent characterization of admissible guidance commands that guarantees forward invariance of a safe airspeed envelope. These conditions are embedded within an offline quadratic programming framework to certify airspeed-safe maneuver primitives for non-ascending flight at the guidance level. The approach is validated using a high-fidelity unpowered fixed-wing aircraft model on gliding trajectories formed by concatenating certified maneuver primitives, demonstrating strict airspeed boundedness. Future work will address unsteady wind fields and flight experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a viability-based airspeed protection method for unpowered fixed-wing aircraft under wind conditions, using Nagumo's tangency condition to derive closed-form admissible guidance commands that guarantee safe airspeed bounds, validated via high-fidelity gliding trajectory simulations.
AI.motivation: Autonomous fixed-wing flight during unpowered operation makes airspeed highly sensitive to guidance commands and wind, risking stall or overspeed; a formal safety guarantee is needed to regulate airspeed through energy exchange.
AI.method: Using Nagumo's tangency condition, the authors derive closed-form, wind-dependent admissible guidance commands that ensure forward invariance of a safe airspeed envelope; these are embedded in an offline quadratic programming framework to certify airspeed-safe maneuver primitives for non-ascending flight.
AI.result: Validation using a high-fidelity unpowered fixed-wing model on gliding trajectories formed by concatenating certified maneuver primitives demonstrates strict airspeed boundedness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively ensures airspeed safety for unpowered fixed-wing flight under steady wind at the guidance level; future work includes extending to unsteady wind fields and experimental flight validation.

title: On the hull of linearized polynomial codes
summary:
Motivated by entanglement-assisted quantum error-correcting codes, where the hull dimension determines the number of required pre-shared entangled pairs, we study hulls of two families of $\mathbb{F}_q$-linear codes defined by $q$-polynomial operators over $\mathbb{F}_{q^m}$. Our main tool is a unified Gram-matrix method. For image codes $\mathcal{C}(\boldsymbolα)=\operatorname{im}Φ_{\boldsymbolα}$, with $Φ_{\boldsymbolα}=\sum_iα_iF_i$, we prove the master hull--rank formula $\dim\operatorname{Hull}(\mathcal{C}(\boldsymbolα))=\operatorname{rank}(Φ_{\boldsymbolα})-\operatorname{rank}(G(\boldsymbolα))$, where $G(\boldsymbolα)$ is the associated Gram matrix over $\mathbb{F}_q$. Specializing to $C_{λ,μ}=\operatorname{im}(λx+μL(x))$, we obtain a quadratic Gram pencil $λ^2G_0+λμG_1+μ^2G_2$ whose determinant describes the LCD locus in $\mathbb{P}^1(\mathbb{F}_q)$.
  We also treat $\mathbb{F}_{q^m}$-linear rank-distance codes $\mathcal{C}=\langle X,F_1,\ldots,F_k\rangle_{\mathbb{F}_{q^m}}$ with the Delsarte inner product, where a $k\times k$ Gram matrix over $\mathbb{F}_{q^m}$ determines the hull dimension. For $L(X)=X^{q^k}$, with $d=\gcd(k,m)$, the resulting circulant Gram matrices yield a closed-form discriminant and a complete classification in three of the four bijectivity configurations over $\mathbb{P}^1(\mathbb{F}_{q^m})$. In the remaining case, the hull dimension equals $δ=\dim_{\mathbb{F}_q}(\operatorname{im}φ_{λ,μ}\cap\kerφ_{λ,μ}^{\dagger})$, and the extremal condition $δ=d$ is characterized by an explicit trace-isotropy criterion. We conclude with an exact count of LCD and non-LCD points, showing that the LCD density tends to $1$ as $q\to\infty$, together with a worked example over $\mathbb{F}_{64}$ and a SageMath verification.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a unified Gram-matrix method to study hulls of two families of linear codes defined by q-polynomial operators, establishing rank formulas for hull dimensions, classifying LCD codes via quadratic Gram pencils, and showing LCD density approaches 1 as q→∞.
AI.motivation: To understand hull dimensions of q-polynomial linear codes, which directly determine the number of pre-shared entangled pairs required in entanglement-assisted quantum error-correcting codes.
AI.method: A unified Gram-matrix method over 𝔽_q and 𝔽_{q^m} is introduced. For image codes, a master hull-rank formula dim Hull = rank(Φ) - rank(G) is proved. For rank-distance codes, circulant Gram matrices and trace-isotropy criteria are used.
AI.result: (1) Master hull-rank formula for image codes; (2) Quadratic Gram pencil for LCD classification over ℙ¹(𝔽_q); (3) Closed-form discriminant and complete classification for L(X)=X^{q^k} in three of four configurations; (4) Explicit trace-isotropy criterion for extremal hull dimension δ=d; (5) Exact LCD/non-LCD point count with density →1 as q→∞.
AI.conclusion: The Gram-matrix approach provides a systematic algebraic framework for computing hull dimensions of q-polynomial codes, with LCD codes becoming overwhelmingly abundant for large q; SageMath verification confirms the results.

title: See No Evil: Semantic Context-Aware Privacy Risk Detection for AR
comment: Proc. IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing (ICASSP), 2026
summary: Augmented reality (AR) systems pose unique privacy risks due to their continuous capture of visual data. Existing AR privacy frameworks lack semantic understanding of visual content, limiting their effectiveness in detecting context-dependent privacy risks. We propose PrivAR, which leverages vision language models (VLMs) with chain-of-thought prompting for contextual privacy risk detection in AR environments. PrivAR uses visual scene cues to infer potential sensitive information types, such as identifying password notes in office environments through contextual reasoning. PrivAR detects and obfuscates textual content, preventing exposure of sensitive information while preserving contextual cues necessary for VLM inference. Additionally, we investigate contextually-informed warning interfaces to enhance user privacy awareness. Experiments on a real-world AR dataset show that PrivAR achieves superior accuracy (81.48%) and F1-score (84.62%) compared to baselines, while reducing privacy leakage rate to 17.58%. User studies evaluating contextually-informed warning interfaces provide insights into effective privacy-aware AR design.

AI.tldr: PrivAR is a privacy framework for AR systems that uses vision language models with chain-of-thought prompting to detect and obfuscate context-dependent privacy risks (e.g., password notes in offices), achieving 81.48% accuracy and 17.58% privacy leakage rate on real-world datasets, plus insights from user studies on warning interfaces.
AI.motivation: Existing AR privacy frameworks lack semantic understanding of visual content, limiting their ability to detect context-dependent privacy risks in continuous visual data capture.
AI.method: PrivAR leverages vision language models (VLMs) with chain-of-thought prompting to infer sensitive information types from visual scene cues, detects and obfuscates textual content while preserving contextual cues, and investigates contextually-informed warning interfaces.
AI.result: 81.48% accuracy, 84.62% F1-score, 17.58% privacy leakage rate on a real-world AR dataset; outperformed baselines.
AI.conclusion: VLMs with chain-of-thought reasoning can effectively detect context-dependent privacy risks in AR environments, and contextually-informed warning interfaces improve user privacy awareness.

title: GS-DOT: Gaussian splatting-based image reconstruction for diffuse optical tomography
summary: This work presents GS-DOT, a novel image reconstruction framework based on Gaussian Splatting (GS) for diffuse optical tomography (DOT). Inspired by GS for rendering applications, absorption coefficients are represented as a sparse sum of anisotropic Gaussian primitives optimized to fit measured time-resolved point-spread functions through analytic gradients and Adam optimization. This is the first adaptation of GS algorithms in the photon diffusion regime, where the ray transport function is replaced by the diffusion functions to enable accurate modeling of light transport in highly scattering media. Validation on synthetic tissue models demonstrate high accuracy in localization and quantification of reconstructed absorption maps for both clean and noisy signals. GS-DOT has demonstrated high robustness to noise and showed a huge reduction in memory demand.

AI.tldr: GS-DOT is a novel image reconstruction framework that adapts Gaussian Splatting for diffuse optical tomography, using anisotropic Gaussian primitives to represent absorption coefficients, achieving high accuracy and robustness with reduced memory demand.
AI.motivation: To adapt Gaussian Splatting algorithms, originally designed for rendering, to the photon diffusion regime for accurate modeling of light transport in highly scattering media, replacing traditional ray transport functions with diffusion functions.
AI.method: Absorption coefficients are represented as a sparse sum of anisotropic Gaussian primitives, optimized via analytic gradients and Adam optimization to fit measured time-resolved point-spread functions in the photon diffusion regime.
AI.result: High accuracy in localization and quantification of reconstructed absorption maps on synthetic tissue models for both clean and noisy signals; high robustness to noise; significant reduction in memory demand.
AI.conclusion: GS-DOT is the first adaptation of GS algorithms for DOT, demonstrating strong performance, noise robustness, and memory efficiency, promising for practical applications in biomedical imaging.

title: From Equations to Algorithms and Data: Transforming Microwave Engineering and Education with Machine Learning
summary: Conventional microwave engineering education relies heavily on analytical methods, canonical circuit topologies, and intuition-driven design, which have proven effective at microwave frequencies. However, as systems increasingly operate in the millimeter-wave and terahertz regimes, parasitic effects, process-dependent electromagnetic interactions, and ultra-wideband performance requirements challenge both topology/layout-constrained traditional design methodologies and existing teaching paradigms. This paper proposes a pedagogical shift in microwave and RFIC (Radio Frequency Integrated Circuit) engineering and education by introducing machine-learning (ML) and data-driven electromagnetic synthesis as a complementary design framework for microwave circuits such as power dividers and combiners, couplers, and baluns. Rather than emphasizing predefined topologies, the proposed approach enables topology-agnostic, performance-oriented exploration of the design space, allowing students to directly engage with electromagnetic behavior through specification-driven synthesis. By integrating machine-learning-based inverse design and multi-objective optimization into the curriculum, the framework enhances physical intuition, encourages design creativity, and better aligns microwave education with emerging industrial practices in high-frequency and ultra-wideband system design.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes replacing traditional microwave engineering education with a machine-learning-based, topology-agnostic design framework that uses data-driven electromagnetic synthesis and inverse design to better prepare students for millimeter-wave and terahertz system design challenges.
AI.motivation: Conventional microwave engineering methods (analytical, topology-constrained, intuition-driven) are becoming insufficient as systems move to millimeter-wave and terahertz frequencies, where parasitic effects, EM interactions, and ultra-wideband requirements overwhelm traditional design and teaching paradigms.
AI.method: The paper introduces a pedagogical shift toward ML and data-driven electromagnetic synthesis, enabling topology-agnostic, performance-oriented design space exploration. This integrates machine-learning-based inverse design and multi-objective optimization into the curriculum for teaching microwave circuits (power dividers, couplers, baluns).
AI.result: The proposed framework enhances physical intuition, encourages design creativity, and aligns microwave education with emerging industrial practices in high-frequency and ultra-wideband system design.
AI.conclusion: ML-driven, specification-based synthesis should complement or replace traditional topology-constrained approaches to better prepare students for modern RFIC and millimeter-wave/THz engineering challenges.

title: Magnetic Indoor Localization through CNN Regression and Rotation Invariance
comment: Published and presented at the 2026 4th International Conference on Mechatronics, Control and Robotics (ICMCR)
summary: Indoor positioning is an essential technology for a wide range of applications in GNSS-denied environments, including indoor navigation and IoT systems. Combining convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and magnetic field-based features offers a low-cost, infrastructure-free solution for precise positioning. While magnetic fingerprints are a promising approach for indoor positioning, models trained on raw 3D magnetometer data are highly sensitive to device orientation. We address this by using two rotation invariant features derived from the 3D magnetic field: the norm (Mn) and the projection onto the gravity axis (Mg). We train a lightweight 7-layer dilated CNN (MagNetS/XL) on magnetic sequences to directly regress (x, y) positions. Using the MagPie dataset (three buildings, handheld trajectories), we systematically evaluate fixed and random rotations of test and/or train data. Raw 3D inputs (Mx, My , Mz) exhibit isotropic error increases under fixed 90° rotations and further degrade with growing random rotations. In contrast, 2D (Mn, Mg) inputs maintain rotation invariant accuracy and surpass the 3D inputs once rotation exceeds building-specific thresholds for three reference buildings: 0° for Loomis (large), 5° for Talbot (medium), and 6° for CSL (small). MagNetXL achieves or exceeds state-of-the-art accuracy on the MagPie dataset, and MagNetS delivers similar performance with roughly one third of the parameters, favoring mobile deployment. These results show that the robustness gained from rotation invariant inputs outweighs the loss of input dimensionality in realistic usage, allowing mapping and localization without orientation alignment or added infrastructure.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a lightweight CNN-based indoor positioning system using rotation-invariant magnetic features (norm and gravity projection) that achieves robust localization without requiring device orientation alignment.
AI.motivation: Indoor positioning in GNSS-denied environments often suffers from device orientation sensitivity. Raw 3D magnetic fingerprints vary with phone rotation, degrading accuracy. The authors aim to eliminate orientation dependency without adding infrastructure.
AI.method: Two rotation-invariant features (Mn: magnetic norm, Mg: gravity-axis projection) are extracted from 3D magnetometer data. A 7-layer dilated CNN (MagNetS/XL) is trained on magnetic sequences for direct (x,y) regression. Models are evaluated under fixed and random rotations using the MagPie dataset across three buildings.
AI.result: 2D (Mn, Mg) inputs maintain rotation-invariant accuracy and outperform raw 3D inputs when rotation exceeds building-specific thresholds (0°, 5°, 6°). MagNetXL achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on MagPie; MagNetS delivers comparable results with ~1/3 parameters, suitable for mobile deployment.
AI.conclusion: Rotation-invariant magnetic features provide robustness that compensates for reduced input dimensionality, enabling effective indoor mapping and localization without orientation alignment or extra infrastructure.

title: Analysis of Efficient Scheduling in Layered Decoding of GLDPC Codes
summary: In this study, we investigate the characteristics of scheduling sequences that enable efficient decoding of generalized low-density parity-check (GLDPC) codes under the layered message-passing algorithm. In particular, we show that scheduling sequences leading to higher decoding efficiency should prioritize the update of constraint nodes corresponding to subcodes with larger minimum distance, fewer minimum-weight codewords, and shorter code length. Based on these characteristics, we design a scheduling algorithm, which further demonstrates the effectiveness of these characteristics through simulation experiments.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies key characteristics of efficient scheduling sequences for GLDPC decoding and proposes a scheduling algorithm that prioritizes constraint nodes with larger minimum distance, fewer minimum-weight codewords, and shorter code length.
AI.motivation: To improve decoding efficiency of GLDPC codes under layered message-passing algorithms by understanding what makes scheduling sequences effective.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze scheduling sequence characteristics, identifying that efficient decoding prioritizes constraint nodes with larger minimum distance, fewer minimum-weight codewords, and shorter code length. They then design a scheduling algorithm based on these insights.
AI.result: Simulation experiments validate that the proposed scheduling algorithm, based on the identified characteristics, achieves higher decoding efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Scheduling sequences for GLDPC decoding are more efficient when they prioritize constraint nodes with certain desirable properties (larger minimum distance, fewer minimum-weight codewords, shorter code length), and a practical algorithm based on these principles is effective.

title: FreqFormer: Hierarchical Frequency-Domain Attention with Adaptive Spectral Routing for Long-Sequence Video Diffusion Transformers
summary: Long-sequence video diffusion transformers hit a quadratic self-attention cost that dominates runtime and memory for very long token sequences. Most efficient attention methods use one approximation everywhere, yet video features are spectrally structured: low frequencies carry global layout and coarse motion; high frequencies carry texture and fine detail. We present FreqFormer, a frequency-aware heterogeneous attention framework. Token features are split into spectral bands with different operators: dense global attention on compressed low-frequency content, structured block-sparse attention on mid frequencies, and sliding-window local attention on high frequencies. A lightweight spectral routing network allocates heads across bands using layer statistics and the diffusion timestep, shifting compute toward global structure early in denoising and detail later. Cross-band summary tokens provide cheap residual exchange. FreqFormer is paired with a fused GPU execution plan that co-schedules dense, sparse, and local branches to cut kernel launches and memory traffic. We give a consistent complexity model, an orthonormal-decomposition view of approximation, and simulation-based systems numbers (throughput, arithmetic intensity, memory traffic, duration scaling). In simulations from 64K to 1M tokens, FreqFormer substantially reduces estimated attention FLOPs and KV-related memory traffic versus dense attention while keeping a hardware-friendly pattern, supporting spectrally structured heterogeneous attention as a practical direction for long-video diffusion transformers.

AI.tldr: FreqFormer is a frequency-aware heterogeneous attention framework for long-video diffusion transformers that splits token features into spectral bands (low/mid/high frequencies) and applies different attention operators (dense global, block-sparse, sliding-window) to each, reducing quadratic attention costs while maintaining output quality.
AI.motivation: Long-sequence video diffusion transformers suffer from quadratic self-attention cost in runtime and memory. Existing efficient attention methods apply a single approximation uniformly, ignoring that video features have spectral structure—low frequencies carry global layout/coarse motion, high frequencies carry texture/fine detail.
AI.method: FreqFormer splits token features into spectral bands with different operators: dense global attention on compressed low-frequency content, structured block-sparse attention on mid frequencies, and sliding-window local attention on high frequencies. A lightweight spectral routing network allocates heads across bands based on layer statistics and diffusion timestep. Cross-band summary tokens provide cheap residual exchange. A fused GPU execution plan co-schedules all branches.
AI.result: In simulations from 64K to 1M tokens, FreqFormer substantially reduces estimated attention FLOPs and KV-related memory traffic versus dense attention while maintaining a hardware-friendly pattern.
AI.conclusion: The paper supports spectrally structured heterogeneous attention as a practical direction for long-video diffusion transformers, backed by a consistent complexity model, orthonormal-decomposition view of approximation, and simulation-based system performance metrics.

title: The Spectral Lifecycle of Transformer Training: Transient Compression Waves, Persistent Spectral Gradients, and the Q/K--V Asymmetry
summary: We present the first systematic study of weight matrix singular value spectra \emph{during} transformer pretraining, tracking full SVD decompositions of every weight matrix at 25-step intervals across three model scales (30M--285M parameters). We discover three phenomena: \textbf{(1)~Transient Compression Waves:} stable rank compression propagates as a traveling wave from early to late layers, creating a dramatic gradient that peaks early then \emph{reverses} -- late layers eventually over-compress past early layers. \textbf{(2)~Persistent Spectral Gradients:} the power-law exponent~$α$ develops a permanent depth gradient forming a non-monotonic inverted-U in deeper models, with peaks shifting toward earlier layers as depth increases. \textbf{(3)~Q/K--V Functional Asymmetry:} value/output projections compress uniformly while query/key projections carry the full depth-dependent dynamics. The dissociation between transient compression and persistent spectral shape reveals that \emph{rank and spectral shape encode fundamentally different information about training}. We formalize this as a two-timescale dynamical model and derive scaling laws ($Δα\propto L^{0.26}$, $R^2{=}0.99$). We validate on nine models across three families (custom, GPT-2, Pythia; 30M--1B parameters; 8--36 layers), demonstrate that $α$ predicts layer importance ($ρ{=}0.69$--$0.84$, $p{<}0.02$), and show that spectral-guided pruning outperforms Last-N heuristics by $1.1{\times}$--$3.6{\times}$ across seven models in two families (GPT-2 124M--774M, Pythia 160M--1B), with worst-vs-best gaps up to $23.7{\times}$ confirming the causal role of spectral structure.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically studies singular value spectra of weight matrices during transformer pretraining, discovering three phenomena: transient compression waves propagating from early to late layers, persistent spectral gradients forming an inverted-U pattern, and Q/K-V functional asymmetry. The authors formalize a two-timescale dynamical model with scaling laws and show spectral-guided pruning outperforms Last-N heuristics by 1.1×-3.6×.
AI.motivation: To understand how weight matrix singular value spectra evolve during transformer pretraining, revealing fundamental dynamics of rank and spectral shape that encode different training information.
AI.method: Tracked full SVD decompositions of every weight matrix at 25-step intervals across three model scales (30M-285M parameters), validated on nine models across three families (custom, GPT-2, Pythia; 30M-1B parameters; 8-36 layers).
AI.result: Found three phenomena: (1) transient compression waves with rank compression traveling from early to late layers, (2) persistent spectral gradients with power-law exponent α forming depth-dependent inverted-U patterns, (3) Q/K-V functional asymmetry. Derived scaling laws (Δα ∝ L^0.26, R²=0.99). α predicts layer importance (ρ=0.69-0.84, p<0.02). Spectral-guided pruning outperforms Last-N by 1.1×-3.6×.
AI.conclusion: Rank and spectral shape encode fundamentally different information about training, formalized as a two-timescale dynamical model. Spectral structure has causal importance for transformer performance, validated through pruning experiments.

title: Shared-kernel Wavelet Neural Networks for Poisson Image Reconstruction
summary: The Laplacian operator transforms the image into its Laplacian field, which usually is sparse and satisfies a stable distribution. On the other hand, an image can be uniquely reconstructed from its Laplacian field via solving a Poisson equation with a proper boundary condition. Such uniqueness is mathematically guaranteed. Thanks to these properties, we propose to use the sparse Laplacian field to present the image. We first show that the Laplacian field is sparse and satisfies a stable distribution on hundreds images. Then, we show that the image can be accurately reconstruct from its Laplacian field. For the reconstruction task, we propose a shared-kernel wavelet neural network, which solves the Poisson equation and has three advantages. First, it has less than {\bf 0.0002M} parameters, which is compact enough for most of devices. Second, it has linear computation complexity, leading to a real-time reconstruction. Third, it achieves higher accuracy than previous methods. Several numerical experiments are conducted to show the effectiveness and efficiency of the sparse Laplacian field and the proposed Poisson solver. The proposed method can be applied in a large range of applications such as image compression, low light enhancement, object tracking, etc.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes representing images using a sparse Laplacian field, which can be accurately reconstructed via a compact, linear-complexity shared-kernel wavelet neural network Poisson solver with less than 0.0002M parameters, enabling real-time performance for applications like image compression, low-light enhancement, and object tracking.
AI.motivation: The Laplacian field of an image is sparse and follows a stable distribution, and an image can be uniquely reconstructed from its Laplacian field via solving a Poisson equation. These properties motivate using the sparse Laplacian field as an efficient image representation.
AI.method: A shared-kernel wavelet neural network is proposed to solve the Poisson equation for reconstructing images from their sparse Laplacian fields. The network has less than 0.0002M parameters and linear computation complexity.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves higher reconstruction accuracy than previous methods, is compact enough for most devices, and enables real-time reconstruction due to linear complexity.
AI.conclusion: The sparse Laplacian field combined with the proposed Poisson solver is effective and efficient, with broad applicability to image compression, low-light enhancement, object tracking, etc.

title: An AI-Ready Pipeline for Impedance-Resolved QCM Biosensor: Interpretable Line-Shape Features, Redundancy Control, and Robust Regression
summary: Accurate inference from quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) measurements in liquids is often limited by reducing resonance behavior to two scalar endpoints (frequency and dissipation shifts, $Δf$ and $ΔD$) or by relying on single-equation analytical models (Kanazawa-model). We propose an AI-ready, impedance-resolved workflow that preserves full resonance line-shape information and converts it into compact, physically interpretable features for supervised regression. A passive microfluidic mixer generates glycerol--water concentration gradients under a constant total flow rate (50~$μ$L/min), while complex impedance spectra of a 10~MHz AT-cut quartz crystal are recorded in real time. Each sweep of nine spectra is parameterized via constrained Gaussian/Lorentzian models to yield 52 line-shape descriptors spanning extrema of $|Z|$, $X$, and $B$ and peaks of $R$, phase, and $G$. The pipeline integrates consensus outlier handling, redundancy-aware feature ranking (mRMR), and cross-validated regression across linear, kernel, and ensemble models. Compared with the classical Kanazawa baseline, the impedance line-shape approach reduces concentration-prediction error from 0.456 to 0.148~\%v/v RMSE (3.09$\times$ lower). The results demonstrate that impedance-resolved line-shape features provide a robust and interpretable basis for machine-learning-assisted QCM inference and illustrate a generalizable pattern for AI-enabled spectral sensing.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an AI-ready, impedance-resolved workflow for quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) measurements that preserves full resonance line-shape information, extracting 52 physically interpretable features. Applied to glycerol-water concentration prediction, this approach reduces error by 3.09× compared to the classical Kanazawa model (RMSE 0.148 vs 0.456 %v/v).
AI.motivation: Traditional QCM analysis reduces complex resonance behavior to just two scalar endpoints (frequency and dissipation shifts, Δf and ΔD) or relies on simplified single-equation analytical models like the Kanazawa model, which limits accurate inference from liquid-phase measurements.
AI.method: The workflow uses a passive microfluidic mixer to generate glycerol-water concentration gradients at constant flow rate (50 μL/min), records complex impedance spectra of a 10 MHz AT-cut quartz crystal in real time, parameterizes nine spectra per sweep via constrained Gaussian/Lorentzian models to yield 52 line-shape descriptors (extrema of |Z|, X, B; peaks of R, phase, G), and integrates consensus outlier handling, mRMR feature ranking, and cross-validated regression across linear, kernel, and ensemble models.
AI.result: The impedance line-shape approach achieves concentration-prediction RMSE of 0.148 %v/v, compared to 0.456 %v/v for the classical Kanazawa baseline—a 3.09× reduction in error.
AI.conclusion: Impedance-resolved line-shape features provide a robust and interpretable basis for machine-learning-assisted QCM inference, and this workflow illustrates a generalizable pattern for AI-enabled spectral sensing.

title: RecoverFormer: End-to-End Contact-Aware Recovery for Humanoid Robots
summary: Humanoid robots operating in unstructured environments must recover from unexpected disturbances-a capability that remains challenging for end-to-end control policies. We present RECOVERFORMER, a fully end-to-end humanoid recovery policy that learns when and how to switch among recovery behaviors-including compensatory stepping, hand-environment contact, and center-of-mass reshaping-while maintaining robust performance under model mismatch. The architecture combines a causal transformer over a 50-step observation history with two novel heads: a latent recovery mode that enables smooth transitions among distinct recovery strategies, and a contact affordance head that predicts which environmental surfaces (walls, railings, table edges) are beneficial for stabilization. We evaluate RECOVERFORMER on the Unitree G1 humanoid in MuJoCo. Trained only on open floor, RECOVERFORMER transfers zero shot to walled environments, achieving 100% recovery success across 100-300 N pushes and across wall distances from 0.25-1.4m. Under zero-shot dynamics mismatch, RECOVERFORMER reaches 75.5% at plus +25% mass, 89% under 30 ms latency, 91.5% at low friction, and 99% under compound friction, latency and mass perturbation. The learned latent modes specialize across force regimes without mode-level supervision, validated by t-SNE analysis of 300 episodes. Taken together, these results show that a single end-to-end policy can deliver multi-modal, contact aware humanoid recovery that generalizes across perturbation magnitude, contact geometry, and dynamics shift.

AI.tldr: RECOVERFORMER is an end-to-end humanoid recovery policy using a causal transformer with two novel heads (latent recovery mode and contact affordance) that enables multi-modal recovery behaviors (stepping, hand-contact, COM reshaping) and achieves 100% recovery success on the Unitree G1 across diverse pushes, zero-shot transfers to walled environments, and robust performance under dynamics mismatches.
AI.motivation: Humanoid robots in unstructured environments need to recover from unexpected disturbances, but end-to-end control policies still struggle with this capability, especially when needing to switch among different recovery strategies.
AI.method: A causal transformer over 50-step observation history with two novel heads: (1) a latent recovery mode for smooth transitions among recovery strategies, and (2) a contact affordance head predicting beneficial environmental surfaces for stabilization.
AI.result: 100% recovery success on Unitree G1 across 100-300 N pushes and wall distances 0.25-1.4m; zero-shot transfer to walled environments; under dynamics mismatch: 75.5% (+25% mass), 89% (30ms latency), 91.5% (low friction), 99% (compound perturbations).
AI.conclusion: A single end-to-end policy can deliver multi-modal, contact-aware humanoid recovery that generalizes across perturbation magnitude, contact geometry, and dynamics shift.

title: A Unified Fractional Regularization Framework for Sparse Recovery
summary: We propose a unified fractional regularization framework for sparse signal recovery based on the $\ell_1/\ell_p^q$ model. Our main theoretical contribution is the characterization of the equivalence between the first-order stationary points of the $\ell_1/\ell_p^q$ formulation and the subtractive $\ell_1 - α\ell_p$ model, providing a unified perspective on these nonconvex regularizers. In addition, we establish a new sufficient recovery condition under the Restricted Isometry Property (RIP), showing that the framework's robustness even under high-coherence sensing matrices. To solve the resulting problem, we develop a majorization-minimization (MM) algorithm and prove its convergence via the Kurdyka-Lojasiewicz (KL) property. Numerical experiments on different sensing matrices and MRI reconstruction demonstrate that the proposed approach consistently outperforms existing methods.

AI.tldr: A unified fractional regularization framework ($\ell_1/\ell_p^q$) for sparse signal recovery is proposed, with theoretical equivalence to $\ell_1 - α\ell_p$ models, new RIP-based recovery conditions, and a provably convergent MM algorithm; it outperforms existing methods on numerical and MRI experiments.
AI.motivation: To unify and generalize existing nonconvex regularizers for sparse signal recovery, and to provide a theoretical bridge between different formulations while improving robustness under high-coherence sensing matrices.
AI.method: The paper proposes an $\ell_1/\ell_p^q$ fractional regularization model, establishes equivalence to subtractive $\ell_1 - α\ell_p$ models, derives new RIP-based sufficient recovery conditions, and develops an MM algorithm with convergence guaranteed via the KL property.
AI.result: The proposed framework consistently outperforms existing methods on various sensing matrices and in MRI reconstruction tasks.
AI.conclusion: The $\ell_1/\ell_p^q$ framework provides a unified, theoretically grounded, and practically effective approach for sparse signal recovery under challenging conditions.

title: DO-Bench: An Attributable Benchmark for Diagnosing Object Hallucination in Vision-Language Models
summary: Object level hallucination remains a central reliability challenge for vision language models (VLMs), particularly in binary object existence verification. Existing benchmarks emphasize aggregate accuracy but rarely disentangle whether errors stem from perceptual limitations or from the influence of contextual textual priors, leaving underlying failure mechanisms ambiguous. We introduce DO-Bench, a controlled diagnostic benchmark that isolates these sources through structured multimodal interventions. Rather than evaluating models in unconstrained settings, DO-Bench probes two complementary dimensions: the Prior Override dimension progressively strengthens contextual textual priors while holding visual evidence constant to assess resistance to prior pressure, and the Perception-Limited dimension incrementally enhances visual evidence from full-scene context to localized object crops to measure perceptual grounding strength. This paired design enables attribution of errors to prior suppression, perceptual insufficiency, or their interaction. We further define two diagnostic metrics, PriorRobust and PerceptionAbility, to quantify these behaviors consistently. Evaluations across diverse open- and closed-source VLMs reveal systematic differences in prior sensitivity and perceptual reliability, demonstrating that object hallucination reflects heterogeneous, mechanism dependent failure patterns beyond aggregate accuracy.

AI.tldr: DO-Bench is a diagnostic benchmark that separates object hallucination errors in VLMs into two categories: those caused by contextual textual priors (Prior Override) and those caused by perceptual limitations (Perception-Limited), revealing that VLM failures are mechanism-dependent rather than uniform.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks measure aggregate accuracy of object hallucination but fail to distinguish whether errors come from perceptual limitations or contextual textual priors, leaving failure mechanisms ambiguous.
AI.method: DO-Bench uses controlled multimodal interventions with two dimensions: Prior Override (strengthen textual priors while keeping visual evidence constant) and Perception-Limited (enhance visual evidence from full scene to localized crops). Two diagnostic metrics are defined: PriorRobust and PerceptionAbility.
AI.result: Evaluation across diverse open- and closed-source VLMs shows systematic differences in prior sensitivity and perceptual reliability, indicating object hallucination reflects heterogeneous, mechanism-dependent failure patterns.
AI.conclusion: Object hallucination in VLMs is not a single failure mode but arises from distinct mechanisms (prior suppression vs. perceptual insufficiency), which aggregate accuracy metrics alone cannot capture.

title: KARL: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLMs via Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reinforcement Learning
summary: Enabling large language models (LLMs) to appropriately abstain from answering questions beyond their knowledge is crucial for mitigating hallucinations. While existing reinforcement learning methods foster autonomous abstention, they often compromise answer accuracy because their static reward mechanisms, agnostic to models' knowledge boundaries, drive models toward excessive caution. In this work, we propose KARL, a novel framework that continuously aligns an LLM's abstention behavior with its evolving knowledge boundary. KARL introduces two core innovations: a Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reward that performs online knowledge boundary estimation using within-group response statistics, dynamically rewarding correct answers or guided abstention; and a Two-Stage RL Training Strategy that first explores the knowledge boundary and bypasses the "abstention trap", and subsequently converts incorrect answers beyond the knowledge boundary into abstentions without sacrificing accuracy. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that KARL achieves a superior accuracy-hallucination trade-off, effectively suppressing hallucinations while maintaining high accuracy across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios.

AI.tldr: KARL is a framework that aligns LLM abstention with evolving knowledge boundaries using a Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reward and Two-Stage RL Training, achieving better accuracy-hallucination trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing reinforcement learning methods for LLM abstention use static reward mechanisms that are agnostic to models' knowledge boundaries, leading to excessive caution and compromised answer accuracy.
AI.method: KARL introduces (1) a Knowledge-Boundary-Aware Reward that estimates knowledge boundaries online via within-group response statistics, and (2) a Two-Stage RL Training Strategy that explores the knowledge boundary, avoids the "abstention trap", and converts incorrect answers beyond knowledge into abstentions.
AI.result: KARL achieves a superior accuracy-hallucination trade-off across multiple benchmarks, effectively suppressing hallucinations while maintaining high accuracy in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Continuously aligning abstention behavior with evolving knowledge boundaries is an effective approach to mitigate hallucinations without sacrificing answer accuracy.

title: Deep Learning-Enabled Dissolved Oxygen Sensing in Biofouling Environments for Ocean Monitoring
summary: The escalating climate crisis and ecosystem degradation demand intelligent, low-cost sensors capable of robust, long-term monitoring in real-world environments. Absolute dissolved oxygen (DO) concentration is a key parameter for predicting climate tipping points. Inexpensive optoelectronic sensors based on microstructured polymer films doped with phosphorescent dyes could be readily deployable; however, signal drift and marine biofouling remain major challenges. Here, we introduce a sensing paradigm that combines camera-based DO sensors with a visual transformer (ViT)-based physics-informed neural network (PINN) for high-fidelity sensing under biofouling conditions. Training and testing data were obtained from an algae-laden water tank over 14 days to capture accelerated biofouling. The ViT-PINN, which embeds the Stern-Volmer (SV) equation into the loss function, reduces mean average error (MAE) by 92% and 89% compared to classical statistical and ML approaches, achieving ~2 umol/L absolute error. A deep ensemble further quantifies predictive uncertainty, enabling self-diagnostic sensing.

AI.tldr: A camera-based dissolved oxygen sensor using a ViT-PINN model reduces error by ~92% over classical methods, achieving ~2 μmol/L accuracy under biofouling conditions.
AI.motivation: The climate crisis and ecosystem degradation require low-cost, robust sensors for long-term environmental monitoring, but existing optoelectronic DO sensors suffer from signal drift and marine biofouling.
AI.method: A camera-based DO sensor combined with a visual transformer (ViT)-based physics-informed neural network (PINN) that embeds the Stern-Volmer equation into the loss function; tested on 14-day algae-laden water tank data. A deep ensemble quantifies uncertainty.
AI.result: ViT-PINN reduces MAE by 92% and 89% compared to classical statistical and ML approaches, achieving ~2 μmol/L absolute error. The deep ensemble enables self-diagnostic uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ViT-PINN paradigm significantly improves DO sensing fidelity under biofouling, offering a promising path toward reliable, self-diagnostic environmental monitoring.

title: Applied AI-Enhanced RF Interference Rejection
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures, Accepted to the 2nd IEEE International Conference on AI and Data Analytics (ICAD 2026)
summary: AI-enhanced interference rejection in radio frequency (RF) transmissions has recently attracted interest because deep learning approaches trained on both the signal of interest (SOI) and the signal mixture (SOI plus interference) can outperform traditional approaches which only consider the SOI. The goal is to detect, demodulate, and decode signals over a range of signal-to-interference-plus-noise (SINR) levels without having a detailed, design-level knowledge of the interfering signal or the propagation conditions. Our present AI interference suppression results are based on Autoregressive Transformer Decoder models which exhibit orders of magnitude faster throughput at inference time than WaveNet models developed in earlier work. As a specific example, we investigate an analog FM "Walkie Talkie" radio signal of interest in the presence of an Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiplexing (OFDM) interferer. This type of interferer is near-ubiquitous in the current RF landscape. Our results clearly show the benefits of transformer-based interference mitigation in tactical settings. We show that unintelligible transmissions become intelligible via metrics such as Perceptual Evaluation of Speech Quality (PESQ), while overall latency is kept to a minimum using readily available lightweight GPUs such as a Jetson AGX Orin. We believe these same techniques can also be applied to a broader set of national security scenarios, as well as having commercial applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents transformer-based AI models for RF interference suppression, achieving faster throughput than WaveNet and successfully restoring intelligibility of FM signals corrupted by OFDM interference using lightweight GPUs.
AI.motivation: Traditional interference rejection approaches only consider the signal of interest (SOI) and require detailed knowledge of interference and propagation. AI-enhanced methods that learn from both SOI and signal mixtures can outperform these traditional approaches across various SINR levels without requiring such design-level knowledge.
AI.method: The authors use Autoregressive Transformer Decoder models for interference suppression, comparing them to WaveNet models. They specifically evaluate on analog FM "Walkie Talkie" signals contaminated by OFDM interference (a common RF interferer).
AI.result: Transformer models achieve orders of magnitude faster inference throughput than WaveNet models. Unintelligible transmissions become intelligible (measured via PESQ), with minimal latency on lightweight GPUs like Jetson AGX Orin.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based interference mitigation shows clear benefits in tactical settings and can be extended to broader national security and commercial applications.

title: Collaborative Trajectory Prediction via Late Fusion
summary: Predicting future trajectories of surrounding traffic agents is critical for safe autonomous navigation and collision avoidance. Despite all advances in the trajectory forecasting realm, the prediction models remains vulnerable to uncertainty caused by occlusions, limited sensing range, and perception errors. Collaborative vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) approaches help reduce this uncertainty by sharing complementary information. Existing collaborative trajectory prediction methods typically fuse feature maps at the perception stage to construct a holistic scene view. Further this holistic representation is decoded into the future trajectories. Such design incurs substantial communication overhead due to the exchange of high-dimensional feature representations and often assumes idealized bandwidth and synchronization, limiting practical deployment. We address these limitations by shifting collaboration from perception to the prediction module and introducing a late-fusion framework for shared forecasts. The framework is model-agnostic and treats collaborating vehicles as independent asynchronous agents. We evaluate the approach on the OPV2V, V2V4Real, and DeepAccident datasets, comparing individual and collaborative forecasting. Across all datasets, late fusion consistently reduces miss rate and improves trajectory success rate ($\mathrm{TSR}_{0.5}$), defined as the fraction of ground-truth agents with final displacement error below 0.5 m. On the real-world V2V4Real dataset, collaborative prediction improves the success rate by $1.69\%$ and $1.22\%$ for both intelligent vehicles, respectively, compared with individual forecasting.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a late-fusion framework for collaborative trajectory prediction that shifts collaboration from perception to the prediction module, reducing communication overhead while improving prediction accuracy across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing collaborative trajectory prediction methods fuse feature maps at the perception stage, incurring high communication overhead and assuming idealized bandwidth/synchronization, which limits practical deployment. The goal is to address these limitations.
AI.method: The authors introduce a late-fusion framework that shifts collaboration from the perception stage to the prediction module. It is model-agnostic, treats collaborating vehicles as independent asynchronous agents, and fuses shared forecasts at the output level rather than intermediate feature representations.
AI.result: Evaluated on OPV2V, V2V4Real, and DeepAccident datasets. Late fusion consistently reduces miss rate and improves TSR_{0.5} (success rate with final displacement error <0.5m). On V2V4Real, collaborative prediction improved success rate by 1.69% and 1.22% for two intelligent vehicles vs. individual forecasting.
