Additionally, we design a Temporal Frame graph Reasoning Module (TFRM) to explicitly model temporal coherence between video frames through graph structure learning. To enhance emotional semantic control, a large language model is employed to extract textual descriptions from real videos, serving as high-level semantic guidance. Experiments on the HDTF and MEAD datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms existing methods in terms of lip-sync accuracy, temporal consistency, and emotional accuracy.

AI.tldr: EAD-Net is a diffusion-based framework for emotionally talking head generation that uses SyncNet supervision and TREPA to prevent lip-sync degradation, STDA mechanism for global motion patterns, TFRM for temporal coherence, and LLM-extracted semantic descriptions for enhanced emotional control, outperforming existing methods on HDTF and MEAD datasets.
AI.motivation: Current emotionally talking head generation methods rely on simple emotional labels with insufficient semantic information. Introducing high-level semantics enhances expressiveness but causes lip-sync degradation. Mainstream methods also struggle to balance computational efficiency and global motion awareness in long videos with poor temporal coherence.
AI.method: EAD-Net introduces: (1) SyncNet supervision and Temporal Representation Alignment (TREPA) to mitigate lip-sync degradation from multi-modal fusion; (2) Spatio-Temporal Directional Attention (STDA) for capturing global motion patterns via strip attention; (3) Temporal Frame graph Reasoning Module (TFRM) for modeling temporal coherence through graph structure learning; (4) LLM-based semantic extraction from real videos for high-level emotional guidance.
AI.result: Experiments on HDTF and MEAD datasets show the method outperforms existing approaches in lip-sync accuracy, temporal consistency, and emotional accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EAD-Net effectively addresses the trade-off between emotional expressiveness and lip-sync accuracy, and improves temporal coherence for long video generation.

title: Sparsity-Aware Event-Driven Impulse Radio Transceivers for Reliable Neuromorphic Inference
summary: The growing number of Internet-of-Things (IoT) based artificial intelligence (AI) applications deployed at resource-constrained network edge call for ultra-reliable and low-latency data processing pipelines from distributed front-end sensors to remote inference units. Meanwhile, brain-inspired neuromorphic computing featuring spiking neural networks (SNNs) have arisen as a new paradigm for energy-efficient AI inference. However, significant energy and time expenses incurred in high-complexity transceivers that combat fading and multi-user interference hinder implementations of multi-user neuromorphic inference for edge intelligence. To address this challenge, we consider in this paper a broadband multi-user remote inference system that integrates event-based sensing and time-hopping (TH) on-off keying (OOK) based ultra-wideband (UWB) communications for reliable neuromorphic inference. Specifically, we propose a novel two-timescale repetition coding that leverages intra-frame pulse sparsity for low-latency repetition. We also develop two neuromorphic inference schemes based on: (i) digital spike encoding that recovers each pixel of the event-frame by threshold-adaptive detection via an SNN based sparsity estimator; and (ii) analog spike encoding that converts noisy correlator outputs at the receiver into analog-valued inputs for end-to-end (E2E) classification. Finally, numerical results validate the effectiveness of the proposed coding schemes, and reveal a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)-dependent performance crossover between the two inference schemes, indicating that analog spike encoding based schemes are preferable with mild or high SNR while digital spike encoding based schemes remain robust in low SNR regime.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a broadband multi-user remote inference system combining event-based sensing with time-hopping ultra-wideband communications for energy-efficient neuromorphic inference at the edge. It introduces two-timescale repetition coding leveraging intra-frame pulse sparsity, and develops two inference schemes (digital and analog spike encoding) with SNR-dependent performance trade-offs.
AI.motivation: The growing deployment of IoT-based AI applications at resource-constrained network edges requires ultra-reliable low-latency processing, but high-complexity transceivers for combating fading and multi-user interference hinder multi-user neuromorphic inference for edge intelligence.
AI.method: The authors integrate event-based sensing with TH-OOK based UWB communications, propose a two-timescale repetition coding exploiting intra-frame pulse sparsity, and develop two neuromorphic inference schemes: (i) digital spike encoding with threshold-adaptive SNN-based sparsity estimation, and (ii) analog spike encoding with end-to-end classification from noisy correlator outputs.
AI.result: Numerical results validate the proposed coding schemes' effectiveness and reveal an SNR-dependent performance crossover: analog spike encoding performs better under mild/high SNR, while digital spike encoding remains more robust in low SNR regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed integration of neuromorphic sensing with UWB communication enables reliable multi-user edge inference, with the choice between analog and digital spike encoding schemes depending on the operating SNR conditions.

title: A Lightweight Toggleable Adhesion Prototype for Multirotor UAV Landing on Tilting Platforms
comment: To be published in the proceedings of the International Conference on Unmanned Aircraft Systems (ICUAS) 2026
summary: Autonomous multirotor landings on uncrewed surface vessels (USVs) are critical for persistent maritime operations but remain challenging due to wave-induced tilt, wind disturbances, and limited landing area. Many existing approaches exhibit small pose tolerance for reliable landing. This paper presents a lightweight toggleable adhesion mechanism to improve landing reliability. The system uses a motor-driven corkscrew that engages hook-and-loop material on the landing surface, enabling active adhesion during landing and controlled release during takeoff. We evaluate a prototype using a modified Crazyflie 2.0 and a custom tilting platform at fixed angles representative of extreme wave conditions. Using only a simple vertical PID controller, the proposed approach increases landing success from an average of 40% (baseline) to 80% across platform tilts up to 43 degrees using appropriately selected actuation settings.

AI.tldr: A lightweight corkscrew-based adhesion mechanism using hook-and-loop material improves multirotor landing success on tilted surfaces (up to 43°) from 40% to 80% with a simple PID controller.
AI.motivation: Autonomous multirotor landings on USVs are challenging due to wave-induced tilt, wind disturbances, and limited landing area; existing approaches have small pose tolerance for reliable landing.
AI.method: A motor-driven corkscrew engages hook-and-loop material on the landing surface, enabling active adhesion during landing and controlled release during takeoff. Tested on a modified Crazyflie 2.0 with a tilting platform using a vertical PID controller.
AI.result: Landing success improved from 40% (baseline) to 80% across platform tilts up to 43 degrees using appropriately selected actuation settings.
AI.conclusion: The toggleable adhesion mechanism significantly improves landing reliability on tilted surfaces, offering a lightweight solution for autonomous maritime operations.

title: Sub-Nyquist Sampling for Reaching Theoretical Minimal Sampling Rate Boundary
summary: Wideband spectrum sensing motivates sub-Nyquist sampling architectures that exploit spectral sparsity, yet in blind scenarios where subband locations are unknown, existing schemes require sampling rates at least twice the theoretical minimum. To this end, we propose a dual-frequency aliasing wideband converter (DAWC), which partitions the multiband spectrum into non-uniform frequency intervals and selectively samples only a subset of them, requiring no prior knowledge of subband locations. We demonstrate that under mild conditions on the signal and the system, DAWC achieves perfect subband localization and waveform reconstruction at the theoretical minimum rate. Moreover, we introduce an innovative side-information-aided subspace pursuit (MSSP) algorithm exploiting the common support structure inherent in the signal column submatrices for exact recovery of the spectrum support set. Based on the restricted isometry property (RIP), we provide stable recovery guarantees for MSSP in the presence of noise. Numerical simulations show that the proposed scheme achieves superior spectrum recovery accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Dual-Frequency Aliasing Wideband Converter (DAWC) and a Modified Side-information-aided Subspace Pursuit (MSSP) algorithm that achieve perfect subband localization and waveform reconstruction at the theoretical minimum sampling rate for blind wideband spectrum sensing, with superior performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing sub-Nyquist sampling schemes for blind wideband spectrum sensing require sampling rates at least twice the theoretical minimum because subband locations are unknown. This paper aims to close that gap.
AI.method: Proposes DAWC that partitions the multiband spectrum into non-uniform frequency intervals and selectively samples only a subset; introduces MSSP algorithm exploiting common support structure in signal column submatrices for exact spectrum support set recovery.
AI.result: DAWC achieves perfect subband localization and waveform reconstruction at the theoretical minimum rate under mild conditions; MSSP provides stable recovery guarantees under noise based on RIP; numerical simulations show superior spectrum recovery accuracy over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DAWC architecture and MSSP algorithm enable blind wideband spectrum sensing at the theoretical minimum sampling rate with robust performance guarantees and superior accuracy.

title: MetaEarth3D: Unlocking World-scale 3D Generation with Spatially Scalable Generative Modeling
summary: Recent generative AI models have achieved remarkable breakthroughs in language and visual understanding. However, although these models can generate realistic visual content, their spatial scale remains confined to bounded environments, preventing them from capturing how geographic environments evolve across thousands of kilometers or from modeling the spatial structure of the large-scale physical world. This limitation poses a critical challenge for ultra-wide-area spatial intelligence in Earth observation and simulation, revealing a deeper gap in generative AI: progress has relied primarily on scaling model parameters and training data, while overlooking spatial scale as a core dimension of intelligence. Here, motivated by this missing dimension, we investigate spatial scale as a new scaling axis in foundation models and present MetaEarth3D, the first generative foundation model capable of spatially consistent generation at the planetary scale. Taking optical Earth observation simulation as a testbed, MetaEarth3D enables the generation of multi-level, unbounded, and diverse 3D scenes spanning large-scale terrains, medium-scale cities, and fine-grained street blocks. Built upon 10 million globally distributed real-world training images, MetaEarth3D demonstrates both strong visual realism and geospatial statistical realism. Beyond generation, MetaEarth3D serves as a generative data engine for diverse virtual environments in ultra-wide spatial intelligence. We argue that this study may help empower next-generation spatial intelligence for Earth observation.

AI.tldr: MetaEarth3D is the first generative foundation model for planetary-scale, spatially consistent 3D scene generation, addressing spatial scale as a missing dimension in generative AI beyond scaling parameters and data.
AI.motivation: Current generative AI models lack spatial scale awareness—they cannot capture geographic environments across thousands of kilometers or model the spatial structure of the large-scale physical world. This reveals a gap: AI progress has overlooked spatial scale as a core dimension of intelligence.
AI.method: MetaEarth3D is a generative foundation model built on 10 million globally distributed real-world training images, enabling multi-level, unbounded, and diverse 3D scene generation at planetary scale (large-scale terrains, medium-scale cities, fine-grained street blocks).
AI.result: MetaEarth3D demonstrates both strong visual realism and geospatial statistical realism, producing spatially consistent outputs across multiple scales.
AI.conclusion: MetaEarth3D serves as a generative data engine for ultra-wide spatial intelligence and may empower next-generation Earth observation and spatial intelligence systems.

title: AutoCompress: Critical Layer Isolation for Efficient Transformer Compression
summary: We present AutoCompress, a transformer compression method motivated by an empirical finding: in small transformers, Layer 0 carries disproportionately high task-critical information, with an NTK-based importance score of 3.6 compared to a maximum of 0.054 for all other layers -- a gap of over 60x. Based on this finding, we propose Critical Layer Isolation (CLI), an architecture that protects Layer 0 at full dimensionality, compresses all intermediate layers through a learned bottleneck, and restores the full dimension at the final layer. Applied to GPT-2 Medium (354.8M parameters), CLI-GPT2 achieves 204.5 perplexity on WikiText-103 with only 143.8M parameters -- a 2.47x compression ratio and 59.5% parameter reduction. Crucially, an ablation study demonstrates that a uniform bottleneck baseline of comparable size achieves only 571.8 perplexity under identical training conditions, confirming that the architectural decision to protect Layer 0 -- rather than simply reducing model size -- is the primary driver of performance. Code and checkpoints are publicly available.

AI.tldr: AutoCompress compresses transformers by identifying Layer 0 as critically important (60x higher NTK score), protecting it fully while aggressively compressing other layers, achieving 2.47x compression on GPT-2 Medium with reasonable perplexity.
AI.motivation: The paper finds that in small transformers, Layer 0 carries disproportionately high task-critical information (NTK score 3.6 vs ≤0.054 for other layers — a 60x gap), motivating a compression strategy that preserves this layer.
AI.method: Critical Layer Isolation (CLI) architecture: protect Layer 0 at full dimensionality, compress intermediate layers via a learned bottleneck, and restore full dimension at the final layer.
AI.result: CLI-GPT2 on WikiText-103: 204.5 perplexity with 143.8M parameters (from 354.8M), achieving 2.47x compression and 59.5% parameter reduction. A uniform bottleneck baseline at comparable size yields only 571.8 perplexity.
AI.conclusion: Protecting Layer 0 specifically — not simply reducing model size — is the primary driver of compression performance, as shown by the ablation study where uniform compression fails badly.

title: PExA: Parallel Exploration Agent for Complex Text-to-SQL
summary: LLM-based agents for text-to-SQL often struggle with latency-performance trade-off, where performance improvements come at the cost of latency or vice versa. We reformulate text-to-SQL generation within the lens of software test coverage where the original query is prepared with a suite of test cases with simpler, atomic SQLs that are executed in parallel and together ensure semantic coverage of the original query. After iterating on test case coverage, the final SQL is generated only when enough information is gathered, leveraging the explored test case SQLs to ground the final generation. We validated our framework on a state-of-the-art benchmark for text-to-SQL, Spider 2.0, achieving a new state-of-the-art with 70.2% execution accuracy.

AI.tldr: A novel text-to-SQL framework reformulates query generation as software test coverage, using parallel atomic SQL test cases to achieve SOTA 70.2% accuracy on Spider 2.0 while balancing latency and performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based text-to-SQL agents face a trade-off between latency and performance, where improving accuracy typically increases response time.
AI.method: The approach treats SQL generation like software test coverage: decompose the original query into simpler, atomic SQL test cases executed in parallel for semantic coverage, iterate on test coverage, then generate the final SQL only when sufficient information is gathered from the explored test cases.
AI.result: Achieved 70.2% execution accuracy on the Spider 2.0 benchmark, establishing a new state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: The test-coverage-inspired parallel execution approach effectively resolves the latency-performance trade-off, producing higher accuracy without increasing latency.

title: ZID-Net: Zero-Inference Diffusion Prior Decoupling Network for Single Image Dehazing
comment: Submitted to Neurocomputing. Includes 12 figures and 8 tables
summary: Single image dehazing is often constrained by a trade-off between restoration quality and computational efficiency. While efficient, CNN networks struggle to learn robust priors for dense and non-homogeneous haze. Conversely, diffusion models provide strong generative priors but suffer from severe inference latency and sampling instability. To address these limitations, we propose ZID-Net, a novel framework that explicitly decouples diffusion supervision from feed-forward inference. For efficient inference, we design a frequency-spatial decoupled feed-forward backbone. Within this backbone, a Channel-Spatial Laplacian Mask (CSLM) filters haze-amplified noise to extract purified structural details, while Lightweight Global Context Blocks (LGCBs) establish long-range spatial dependencies to capture the global variations of haze. A Dynamic Feature Arbitration Block (DFAB) then adaptively fuses these semantic and structural features for robust reconstruction. To provide this backbone with physical priors without the inference cost, we introduce a Zero-Inference Prior Propagation Head (ZI-PPH) during training. ZI-PPH leverages a conditional diffusion process to predict residual noise, providing degradation-aware structural supervision to the backbone. By discarding the diffusion branch at test time, ZID-Net integrates diffusion priors into a pure feed-forward architecture for accurate and efficient restoration. ZID-Net achieves 40.75 dB PSNR on the synthetic RESIDE dataset and outperforms existing methods with a 1.13 dB gain on real-world datasets. Additionally, it yields a 3.06 dB PSNR gain on the StateHaze1k remote sensing dataset with an inference time of just 19.35 ms. The project code is available at: https://github.com/XoomitLXH/ZID-Net.

AI.tldr: ZID-Net is a single image dehazing framework that decouples diffusion supervision from feed-forward inference, achieving state-of-the-art results (40.75 dB PSNR on RESIDE) with fast inference (19.35 ms) by using a frequency-spatial decoupled backbone trained with a diffusion-based prior propagation head that is discarded at test time.
AI.motivation: Existing dehazing methods face a trade-off: CNN networks are efficient but struggle with dense/non-homogeneous haze due to weak priors, while diffusion models offer strong generative priors but suffer from slow inference and sampling instability. The goal is to combine the accuracy of diffusion priors with the efficiency of feed-forward inference.
AI.method: ZID-Net proposes (1) a frequency-spatial decoupled feed-forward backbone with CSLM (filters haze-amplified noise) and LGCBs (long-range dependencies), plus DFAB (adaptive feature fusion); (2) a Zero-Inference Prior Propagation Head (ZI-PPH) that uses conditional diffusion during training only to provide degradation-aware structural supervision; (3) the diffusion branch is discarded at test time, leaving a pure feed-forward architecture.
AI.result: ZID-Net achieves 40.75 dB PSNR on synthetic RESIDE, outperforms existing methods with a 1.13 dB gain on real-world datasets, and achieves a 3.06 dB PSNR gain on StateHaze1k remote sensing dataset with only 19.35 ms inference time.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling diffusion supervision from inference, ZID-Net successfully integrates strong generative priors into an efficient feed-forward architecture, breaking the traditional trade-off between restoration quality and computational efficiency for single image dehazing.

title: Plug-and-Play Consistency Models for MIMO Channel Estimation
summary: Consistency models (CMs) learn a consistent mapping from multiple noise levels to the data endpoint and can therefore perform generative inference in one or a few steps. This property makes them attractive as learned priors for low-latency inverse problems. Multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) channel estimation under limited pilot overhead can be formulated as a high-dimensional linear inverse problem with an explicit measurement matrix, where data consistency alone is often insufficient for stable angular-domain channel recovery. This paper applies the plug-and-play consistency model (PnP-CM) framework to pilot-aided MIMO channel estimation. The PnP-CM inference procedure enforces the pilot observation model in the data-consistency update and invokes a pretrained CM denoiser in the prior update, thereby recovering the angular-domain channel vector within a small number of outer iterations. Preliminary experiments validate the feasibility of using CMs as low-latency channel-estimation priors and show that adaptive parameter scheduling and cross-scenario robustness remain important directions for further improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper applies plug-and-play consistency models (PnP-CM) to MIMO channel estimation, enabling few-step generative inference for low-latency angular-domain channel recovery under limited pilot overhead.
AI.motivation: MIMO channel estimation with limited pilot overhead is a high-dimensional inverse problem where data consistency alone is insufficient for stable recovery; there is a need for low-latency priors.
AI.method: The authors use a plug-and-play consistency model (PnP-CM) framework that alternates between enforcing the pilot observation model (data consistency) and invoking a pretrained CM denoiser (prior update) over a small number of outer iterations.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments confirm the feasibility of CMs as low-latency channel-estimation priors, though adaptive parameter scheduling and cross-scenario robustness require further improvement.
AI.conclusion: Consistency models show promise for efficient MIMO channel estimation, but further work is needed on adaptive scheduling and robustness across different scenarios.

title: Breaking Lock-In: Preserving Steerability under Low-Data VLA Post-Training
summary: Have you ever post-trained a generalist vision-language-action (VLA) policy on a small demonstration dataset, only to find that it stops responding to new instructions and is limited to behaviors observed during post-training? We identify this phenomenon as lock-in: after low-data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), the policy becomes overly specialized to the post-training data and fails to generalize to novel instructions, manifesting as concept lock-in (fixation on training objects/attributes) and spatial lock-in (fixation on training spatial targets). Many existing remedies introduce additional supervision signals, such as those derived from foundation models or auxiliary objectives, or rely on augmented datasets to recover generalization. In this paper, we show that the policy's internal pre-trained knowledge is sufficient: DeLock mitigates lock-in by preserving visual grounding during post-training and applying test-time contrastive prompt guidance to steer the policy's denoising dynamics according to novel instructions. Across eight simulation and real-world evaluations, DeLock consistently outperforms strong baselines and matches or exceeds the performance of a state-of-the-art generalist policy post-trained with substantially more curated demonstrations.

AI.tldr: DeLock mitigates lock-in (concept and spatial specialization) in post-trained VLA policies by preserving visual grounding and applying test-time contrastive prompt guidance, matching SOTA performance without extra supervision or data.
AI.motivation: Post-trained VLA policies suffer from "lock-in"—over-specialization to small demonstration datasets, causing failure to generalize to novel instructions (concept and spatial lock-in). Existing fixes require extra supervision or augmented data.
AI.method: DeLock preserves visual grounding during post-training and uses test-time contrastive prompt guidance to steer denoising dynamics according to novel instructions, leveraging the policy's existing pre-trained knowledge.
AI.result: Across 8 simulation and real-world evaluations, DeLock consistently outperforms strong baselines and matches/exceeds a SOTA generalist policy trained with substantially more curated demonstrations.
AI.conclusion: The policy's internal pre-trained knowledge alone is sufficient to overcome lock-in; extra supervision or augmented data is not required.

title: Fisher Information and Dynamical Sampling I
summary: Information theory is a powerful framework to capture aspects of dynamical systems with multiple degrees of freedom. Mathematically, the dynamics can be represented as a continuous curve $\mathcal{C}$ on a suitable hyperplane in flat space and the Fisher information provides the norm of an infinitesimal displacement along this curve. In many applications, however, we do not have direct access to $\mathcal{C}$. Instead, we have to reconstruct the latter from a time-series of measurements (obtained as samples of size $n$), which are represented by an ordered set of points $\widehat{\mathcal{C}}$ on the same hyperplane. In this work, we calculate the bias of the Fisher information for large $n$, which provides a quantitative estimation for how accurately the dynamics of a system can be reconstructed from a given set of sampled data. Based on this result, we show that a clustering of the degrees of freedom reduces the bias and thus improves the accuracy with which the new system can be described with the same data. Inspired by a recent proposal for such a clustering, we provide a quantitive assessment of the loss of information, which allows to estimate how much information about the dynamics of a system can reliably be extracted based on a given set of data. We illustrate our findings in the case of a simple compartmental model. Although the latter is inspired by epidemiology, the results of this work are applicable to very general dynamical models with multiple degrees of freedom.

AI.tldr: This paper calculates the bias of Fisher information for large sample sizes when reconstructing dynamical systems from time-series data. It shows that clustering degrees of freedom reduces this bias, improving reconstruction accuracy, and provides a quantitative framework for assessing information loss from sampled data.
AI.motivation: Information theory captures dynamics of systems with multiple degrees of freedom via Fisher information. However, direct access to the true dynamical curve is often unavailable; we must reconstruct it from sampled time-series data. The authors aim to quantify how accurately dynamics can be reconstructed from finite samples and how to improve this accuracy.
AI.method: The authors analytically calculate the bias of Fisher information estimators for large n, derive how clustering degrees of freedom reduces this bias, and provide a quantitative assessment of information loss. They illustrate findings using a compartmental model inspired by epidemiology.
AI.result: Clustering degrees of freedom reduces the bias of Fisher information estimation, thereby improving the accuracy of dynamical system reconstruction from sampled data. The paper provides a quantitative measure for how much reliable information can be extracted given finite data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables quantitative estimation of information loss in reconstructed dynamics, with broad applicability beyond epidemiology to general multi-degree-of-freedom dynamical systems.

title: Lost in the Vibrations: Vision Language Models Fail the Dynamic Gauges Test
summary: The digital transformation of industrial manufacturing increasingly relies on the ability of autonomous robots to interact with legacy infrastructure, particularly analog gauges. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated potential in zero-shot instrument recognition, their deployment in measurement systems remains constrained by an inherent inability to accurately analyze high-frequency temporal events and needle vibrations. This paper evaluates state-of-the-art models, including GPT-5 and Gemini 3, against the strict requirements of metrology and uncertainty quantification. To facilitate this evaluation, we introduce a novel dataset comprising video sequences of various gauge types: circular, linear, and Vernier, under diverse motion speed profiles. Our findings indicate that current VLMs exhibit limited ability in interpreting needle trajectories and scale semantics, failing to provide the traceability and reliability needed for safety-critical monitoring. The results demonstrate that these models have not yet achieved the performance necessary to be classified as trustworthy synthetic instruments under existing IEEE and ISO standards.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates VLMs (GPT-5, Gemini 3) for analog gauge reading in industrial settings, introduces a new multi-gauge video dataset, and finds these models fail to meet metrology standards for safety-critical monitoring.
AI.motivation: Industrial digital transformation requires autonomous robots to read legacy analog gauges, but current VLMs cannot handle high-frequency temporal events and needle vibrations needed for precise measurement.
AI.method: The authors evaluate state-of-the-art VLMs (GPT-5, Gemini 3) on a novel dataset of video sequences featuring circular, linear, and Vernier gauges under varying motion speed profiles.
AI.result: VLMs show limited ability in interpreting needle trajectories and scale semantics, failing to provide traceability and reliability required for safety-critical monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs have not achieved the performance needed to be classified as trustworthy synthetic instruments under existing IEEE and ISO standards.

title: Conformal PM2.5 Mapping Under Spatial Covariate Shift: Satellite-Reanalysis Fusion for Africa's Green Industrial Transition
comment: 9 pages, 8 figures, 6 tables. Index Terms: PM2.5 mapping, conformal prediction, covariate shift, spatial cross-validation, air quality, green industrialisation, trustworthy AI, Africa
summary: Africa's green industrialization imperative demands reliable infrastructure for monitoring air quality. We present a satellite-reanalysis PM2.5 fusion system trained on 2,068,901 records from 404 monitoring locations in 29 African countries (OpenAQ, 2017-2022), combining LightGBM with leakage-resistant spatial cross-validation and conformal prediction to quantify predictions and their geographic applicability limits. Under 5-fold location-grouped spatial cross-validation, LightGBM achieves RMSE = 30.83 +/- 5.07 ug/m3, MAE = 14.54 +/- 1.66 ug/m3, R2 = 0.134 +/- 0.023, and macro F1 = 0.336 +/- 0.018. This R2 is substantially below random-split benchmarks (>0.90) but reflects true geographic generalisation difficulty rather than model failure. Split conformal prediction targeting 90% marginal coverage reveals severe East Africa degradation (actual PICP = 65.3% vs. nominal 90%), consistent with medium-strength covariate shift (humidity KS = 0.2237, sat_pblh KS = 0.2558). We operationalise these findings through regional reliability flags (High/Medium/Low/Unreliable) and a monitor prioritisation score directing infrastructure expansion toward highest-burden unmonitored populations, directly supporting Africa's green industrial transition and SDGs 3.9, 7.1.2, 9, 11.6.2, and 13.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a satellite-reanalysis PM2.5 fusion system for Africa using LightGBM trained on 2M+ records from 404 monitoring sites across 29 countries, revealing poor geographic generalization (R2=0.13) under spatial cross-validation despite high random-split performance (>0.90), and provides reliability flags and prioritization scores to guide air quality monitoring infrastructure expansion for Africa's green industrial transition.
AI.motivation: Africa's green industrialization requires reliable air quality monitoring infrastructure, but the continent lacks sufficient ground monitoring networks. The paper aims to address this gap by creating a predictive system that works across diverse African regions and quantifies where predictions are reliable versus unreliable.
AI.method: The authors combine LightGBM with leakage-resistant spatial cross-validation (5-fold location-grouped) and split conformal prediction, trained on 2,068,901 records from 404 monitoring locations in 29 African countries (OpenAQ, 2017-2022). They also develop regional reliability flags (High/Medium/Low/Unreliable) and a monitor prioritization score.
AI.result: Under spatial cross-validation: RMSE=30.83±5.07 µg/m³, MAE=14.54±1.66 µg/m³, R²=0.134±0.023, macro F1=0.336±0.018. Split conformal prediction targeting 90% coverage showed severe East Africa degradation (actual PICP=65.3%). Covariate shift detected (humidity KS=0.2237, sat_pblh KS=0.2558).
AI.conclusion: The low R² reflects true geographic generalization difficulty in Africa rather than model failure, as evidenced by the large gap between random-split (>0.90) and spatial cross-validation performance. The paper operationalizes findings through reliability flags and a monitor prioritization score to directly support Africa's green industrial transition and multiple SDGs.

title: The Power of Power Law: Asymmetry Enables Compositional Reasoning
summary: Natural language data follows a power-law distribution, with most knowledge and skills appearing at very low frequency. While a common intuition suggests that reweighting or curating data towards a uniform distribution may help models better learn these long-tail skills, we find a counterintuitive result: across a wide range of compositional reasoning tasks, such as state tracking and multi-step arithmetic, training under power-law distributions consistently outperforms training under uniform distributions. To understand this advantage, we introduce a minimalist skill-composition task and show that learning under a power-law distribution provably requires significantly less training data. Our theoretical analysis reveals that power law sampling induces a beneficial asymmetry that improves the pathological loss landscape, which enables models to first acquire high-frequency skill compositions with low data complexity, which in turn serves as a stepping stone to efficiently learn rare long-tailed skills. Our results offer an alternative perspective on what constitutes an effective data distribution for training models.

AI.tldr: Training on power-law data distributions (where most skills appear rarely) consistently outperforms uniform data distribution training across compositional reasoning tasks, as power-law sampling creates a beneficial learning asymmetry that helps models first acquire common skills as stepping stones to learn rare ones more efficiently.
AI.motivation: To challenge the common intuition that reweighting or curating data towards a uniform distribution helps models learn long-tail skills, by investigating whether power-law distributions (which naturally occur in language data) might actually be more effective.
AI.method: The authors conduct experiments on compositional reasoning tasks (state tracking, multi-step arithmetic) and introduce a minimalist skill-composition task with theoretical analysis to prove the advantages of power-law distributions over uniform distributions.
AI.result: Power-law distribution training consistently outperforms uniform distribution training across various compositional reasoning tasks. Theoretical analysis proves that power-law sampling requires significantly less training data and improves the pathological loss landscape through beneficial asymmetry.
AI.conclusion: Power-law distributions offer a natural advantage for learning compositional skills, where high-frequency compositions serve as stepping stones for acquiring rare, long-tail skills. This provides an alternative perspective on effective data distribution design for model training.

title: Robust Grounding with MLLMs against Occlusion and Small Objects via Language-guided Semantic Cues
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have enhanced grounding capabilities in general scenes, their robustness in crowded scenes remains underexplored. Crowded scenes entail visual challenges (i.e., occlusion and small objects), which impair object semantics and degrade grounding performance. In contrast, language expressions are immune to such degradation and preserve object semantics. In light of these observations, we propose a novel method that overcomes such constraints by leveraging Language-Guided Semantic Cues (LGSCs). Specifically, our approach introduces a Semantic Cue Extractor (SCE) to derive semantic cues of objects from the visual pipeline of an MLLM. We then guide these cues using corresponding text embeddings to produce LGSCs as linguistic semantic priors. Subsequently, they are reintegrated into the original visual pipeline to refine object semantics. Extensive experiments and analyses demonstrate that incorporating LGSCs into an MLLM effectively improves grounding accuracy in crowded scenes.

AI.tldr: MLLMs struggle with grounding in crowded scenes due to occlusion and small objects. The authors propose using Language-Guided Semantic Cues (LGSCs) extracted via a Semantic Cue Extractor (SCE) to refine object semantics and improve grounding accuracy.
AI.motivation: MLLMs have limited robustness in crowded scenes where visual occlusions and small objects degrade object semantics and grounding performance.
AI.method: A Semantic Cue Extractor (SCE) extracts semantic cues from the visual pipeline, which are then guided by text embeddings to produce LGSCs. These are reintegrated into the visual pipeline to refine object semantics.
AI.result: Incorporating LGSCs into MLLMs effectively improves grounding accuracy in crowded scenes.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging language-guided semantic cues is an effective approach to overcome visual degradation in crowded scenes for MLLM grounding tasks.

title: Intention-Aware Semantic Agent Communications for AI Glasses
summary: Smart glasses are emerging as a promising interface between humans and artificial intelligence (AI) agents, enabling first-person perception, contextual awareness, and real-time assistance. However, continuous offloading of visual data from wearable devices to cloud-based vision-language models (VLMs) is fundamentally constrained by limited wireless bandwidth and energy resources. This paper proposes an intention-aware semantic agent communication framework for AI glasses, where data transmission is guided by user intention rather than raw pixel fidelity. In the proposed architecture, AI glasses act as an edge semantic agent while a server-side VLM executes high-level cognition and reasoning. The user intention can be inferred by the server-side VLM through the current transmitted content and the historical prompts. Driven by specific user intentions, the glasses adaptively preserve textual content, document layout, or object semantics before transmission. We evaluate three representative scenarios with different lightweight preprocessing tools on the AI glasses. Simulation results demonstrate that intention-aware preprocessing significantly achieves more than 50% bandwidth reduction depending on the current task while maintaining task performance. Moreover, semantic transmission exhibits graceful degradation under low SNRs. The findings demonstrate that aligning communication resources with user intention is essential for robust and efficient wearable AI agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an intention-aware semantic communication framework for AI glasses, where data transmission is guided by user intent rather than pixel fidelity. By preprocessing visual data based on user intention (text, layout, or object semantics), the system achieves over 50% bandwidth reduction while maintaining task performance, with graceful degradation under low signal-to-noise ratios.
AI.motivation: Continuous offloading of visual data from wearable devices (smart glasses) to cloud-based VLMs is constrained by limited wireless bandwidth and energy resources, making raw pixel transmission impractical for real-time AI assistance.
AI.method: An intention-aware semantic agent framework where AI glasses act as edge semantic agents, adaptively preprocessing visual data (preserving text, layout, or object semantics) based on inferred user intention from server-side VLM. Lightweight preprocessing tools are applied before transmission.
AI.result: Over 50% bandwidth reduction compared to task-agnostic transmission while maintaining task performance. Semantic transmission shows graceful degradation under low SNR conditions.
AI.conclusion: Aligning communication resources with user intention is essential for robust and efficient wearable AI agent systems, enabling practical deployment of smart glasses with cloud-based VLMs.

title: Cooperative Informative Sensing for Monitoring Dynamic Indoor Environments via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Monitoring human activity in indoor environments is important for applications such as facility management, safety assessment, and space utilization analysis. While mobile robot teams offer the potential to actively improve observation quality, existing multi-robot monitoring and active perception approaches typically rely on coverage or visitation based objectives that are weakly aligned with the accuracy requirements of human-centric monitoring tasks. In this work, we formulate cooperative active observation as a decentralized control problem in which multiple robots adjust their motion to directly optimize monitoring accuracy under partial observability. We propose a learning-based framework for cooperative policies from decentralized observations using multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), supported by an architecture that handles variable numbers of humans and temporal dependencies. Simulation results across diverse indoor environments and monitoring tasks show that the proposed approach consistently outperforms classical coverage, persistent monitoring, and learning-free multi-robot baselines, while remaining robust to changes in the number of observed humans.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a MARL-based framework for multi-robot cooperative active observation that directly optimizes monitoring accuracy of human activities in indoor environments, outperforming classical coverage and persistent monitoring baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-robot monitoring approaches rely on coverage or visitation objectives that are weakly aligned with the accuracy requirements of human-centric monitoring tasks.
AI.method: Formulates cooperative active observation as a decentralized control problem using multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with an architecture handling variable numbers of humans and temporal dependencies.
AI.result: The proposed approach consistently outperforms classical coverage, persistent monitoring, and learning-free multi-robot baselines across diverse indoor environments and tasks, while remaining robust to changes in the number of observed humans.
AI.conclusion: MARL-based cooperative active observation is effective for improving monitoring accuracy in multi-robot human-centric indoor monitoring tasks.

title: Densification Converses for Walker Constellations With Explicit Constants and Reuse Scaling Laws
summary: We establish densification converses for Walker LEO constellations under nearest-visible association in the full-frequency-reuse setting. Performance is evaluated under the invariant (stationary) measure induced by the constellation/Earth dynamics on the user--constellation ``phase state.'' A key Walker-specific feature, absent from unbounded planar models, is that association is restricted to a bounded visible cap determined by Earth geometry. Under power-law path-loss, a two-level antenna-gain model, i.i.d.\ nonnegative fading with unit mean and finite second moment, and nonzero noise, we prove that increasing the total satellite count $N=N_oN_s$ forces the aggregate interference to grow at least linearly in $N$, while the useful signal remains uniformly bounded above. Consequently, the downlink SINR coverage probability at any fixed threshold and the ergodic spectral efficiency both vanish as $N\to\infty$. The key technical ingredient is a deterministic visibility-annulus block lemma, uniform over all sufficiently large constellations and all "phase states", showing that a fixed fraction of visible satellites lies in a distance annulus strictly inside the horizon; this yields explicit finite-$N$ collapse bounds. In particular, we derive nonasymptotic $O(1/N)$ upper bounds on both coverage and ergodic spectral efficiency. Finally, in the case of frequency reuse through independent thinning, with activity probability $q$, we show that avoiding densification collapse necessarily requires $qN=O(1)$, equivalently a reuse factor $Ω(N)$, and we obtain a corresponding explicit $O(1/(qN))$ upper bound.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that increasing satellite count in Walker LEO constellations causes densification collapse—SINR coverage and spectral efficiency vanish at rate O(1/N)—due to bounded visible caps and linear interference growth.
AI.motivation: To understand whether increasing satellite density in LEO constellations (e.g., Starlink, OneWeb) degrades performance under full frequency reuse, and to establish fundamental limits unlike unbounded planar models that ignore Earth geometry constraints.
AI.method: Analytical proofs using a deterministic visibility-annulus block lemma under invariant measure, power-law path-loss, two-level antenna gain, i.i.d. fading, and nonzero noise. Nonasymptotic O(1/N) bounds derived for coverage probability and spectral efficiency.
AI.result: Aggregate interference grows at least linearly in N while useful signal remains bounded, causing SINR coverage and ergodic spectral efficiency to vanish as N→∞. For frequency reuse with activity probability q, avoiding collapse requires qN=O(1), i.e., reuse factor Ω(N).
AI.conclusion: Walker LEO constellations inevitably suffer densification collapse under full frequency reuse; interference mitigation via aggressive frequency reuse (activity scaling as 1/N) is necessary to sustain performance.

title: 2D Pre-Training for 3D Pose Estimation
comment: This work was completed as a graduate course project more than four years prior to this preprint. It is shared for archival and educational purposes. We open-source our code fork here: https://github.com/ECE740F21T01/pytorch-pose-hg-3d
summary: Pre-training is a general method that is used in a range of deep learning tasks. By first training a model on one task, and then further training on the downstream task used for final evaluation, the model is forced to learn a more general understanding of the input data. While pre-training has been applied to 3D Human Pose Estimation (HPE) previously, the scope of datasets used is typically very limited to some strong benchmarks, like Human3.6M. Therefore, in this project, we expand the scope of an existing 3D HPE scheme to be compatible with additional 2D and 3D HPE datasets, like Occlusion Person. We perform an extensive study on how aspects of 2D pre-training, such as model size, affect downstream performance, and to what extent pre-training can help the model generalize to different datasets. Experimental results show that 2D pre-training consistently outperforms training on 3D data alone, particularly in terms of computational efficiency. Finally, using MPII and Human3.6M, we are able to obtain an MPJPE score of under 64.5mm.

AI.tldr: This paper expands 2D pre-training for 3D Human Pose Estimation to more datasets, showing it consistently outperforms 3D-only training in efficiency and generalization, achieving under 64.5mm MPJPE.
AI.motivation: Previous 3D HPE pre-training methods were limited to strong benchmarks like Human3.6M; this work aims to expand pre-training to additional 2D and 3D datasets for better generalization.
AI.method: Extends an existing 3D HPE scheme to be compatible with additional datasets (e.g., Occlusion Person, MPII) and systematically studies how 2D pre-training factors (e.g., model size) affect downstream performance.
AI.result: 2D pre-training consistently outperforms 3D-only training, especially in computational efficiency; achieves MPJPE under 64.5mm on MPII and Human3.6M.
AI.conclusion: Expanding 2D pre-training to more datasets improves generalization and efficiency for 3D HPE, demonstrating clear advantages over training on 3D data alone.

title: Avionic Main Fuel Pump Simulation and Fault-Diagnosis Benchmark
summary: In many cyber-physical systems, especially in critical applications such as aeroplanes, data to train anomaly detection and diagnosis algorithms is lacking due to data protection issues and partial observability. To combat this inherent lack of data, we introduce a high-fidelity, physics-informed co-simulation of a common aircraft main-fuel-pump system modelled in \textsc{MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Fluids}. We also describe its generated time-series data with health and fault mode annotations. To show feasibility of our benchmark, we apply an unsupervised Recurrent Variational Autoencoder (RNN-VAE) for anomaly detection and a SOM-VAE for operating mode discretization, trained to separate healthy and faulty conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a physics-informed co-simulation benchmark for aircraft fuel pump systems using MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Fluids, generates annotated time-series data, and validates its feasibility with unsupervised RNN-VAE and SOM-VAE models for anomaly detection and mode discretization.
AI.motivation: Lack of training data for anomaly detection in critical cyber-physical systems (e.g., aircraft) due to data protection and partial observability.
AI.method: High-fidelity physics-informed co-simulation of an aircraft main-fuel-pump system in MATLAB/Simulink Simscape Fluids; unsupervised RNN-VAE for anomaly detection and SOM-VAE for operating mode discretization.
AI.result: Generated time-series data with health and fault annotations; demonstrated feasibility of the benchmark using unsupervised RNN-VAE and SOM-VAE models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed co-simulation benchmark effectively addresses data scarcity and shows promise for anomaly detection and diagnosis in safety-critical systems.

title: On the Existence of an Inverse Solution for Preference-Based Reductions in Argumentation
summary: Preference-based argumentation frameworks (PAFs) extend Dung's approach to abstract argumentation (AAFs) by encoding preferences over arguments. Such preferences control the transformation of attacks into defeats, and different approaches to doing so result in different reductions from a PAF to an AAF. In this paper we consider a PAF inverse problem which takes an argumentation graph, a labelling and a semantics as an input, and outputs a ``yes" or ``no" as to whether there is a preference relation between the arguments which can yield the desired labelling. This inverse problem has applications in areas including preference elicitation and explainability. We consider this problem in the context of the four most widely-used preference based reductions under the complete semantics. We show that in most cases, the problem can be answered in polynomial time.

AI.tldr: The paper studies the inverse problem in Preference-based Argumentation Frameworks (PAFs): given a graph, labelling, and semantics, determine if a preference relation exists that yields that labelling under complete semantics. It shows this problem is solvable in polynomial time for most cases.
AI.motivation: To address the inverse problem of determining whether a given argument graph and labelling can be realized by some preference relation in PAFs, which has applications in preference elicitation and explainability.
AI.method: The authors analyze four widely-used preference-based reductions from PAFs to AAFs under complete semantics, studying whether a given labelling can be achieved by some preference relation.
AI.result: The inverse problem can be answered in polynomial time for most of the considered reductions.
AI.conclusion: The inverse problem in PAFs is tractable under common reductions and complete semantics, enabling practical applications.

title: Bridging Restoration and Generation Manifolds in One-Step Diffusion for Real-World Super-Resolution
summary: Pretrained diffusion models have revolutionized real-world image super-resolution (Real-ISR) but suffer from computational bottlenecks due to iterative sampling. Recent single-step distillation accelerates inference but faces a stark perception-distortion trade-off due to rigid timestep initialization, distributional trajectory mismatches, and fragile stochastic modulation. To address this, we present Adaptive Inversion and Degradation-aware Sampling for Real-ISR (IDaS-SR), a one-step framework bridging the deterministic restoration and stochastic generation manifolds. At its core, the Manifold Inversion Noise Estimator (MINE) resolves these initialization and trajectory mismatches by predicting a severity-aware timestep and inversion noise, precisely anchoring low-quality latents onto the diffusion trajectory. Furthermore, to mitigate fragile stochastic modulation, we propose CHARIOT, a continuous generative steering mechanism. By rescheduling trajectories and interpolating noise, it enables explicit navigation of the perception-distortion boundary without compromising structural priors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IDaS-SR outperforms state-of-the-art methods, seamlessly transitioning from a rigorous structural restorer to a sophisticated texture hallucinator in a single inference step.

AI.tldr: IDaS-SR is a one-step real-world image super-resolution framework that uses Manifold Inversion Noise Estimator (MINE) and CHARIOT steering to overcome the perception-distortion trade-off in single-step diffusion models, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Pretrained diffusion models for Real-ISR suffer from computational bottlenecks due to iterative sampling, and single-step distillation faces a stark perception-distortion trade-off caused by rigid timestep initialization, distributional trajectory mismatches, and fragile stochastic modulation.
AI.method: Proposes IDaS-SR with two key components: (1) MINE (Manifold Inversion Noise Estimator) that predicts severity-aware timestep and inversion noise to anchor low-quality latents onto the diffusion trajectory; (2) CHARIOT, a continuous generative steering mechanism that reschedules trajectories and interpolates noise to navigate the perception-distortion boundary.
AI.result: IDaS-SR outperforms state-of-the-art methods, seamlessly transitioning from a structural restorer to a texture hallucinator in a single inference step.
AI.conclusion: The proposed one-step framework effectively bridges deterministic restoration and stochastic generation manifolds, resolving key limitations of single-step distillation for Real-ISR.

title: Coordinated Multipoint Anti-jamming Beam Pattern Synthesis: From AI Accelerated Algorithm to Hardware Implementation
summary: This paper presents a deep unfolding-supported coordinated multipoint beam pattern synthesis (DUCoMP-BPS) scheme to overcome the high complexity, poor adaptability, and limited scalability of traditional cell-free anti-jamming beamforming. In the proposed design, access points (APs) independently determine analog beamforming using local angle information, while the central processing unit (CPU) performs cooperative digital beamforming with only a single AP-CPU interaction, significantly reducing fronthaul overhead. To further improve efficiency, a deep unfolding strategy transforms the costly step size search in analog beamforming into a trainable parameter, where an offline-trained complex-valued neural network enables fast and adaptive online inference. Simulation results show that the complexity of DUCoMP-BPS scales linearly with the number of APs, reduces single-AP analog beamforming runtime by about 67% compared to conventional optimization, and achieves superior nulling performance over purely data-driven approaches. Hardware feasibility is validated on an Advanced RISC Machine-Field Programmable Gate Array (ARM-FPGA) heterogeneous platform, where algorithm-hardware co-verification and hardware-software decoupling enable efficient parallelism and low-latency execution. Finally, anechoic chamber measurements under practical hardware imperfections confirm robust beamforming performance, demonstrating the strong potential of DUCoMP-BPS for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes DUCoMP-BPS, a deep unfolding-supported coordinated multipoint beam pattern synthesis scheme for cell-free anti-jamming beamforming, which reduces complexity and fronthaul overhead while achieving fast, scalable, and hardware-validated performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome the high complexity, poor adaptability, and limited scalability of traditional cell-free anti-jamming beamforming methods.
AI.method: A hybrid architecture where APs independently determine analog beamforming using local angle information, the CPU performs cooperative digital beamforming with minimal interaction, and a deep unfolding strategy trains a complex-valued neural network to replace costly step size searches, enabling fast online inference.
AI.result: Complexity scales linearly with AP count; single-AP analog beamforming runtime reduced by ~67% versus conventional optimization; superior nulling performance over purely data-driven approaches; validated on ARM-FPGA hardware and in anechoic chamber measurements.
AI.conclusion: DUCoMP-BPS demonstrates strong potential for real-world deployment due to its low complexity, fast inference, robustness under hardware imperfections, and hardware-software co-verification success.

title: BridgeACT: Bridging Human Demonstrations to Robot Actions via Unified Tool-Target Affordances
summary: Learning robot manipulation from human videos is appealing due to the scale and diversity of human demonstrations, but transferring such demonstrations to executable robot behavior remains challenging. Prior work either relies on robot data for downstream adaptation or learns affordance representations that remain at the perception level and do not directly support real-world execution. We present BridgeACT, an affordance-driven framework that learns robotic manipulation directly from human videos without requiring any robot demonstration data. Our key idea is to model affordance as an embodiment-agnostic intermediate representation that bridges human demonstrations and robot actions. BridgeACT decomposes manipulation into two complementary problems: where to grasp and how to move. To this end, BridgeACT first grounds task-relevant affordance regions in the current scene, and then predicts task-conditioned 3D motion affordances from human demonstrations. The resulting affordances are mapped to robot actions through a grasping module and a lightweight closed-loop motion controller, enabling direct deployment on real robots. In addition, we represent complex manipulation tasks as compositions of affordance operations, which allows a unified treatment of diverse tasks and object-to-object interactions. Experiments on real-world manipulation tasks show that BridgeACT outperforms prior baselines and generalizes to unseen objects, scenes, and viewpoints.

AI.tldr: BridgeACT learns robotic manipulation directly from human videos without robot data, using affordance as an intermediate representation to bridge human demonstrations and robot actions by decomposing tasks into where to grasp and how to move.
AI.motivation: Learning robot manipulation from human videos is appealing due to scale and diversity, but transferring demonstrations to executable robot behavior remains challenging, as prior work either needs robot data or learns perception-level affordances that don't support real-world execution.
AI.method: BridgeACT models affordance as an embodiment-agnostic intermediate representation, decomposing manipulation into where to grasp (grounding task-relevant affordance regions) and how to move (predicting task-conditioned 3D motion affordances from human videos), then maps them to robot actions via a grasping module and lightweight closed-loop motion controller.
AI.result: BridgeACT outperforms prior baselines on real-world manipulation tasks and generalizes to unseen objects, scenes, and viewpoints.
AI.conclusion: BridgeACT demonstrates that affordance-driven learning from human videos alone is sufficient for real-world robot manipulation, and representing complex tasks as compositions of affordance operations enables unified treatment of diverse tasks and object-to-object interactions.

title: Shape of Memory: a Geometric Analysis of Machine Unlearning in Second-Order Optimizers
comment: Full experiment data available at secondstreetlabs.io
summary: We argue that current definitions of machine unlearning are underspecified for second-order optimizers. We compare first-order and second-order learners for their ability to handle the data deletion task with varying degrees of eigendecomposition to mimic the loss model memory. While both first and second-order methods realign with the ideal counterfactul in terms of performance and gradient, the second-order optimizer shows significant volatility in the optimizer state. This indicates residual information, supposedly deleted, that isn't detectable by first-order analysis. Various eigendecay treatments show that stability and information loss is regained only under controlled state pertubation where geometric information (or memory) is erased.

AI.tldr: Current machine unlearning definitions are insufficient for second-order optimizers. Second-order methods show hidden residual information after deletion that persists in the optimizer state despite first-order metrics suggesting successful unlearning. Only by directly erasing geometric memory (via eigendecay treatments) can stability and true information removal be achieved.
AI.motivation: To expose that standard machine unlearning evaluations based on first-order analysis fail to detect residual information in second-order optimizers, and to propose more rigorous unlearning criteria that account for optimizer state.
AI.method: Compare first-order vs second-order learners on data deletion tasks while varying degrees of eigendecomposition to mimic loss of model memory. Analyze performance, gradient alignment, and optimizer state volatility.
AI.result: Both methods realign with ideal counterfactual in performance and gradient. However, second-order optimizer shows significant state volatility, revealing residual information undetectable by first-order analysis. Controlled state perturbation (erasing geometric information) restores stability and true information loss.
AI.conclusion: Definitions of machine unlearning need to account for optimizer state, not just model output/gradients. Second-order optimizers can retain hidden memory of deleted data, requiring explicit manipulation of geometric information for complete unlearning.

title: Intervention-Aware Multiscale Representation Learning from Imaging Phenomics and Perturbation Transcriptomics
summary: Microscopy-based phenotypic profiling is scalable for drug discovery but lacks the mechanistic depth of transcriptomics, which remains costly and scarce. Existing multimodal approaches either use images to support other modalities or naively align representations by sample identity, ignoring cell-type and dose variations in weakly paired data-limiting generalization to unseen interventions. In this paper, we introduce an intervention-aware distillation framework that leverages perturbational transcriptomics to guide image representation learning. A transcriptome-conditioned teacher integrates gene expression and intervention metadata to produce soft distributions over a chemistry-aware codebook organized by drug similarity. The teacher employs a fine-tuned single-cell foundation model to encode cell-type context and disentangle dose effects. An image-only student learns to predict these distributions from microscopy alone, distilling mechanistic knowledge while operating independently at test time. This design emphasizes intervention semantics rather than identity alignment and explicitly handles dose and cell-type mismatches. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that transcriptomic guidance tightens the risk bound for image-based prediction. On Cell Painting and RxRx datasets paired with L1000, our method significantly improves one-shot transfer to unseen interventions and drug-target gene discovery compared to self-supervised and alignment baselines.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an intervention-aware distillation framework that transfers mechanistic knowledge from transcriptomics to microscopy images using a chemistry-aware codebook and single-cell foundation models, improving one-shot transfer to unseen interventions without requiring paired data at test time.
AI.motivation: Microscopy-based phenotypic profiling is scalable but lacks mechanistic depth; transcriptomics provides rich mechanistic insights but is costly and scarce. Existing multimodal methods either use images to support other modalities or naively align by sample identity, failing to generalize to unseen interventions due to cell-type and dose variations.
AI.method: A transcriptome-conditioned teacher integrates gene expression and intervention metadata to produce soft distributions over a chemistry-aware codebook organized by drug similarity, using a fine-tuned single-cell foundation model for cell-type context and dose disentanglement. An image-only student learns to predict these distributions from microscopy alone, distilling mechanistic knowledge without needing transcriptomics at test time.
AI.result: On Cell Painting and RxRx datasets paired with L1000, the method significantly improves one-shot transfer to unseen interventions and drug-target gene discovery compared to self-supervised and alignment baselines.
AI.conclusion: The intervention-aware distillation framework emphasizes intervention semantics over identity alignment and handles dose/cell-type mismatches, with theoretical guarantees that transcriptomic guidance tightens risk bounds for image-based prediction.

title: Towards Understanding the Expressive Power of GNNs with Global Readout
summary: We study the expressive power of message-passing aggregate-combine-readout graph neural networks (ACR-GNNs). Particularly, we focus on the first-order (FO) properties expressible by this formalism. While a tight logical characterisation remains a difficult open question, we make two contributions towards answering it. First, we show that sum aggregation and readout suffice for GNNs to capture FO properties that cannot be expressed in the logic C2 on both directed and undirected graphs. This strengthens known results by Hauke and Wał{\k e}ga (2026) where aggregation and readout functions are specially crafted for the task. Second, we identify two natural ways of restoring characterisability (with regard to C2) for ACR-GNNs. One option is to limit local aggregation (without imposing restrictions on global readout), whilst the second is to run ACR-GNNs over graphs of bounded degree (but unbounded size). In both cases, the FO properties captured by GNNs are exactly those definable by a formula in graded modal logic with global counting modalities. Our results thus establish an innate lower- and upper-bound in terms of how far (fragments of) C2 can be taken to characterise GNNs, and imply that is indeed the unbounded interaction of aggregation and readout that pushes the logical expressive power of GNNs above C2.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the expressive power of ACR-GNNs in terms of first-order logic, showing that sum aggregation/readout allows capturing FO properties beyond C2 logic, and identifies two ways (bounded aggregation or bounded-degree graphs) to restore characterizability by C2 fragments.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to tightly characterize the logical expressive power of message-passing GNNs, which remains an open question. It seeks to understand how aggregation and readout functions affect what FO properties GNNs can express.
AI.method: The authors analyze ACR-GNNs (aggregate-combine-readout GNNs) through a logical lens, comparing their expressiveness to fragments of C2 logic (first-order logic with counting quantifiers). They prove positive results (sum aggregation suffices to exceed C2) and two restrictive settings where characterizability is restored.
AI.result: (1) Sum aggregation+readout enables GNNs to express FO properties beyond C2 on both directed and undirected graphs. (2) Bounded local aggregation or bounded-degree graphs restore characterizability, exactly matching graded modal logic with global counting modalities.
AI.conclusion: The unbounded interaction between aggregation and readout is what pushes GNNs' logical expressiveness beyond C2, establishing precise lower and upper bounds for when C2 fragments can characterize GNNs.

title: Towards Causally Interpretable Wi-Fi CSI-Based Human Activity Recognition with Discrete Latent Compression and LTL Rule Extraction
summary: We address Human Activity Recognition (HAR) utilizing Wi-Fi Channel State Information (CSI) under the joint requirements of causal interpretability, symbolic controllability, and direct operation on high-dimensional raw signals. Deep neural models achieve strong predictive performance on CSI-based HAR (CHAR), yet rely on continuous latent representations that are opaque and difficult to modify; purely symbolic approaches, in contrast, cannot process raw CSI streams. We propose a fully automatic and strictly decoupled pipeline in which CSI magnitude windows are compressed by a categorical variational autoencoder with Gumbel-Softmax latent variables under a capacity-controlled objective, yielding a compact discrete representation. The encoder is then frozen and used as a deterministic mapping to one-hot latent trajectories. Causal discovery is performed on these trajectories to estimate class-conditional temporal dependency graphs. Statistically supported lagged dependencies are translated into Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) rules, producing a fully symbolic and deterministic classifier based solely on rule evaluation and aggregation, without any learned discriminative head. Because rules are defined over discrete latent variables, antenna-specific rule sets can in principle be combined at the symbolic level, enabling structured multi-antenna fusion without retraining the encoder. Results from CHAR Latent Temporal Rule Extraction (CHARL-TRE) indicate competitive performance while preserving explicit temporal and causal structure, showing that deterministic symbolic classification grounded in unsupervised discrete latent representations constitutes a viable alternative to end-to-end black-box models for wireless HAR.

AI.tldr: Proposes CHARL-TRE, a fully automatic pipeline that combines a categorical VAE with Gumbel-Softmax for discrete latent representation from raw CSI, then performs causal discovery on latent trajectories to extract LTL rules for symbolic, interpretable HAR classification without any learned discriminative head.
AI.motivation: Deep neural models for CSI-based HAR achieve strong predictive performance but rely on opaque, continuous latent representations that are difficult to interpret and modify. Purely symbolic approaches cannot process raw CSI streams. There is a need for a method that combines interpretability, controllability, and direct raw-signal operation.
AI.method: (1) Categorical VAE with Gumbel-Softmax latent variables under capacity-controlled objective compresses CSI magnitude windows into discrete representations. (2) Frozen encoder maps raw CSI to deterministic one-hot latent trajectories. (3) Causal discovery estimates class-conditional temporal dependency graphs. (4) Statistically supported lagged dependencies are translated into LTL rules. (5) Rules are evaluated and aggregated to form a fully symbolic, deterministic classifier without any learned discriminative head.
AI.result: CHARL-TRE achieves competitive performance on CHAR tasks while preserving explicit temporal and causal structure. Antenna-specific rule sets can be combined symbolically for structured multi-antenna fusion without encoder retraining.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic symbolic classification grounded in unsupervised discrete latent representations is a viable alternative to end-to-end black-box models for wireless HAR, offering causal interpretability and symbolic controllability alongside competitive performance.

title: Fluid Antenna Enabled Compact Ultra Massive Antenna Array for Satellite Communications
summary: Satellites provide seamless coverage and are critical for emergency communications during natural disasters. However, their performance is constrained by limited spectrum and high deployment cost. To address these issues, we propose a fluid antenna system (FAS)-based solution that enables dynamic signal adaptation. Building on this concept, a compact ultra-massive antenna array (CUMA) is introduced, where multiple ports are simultaneously activated to coherently combine signal components. This design mitigates interference while reducing cost, as each fluid antenna requires only a single RF chain yet achieves significant improvement in the received signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR). We consider a satellite CUMA network where all ground users share the same satellite for uplink transmission, and CUMA is employed to suppress inter-user interference. Closed-form expressions for the received signal power, interference power, and their distributions are derived. Based on these results, the outage probability is obtained in a unified form along with an accurate approximation, and the ergodic rate is characterized. Our analysis identifies the conditions under which CUMA outperforms maximum ratio combining in satellite systems. Notably, with sufficiently compact fluid antenna configurations, the received signal becomes deterministic, indicating that system performance is dominated by interference statistics. Moreover, increasing the number of ports yields a linear beamforming gain. Numerical results further compare orthogonal and non-orthogonal multiple access CUMA, showing that the latter achieves superior performance under wideband conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a fluid antenna system (FAS) and compact ultra-massive antenna array (CUMA) to improve satellite communication by mitigating interference, reducing cost, and achieving linear beamforming gain, with closed-form performance analysis showing superiority over conventional methods.
AI.motivation: Satellites face limited spectrum and high deployment costs, motivating the need for a cost-effective solution that improves signal quality and suppresses interference in emergency communication scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose a FAS-based CUMA where multiple ports are activated simultaneously with a single RF chain, and derive closed-form expressions for received signal power, interference power, outage probability, and ergodic rate.
AI.result: CUMA achieves linear beamforming gain with more ports; received signal becomes deterministic under compact configurations; and non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) outperforms orthogonal multiple access (OMA) under wideband conditions.
AI.conclusion: CUMA is a promising low-cost solution for satellite networks, with conditions identified where it surpasses maximum ratio combining, and system performance dominated by interference rather than signal statistics.

title: Modular Sensory Stream for Integrating Physical Feedback in Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Humans understand and interact with the real world by relying on diverse physical feedback beyond visual perception. Motivated by this, recent approaches attempt to incorporate physical sensory signals into Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs). However, they typically focus on a single type of physical signal, failing to capture the heterogeneous and complementary nature of real-world interactions. In this paper, we propose MoSS, a modular sensory stream framework that adapts VLAs to leverage multiple sensory signals for action prediction. Specifically, we introduce decoupled modality streams that integrate heterogeneous physical signals into the action stream via joint cross-modal self-attention. To enable stable incorporation of new modalities, we adopt a two-stage training scheme that freezes pretrained VLA parameters in the early stage. Furthermore, to better capture contact interaction dynamics, we incorporate an auxiliary task that predicts future physical signals. Through extensive real-world experiments, we demonstrate that MoSS successfully augments VLAs to leverage diverse physical signals (i.e., tactile and torque), integrating multiple signals to achieve synergistic performance gains.

AI.tldr: MoSS is a modular sensory stream framework that enhances Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) by integrating multiple physical signals (tactile, torque) through decoupled modality streams and two-stage training, achieving superior action prediction in real-world experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing VLAs primarily rely on visual perception and incorporate at most a single type of physical signal, failing to capture the heterogeneous and complementary nature of real-world physical interactions.
AI.method: MoSS introduces (1) decoupled modality streams with joint cross-modal self-attention to integrate heterogeneous physical signals; (2) a two-stage training scheme freezing pretrained VLA parameters in the early stage; (3) an auxiliary task predicting future physical signals to better capture contact interaction dynamics.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show MoSS successfully augments VLAs to leverage diverse physical signals (tactile and torque), achieving synergistic performance gains by integrating multiple signals.
AI.conclusion: MoSS demonstrates that incorporating multiple complementary physical sensory signals through a modular framework can significantly improve VLA performance for real-world action prediction tasks.

title: Information-Theoretic Measures in AI: A Practical Decision Guide
