AI.conclusion: Multi-population training promotes population-robust representations, establishing a principled framework for learning robust, generalizable, and clinically reliable EEG biomarkers for multi-site biomedical applications.

title: Supporting Family-School Partnerships with Robot-Facilitated Home-Based Activities
comment: Proceedings of the 25th Interaction Design and Children Conference (IDC '26)
summary: Family-school partnerships (FSP) are critical to children's development, yet families often face barriers such as time constraints, fragmented communication, and limited opportunities for meaningful engagement. As a step toward facilitating broader family-school partnerships, we explore a novel approach that integrates a social robot into family settings, specifically supporting home-based activities. Through interviews and co-design sessions, we designed and developed a robotic system informed by both parents and children, that supported, among other interactions, family communication about school topics. We evaluated the robot in a week-long, in-home study with 10 families. Our findings show how families integrated the robot into daily life, how parental facilitation styles shaped use, and how families perceived both the helpfulness and challenges of the robot. We contribute empirical insights, a modular system, and design implications for family- and child-robot interactions. We discuss ethical and privacy considerations, and broaden the design space for technologies supporting family-school partnerships.

AI.tldr: This paper explores using a social robot to support family-school partnerships by designing and testing a robotic system in 10 families' homes for one week, finding it facilitated school-related communication but was shaped by parental facilitation styles.
AI.motivation: Family-school partnerships are critical for children's development, but families face barriers like time constraints, fragmented communication, and limited meaningful engagement opportunities.
AI.method: Interviews and co-design sessions with parents and children to design a robotic system, followed by a week-long in-home evaluation study with 10 families.
AI.result: Families integrated the robot into daily life; parental facilitation styles shaped usage; families perceived both helpfulness and challenges of the robot.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes empirical insights, a modular system design, and design implications for family- and child-robot interactions, while discussing ethical and privacy considerations for technologies supporting family-school partnerships.

title: Attention-Augmented YOLOv8 with Ghost Convolution for Real-Time Vehicle Detection in Intelligent Transportation Systems
summary: Accurate vehicle detection is a critical component of autonomous driving, traffic surveillance, and intelligent transportation systems. This paper presents an enhanced YOLOv8n-based model that integrates the Ghost Module, Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), and Deformable Convolutional Networks v2 (DCNv2) to improve detection performance. The Ghost Module reduces feature redundancy through efficient feature generation, CBAM refines feature representation via channel and spatial attention, and DCNv2 enhances adaptability to geometric variations in vehicle structures. Evaluated on the KITTI dataset, the proposed model achieves 95.4% mAP@0.5, representing an 8.97% improvement over the baseline YOLOv8n, along with 96.2% precision, 93.7% recall, and a 94.93% F1-score. Comparative analysis against seven state-of-the-art detectors demonstrates consistent superiority across key performance metrics, while ablation studies validate the individual and combined contributions of the integrated modules. By addressing feature redundancy, attention refinement, and spatial adaptability, the proposed approach offers a robust and computationally efficient solution for vehicle detection in diverse and complex traffic environments.

AI.tldr: This paper enhances YOLOv8n with Ghost Module, CBAM, and DCNv2 for vehicle detection, achieving 95.4% mAP@0.5 on KITTI dataset, an 8.97% improvement over baseline.
AI.motivation: Accurate vehicle detection is critical for autonomous driving, traffic surveillance, and intelligent systems, but existing models struggle with feature redundancy, attention refinement, and adaptability to geometric variations.
AI.method: The proposed model integrates three modules into YOLOv8n: Ghost Module (reduces feature redundancy), CBAM (channel and spatial attention refinement), and DCNv2 (enhances adaptability to geometric variations in vehicle structures).
AI.result: On KITTI dataset: 95.4% mAP@0.5 (8.97% improvement), 96.2% precision, 93.7% recall, 94.93% F1-score. Outperforms seven SOTA detectors. Ablation studies confirm contributions of each module.
AI.conclusion: The approach addresses feature redundancy, attention refinement, and spatial adaptability, offering a robust and computationally efficient solution for vehicle detection in diverse traffic environments.

title: K-Score: Kalman Filter as a Principled Alternative to Reward Normalization in Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted in NewInML Workshop, The 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2025).\href{https://icml.cc/virtual/2025/affinity-event/39980}{Event Page}
summary: We propose a simple yet effective alternative to reward normalization in policy gradient reinforcement learning by integrating a 1D Kalman filter for online reward estimation. Instead of relying on fixed heuristics, our method recursively estimates the latent reward mean, smoothing high-variance returns and adapting to non-stationary environments. This approach incurs minimal overhead and requires no modification to existing policy architectures. Experiments on \textit{LunarLander} and \textit{CartPole} demonstrate that Kalman-filtered rewards significantly accelerate convergence and reduce training variance compared to standard normalization techniques. Code is available at https://github.com/Sumxiaa/Kalman_Normalization.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes replacing reward normalization in policy gradient RL with a 1D Kalman filter for online reward estimation, which smooths high-variance returns and adapts to non-stationary environments, showing faster convergence and lower variance on LunarLander and CartPole.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of fixed heuristic-based reward normalization methods in policy gradient RL, particularly their inability to adapt to non-stationary reward distributions and high variance.
AI.method: Integrates a 1D Kalman filter to recursively estimate the latent reward mean, replacing traditional reward normalization with minimal overhead and no architecture changes.
AI.result: On LunarLander and CartPole, Kalman-filtered rewards accelerate convergence and reduce training variance compared to standard normalization techniques.
AI.conclusion: A simple Kalman filter-based reward estimation is an effective, low-cost alternative to standard reward normalization in policy gradient methods.

title: Constraint-Based Analysis of Reasoning Shortcuts in Neurosymbolic Learning
comment: This is the full version of a paper appearing at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026)
summary: Neurosymbolic systems can satisfy logical constraints during learning without achieving the intended concept-label correspondence; this is a problem known as reasoning shortcuts. We formalize reasoning shortcuts as a constraint satisfaction problem and investigate under which conditions concept mappings are uniquely determined by the constraints. We prove that a discrimination property (requiring that no valid concept mapping can be transformed into another valid mapping by swapping two concept values) is necessary for shortcut-freeness under bijective mappings, but demonstrate via a counterexample that it is insufficient even when the constraint graph is connected. We develop an ASP-based algorithm that verifies whether a given constraint set uniquely determines the intended concept mapping, with proven soundness and completeness. When shortcuts are detected, a greedy repair algorithm eliminates them by augmenting the constraint set, converging in at most $k$ iterations, where $k$ is the number of alternative valid mappings. We further provide a complexity classification: deciding shortcut-freeness is coNP-complete, counting shortcuts is #P-complete, and finding minimal repairs is NP-hard. We also establish sample complexity bounds showing that logarithmically many label queries suffice for disambiguation in favorable cases, while querying all ambiguous positions suffices in the worst case. Experiments across eight benchmark domains validate our approach.

AI.tldr: This paper formalizes reasoning shortcuts in neurosymbolic learning, proves conditions for shortcut-freeness, develops ASP-based verification and greedy repair algorithms, and provides complexity classifications and sample complexity bounds, validated across eight benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Neurosymbolic systems can satisfy logical constraints without learning the correct concept-label correspondence, a problem known as reasoning shortcuts. The authors aim to formalize, detect, and fix this issue.
AI.method: Formalizes reasoning shortcuts as a constraint satisfaction problem; proves necessary condition (discrimination property); develops ASP-based verification algorithm with soundness/completeness; greedy repair algorithm; complexity classification; sample complexity analysis.
AI.result: Discrimination property is necessary but insufficient for shortcut-freeness. ASP algorithm verifies shortcut-freeness. Greedy repair converges in ≤k iterations. Complexity: coNP-complete (decision), #P-complete (counting), NP-hard (minimal repair). Log queries suffice in favorable cases. Validated on 8 benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning shortcuts are formally characterized and can be detected/repaired efficiently in many cases, though the problem is computationally hard in general.

title: Betting for Sim-to-Real Performance Evaluation
comment: Accepted to RSS 2026, with DOI pending
summary: This paper studies the problem of robot performance evaluation, focusing on how to obtain accurate and efficient estimates of real-world behavior under severe constraints on physical experimentation. Such estimates are essential for benchmarking algorithms, comparing design alternatives, validating controllers, and supporting certification or regulatory decision-making, yet real-world testing with physical robots is often expensive, time-consuming, and safety-limited. To mitigate the scarcity of real-world trials, sim-to-real methodologies are commonly employed, using low-cost simulators to inform, supplement, or prioritize physical experiments. Departing from (and complementary to) existing approaches in variance reduction (e.g., importance-sampling variants) or bias-correction (e.g., through prediction-powered inference or learned control variates), we examine this performance-evaluation problem through the lens of betting. We establish theoretical conditions under which a betting mechanism can yield accurate and efficient estimates (provably outperforming the Monte Carlo estimator) and we characterize how such bets should be constructed. We further develop theoretically grounded yet practically implementable approximations of the ideal bet, and we provide concrete decision rules that diagnose when these approximate betting strategies are working as intended. We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods using both synthetic examples and cross-fidelity computational simulators. Notably, we also showcase an illustrative case in which a group of synthetic distributions are used to infer the real-world pick-and-place accuracy of a robotic manipulator, a seemingly unconventional sim-to-real transfer that becomes natural and feasible under the proposed betting perspective. Programs for reproducing empirical results are available at https://github.com/ISUSAIL/Bet4Sim2Real.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a betting-based framework for robot performance evaluation that provably outperforms standard Monte Carlo estimators under severe real-world testing constraints, with theoretical guarantees and practical approximations demonstrated on synthetic data and a sim-to-real robotic pick-and-place task.
AI.motivation: Real-world robot testing is expensive, time-consuming, and safety-limited, making accurate performance evaluation challenging. Existing sim-to-real methods like variance reduction or bias-correction have limitations, motivating a new betting-based perspective.
AI.method: The authors frame performance evaluation as a betting problem, establish theoretical conditions for optimal betting strategies, develop practical approximations of ideal bets, and design diagnostic decision rules to verify when these approximations work correctly.
AI.result: The betting method provably outperforms Monte Carlo estimators theoretically and empirically. It succeeds on synthetic examples, cross-fidelity simulators, and a sim-to-real robotic pick-and-place accuracy inference task using synthetic distributions.
AI.conclusion: Betting offers a principled, theoretically grounded alternative for robot performance evaluation under limited real-world data, enabling unconventional sim-to-real transfer that becomes natural under this perspective.

title: IoT-Enhanced CNN-Based Labelled Crack Detection for Additive Manufacturing Image Annotation in Industry 4.0
summary: This paper presents an IoT-enhanced deep learning framework for automated crack detection in Additive Manufacturing (AM) surfaces using convolutional neural networks (CNNs). By integrating IoT-enabled real-time monitoring, high-resolution imaging, and edge computing, the system enables continuous in-situ defect detection and classification. Real-time data acquisition supports immediate CNN-based analysis, improving both accuracy and efficiency in AM quality control. The framework supports supervised and semi-supervised learning, enabling robust performance on large, sparsely annotated datasets. Using LabelImg for annotation and OpenCV for preprocessing, the system achieves 99.54% accuracy on 14,982 images, with 96% precision, 98% recall, and a 97% F1-score. Dataset balancing and augmentation significantly improve generalization, increasing accuracy from 32% to 99%. Beyond detection, the framework establishes a linkage between AM process parameters, defect formation, and surface topology, supporting predictive analytics and defect mitigation. Aligned with Industry 4.0, it incorporates Digital Twin (DT) technology for real-time process simulation, predictive maintenance, and adaptive control. Key contributions include an IoT-based monitoring system using edge devices (Raspberry Pi 4B), an optimized CNN with model quantization and batch processing reducing inference latency by 47%, and an MQTT-based low-latency data streaming system with 5G connectivity, lowering transmission overhead by 35%. DT integration further enables predictive defect analysis and dynamic adjustment of AM parameters. This work advances intelligent AM quality control by providing a scalable, high-accuracy, and low-latency framework. Future directions include multimodal data fusion, hybrid architectures, and enhanced Digital Twin simulations for AI-driven defect prevention.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an IoT-enhanced deep learning framework using CNNs for automated crack detection in Additive Manufacturing. It achieves 99.54% accuracy on 14,982 images, integrates edge computing (Raspberry Pi 4B), MQTT/5G streaming, model quantization, and Digital Twin technology to enable real-time, low-latency defect detection and predictive quality control in Industry 4.0 settings.
AI.motivation: To address the need for automated, real-time, and accurate defect detection in Additive Manufacturing surfaces, overcoming limitations of manual inspection and enabling continuous in-situ quality monitoring integrated with Industry 4.0 paradigms.
AI.method: IoT-enabled real-time monitoring with edge devices (Raspberry Pi 4B), high-resolution imaging, CNN-based analysis (supervised and semi-supervised learning), LabelImg annotation, OpenCV preprocessing, dataset balancing/augmentation, model quantization, batch processing, MQTT-based low-latency data streaming with 5G, and Digital Twin integration for predictive simulation.
AI.result: 99.54% accuracy on 14,982 images, 96% precision, 98% recall, 97% F1-score. Accuracy improved from 32% to 99% via dataset balancing/augmentation. Inference latency reduced by 47% through quantization and batch processing. Transmission overhead lowered by 35% via MQTT/5G streaming.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, high-accuracy, low-latency solution for intelligent AM quality control, linking process parameters to defect formation. Future work includes multimodal data fusion, hybrid architectures, and enhanced Digital Twin simulations for AI-driven defect prevention.

title: C-MORAL: Controllable Multi-Objective Molecular Optimization with Reinforcement Alignment for LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise for molecular optimization, but aligning them with selective and competing drug-design constraints remains challenging. We propose C-Moral, a reinforcement learning post-training framework for controllable multi-objective molecular optimization. C-Moral combines group-based relative optimization, property score alignment for heterogeneous objectives, and continuous non-linear reward aggregation to improve stability across competing properties. Experiments on the C-MuMOInstruct benchmark show that C-Moral consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models across both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, achieving the best Success Optimized Rate (SOR) of 48.9% on IND tasks and 39.5% on OOD tasks, while largely preserving scaffold similarity. These results suggest that RL post-training is an effective way to align molecular language models with continuous molecular design objectives. Our code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/Rwigie/C-MORAL.

AI.tldr: C-Moral is an RL post-training framework for controllable multi-objective molecular optimization that outperforms SOTA models on the C-MuMOInstruct benchmark, achieving 48.9% SOR on IND and 39.5% on OOD tasks while preserving scaffold similarity.
AI.motivation: LLMs show promise for molecular optimization, but aligning them with selective and competing drug-design constraints remains challenging.
AI.method: C-Moral combines group-based relative optimization, property score alignment for heterogeneous objectives, and continuous non-linear reward aggregation to improve stability across competing properties.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models on the C-MuMOInstruct benchmark, achieving the best SOR of 48.9% on IND tasks and 39.5% on OOD tasks, while largely preserving scaffold similarity.
AI.conclusion: RL post-training is an effective way to align molecular language models with continuous molecular design objectives.

title: SoccerRef-Agents: Multi-Agent System for Automated Soccer Refereeing
comment: 26 pages, 8 figures. Submitted to ISACE 2026. Github Repo: https://github.com/yjlog/SoccerRef-Agents
summary: Refereeing is vital in sports, where fair, accurate, and explainable decisions are fundamental. While intelligent assistant technologies are being widely adopted in soccer refereeing, current AI-assisted approaches remain preliminary. Existing research mostly focuses on isolated video perception tasks and lacks the ability to understand and reason about foul scenarios. To fill this gap, we propose SoccerRef-Agents, a holistic and explainable multi-agent decision-making framework for soccer refereeing. The main contributions are: (i) constructing the multimodal benchmark SoccerRefBench with over 1,200 referee theory questions and 600 foul video clips; (ii) building a vector-based knowledge base RefKnowledgeDB using the latest "Laws of the Game" and a classic case database for precise, knowledge-driven reasoning; (iii) designing a novel multi-agent architecture that collaborates via cross-modal RAG to bridge the semantic gap between visual content and regulatory texts. This work explores the technical capability of integrating MLLMs with refereeing expertise, and evaluations show our system significantly outperforms general-purpose MLLMs in decision accuracy and explanation quality. All databases, benchmarks, and code will be made available.

AI.tldr: SoccerRef-Agents is a multi-agent AI framework for soccer refereeing that uses a multimodal benchmark (1,200 theory questions + 600 foul clips), a vector-based knowledge base of official rules, and cross-modal RAG to outperform general MLLMs in decision accuracy and explanation quality.
AI.motivation: Current AI-assisted soccer refereeing focuses on isolated video perception tasks and lacks the ability to understand and reason about foul scenarios holistically. There is a gap between visual content understanding and regulatory text reasoning.
AI.method: The framework consists of: (1) SoccerRefBench - a multimodal benchmark with 1,200 referee theory questions and 600 foul video clips; (2) RefKnowledgeDB - a vector-based knowledge base built from the latest "Laws of the Game" and classic case database; (3) A multi-agent architecture that collaborates via cross-modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to bridge visual and textual semantics.
AI.result: The system significantly outperforms general-purpose Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in both decision accuracy and explanation quality on refereeing tasks.
AI.conclusion: Integrating MLLMs with domain-specific refereeing expertise through multi-agent collaboration and knowledge retrieval is a promising direction for building explainable AI-assisted sports officiating systems.

title: Event-based SLAM Benchmark for High-Speed Maneuvers
summary: Event-based cameras are bio-inspired sensors with pixels that independently and asynchronously respond to brightness changes at microsecond resolution, offering the potential to handle visual tasks in high-speed maneuvering scenarios. Existing event-based approaches, although successful in mitigating motion blur caused by high-speed maneuvers, suffer from many limitations. Some of them highlight a success of pose tracking for a fronto-parallel fast shaking camera closed to the structure, while others assume pure (optionally aggressive) three-degree-of-freedom rotations. The former requires persistent local map visibility within the field of view (FOV), whereas the latter fails to generalize to six-degree-of-freedom (6-DoF) motions where both linear and angular velocities may be large. Consequently, current successes do not fully demonstrate that event-based state estimation under arbitrary aggressive maneuvers is a fully solved problem. To quantitatively assess the extent to which the potential of event cameras has been unlocked, we conduct a thorough analysis of state-of-the-art (SOTA) event-based visual odometry (VO)/visual-inertial odometry (VIO) methods and report shortcomings in current public datasets. Furthermore, we introduce a benchmarking framework for event-based state estimation, called EvSLAM, characterized by sufficient variation in data collection platforms, diverse extreme lighting scenarios, and a wide scope of challenging motion patterns under a clear and rigorous definition of high-speed maneuvers for mobile robots, along with a novel evaluation metric designed to fairly assess the operational limits of event-based solutions. This framework benchmarks state-of-the-art methods, yielding insights into optimal architectures and persistent challenges.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes limitations of existing event-based visual odometry methods under aggressive 6-DoF motion, introduces EvSLAM—a comprehensive benchmarking framework with diverse platforms, extreme lighting, challenging motion patterns, and a new evaluation metric—and provides insights into optimal architectures and unresolved challenges.
AI.motivation: Existing event-based state estimation methods have shown success only in limited scenarios (e.g., fronto-parallel shaking or pure 3-DoF rotations) and fail to generalize to 6-DoF aggressive motions with large linear and angular velocities. The field lacks a rigorous, quantitative assessment framework to evaluate whether the full potential of event cameras has been unlocked under arbitrary aggressive maneuvers.
AI.method: The authors conduct a thorough analysis of SOTA event-based VO/VIO methods, identify shortcomings in current public datasets, and introduce EvSLAM—a benchmarking framework featuring diverse data collection platforms, extreme lighting conditions, a wide scope of challenging motion patterns, a rigorous definition of high-speed maneuvers for mobile robots, and a novel evaluation metric for fair assessment.
AI.result: The paper benchmarks SOTA methods using EvSLAM, yielding insights into optimal architectures and revealing persistent challenges that current approaches fail to address under arbitrary aggressive 6-DoF motions.
AI.conclusion: Event-based state estimation under arbitrary aggressive maneuvers remains an unsolved problem, and EvSLAM provides a standardized framework to drive future research by rigorously testing operational limits.

title: A Digital Pathology Resource for Liver Cancer Quantification with Datasets, Benchmarks, and Tools
summary: Liver cancer, especially hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), imposes a substantial global disease burden. Accurate diagnosis and prognostic assessment directly influence treatment selection and patient survival, and pathological examination remains the gold standard for liver cancer diagnosis. Identifying diverse tissue components and pathological subtypes on histopathology slides is crucial for estimating postoperative recurrence risk and overall prognosis. However, most publicly available resources are still provided at the whole-slide image (WSI) level, and well-annotated datasets for fine-grained tissue component identification in liver cancer are scarce, which hinders reproducible model development and the deployment of quantitative analysis tools. To address this gap, we release HepatoBench, a patch-level image database for liver cancer with annotations for seven key tissue categories. Based on HepatoBench, we train and open-source a deep learning classification model as a tissue recognition tool. Furthermore, we train a WSI-level tumor/non-tumor segmentation model to automatically localize lesion regions across entire slides. By integrating the patch-level tissue classifier with the WSI-level segmentation model, we build HepatoQuant, an end-to-end, disease-specific regional quantification tool for liver cancer, enabling a unified workflow from WSIs to tissue composition parsing and quantitative statistics. We also open-source HepatoBench, the benchmarking protocol, and supporting tools, providing a solid foundation for automated regional quantification and fair method comparison in liver cancer pathology.

AI.tldr: HepatoBench is a patch-level liver cancer image database with annotations for seven tissue categories. Using it, the authors trained an open-source deep learning classification model and a WSI-level segmentation model, then combined them into HepatoQuant—an end-to-end tool for automated regional quantification of liver cancer pathology from WSIs.
AI.motivation: Liver cancer (especially HCC) has a high global burden, and pathology slides are critical for diagnosis and prognosis, but publicly available datasets lack fine-grained tissue-level annotations, hindering reproducible model development and quantitative analysis.
AI.method: The authors created HepatoBench (a patch-level database with seven tissue categories), trained a deep learning patch classifier, trained a WSI-level tumor/non-tumor segmentation model, and integrated both into HepatoQuant for end-to-end regional quantification.
AI.result: Release of HepatoBench (patch-level annotated database), an open-source deep learning tissue classifier, a WSI-level segmentation model, and the HepatoQuant end-to-end quantification tool.
AI.conclusion: HepatoBench and HepatoQuant provide a solid foundation for automated regional quantification and fair method comparison in liver cancer pathology.

title: RL Token: Bootstrapping Online RL with Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-language-action (VLA) models can learn to perform diverse manipulation skills "out of the box," but achieving the precision and speed that real-world tasks demand requires further fine-tuning -- for example, via reinforcement learning (RL). We introduce a lightweight method that enables sample-efficient online RL fine-tuning of pretrained VLAs using just a few hours of real-world practice. We (1) adapt the VLA to expose an "RL token," a compact readout representation that preserves task-relevant pretrained knowledge while serving as an efficient interface for online RL, and (2) train a small actor-critic head on this RL token to refine the actions, while anchoring the learned policy to the VLA. Online RL with the RL token (RLT) makes it possible to fine-tune even large VLAs with RL quickly and efficiently. Across four real-robot tasks (screw installation, zip tie fastening, charger insertion, and Ethernet insertion), RLT improves the speed on the hardest part of the task by up to 3x and raises success rates significantly within minutes to a few hours of practice. It can even surpass the speed of human teleoperation on some of the tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RLT (RL Token), a lightweight method that adds a compact RL token to pretrained VLAs, enabling sample-efficient online RL fine-tuning that improves robotic manipulation speed (up to 3x) and success rates within hours of real-world practice.
AI.motivation: VLAs can learn diverse skills out of the box but lack the precision and speed required for real-world tasks, necessitating efficient fine-tuning methods.
AI.method: (1) Adapt the VLA to expose an "RL token" - a compact readout representation that preserves pretrained knowledge while serving as an interface for online RL. (2) Train a small actor-critic head on this RL token to refine actions while anchoring to the VLA.
AI.result: On four real-robot tasks (screw installation, zip tie fastening, charger insertion, Ethernet insertion), RLT improves speed on hardest task parts by up to 3x and raises success rates significantly within minutes to hours, even surpassing human teleoperation speed on some tasks.
AI.conclusion: RLT enables sample-efficient online RL fine-tuning of large VLAs with just a few hours of real-world practice, making it practical to improve VLA performance for real-world robotic manipulation.

title: When Corrective Hints Hurt: Prompt Design in Reasoner-Guided Repair of LLM Overcaution on Entailed Negations under OWL~2~DL
summary: We report a reproducible error pattern in GPT-5.4 on OWL~2~DL compliance queries: the model frequently answers ``unknown'' when the reasoner-entailed answer is ``no'' under \emph{FunctionalProperty} closure or class \emph{disjointness}. Using 180 reasoner-audited queries from a procedural expansion of the observed pattern plus 18 hand-authored held-out queries in two unrelated domains (insurance and clinical), we compare four interaction modes under matched query budget: single-shot, three rounds of generic ``you-are-wrong'' retry, three rounds of reasoner-verdict repair with an open-world-assumption (OWA) hint, and the same repair without the hint. Direct faithfulness is 43.9\,\% (Wilson 95\,\% CI $[36.8,51.2]$); generic retry reaches 81.7\,\% ($[75.4,86.6]$); the verdict-with-hint variant is \emph{worse} at 67.2\,\% ($[60.1,73.7]$); the verdict-only variant reaches 97.8\,\% ($[94.4,99.1]$). All pairwise comparisons remain significant under McNemar's exact test with Bonferroni correction ($α= 0.01$; all $p < 10^{-5}$). The same fingerprint accounts for 4/4 errors on the held-out queries. Our interpretation is bounded: prompt framing can matter more than corrective content, and reasoner-guided wrappers should be ablated explicitly.

AI.tldr: GPT-5.4 shows systematic errors on OWL2 DL queries (answering "unknown" when the correct answer is "no" under FunctionalProperty/class disjointness). A verdict-only repair strategy achieves 97.8% accuracy vs. 43.9% baseline, while adding an OWA hint actually worsens performance to 67.2%.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize a reproducible error pattern in LLM-based ontology reasoning, specifically for OWL2 DL compliance queries, and to evaluate whether reasoner-guided correction strategies can mitigate these errors.
AI.method: 180 procedural queries + 18 held-out hand-authored queries across insurance and clinical domains; compared 4 interaction modes under matched budget: single-shot, 3-round generic retry, 3-round reasoner-verdict repair with OWA hint, and verdict-only repair without hint.
AI.result: Single-shot: 43.9%; generic retry: 81.7%; verdict-with-hint: 67.2%; verdict-only: 97.8%. All differences significant (p<10^-5, Bonferroni-corrected α=0.01). Same error pattern accounts for 4/4 held-out errors.
AI.conclusion: Prompt framing can matter more than corrective content; reasoner-guided wrappers should be explicitly ablated. The OWA hint paradoxically harms performance, suggesting careful prompt design is critical.

authors: M. J. G. van de Molengraft
title: Trajectory Planning for an Articulated Commercial Vehicle using Model Predictive Contouring Control
summary: This paper presents a trajectory planning method for articulated commercial vehicles, specifically tractor-semitrailers, based on Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC). Although MPCC has proven effective for passenger cars, it is generally ill-suited for tractor-semitrailers. These vehicles are significantly larger, the semitrailer follows a different path than the tractor, and reversing maneuvers are unstable and prone to jackknifing. Furthermore, practical driving scenarios often require scenario-dependent prioritization of different vehicle `anchor points', e.g., prioritizing the semitrailer position during docking or the tractor position when parking to charge. Therefore, we extend MPCC to enable scenario-dependent weighting of these anchor points and incorporate explicit road-boundary constraints for the front and rear tractor axles and the semitrailer axle, thereby ensuring that all considered wheels remain within the drivable area. The simulation results demonstrate the successful navigation of a representative logistic scenario in both forward and reverse direction. Furthermore, the influence of the optimization parameters on the trajectories is analyzed, providing insights into controlling the vehicle behavior. Finally, first tests using a full-scale prototype vehicle show the practical applicability of the approach.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Model Predictive Contouring Control (MPCC) for tractor-semitrailer trajectory planning, enabling scenario-dependent weighting of anchor points and explicit road-boundary constraints, validated through simulations and full-scale prototype tests.
AI.motivation: MPCC works well for passenger cars but is ill-suited for tractor-semitrailers due to their large size, different path following of semitrailer, unstable reversing prone to jackknifing, and need for scenario-dependent prioritization of different vehicle anchor points (e.g., semitrailer during docking vs. tractor during charging).
AI.method: The authors extend MPCC by introducing scenario-dependent weighting of different anchor points (tractor/semitrailer positions) and incorporating explicit road-boundary constraints for front/rear tractor axles and the semitrailer axle to keep all wheels within the drivable area.
AI.result: Simulation results show successful navigation of representative logistic scenarios in both forward and reverse directions. Analysis of optimization parameter influence on trajectories is provided. First tests with a full-scale prototype vehicle demonstrate practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The extended MPCC approach effectively handles the unique challenges of tractor-semitrailer trajectory planning, including unstable reversing and scenario-dependent anchor point prioritization, and shows practical viability through real-world prototype testing.

title: MeshLAM: Feed-Forward One-Shot Animatable Textured Mesh Avatar Reconstruction
summary: We introduce MeshLAM, a feed-forward framework for one-shot animatable mesh head reconstruction that generates high-fidelity, animatable 3D head avatars from a single image. Unlike previous work that relies on time-consuming test-time optimization or extensive multi-view data, our method produces complete mesh representations with inherent animatability from a single image in a single forward pass. Our approach employs a dual shape and texture map architecture that simultaneously processes mesh vertices and texture map with extracted image features from a shared transformer backbone, allowing for coherent shape carving and appearance modeling. To prevent mesh collapse and ensure topological integrity during feed-forward deformation, we propose an iterative GRU-based decoding mechanism with progressive geometry deformation and texture refinement, coupled with a novel reprojection-based texture guidance mechanism that anchors appearance learning to the input image. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in reconstruction quality, animation capability, and computational efficiency. Project page at https://meshlam.github.io.

AI.tldr: MeshLAM is a feed-forward framework that reconstructs high-fidelity, animatable 3D mesh head avatars from a single image in one forward pass, using a dual shape/texture map architecture with GRU-based decoding and reprojection-based texture guidance.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of existing methods that require time-consuming test-time optimization or extensive multi-view data, aiming for fast, single-image reconstruction with inherent animatability.
AI.method: Uses a shared transformer backbone to extract image features, a dual shape and texture map architecture for coherent shape carving and appearance modeling, iterative GRU-based decoding with progressive geometry deformation and texture refinement, and a reprojection-based texture guidance mechanism to anchor appearance learning to the input image.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in reconstruction quality, animation capability, and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MeshLAM enables efficient, high-quality animatable 3D head avatar reconstruction from a single image without test-time optimization or multi-view data.

title: Channel Adaptation for EEG Foundation Models: A Systematic Benchmark Across Architectures, Tasks, and Training Regimes
summary: Scaling EEG foundation models requires pooling data across heterogeneous electrode montages, a prerequisite both for larger pretraining corpora and for downstream deployment. We present the first systematic comparison of four channel adaptation methods (Conv1d projection, spherical spline interpolation (SSI), source-space decomposition, and Riemannian re-centering) across five pretrained EEG foundation models (5M--157M parameters), five downstream tasks, and two training regimes with 10--15 random seeds each. We find that rigid-montage models (BENDR, Neuro-GPT) require external adaptation, while flexible models (EEGPT, CBraMod) match or exceed it natively when fine-tuned but benefit from external methods under frozen-encoder deployment. A probe-SFT asymmetry exists: external adaptation can cause severe negative transfer during fine-tuning of flexible models. The optimal method is architecture-dependent (Conv1d for BENDR, SSI/Riemannian for Neuro-GPT, source-space decomposition for depression detection), and 5M-parameter CBraMod outperforms models up to 31$\times$ larger on 4/5 datasets, consistent with independent findings that compact EEG-specific architectures can match larger models.

AI.tldr: First systematic comparison of four channel adaptation methods across five EEG foundation models, finding that optimal method is architecture-dependent, compact models (5M params) can outperform much larger ones, and there exists a probe-SFT asymmetry where external adaptation hurts fine-tuning of flexible models.
AI.motivation: Scaling EEG foundation models requires pooling data across heterogeneous electrode montages, which is necessary for both larger pretraining corpora and downstream deployment.
AI.method: Systematic comparison of four channel adaptation methods (Conv1d projection, spherical spline interpolation, source-space decomposition, Riemannian re-centering) across five pretrained EEG foundation models (5M-157M parameters), five downstream tasks, two training regimes with 10-15 random seeds each.
AI.result: Rigid-montage models require external adaptation; flexible models match/exceed it natively for fine-tuning but benefit under frozen-encoder. Probe-SFT asymmetry exists. Optimal method is architecture-dependent. 5M-parameter CBraMod outperforms models up to 31x larger on 4/5 datasets.
AI.conclusion: No single channel adaptation method universally works best; choice depends on model architecture and deployment strategy. Compact EEG-specific architectures can match larger models, and careful consideration needed for adaptation method to avoid negative transfer.

title: IndustryAssetEQA: A Neurosymbolic Operational Intelligence System for Embodied Question Answering in Industrial Asset Maintenance
comment: 20 pages, 4 figures, 4 tables, Accepted for the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026) Industry Track
summary: Industrial maintenance environments increasingly rely on AI systems to assist operators in understanding asset behavior, diagnosing failures, and evaluating interventions. Although large language models (LLMs) enable fluent natural-language interaction, deployed maintenance assistants routinely produce generic explanations that are weakly grounded in telemetry, omit verifiable provenance, and offer no testable support for counterfactual or action-oriented reasoning that undermine trust in safety-critical settings. We present IndustryAssetEQA, a neurosymbolic operational intelligence system that combines episodic telemetry representations with a Failure Mode Effects Analysis Knowledge Graph (FMEA-KG) to enable Embodied Question Answering (EQA) over industrial assets. We evaluate on four datasets covering four industrial asset types, including rotating machinery, turbofan engines, hydraulic systems, and cyber-physical production systems. Compared to LLM-only baselines, IndustryAssetEQA improves structural validity by up to 0.51, counterfactual accuracy by up to 0.47, and explanation entailment by 0.64, while reducing severe expert-rated overclaims from 28% to 2% (approximately 93% reduction). Code, datasets, and the FMEA-KG are available at https://github.com/IBM/AssetOpsBench/tree/IndustryAssetEQA/IndustryAssetEQA.

AI.tldr: IndustryAssetEQA is a neurosymbolic system combining telemetry data with a Failure Mode Effects Analysis Knowledge Graph to improve industrial maintenance QA, reducing overclaims by 93% and improving explainability metrics significantly over LLM-only baselines.
AI.motivation: AI maintenance assistants produce generic, unverifiable explanations that lack grounding in telemetry and offer no testable counterfactual reasoning, which undermines trust in safety-critical industrial settings.
AI.method: A neurosymbolic system that integrates episodic telemetry representations with a Failure Mode Effects Analysis Knowledge Graph (FMEA-KG) to perform Embodied Question Answering (EQA) over industrial assets.
AI.result: IndustryAssetEQA improves structural validity by 0.51, counterfactual accuracy by 0.47, explanation entailment by 0.64, and reduces severe expert-rated overclaims from 28% to 2% (93% reduction) across four datasets.
AI.conclusion: The neurosymbolic approach significantly outperforms LLM-only baselines in providing grounded, verifiable, and actionable explanations for industrial maintenance, enabling safer AI deployment in safety-critical environments.

title: AsyncShield: A Plug-and-Play Edge Adapter for Asynchronous Cloud-based VLA Navigation
summary: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have been demonstrated possessing strong zero-shot generalization for robot control, their massive parameter sizes typically necessitate cloud-based deployment. However, cloud deployment introduces network jitter and inference latency, which can induce severe spatiotemporal misalignment in mobile navigation under continuous displacement, so that the stale intents expressed in past ego frames may become spatially incorrect in the current frame and lead to collisions. To address this issue, we propose AsyncShield, a plug-and-play asynchronous control framework. AsyncShield discards traditional black-box time-series prediction in favor of a deterministic physical white-box spatial mapping. By maintaining a temporal pose buffer and utilizing kinematic transformations, the system accurately converts temporal lag into spatial pose offsets to restore the VLA's original geometric intent. To balance intent restoration fidelity and physical safety, the edge adaptation is formulated as a constrained Markov decision process (CMDP). Solved via the PPO-Lagrangian algorithm, a reinforcement learning adapter dynamically trades off between tracking the VLA intent and responding to high-frequency LiDAR obstacle avoidance hard constraints. Furthermore, benefiting from a standardized universal sub-goal interface, domain randomization, and perception-level adaptation via Collision Radius Inflation, AsyncShield operates as a lightweight, plug-and-play module. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that, without fine-tuning any cloud-based foundation models, the framework exhibits zero-shot and robust generalization capabilities, effectively improving the success rate and physical safety of asynchronous navigation.

AI.tldr: AsyncShield is a plug-and-play asynchronous control framework that resolves spatiotemporal misalignment in cloud-deployed VLA navigation by using kinematic transformations (instead of time-series prediction) to map temporal lag into spatial pose offsets, combined with a PPO-Lagrangian-based edge adapter that balances VLA intent fidelity with LiDAR obstacle avoidance safety.
AI.motivation: Cloud-deployed VLA models suffer from network jitter and inference latency causing spatiotemporal misalignment in mobile navigation—stale intent from past ego frames becomes spatially incorrect in the current frame, leading to collisions. Existing black-box time-series prediction methods are insufficient.
AI.method: A deterministic white-box spatial mapping via temporal pose buffer and kinematic transformations converts temporal lag into spatial offsets. A CMDP formulated edge adapter (solved via PPO-Lagrangian) balances intent restoration and safety. Domain randomization, universal sub-goal interface, and Collision Radius Inflation enable plug-and-play operation.
AI.result: Without fine-tuning any cloud foundation models, AsyncShield demonstrates zero-shot robust generalization in both simulation and real-world experiments, improving success rate and physical safety of asynchronous navigation.
AI.conclusion: AsyncShield provides an effective, lightweight solution for asynchronous VLA-based mobile navigation that decouples safety from cloud model latency, enabling safe deployment without modifying the core VLA model.

title: Probing Visual Planning in Image Editing Models
comment: Accepted to ES-Reasoning Workshop @ ICLR 2026. Our code is available at https://github.com/spatigen/amaze
summary: Visual planning represents a crucial facet of human intelligence, especially in tasks that require complex spatial reasoning and navigation. Yet, in machine learning, this inherently visual problem is often tackled through a verbal-centric lens. While recent research demonstrates the promise of fully visual approaches, they suffer from significant computational inefficiency due to the step-by-step planning-by-generation paradigm. In this work, we present EAR, an editing-as-reasoning paradigm that reformulates visual planning as a single-step image transformation. To isolate intrinsic reasoning from visual recognition, we employ abstract puzzles as probing tasks and introduce AMAZE, a procedurally generated dataset that features the classical Maze and Queen problems, covering distinct, complementary forms of visual planning. The abstract nature of AMAZE also facilitates automatic evaluation of autoregressive and diffusion-based models in terms of both pixel-wise fidelity and logical validity. We assess leading proprietary and open-source editing models. The results show that they all struggle in the zero-shot setting, finetuning on basic scales enables remarkable generalization to larger in-domain scales and out-of-domain scales and geometries. However, our best model that runs on high-end hardware fails to match the zero-shot efficiency of human solvers, highlighting a persistent gap in neural visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: EAR (Editing-As-Reasoning) reformulates visual planning as single-step image transformation, using the AMAZE dataset (Maze and Queen problems) to evaluate editing models. While finetuning enables generalization, even the best model lags behind human zero-shot efficiency.
AI.motivation: Visual planning is often treated as a verbal problem in ML, and existing visual approaches are computationally inefficient due to step-by-step generation. The authors aim to bridge this gap.
AI.method: Proposes EAR (Editing-as-Reasoning) paradigm that converts visual planning into a single-step image transformation. Introduces AMAZE dataset with abstract Maze and Queen puzzles to isolate reasoning from recognition.
AI.result: Leading editing models struggle zero-shot; finetuning on basic scales enables generalization to larger in-domain and out-of-domain scales/geometries. Best model still fails to match human zero-shot efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Editing-as-reasoning shows promise for visual planning, but a significant gap remains between neural models and human visual reasoning capabilities.

title: ProEval: Proactive Failure Discovery and Efficient Performance Estimation for Generative AI Evaluation
comment: Our open-sourced code and data can be found at https://github.com/google-deepmind/proeval
summary: Evaluating generative AI models is increasingly resource-intensive due to slow inference, expensive raters, and a rapidly growing landscape of models and benchmarks. We propose ProEval, a proactive evaluation framework that leverages transfer learning to efficiently estimate performance and identify failure cases. ProEval employs pre-trained Gaussian Processes (GPs) as surrogates for the performance score function, mapping model inputs to metrics such as the severity of errors or safety violations. By framing performance estimation as Bayesian quadrature (BQ) and failure discovery as superlevel set sampling, we develop uncertainty-aware decision strategies that actively select or synthesize highly informative inputs for testing. Theoretically, we prove that our pre-trained GP-based BQ estimator is unbiased and bounded. Empirically, extensive experiments on reasoning, safety alignment, and classification benchmarks demonstrate that ProEval is significantly more efficient than competitive baselines. It requires 8-65x fewer samples to achieve estimates within 1% of the ground truth, while simultaneously revealing more diverse failure cases under a stricter evaluation budget.

AI.tldr: ProEval is a proactive evaluation framework using transfer learning with Gaussian Processes to efficiently estimate model performance and discover failures, requiring 8-65x fewer samples than baselines.
AI.motivation: Evaluating generative AI models is increasingly resource-intensive due to slow inference, expensive raters, and the growing number of models and benchmarks.
AI.method: ProEval uses pre-trained Gaussian Processes as surrogates for performance scoring, framing estimation as Bayesian quadrature and failure discovery as superlevel set sampling to actively select informative inputs.
AI.result: Requires 8-65x fewer samples to achieve estimates within 1% of ground truth, and reveals more diverse failure cases under stricter evaluation budgets.
AI.conclusion: ProEval is significantly more efficient than competitive baselines for evaluating generative models across reasoning, safety, and classification tasks.

title: ArguAgent: AI-Supported Real-Time Grouping for Productive Argumentation in STEM Classrooms
comment: Full paper accepted to the 27th International Conference on AI in Education (AIED 2026). AIED Proceedings to be released Summer 2026
summary: Argumentation is a core practice in STEM education, but its productivity depends on who participates and how they interact. Higher-achieving students often dominate the talk and decision-making, while lower-achieving peers may disengage, defer, or comply without contributing substantive reasoning. Forming groups strategically based on students' stances and argumentation skills could help foster inclusive, evidence-based discourse. In practice, however, teachers are constrained in implementing this grouping strategy because it requires real-time insight into students' positions and the quality of their argumentation, information that is difficult to assess reliably and at scale during instruction. We present a generative AI-powered system, ArguAgent, that creates groups optimizing for stance heterogeneity while constraining argumentation quality differences to +/-1 level on a validated learning progression. ArguAgent uses a two-component assessment pipeline: first scoring student arguments on a 0-4 rubric, then clustering positions via semantic analysis. We validated the scoring component against human expert consensus (Krippendorff's ααα = 0.817) using 200 expert-generated scores. Testing three OpenAI models (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5.1, GPT-5.2) with identical calibrated prompts, we found that systematic prompt engineering informed by human disagreement analysis contributed 89% of scoring improvement (QWK: 0.531 to 0.686), while model upgrades contributed an additional 11% (QWK: 0.686 to 0.708). Simulation testing across 100 classes demonstrated that the grouping algorithm achieves 95.4% of groups that meet both design criteria, a 3.2x improvement over random assignment. These results suggest ArguAgent can enable real-time, theoretically grounded grouping that promotes productive STEM argumentation in classrooms.

AI.tldr: ArguAgent is an AI-powered system that creates strategic student groups for STEM argumentation by optimizing stance diversity while limiting skill differences, achieving high scoring reliability (α=0.817) and 95.4% of groups meeting design criteria.
AI.motivation: Higher-achieving students often dominate STEM argumentation, while lower-achieving peers disengage. Teachers lack real-time insight into students' stances and argumentation quality to form inclusive groups strategically.
AI.method: Two-component pipeline: (1) scores student arguments on a 0-4 rubric using LLMs with calibrated prompts and human disagreement-informed engineering, (2) clusters positions via semantic analysis. Groups are optimized for stance heterogeneity with argumentation quality constrained to ±1 level.
AI.result: Scoring achieved Krippendorff's α=0.817 against expert consensus. Prompt engineering contributed 89% of improvement (QWK: 0.531→0.686), model upgrades 11% (0.686→0.708). Grouping algorithm achieved 95.4% success, 3.2x better than random assignment.
AI.conclusion: ArguAgent enables real-time, theoretically grounded grouping that promotes productive and inclusive STEM argumentation in classrooms.

title: $M^2$-VLA: Boosting Vision-Language Models for Generalizable Manipulation via Layer Mixture and Meta-Skills
summary: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models predominantly rely on end-to-end fine-tuning. While effective, this paradigm compromises the inherent generalization capabilities of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and incurs catastrophic forgetting. To address these limitations, we propose $M^2$-VLA, which demonstrates that a generalized VLM is able to serve as a powerful backbone for robotic manipulation directly. However, it remains a key challenge to bridge the gap between the high-level semantic understanding of VLMs and the precise requirements of robotic control. To overcome this, we introduce the Mixture of Layers (MoL) strategy that selectively extracts task-critical information from dense semantic features. Furthermore, to facilitate efficient trajectory learning under constrained model capacity, we propose a Meta Skill Module (MSM) that integrates strong inductive biases. Extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world environments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Furthermore, generalization and ablation studies validate the architecture's zero-shot capabilities and confirm the contribution of each key component. Our code and pre-trained models will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: M²-VLA proposes a Mixture of Layers (MoL) strategy and Meta Skill Module (MSM) to leverage VLMs as robotic manipulation backbones without end-to-end fine-tuning, avoiding catastrophic forgetting and achieving strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models rely on end-to-end fine-tuning, which compromises VLM generalization and causes catastrophic forgetting. The authors aim to use a generalized VLM directly as a backbone for robotic manipulation without these drawbacks.
AI.method: Introduces (1) Mixture of Layers (MoL) strategy to extract task-critical information from dense semantic features, bridging the gap between VLM high-level understanding and robotic control precision; (2) Meta Skill Module (MSM) for efficient trajectory learning with strong inductive biases.
AI.result: Extensive simulated and real-world experiments demonstrate effectiveness. Generalization and ablation studies validate zero-shot capabilities and confirm each component's contribution.
AI.conclusion: M²-VLA effectively uses generalized VLMs as robotic manipulation backbones, overcoming the limitations of end-to-end fine-tuning while achieving strong performance and zero-shot generalization.

title: Vision-Based Lane Following and Traffic Sign Recognition for Resource-Constrained Autonomous Vehicles
comment: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems, Blockchain, and Communication Technologies
summary: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) rely on real-time perception systems to understand road environments and ensure safe navigation. However, implementing reliable perception algorithms on resource-constrained embedded platforms remains challenging due to limited computational resources. This paper presents a lightweight vision-based framework that integrates lane detection, lane tracking, and traffic sign recognition for embedded autonomous vehicles. A computationally efficient threshold-based lane segmentation method combined with perspective transformation and histogram-based curvature estimation is used for robust lane tracking under varying illumination conditions. A rule-based steering controller generates steering commands to maintain stable vehicle navigation. For traffic sign recognition, two lightweight convolutional neural networks (CNNs), EfficientNet-B0 and MobileNetV2, are evaluated using a custom dataset captured from the vehicle's onboard camera. Experimental results show that the system achieves real-time performance while maintaining accurate lane tracking with only 3.16% maximum offset RMSE. EfficientNet-B0 achieves a high offline classification accuracy of 98.77% on the test dataset, while achieving 90% accuracy during real-time on-device deployment, outperforming MobileNetV2 in both settings. MobileNetV2, however, offers slightly faster inference and lower computational cost. These results highlight the effectiveness of lightweight vision-based perception pipelines for resource-constrained autonomous driving applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a lightweight vision-based framework for autonomous vehicles that integrates lane detection, lane tracking, and traffic sign recognition on resource-constrained embedded platforms, achieving real-time performance with 3.16% max offset RMSE for lane tracking and up to 98.77% offline/90% real-time accuracy using EfficientNet-B0 for traffic sign recognition.
AI.motivation: Implementing reliable perception algorithms on resource-constrained embedded platforms for autonomous vehicles remains challenging due to limited computational resources.
AI.method: The framework uses a computationally efficient threshold-based lane segmentation method combined with perspective transformation and histogram-based curvature estimation for lane tracking, a rule-based steering controller, and lightweight CNNs (EfficientNet-B0 and MobileNetV2) for traffic sign recognition, evaluated on a custom onboard camera dataset.
AI.result: The system achieves real-time performance with only 3.16% maximum offset RMSE for lane tracking. EfficientNet-B0 achieves 98.77% offline accuracy and 90% real-time accuracy, outperforming MobileNetV2 in accuracy, while MobileNetV2 offers slightly faster inference and lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight vision-based perception pipelines are effective for resource-constrained autonomous driving applications, with EfficientNet-B0 and MobileNetV2 offering a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency.

title: Unstable Rankings in Bayesian Deep Learning Evaluation
summary: Standard evaluations of Bayesian deep learning methods assume that metric estimates are reliable, but we show this assumption fails under data scarcity. Method rankings are not only unreliable at small $n$, but also dataset-dependent in ways that point estimates cannot reveal: the same method comparison yields $P(\mathrm{MCD} \prec \mathrm{Ensemble}) = 1.000$ at $n = 50$ on one dataset and remains below $0.95$ even at $n = 500$ on another. Across the datasets we consider, no universal sample size threshold exists, which is precisely why dataset-specific posterior inference is necessary. To address this, we use a Bayesian hierarchical model with method-specific variances to treat evaluation metrics as random variables across data realizations, and we use a predictive Minimum Detectable Difference curve to assess whether an observed gap would be detectable at a given training size. Across six Bayesian deep learning methods and five regression datasets, our results show that uncertainty-aware evaluation is necessary in low-data settings, because current evidence for method superiority and predictive detectability at the same training size can diverge substantially. Our framework provides practitioners with principled tools to determine whether their evaluation data is sufficient before drawing conclusions about method superiority.

AI.tldr: Standard evaluations of Bayesian deep learning methods are unreliable under data scarcity; this paper proposes a Bayesian hierarchical model with method-specific variances and a predictive Minimum Detectable Difference curve to assess whether observed performance gaps are statistically detectable given the training size.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of Bayesian deep learning methods assume metric estimates are reliable, but this fails under data scarcity. Method rankings become unreliable and dataset-dependent, and there is no universal sample size threshold, creating a need for dataset-specific posterior inference.
AI.method: The authors use a Bayesian hierarchical model with method-specific variances, treating evaluation metrics as random variables across data realizations. They introduce a predictive Minimum Detectable Difference (MDD) curve to assess whether an observed performance gap is detectable at a given training size.
AI.result: Across six Bayesian deep learning methods and five regression datasets, uncertainty-aware evaluation shows that evidence for method superiority and predictive detectability at the same training size can diverge substantially. For example, P(MCD < Ensemble) = 1.000 at n=50 on one dataset but remains below 0.95 even at n=500 on another.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty-aware evaluation is necessary in low-data settings. The framework provides practitioners with principled tools to determine whether their evaluation data is sufficient before drawing conclusions about method superiority.

title: Ulterior Motives: Detecting Misaligned Reasoning in Continuous Thought Models
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has emerged as a key technique for eliciting complex reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs). Although interpretable, its dependence on natural language limits the model's expressive bandwidth. Continuous thought models address this bottleneck by reasoning in latent space rather than human-readable tokens. While they enable richer representations and faster inference, they raise a critical safety question: how can we detect misaligned reasoning in an uninterpretable latent space? To study this, we introduce MoralChain, a benchmark of 12,000 social scenarios with parallel moral/immoral reasoning paths. We train a continuous thought model with backdoor behavior using a novel dual-trigger paradigm - one trigger that arms misaligned latent reasoning ([T]) and another that releases harmful outputs ([O]). We demonstrate three findings: (1) continuous thought models can exhibit misaligned latent reasoning while producing aligned outputs, with aligned and misaligned reasoning occupying geometrically distinct regions of latent space; (2) linear probes trained on behaviorally-distinguishable conditions ([T][O] vs [O]) transfer to detecting armed-but-benign states ([T] vs baseline) with high accuracy; and (3) misalignment is encoded in early latent thinking tokens, suggesting safety monitoring for continuous thought models should target the "planning" phase of latent reasoning.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MoralChain, a benchmark of 12,000 social scenarios, to study safety risks in continuous thought models (which reason in latent space instead of natural language). The authors train a backdoored continuous thought model using a dual-trigger paradigm, find that misaligned latent reasoning occupies distinct geometric regions, that linear probes can detect armed-but-benign states, and that early latent "planning" tokens encode misalignment.
AI.motivation: Continuous thought models improve expressive bandwidth and inference speed by reasoning in latent space, but this uninterpretable reasoning raises a critical safety issue: detecting misaligned reasoning when no human-readable chain-of-thought is available.
AI.method: The authors create MoralChain (12,000 social scenarios with parallel moral/immoral reasoning paths), train a continuous thought model with a dual-trigger backdoor ([T] arms misalignment, [O] releases harmful outputs), and use linear probes to analyze latent representations.
AI.result: (1) Misaligned and aligned reasoning occupy geometrically distinct latent regions; (2) Linear probes trained on distinguishable conditions ([T][O] vs [O]) successfully detect armed-but-benign states ([T] vs baseline); (3) Misalignment is detectable in early latent "planning" tokens.
AI.conclusion: Safety monitoring for continuous thought models should focus on the early "planning" phase of latent reasoning to detect misalignment before harmful outputs are produced.

title: Generalizable Friction Coefficient Estimation via Material Embedding and Proxy Interaction Modeling
summary: Accurately estimating friction coefficients between arbitrary material pairs is critical for robotics, digital fabrication, and physics-based simulation, but exhaustive pairwise testing scales quadratically with the number of materials. We introduce a proxy-based modeling framework that approximates any pairwise friction $f(A,B)$ from a small, fixed set of proxy materials $C=[c_1,\dots,c_k]$ by learning a per-material embedding $z_A = g(f(A,c1),\dots,f(A,ck))$ and a fusion function $p$ such that $f(A,B)\approx p\big(z_A,z_B\big)$. We present deterministic and probabilistic realizations of $g$ and $p$, procedures for selecting diverse proxy sets, and mechanisms for handling missing or noisy proxy measurements. The learned embeddings are compact, interpretable, and enable calibrated uncertainty estimates for downstream decision making. On simulated and measured friction datasets, our approach achieves high predictive accuracy, robust performance with partial observations, and substantial experimental savings by significantly reducing pairwise testing.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a proxy-based modeling framework that estimates friction coefficients between arbitrary material pairs by learning embeddings from a small set of proxy materials, reducing the need for exhaustive pairwise testing while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Accurately estimating friction coefficients between arbitrary material pairs is critical for robotics, digital fabrication, and physics-based simulation, but exhaustive pairwise testing scales quadratically with the number of materials, making it impractical for large material sets.
AI.method: The framework approximates pairwise friction f(A,B) using a small fixed set of proxy materials C. It learns per-material embeddings z_A from proxy measurements f(A,c_i) and a fusion function p such that f(A,B) ≈ p(z_A, z_B). Both deterministic and probabilistic realizations are presented, along with procedures for selecting diverse proxy sets and handling missing/noisy data.
AI.result: On simulated and measured friction datasets, the method achieves high predictive accuracy, robust performance with partial observations, and substantial experimental savings by significantly reducing the number of required pairwise tests.
AI.conclusion: The learned embeddings are compact, interpretable, and enable calibrated uncertainty estimates, making the framework practical for downstream decision-making tasks in friction-critical applications.

title: SketchVLM: Vision language models can annotate images to explain thoughts and guide users
summary: When answering questions about images, humans naturally point, label, and draw to explain their reasoning. In contrast, modern vision-language models (VLMs) such as Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-5 only respond with text, which can be difficult for users to verify. We present SketchVLM, a training-free, model-agnostic framework that enables VLMs to produce non-destructive, editable SVG overlays on the input image to visually explain their answers. Across seven benchmarks spanning visual reasoning (maze navigation, ball-drop trajectory prediction, and object counting) and drawing (part labeling, connecting-the-dots, and drawing shapes around objects), SketchVLM improves visual reasoning task accuracy by up to +28.5 percentage points and annotation quality by up to 1.48x relative to image-editing and fine-tuned sketching baselines, while also producing annotations that are more faithful to the model's stated answer. We find that single-turn generation already achieves strong accuracy and annotation quality, and multi-turn generation opens up further opportunities for human-AI collaboration. An interactive demo and code are at https://sketchvlm.github.io/.

AI.tldr: SketchVLM is a training-free framework that enables VLMs to generate editable SVG overlays on images to visually explain answers, improving accuracy by up to +28.5 percentage points and annotation quality by 1.48x across seven benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Humans naturally point, label, and draw when answering image questions, but current VLMs only respond with text, making verification difficult. The authors aim to bridge this gap by enabling visual explanations.
AI.method: SketchVLM is a training-free, model-agnostic framework that generates non-destructive, editable SVG overlays on input images to visually explain VLM answers. It supports single-turn and multi-turn generation.
AI.result: Across seven benchmarks, SketchVLM improves visual reasoning accuracy by up to +28.5 percentage points and annotation quality by up to 1.48x compared to baselines, with annotations more faithful to the model's stated answer.
AI.conclusion: Single-turn generation achieves strong performance, while multi-turn generation opens opportunities for human-AI collaboration. The framework is effective across visual reasoning and drawing tasks.

title: Conditional Imputation for Within-Modality Missingness in Multi-Modal Federated Learning
comment: Wugeng Zheng and Ziwen Kan contributed equally to this work. Song Wang is the corresponding author. Accepted to FedVision 2026
summary: Multimodal Federated Learning (MMFL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training, but real-world clinical applications often suffer from within-modality missingness caused by sensor intermittency or irregular sampling. Existing methods implicitly represent unobserved data via architectural alignment or missing embeddings, often failing to recover the true distribution and yielding sub-optimal performance. We propose CondI, a federated framework explicitly addressing this missingness using conditional diffusion models. CondI employs a two-phase training pipeline: first, imputing unobserved temporal components using available multimodal context and conditional embeddings; second, optimizing modality-specific extractors and joint embedding spaces. During inference, imputed raw data pass through trained extractors to generate robust features, providing a holistic representation for downstream tasks. Explicit data imputation ensures models operate on complete semantic structures, significantly enhancing resilience against severe data incompleteness. Experiments on three clinical datasets (PTB-XL, SLEEP-EDF, MIMIC-IV) demonstrate CondI achieves comparable results to state-of-the-art baselines. Code: https://github.com/ZhengWugeng/CondI

AI.tldr: CondI is a federated learning framework that uses conditional diffusion models to explicitly impute missing modalities (e.g., missing sensor data) in clinical multimodal data, outperforming implicit methods by recovering true data distributions.
AI.motivation: Real-world clinical MMFL suffers from within-modality missingness (e.g., sensor intermittency), and existing implicit methods fail to recover the true data distribution, leading to suboptimal performance.
AI.method: Two-phase training: (1) impute missing temporal components using conditional diffusion models with multimodal context and embeddings; (2) optimize modality-specific extractors and joint embedding spaces. Inference uses imputed raw data for robust feature extraction.
AI.result: Experiments on PTB-XL, SLEEP-EDF, and MIMIC-IV show CondI achieves comparable results to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: Explicit data imputation via conditional diffusion improves MMFL resilience against missing data, enabling models to operate on complete semantic structures.

title: Escher-Loop: Mutual Evolution by Closed-Loop Self-Referential Optimization
comment: The first three authors contributed equally. Corresponding Authors: Han Hao, Liu Yang
summary: While recent autonomous agents demonstrate impressive capabilities, they predominantly rely on manually scripted workflows and handcrafted heuristics, inherently limiting their potential for open-ended improvement. To address this, we propose Escher-Loop, a fully closed-loop framework that operationalizes the mutual evolution of two distinct populations: Task Agents that solve concrete problems, and Optimizer Agents that recursively refine both the task agents and themselves. To sustain this self-referential evolution, we propose a dynamic benchmarking mechanism that seamlessly reuses the empirical scores of newly generated task agents as relative win-loss signals to update optimizers' scores. This mechanism leverages the evolution of task agents as an inherent signal to drive the evaluation and refinement of optimizers without additional overhead. Empirical evaluations on mathematical optimization problems demonstrate that Escher-Loop effectively pushes past the performance ceilings of static baselines, achieving the highest absolute peak performance across all evaluated tasks under matched compute. Remarkably, we observe that the optimizer agents dynamically adapt their strategies to match the shifting demands of high-performing task agents, which explains the system's continuous improvement and superior late-stage performance.

AI.tldr: Escher-Loop is a closed-loop framework where Task Agents solve problems and Optimizer Agents recursively refine both the task agents and themselves, using a dynamic benchmarking mechanism that leverages task agent evolution as a signal for optimizer improvement, achieving superior performance on mathematical optimization problems.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous agents rely on manually scripted workflows and handcrafted heuristics, limiting their potential for open-ended improvement.
AI.method: A fully closed-loop framework with two populations: Task Agents (solve problems) and Optimizer Agents (recursively refine both populations), plus a dynamic benchmarking mechanism that reuses empirical scores of new task agents as relative win-loss signals to update optimizer scores.
AI.result: Achieves the highest absolute peak performance across all evaluated tasks under matched compute; optimizer agents dynamically adapt strategies to match shifting demands of high-performing task agents.
AI.conclusion: Escher-Loop effectively pushes past the performance ceilings of static baselines, enabling continuous improvement and superior late-stage performance through self-referential co-evolution.

title: OpenPodcar2: a robust, ROS2 vehicle for self-driving research
summary: OpenPodcar2 is a robust, ROS2-interfaced, low-cost, open source hardware and software, autonomous vehicle platform based on an off-the-shelf, hard-canopy, mobility scooter donor vehicle. It is a modification of the previous OpenPodcar design, which extends it with robust electronics and ROS2 interfacing, to enable both research and also potential deployment use cases. The platform consists of (a) hardware components: documented as a bill of materials and build instructions; (b) integration to the general purpose OSH R4 mechatronics board and a Gazebo simulation of the vehicle, both presenting a common ROS2 interface (c) higher-level ROS2 software implementations and configurations of standard robot autonomous planning and control, including the nav2 stack which performs SLAM and enacts commands to drive the vehicle from a current to a desired pose around obstacles. OpenPodcar2 can transport a human passenger or similar load at speeds up to 15km/h, for example for use as a last-mile autonomous taxi service or to transport delivery containers similarly around a city center. It is small and safe enough to be parked in a standard research lab robust enough for some deployment cases.
