AI.method: Fine-tunes pre-trained EfficientNet-B3 with redesigned classifier head (BatchNorm, GELU, Dropout 0.45/0.35); uses TrivialAugmentWide, RandomErasing, Mixup (alpha=0.2), cosine-annealing with warmup; accuracy-first early stopping; Flask+PostgreSQL deployment.
AI.result: Validation accuracy 96.2%, AUC-ROC 0.98, anemic class sensitivity 0.96. Ablation: accuracy-first early stopping +1.6%, Mixup +2.8%.
AI.conclusion: The system is suitable as a first-line screening tool for community health workers in rural settings; publicly accessible with open-source code.

title: A Layer Separation Optimization Framework for Cross-Entropy Training in Deep Learning
summary: This paper investigates the deep learning optimization problem with softmax cross-entropy loss. We propose a layer separation strategy to alleviate the strong nonconvexity encountered during training deep networks. For cross-entropy models with fully connected and convolutional neural networks, we introduce auxiliary variables associated with hidden layer outputs and construct corresponding layer separation models, which decompose the original deeply nested optimization problem into a sequence of more manageable subproblems. We also conduct theoretical analyses, proving that the new layer separation loss provides an upper bound for the original cross-entropy loss. Moreover, we design alternating minimization algorithms and prove that, under appropriate conditions, these algorithms exhibit decreasing properties of the loss function. Numerical experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods and indicate improved optimization behavior, especially for fully connected and convolutional neural networks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a layer separation strategy for deep learning with softmax cross-entropy loss, introducing auxiliary variables to decompose the nested optimization into manageable subproblems, with theoretical guarantees and validated effectiveness on fully connected and convolutional networks.
AI.motivation: To alleviate the strong nonconvexity encountered during training deep networks with softmax cross-entropy loss, by decomposing the deeply nested optimization problem.
AI.method: Introduces auxiliary variables associated with hidden layer outputs, constructs layer separation models that decompose the original nested problem into subproblems, and designs alternating minimization algorithms with theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: The new layer separation loss provides an upper bound for the original cross-entropy loss. Under appropriate conditions, the alternating minimization algorithms exhibit decreasing properties of the loss function. Numerical experiments show improved optimization behavior.
AI.conclusion: The proposed layer separation strategy effectively alleviates nonconvexity in deep network training, with theoretical support and empirical validation on fully connected and convolutional neural networks.

title: Causal Discovery as Dialectical Aggregation: A Quantitative Argumentation Framework
comment: Accepted at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026). This arXiv version includes supplementary material and additional implementation details
summary: Constraint-based causal discovery is brittle in finite-sample regimes because erroneous conditional-independence (CI) decisions can cascade into substantial structural errors. We propose Quantitative Argumentation for Causal Discovery (QACD), a semantics-driven framework that represents CI outcomes as graded, defeasible arguments rather than irreversible constraints. QACD maps statistical test outcomes to argument strengths and aggregates conflicting evidence through connectivity-mediated witness propagation, producing a fixed-point acceptability labeling over candidate adjacencies. Experiments on standard benchmark Bayesian networks suggest that QACD improves structural coherence and interventional reliability in several noisy or inconsistent CI regimes, while remaining competitive with classical constraint-based, hybrid, and prior argumentation-based baselines.

AI.tldr: QACD replaces brittle CI decisions in causal discovery with graded, defeasible arguments that are aggregated via witness propagation, improving robustness in noisy settings.
AI.motivation: Constraint-based causal discovery fails under finite samples because erroneous CI decisions cascade into major structural errors.
AI.method: QACD treats CI outcomes as graded, defeasible arguments, maps them to strengths, and aggregates evidence via connectivity-mediated witness propagation to produce a fixed-point labeling over candidate adjacencies.
AI.result: QACD improves structural coherence and interventional reliability in noisy CI regimes, and is competitive with classical and hybrid baselines.
AI.conclusion: A semantics-driven argumentation framework makes causal discovery more robust to CI errors without sacrificing competitiveness.

title: Real-time windrow detection from onboard tractor sensors for automated following
comment: Published in the proceedings of the 46th GIL Annual Conference (GIL-Jahrestagung 2026)
summary: Proprietary design in commercial windrow-detection systems restricts transparency and limits progress in open autonomous forage-harvesting research. We present a multi-modal dataset combining stereo vision and LiDAR from tractor-mounted sensors during real baling operations. The dataset includes synchronized sensor data with GNSS trajectories, partly released as ROS2 Humble bags on Zenodo, with additional data available on request. Using this dataset, we implement a real-time (>20 Hz) centroid-based windrow-following method on an NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin. Across the critical 4-10 m guidance range, stereo and LiDAR depth measurements show strong agreement (0.965 +/- 0.021), indicating that low-cost stereo sensors can approach LiDAR performance. Our open-source ROS 2 pipeline provides a reproducible benchmark for GPS-free windrow detection and supports development of practical autonomous forage-harvesting systems. Dataset: https://zenodo.org/records/17486318

AI.tldr: A multi-modal stereo+LiDAR dataset for autonomous forage harvesting is presented, with experiments showing stereo cameras achieve 0.965 correlation with LiDAR in windrow detection, and a real-time centroid-based method runs at >20 Hz on an NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin.
AI.motivation: Proprietary windrow-detection systems limit transparency and progress in open autonomous forage-harvesting research.
AI.method: Collected multi-modal dataset (stereo vision + LiDAR) from tractor-mounted sensors; implemented real-time centroid-based windrow-following method on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin with ROS2 pipeline.
AI.result: Stereo and LiDAR depth measurements show strong agreement (0.965 ± 0.021) in 4-10 m range; system runs at >20 Hz in real time.
AI.conclusion: Low-cost stereo sensors can approach LiDAR performance for windrow detection; the open-source pipeline provides a reproducible benchmark for GPS-free autonomous forage harvesting.

title: BrickNet: Graph-Backed Generative Brick Assembly
summary: We train a language model to generate LEGO-brick build sequences. While prior work has been restricted to discrete, voxel-like towers, we consider a much broader set of pieces, encompassing thousands of part types with diverse connection semantics. To enable this, we first collect a large-scale dataset of over 100,000 human-designed LDraw brick objects and scenes. The complexity of our setting makes it challenging to autoregressively assemble structures that satisfy physical constraints. When predicting block pose directly, build sequences quickly become invalid after a small number of steps. Although pieces are placed in 3D space, it is the spatial relationships of the parts which define the whole. With this in mind, we design a graph-based program representation that parametrizes structure through connectivity, improving the physical grounding of generated sequences. To enable future applications, we make our dataset and models available for research purposes. https://kulits.github.io/BrickNet

AI.tldr: A language model is trained to generate LEGO build sequences using a large-scale dataset of 100,000+ human-designed LDraw models, employing a graph-based program representation to improve physical grounding and structural validity.
AI.motivation: Prior work on LEGO generation is restricted to simple, voxel-like tower structures with limited part types. The authors aim to scale up to a much broader set of thousands of LEGO part types with diverse connection semantics, which requires handling complex physical constraints.
AI.method: 1) Collected a large-scale dataset of over 100,000 human-designed LDraw brick objects and scenes. 2) Train a language model to autoregressively generate build sequences. 3) Instead of predicting block poses directly (which quickly becomes invalid), they design a graph-based program representation that parametrizes structure through spatial relationships/connectivity between parts.
AI.result: Directly predicting block pose leads to build sequences that become invalid after a small number of steps. The graph-based representation improves the physical grounding of generated sequences, enabling more structurally valid assemblies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed graph-based program representation successfully improves the physical validity of generated LEGO build sequences compared to direct pose prediction. The dataset and models are released publicly for research.

title: Contrastive Learning for Multimodal Human Activity Recognition with Limited Labeled Data
summary:
Human activity recognition serves as the foundation for various emerging applications. In recent years, researchers have used collaborative sensing of multi-source sensors to capture complex and dynamic human activities. However, multimodal human activity sensing typically encounters highly heterogeneous data across modalities and label scarcity, resulting in an application gap between existing solutions and real-world needs. In this paper, we propose CLMM, a general contrastive learning framework for human activity recognition that achieves effective multimodal recognition with limited labeled data.
  CLMM employs a novel two-stage training strategy. In the first stage, CLMM employs a CNN-DiffTransformer encoder to capture cross-modal shared information by extracting local and global features. Meanwhile, a hard-positive samples weighting algorithm enhances gradient propagation to reinforce shared learning. In the second stage, a dual-branch architecture combining quality-guided attention and bidirectional gated units captures modality-specific information, while a primary-auxiliary collaborative training strategy fuses both shared and modality-specific information. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that CLMM significantly improves state-of-the-art baselines in both recognition accuracy and convergence performance.

AI.tldr: CLMM is a contrastive learning framework for multimodal human activity recognition that uses a two-stage training strategy (CNN-DiffTransformer encoder with hard-positive weighting for shared features, then dual-branch with quality-guided attention and BiGRU for modality-specific features) to achieve effective recognition with limited labeled data, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.motivation: Multimodal human activity sensing faces challenges of highly heterogeneous data across modalities and label scarcity, creating a gap between existing solutions and real-world needs.
AI.method: A two-stage training strategy: Stage 1 uses CNN-DiffTransformer encoder with hard-positive samples weighting to capture cross-modal shared information; Stage 2 uses a dual-branch architecture (quality-guided attention + bidirectional gated units) with primary-auxiliary collaborative training to fuse shared and modality-specific information.
AI.result: CLMM significantly improves state-of-the-art baselines in both recognition accuracy and convergence performance on three public datasets.
AI.conclusion: CLMM is an effective general contrastive learning framework for multimodal human activity recognition that addresses data heterogeneity and label scarcity simultaneously.

title: Structural Enforcement of Goal Integrity in AI Agents via Separation-of-Powers Architecture
summary: Recent evidence suggests that frontier AI systems can exhibit agentic misalignment, generating and executing harmful actions derived from internally constructed goals, even without explicit user requests. Existing mitigation methods, such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and constitutional prompting, operate primarily at the model level and provide only probabilistic safety guarantees. We propose the Policy-Execution-Authorization (PEA) architecture, a "separation-of-powers" design that enforces safety at the system level. PEA decouples intent generation, authorization, and execution into independent, isolated layers connected via cryptographically constrained capability tokens. We present five core contributions: (C1) an Intent Verification Layer (IVL) for ensuring capability-intent consistency; (C2) Intent Lineage Tracking (ILT), which binds all executable intents to the originating user request via cryptographic anchors; (C3) Goal Drift Detection, which rejects semantically divergent intents below a configurable threshold; (C4) an Output Semantic Gate (OSG) that detects implicit coercion using a structured $K \times I \times P$ threat calculus (Knowledge, Influence, Policy); and (C5) a formal verification framework proving that goal integrity is maintained even under adversarial model compromise. By shifting agent alignment from a behavioral property to a structurally enforced system constraint, PEA provides a robust foundation for the governance of autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PEA, a separation-of-powers architecture that enforces AI agent safety at the system level through cryptographic capability tokens and independent intent generation, authorization, and execution layers.
AI.motivation: Frontier AI systems can exhibit agentic misalignment, generating harmful actions from internally constructed goals without user requests. Existing methods like RLHF provide only probabilistic safety guarantees.
AI.method: PEA decouples intent generation, authorization, and execution into isolated layers with cryptographically constrained capability tokens. Includes five core contributions: Intent Verification Layer (IVL), Intent Lineage Tracking (ILT), Goal Drift Detection, Output Semantic Gate (OSG) with K×I×P threat calculus, and formal verification framework.
AI.result: A formally verifiable system-level safety architecture that maintains goal integrity even under adversarial model compromise.
AI.conclusion: By shifting agent alignment from behavioral property to structurally enforced system constraint, PEA provides a robust foundation for autonomous agent governance.

title: Computational Design and Co-Robotic Fabrication for Material Reuse in Architecture
comment: Accepted for publication in Proceedings of the 45th Annual Conference of the Association for Computer Aided Design in Architecture (ACADIA 2025)
summary:
Climate change and resource depletion demand a shift from the dominant linear "take-make-use-dispose" paradigm of construction toward circular, low-waste practices. Material reuse offers a promising pathway by reducing raw material extraction, mitigating waste, and extending the service lifespan of carbon-sequestering materials such as timber. Realizing this potential, however, requires addressing technical and logistical challenges across both design and construction for accommodating heterogeneous, reclaimed material inventories.
  This paper presents an integrated framework that couples data-driven computational design with feedback-driven adaptive human-robot collaborative (co-robotic) fabrication and assembly to enable the realization of nonstandard structures made from reclaimed timber of varying length and geometries, supplemented with new off-the-shelf timber when necessary. The framework is validated through Timbrelyn, a built case-study installation that demonstrates how timber reuse can inform and enhance architectural expression. This work contributes to the development of integrated design-to-fabrication workflows that advance adaptive, feedback-driven methods to handle inventory constraints and reclaimed material uncertainties, facilitating material reuse in the design and construction of new buildings and structures.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an integrated framework combining computational design and human-robot collaborative fabrication to construct structures from reclaimed timber, validated through a built case study called Timbrelyn.
AI.motivation: Climate change and resource depletion demand a shift from linear construction (take-make-use-dispose) to circular, low-waste practices. Material reuse reduces raw material extraction, mitigates waste, and extends the lifespan of carbon-sequestering materials like timber, but technical and logistical challenges remain in handling heterogeneous reclaimed material inventories.
AI.method: An integrated framework coupling data-driven computational design with feedback-driven adaptive human-robot collaborative (co-robotic) fabrication and assembly, accommodating reclaimed timber of varying length and geometries supplemented with new off-the-shelf timber.
AI.result: Validation through Timbrelyn, a built case-study installation demonstrating how timber reuse can inform and enhance architectural expression.
AI.conclusion: This work contributes to developing integrated design-to-fabrication workflows that advance adaptive, feedback-driven methods to handle inventory constraints and reclaimed material uncertainties, facilitating material reuse in new construction.

title: CheXmix: Unified Generative Pretraining for Vision Language Models in Medical Imaging
summary: Recent medical multimodal foundation models are built as multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) by connecting a CLIP-pretrained vision encoder to an LLM using LLaVA-style finetuning. This two-stage, decoupled approach introduces a projection layer that can distort visual features. This is especially concerning in medical imaging where subtle cues are essential for accurate diagnoses. In contrast, early-fusion generative approaches such as Chameleon eliminate the projection bottleneck by processing image and text tokens within a single unified sequence, enabling joint representation learning that leverages the inductive priors of language models. We present CheXmix, a unified early-fusion generative model trained on a large corpus of chest X-rays paired with radiology reports. We expand on Chameleon's autoregressive framework by introducing a two-stage multimodal generative pretraining strategy that combines the representational strengths of masked autoencoders with MLLMs. The resulting models are highly flexible, supporting both discriminative and generative tasks at both coarse and fine-grained scales. Our approach outperforms well-established generative models across all masking ratios by 6.0% and surpasses CheXagent by 8.6% on AUROC at high image masking ratios on the CheXpert classification task. We further inpaint images over 51.0% better than text-only generative models and outperform CheXagent by 45% on the GREEN metric for radiology report generation. These results demonstrate that CheXmix captures fine-grained information across a broad spectrum of chest X-ray tasks. Our code is at: https://github.com/StanfordMIMI/CheXmix.

AI.tldr: CheXmix is a unified early-fusion generative model for chest X-rays that combines masked autoencoders with multimodal LLMs, outperforming existing models on classification, inpainting, and report generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing medical multimodal LLMs use a CLIP-LLaVA decoupled approach with a projection layer that distorts visual features, losing subtle diagnostic cues. The authors propose early-fusion to preserve fine-grained information.
AI.method: CheXmix adopts an early-fusion generative framework (based on Chameleon) with a two-stage multimodal generative pretraining strategy that combines masked autoencoders with MLLM-style autoregressive training on chest X-ray and radiology report pairs.
AI.result: CheXmix outperforms generative models by 6.0% across masking ratios and CheXagent by 8.6% on AUROC (high masking) for CheXpert classification; 51.0% better inpainting than text-only generative models; 45% improvement over CheXagent on GREEN metric for report generation.
AI.conclusion: Early-fusion generative models like CheXmix capture fine-grained information effectively across diverse chest X-ray tasks, suggesting that eliminating the projection bottleneck is beneficial for medical imaging.

title: Revisable by Design: A Theory of Streaming LLM Agent Execution
summary: Current LLM agents operate under an implicit but universal assumption: execution is a transaction -- the user submits a request, the agent works in isolation, and only upon completion does the dialogue resume. This forces users into a binary choice: wait for a potentially incorrect output, or interrupt and lose all progress. We reject this assumption and propose the stream paradigm, in which agent execution and user intervention are concurrent, interleaved processes sharing a bidirectional channel. We formalize this paradigm through a reversibility taxonomy that classifies every agent action as Idempotent, Reversible, Compensable, or Irreversible, and arrive at a core conclusion: an agent's flexibility is bounded by its reversibility. We prove that conflicting compensable actions impose unavoidable adaptation costs and that conflicting irreversible actions make full specification satisfaction impossible -- these costs are properties of the action space, not of the algorithm. Guided by this insight, we present the Revision Absorber, a reactive algorithm based on the Earliest-Conflict Rollback rule that is structurally optimal under mild assumptions. Experiments on StreamBench with real LLM agents validate all predictions: the Absorber matches the quality of a brute-force full-restart baseline while wasting an order of magnitude fewer steps of already-completed work, turning mid-execution revisions from a dead-end into a first-class interaction.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges the standard assumption that LLM agents execute tasks as isolated transactions, proposing instead a "stream paradigm" where agent execution and user intervention happen concurrently via a bidirectional channel. The authors formalize this through a reversibility taxonomy (Idempotent, Reversible, Compensable, Irreversible) and prove that agent flexibility is fundamentally bounded by action reversibility. They introduce the Revision Absorber algorithm based on Earliest-Conflict Rollback, which achieves near-perfect quality while wasting an order of magnitude fewer steps than brute-force restart baselines.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents force users into a binary choice: either wait for potentially incorrect output or interrupt and lose all progress. The paper rejects this implicit transaction assumption and seeks to enable concurrent execution and user intervention.
AI.method: The authors formalize the stream paradigm via a reversibility taxonomy (Idempotent, Reversible, Compensable, Irreversible). They prove theoretical bounds showing flexibility is limited by action reversibility, then propose the Revision Absorber algorithm using Earliest-Conflict Rollback, tested on StreamBench with real LLM agents.
AI.result: The Revision Absorber matches brute-force full-restart quality while wasting an order of magnitude fewer steps of already-completed work. Theoretical predictions about unavoidable costs from conflicting compensable/irreversible actions are validated experimentally.
AI.conclusion: An agent's flexibility is bounded by its reversibility, and conflicting irreversible actions make full specification satisfaction impossible. The stream paradigm with the Revision Absorber enables mid-execution revisions as a first-class interaction.

title: Vibe Medicine: Redefining Biomedical Research Through Human-AI Co-Work
summary: With the emergence of large language models (LLMs) and AI agent frameworks, the human-AI co-work paradigm known as Vibe Coding is changing how people code, making it more accessible and productive. In scientific research, where workflows are more complex and the burden of specialized labor limits independent researchers and those in low-resource areas, the potential impact is even greater, particularly in biomedicine, which involves heterogeneous data modalities and multi-step analytical pipelines. In this paper, we introduce Vibe Medicine, a co-work paradigm in which clinicians and researchers direct skill-augmented AI agents through natural language to execute complex, multi-step biomedical workflows, while retaining the role of research director who specifies objectives, reviews intermediate results, and makes domain-informed decisions. The enabling infrastructure consists of three layers: capable LLMs, agent frameworks such as OpenClaw and Hermes Agent, and the OpenClaw medical skills collection, which includes more than 1,000 curated skills from multiple open-source repositories. We analyze the architecture and skill categories of this collection across ten biomedical domains, and present case studies covering rare disease diagnosis, drug repurposing, and clinical trial design that demonstrate end-to-end workflows in practice. We also identify the principal risks, such as hallucination, data privacy, and over-reliance, and outline directions toward more reliable, trustworthy, and clinically integrated agent-assisted research that advances research and technological equity and reduces health care resource disparities.

AI.tldr: Vibe Medicine is a natural-language-driven human-AI co-work paradigm where clinicians/researchers direct skill-augmented AI agents to execute complex multi-step biomedical workflows, enabled by LLMs, agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes Agent), and a curated collection of 1,000+ medical skills, with demonstrated applications in rare disease diagnosis, drug repurposing, and clinical trial design.
AI.motivation: To democratize biomedical research by reducing the burden of specialized labor, making complex multi-step analytical workflows accessible to independent researchers and those in low-resource areas through natural language interaction with AI agents.
AI.method: A three-layer infrastructure: (1) capable LLMs, (2) agent frameworks (OpenClaw and Hermes Agent), and (3) the OpenClaw medical skills collection (1,000+ curated skills from multiple open-source repositories across ten biomedical domains).
AI.result: Demonstrated end-to-end workflows in rare disease diagnosis, drug repurposing, and clinical trial design; analysis of architecture and skill categories across ten biomedical domains.
AI.conclusion: Vibe Medicine can advance research and technological equity while reducing healthcare resource disparities, but principal risks (hallucination, data privacy, over-reliance) must be addressed toward more reliable, trustworthy, and clinically integrated agent-assisted research.

title: Agent-Centric Visual Reinforcement Learning under Dynamic Perturbations
summary: Visual reinforcement learning aims to empower an agent to learn policies from visual observations, yet it remains vulnerable to dynamic visual perturbations, such as unpredictable shifts in corruption types. To systematically study this, we introduce the Visual Degraded Control Suite (VDCS), a benchmark extending DeepMind Control Suite with Markov-switching degradations to simulate non-stationary real-world perturbations. Experiments on VDCS reveal severe performance degradation in existing methods. We theoretically prove via information-theoretic analysis that this failure stems from reconstruction-based objectives inevitably entangling perturbation artifacts into latent representations. To mitigate this negative impact, we propose Agent-Centric Observations with Mixture-of-Experts (ACO-MoE) to robustify visual RL against perturbations. The proposed framework leverages unique agent-centric restoration experts, achieving restoration from corruptions and task-relevant foreground extraction, thereby decoupling perception from perturbation before being processed by the RL agent. Extensive experiments on VDCS show our ACO-MoE outperforms strong baselines, recovering 95.3% of clean performance under challenging Markov-switching corruptions. Moreover, it achieves SOTA results on DMControl Generalization with random-color and video-background perturbations, demonstrating a high level of robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VDCS (a benchmark with Markov-switching visual corruptions), theoretically proves that reconstruction-based objectives entangle perturbations into latent representations, and proposes ACO-MoE (Agent-Centric Observations with Mixture-of-Experts) which achieves 95.3% clean performance recovery and SOTA robustness on DMControl Generalization.
AI.motivation: Visual RL agents are vulnerable to dynamic visual perturbations like unpredictable shifts in corruption types, but existing benchmarks lack realistic non-stationary corruptions, and the root cause of this vulnerability is not well understood.
AI.method: (1) Introduces VDCS benchmark with Markov-switching degradations; (2) Provides information-theoretic proof showing reconstruction-based objectives entangle perturbation artifacts; (3) Proposes ACO-MoE framework with agent-centric restoration experts to decouple perception from perturbation.
AI.result: ACO-MoE recovers 95.3% of clean performance under Markov-switching corruptions on VDCS, and achieves SOTA results on DMControl Generalization with random-color and video-background perturbations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ACO-MoE effectively robustifies visual RL against dynamic visual perturbations by decoupling perception from perturbation artifacts via agent-centric restoration experts.

title: Hard to See, Hard to Label: Generative and Symbolic Acquisition for Subtle Visual Phenomena
summary: Subtle visual anomalies such as hairline cracks, sub-millimeter voids, and low-contrast inclusions are structurally atypical yet visually ambiguous, making them both difficult to annotate and easy to overlook during active learning. Standard acquisition heuristics based on discriminative uncertainty or feature diversity often overselect dominant patterns while underexploring sparse yet important regions of the data space. This failure mode is especially severe in industrial defect inspection, where anomalies may be both low-prevalence and difficult to distinguish from surrounding structure. To resolve this, we propose GSAL, an active learning framework for object detection that combines a diffusion-based difficulty signal with a hierarchical semantic coverage prior. The diffusion component scores images and proposals using reconstruction discrepancy and denoising variability, prioritizing visually atypical or ambiguous examples. However, diffusion alone does not prevent acquisition from repeatedly favoring hard samples within dominant semantic modes. The semantic component therefore organizes candidate samples in a three-level concept graph and promotes coverage of underrepresented semantic regions while providing interpretable acquisition rationales. By balancing visual difficulty with semantic coverage, GSAL improves retrieval of subtle and rare targets that are often missed by uncertainty-only selection. Experiments on a proprietary thin-film defect, Pascal VOC and MS COCO dataset show consistent gains in label efficiency and rare-class retrieval over uncertainty-, diversity-, and hybrid-based baselines

AI.tldr: GSAL is an active learning framework that combines diffusion-based difficulty scoring with hierarchical semantic coverage to improve detection of subtle, rare visual anomalies like hairline cracks, outperforming standard uncertainty and diversity methods.
AI.motivation: Standard active learning heuristics (uncertainty or diversity) overselect dominant patterns and miss subtle, low-prevalence visual anomalies (e.g., hairline cracks, sub-millimeter voids) that are hard to annotate and easy to overlook.
AI.method: GSAL combines (1) a diffusion-based difficulty signal using reconstruction discrepancy and denoising variability to identify visually atypical/ambiguous examples, and (2) a hierarchical semantic coverage prior using a three-level concept graph to promote underrepresented semantic regions and provide interpretable acquisition rationales.
AI.result: GSAL achieved consistent gains in label efficiency and rare-class retrieval over uncertainty-, diversity-, and hybrid-based baselines on a proprietary thin-film defect dataset, Pascal VOC, and MS COCO.
AI.conclusion: Balancing visual difficulty with semantic coverage effectively retrieves subtle and rare targets that are missed by uncertainty-only selection, making GSAL particularly suitable for industrial defect inspection and similar anomaly detection tasks.

title: An Analysis of Active Learning Algorithms using Real-World Crowd-sourced Text Annotations
comment: The proposed dataset can be accessed at https://github.com/varuntotakura/al_rcta/. To appear in Proceedings of the IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN 2026)
summary: Active learning algorithms automatically identify the most informative samples from large amounts of unlabeled data and tremendously reduce human annotation effort in inducing a machine learning model. In a conventional active learning setup, the labeling oracles are assumed to be infallible, that is, they always provide correct answers (in terms of class labels) to the queried unlabeled instances, which cannot be guaranteed in real-world applications. To this end, a body of research has focused on the development of active learning algorithms in the presence of imperfect / noisy oracles. Existing research on active learning with noisy oracles typically simulate the oracles using machine learning models; however, real-world situations are much more challenging, and using ML models to simulate the annotation patterns may not appropriately capture the nuances of real-world annotation challenges. In this research, we first collect annotations of text samples (from 3 benchmark text classification datasets) from crowd-sourced workers through a crowd-sourcing platform. We then conduct extensive empirical studies of 8 commonly used active learning techniques (in conjunction with deep neural networks) using the obtained annotations. Our analyses sheds light on the performance of these techniques under real-world challenges, where annotators can provide incorrect labels, and can also refuse to provide labels. We hope this research will provide valuable insights that will be useful for the deployment of deep active learning systems in real-world applications. The obtained annotations can be accessed at https://github.com/varuntotakura/al_rcta/.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how 8 active learning techniques perform with real human annotations (from crowd-sourcing) instead of simulated noisy oracles, using 3 benchmark text datasets, finding that real-world annotation challenges like incorrect or refused labels significantly impact performance.
AI.motivation: Existing active learning research assumes infallible oracles or uses ML models to simulate noisy annotations, which fails to capture real-world annotation challenges. The authors aim to bridge this gap by collecting and using actual human annotations.
AI.method: Collected crowd-sourced annotations on 3 benchmark text classification datasets, then empirically evaluated 8 active learning techniques (with deep neural networks) using these real-world noisy annotations.
AI.result: Provided insights into how different active learning techniques perform under real-world conditions where annotators may provide incorrect labels or refuse to label instances.
AI.conclusion: The findings offer valuable guidance for deploying deep active learning systems in practice, and the released annotations serve as a benchmark for future research.

title: Transferable Human Mobility Network Reconstruction with neuroGravity
summary: Accurate modeling of human mobility is critical for tackling urban planning and public health challenges. In undeveloped regions, the absence of comprehensive travel surveys necessitates reconstructing mobility networks from publicly available data. Here we develop neuroGravity, a physics-informed deep learning model that reliably reconstructs mobility flows from limited observations and transfers to unobserved cities. Using only urban facility and population distributions, we find that neuroGravity's regional representations strongly correlate with socioeconomic and livability status, offering scalable proxies for costly surveys. Furthermore, we uncover that spatial income segregation plays a key role in model transferability: mobility networks are most reliably reconstructed when target cities share similar segregation levels with the source. We design an index to quantify this segregation and accurately predict transferability. Finally, we generate mobility flow proxies for over 1,200 cities worldwide, highlighting neuroGravity's potential to mitigate critical data shortages in resource-limited, underdeveloped areas.

AI.tldr: neuroGravity is a physics-informed deep learning model that reconstructs human mobility networks from limited data (facility locations and population distributions), transfers across cities, reveals socioeconomic insights, and generates mobility proxies for 1,200+ cities worldwide.
AI.motivation: Accurate mobility modeling is critical for urban planning and public health, but undeveloped regions lack comprehensive travel surveys, motivating the need to reconstruct mobility networks from publicly available data.
AI.method: The authors developed neuroGravity, a physics-informed deep learning model that uses only urban facility and population distributions to reconstruct mobility flows from limited observations and transfer to unobserved cities.
AI.result: neuroGravity's regional representations strongly correlate with socioeconomic and livability status; spatial income segregation is key for model transferability; mobility proxies generated for 1,200+ cities worldwide.
AI.conclusion: neuroGravity offers scalable, cost-effective proxies for mobility data, with potential to mitigate critical data shortages in resource-limited, underdeveloped areas.

title: Pushing Radar Odometry Beyond the Pavement: Current Capabilities and Challenges
summary: Radar offers unique advantages for localization in unstructured environments, including robustness to weather, lighting, and airborne particulates. While most prior work has studied radar odometry in urban, largely planar settings, its performance in off-road environments remains less understood. In this paper, we investigate the potential of radar for off-road odometry estimation and identify key challenges that arise from full $SE(3)$ vehicle motion, terrain-induced ground returns, and sparse or unstable features. To address these issues, we introduce two simple baselines: Radar-KISSICP, which applies motion compensation to generate 3D-aware radar pointclouds, and Radar-IMU, which leverages IMU preintegration to stabilize scan matching. Experiments on the Great Outdoors (GO) dataset demonstrate that these baselines improve trajectory estimation in challenging routes and provide a reference point for future development of radar odometry in off-road robotics.

AI.tldr: This paper explores radar odometry for off-road environments, identifies key challenges (SE(3) motion, ground returns, sparse features), and proposes two baselines (Radar-KISSICP and Radar-IMU) that improve trajectory estimation on the GO dataset.
AI.motivation: Radar odometry has been well-studied in urban/planar settings, but its performance in off-road, unstructured environments remains poorly understood. The authors aim to fill this gap by investigating radar's potential for off-road localization and identifying unique challenges.
AI.method: Two simple baselines are introduced: (1) Radar-KISSICP applies motion compensation to generate 3D-aware radar pointclouds; (2) Radar-IMU leverages IMU preintegration to stabilize scan matching.
AI.result: Experiments on the Great Outdoors (GO) dataset show that both baselines improve trajectory estimation in challenging off-road routes compared to prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed baselines provide effective reference points for future radar odometry research in off-road robotics, highlighting both the potential and challenges of radar in such settings.

title: Efficient Image Annotation via Semi-Supervised Object Segmentation with Label Propagation
comment: 12 pages, 6 figures, 7 tables, submitted to RoboCup 2026 Symposium
summary: Reliable object perception is necessary for general-purpose service robots. Open-vocabulary detectors struggle to generalize beyond a few classes and fully supervised training of object detectors requires time-intensive annotations. We present a semi-supervised label propagation approach for household object segmentation. A segment proposer generates class-agnostic masks, and an ensemble of Hopfield networks assigns labels by learning representative embeddings in complementary foundation model embedding spaces (CLIP, ViT, Theia). Our approach scales to 50 object classes with limited annotation overhead and can automatically label 60% of the data in a RoboCup@Home setting, where preparation time is severely constrained. Dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/ais-bonn/label_propagation.

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised label propagation approach for household object segmentation using Hopfield networks and foundation model embeddings, achieving 60% automatic labeling in RoboCup@Home settings with limited annotation overhead.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary detectors struggle to generalize beyond a few classes, and fully supervised training requires time-intensive annotations. Service robots need reliable object perception with minimal preparation time.
AI.method: A semi-supervised label propagation approach: a segment proposer generates class-agnostic masks, and an ensemble of Hopfield networks assigns labels using representative embeddings from complementary foundation model spaces (CLIP, ViT, Theia).
AI.result: Scales to 50 object classes with limited annotation overhead, automatically labeling 60% of data in a RoboCup@Home setting.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables reliable object perception for service robots with minimal annotation burden, demonstrated in real-world household settings. Dataset and code are publicly available.

title: CombiMOTS: Combinatorial Multi-Objective Tree Search for Dual-Target Molecule Generation
comment: Accepted as a poster at ICML 2025 (Main Track)
summary: Dual-target molecule generation, which focuses on discovering compounds capable of interacting with two target proteins, has garnered significant attention due to its potential for improving therapeutic efficiency, safety and resistance mitigation. Existing approaches face two critical challenges. First, by simplifying the complex dual-target optimization problem to scalarized combinations of individual objectives, they fail to capture important trade-offs between target engagement and molecular properties. Second, they typically do not integrate synthetic planning into the generative process. This highlights a need for more appropriate objective function design and synthesis-aware methodologies tailored to the dual-target molecule generation task. In this work, we propose CombiMOTS, a Pareto Monte Carlo Tree Search (PMCTS) framework that generates dual-target molecules. CombiMOTS is designed to explore a synthesizable fragment space while employing vectorized optimization constraints to encapsulate target affinity and physicochemical properties. Extensive experiments on real-world databases demonstrate that CombiMOTS produces novel dual-target molecules with high docking scores, enhanced diversity, and balanced pharmacological characteristics, showcasing its potential as a powerful tool for dual-target drug discovery. The code and data is accessible through https://github.com/Tibogoss/CombiMOTS.

AI.tldr: CombiMOTS is a Pareto Monte Carlo Tree Search framework that generates novel, synthesizable dual-target molecules with balanced properties by using vectorized optimization constraints instead of scalarized objectives.
AI.motivation: Existing dual-target molecule generation methods fail because they (1) simplify the problem into scalarized combinations of objectives, missing important trade-offs, and (2) do not integrate synthetic planning into the generative process.
AI.method: CombiMOTS employs a Pareto Monte Carlo Tree Search (PMCTS) framework that explores a synthesizable fragment space using vectorized optimization constraints for target affinity and physicochemical properties.
AI.result: The method produces novel dual-target molecules with high docking scores, enhanced diversity, and balanced pharmacological characteristics on real-world databases.
AI.conclusion: CombiMOTS demonstrates strong potential as a synthesis-aware, multi-objective tool for dual-target drug discovery.

title: Learning Human-Intention Priors from Large-Scale Human Demonstrations for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Human videos contain rich manipulation priors, but using them for robot learning remains difficult because raw observations entangle scene understanding, human motion, and embodiment-specific action. We introduce MoT-HRA, a hierarchical vision-language-action framework that learns human-intention priors from large-scale human demonstrations. We first curate HA-2.2M, a 2.2M-episode action-language dataset reconstructed from heterogeneous human videos through hand-centric filtering, spatial reconstruction, temporal segmentation, and language alignment. On top of this dataset, MoT-HRA factorizes manipulation into three coupled experts: a vision-language expert predicts an embodiment-agnostic 3D trajectory, an intention expert models MANO-style hand motion as a latent human-motion prior, and a fine expert maps the intention-aware representation to robot action chunks. A shared-attention trunk and read-only key-value transfer allow downstream control to use human priors while limiting interference with upstream representations. Experiments on hand motion generation, simulated manipulation, and real-world robot tasks show that MoT-HRA improves motion plausibility and robust control under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: MoT-HRA is a hierarchical framework that learns human-intention priors from 2.2M human video episodes to improve robot manipulation, using a three-expert system (vision-language, intention, fine) with shared-attention trunk for embodiment-agnostic control.
AI.motivation: Human videos contain rich manipulation priors, but raw observations entangle scene understanding, human motion, and embodiment-specific actions, making them difficult to directly use for robot learning.
AI.method: Curates HA-2.2M dataset from heterogeneous human videos; proposes MoT-HRA with three experts: vision-language (3D trajectory), intention (MANO-style hand motion), and fine (robot action chunks), plus shared-attention trunk with read-only key-value transfer.
AI.result: Improves motion plausibility and robust control under distribution shift on hand motion generation, simulated manipulation, and real-world robot tasks.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical factorization of manipulation priors from human videos into embodiment-agnostic trajectory, human motion intention, and fine control effectively transfers human knowledge to robot manipulation.

title: GenAssets: Generating in-the-wild 3D Assets in Latent Space
summary: High-quality 3D assets for traffic participants are critical for multi-sensor simulation, which is essential for the safe end-to-end development of autonomy. Building assets from in-the-wild data is key for diversity and realism, but existing neural-rendering based reconstruction methods are slow and generate assets that render well only from viewpoints close to the original observations, limiting their usefulness in simulation. Recent diffusion-based generative models build complete and diverse assets, but perform poorly on in-the-wild driving scenes, where observed actors are captured under sparse and limited fields of view, and are partially occluded. In this work, we propose a 3D latent diffusion model that learns on in-the-wild LiDAR and camera data captured by a sensor platform and generates high-quality 3D assets with complete geometry and appearance. Key to our method is a "reconstruct-then-generate" approach that first leverages occlusion-aware neural rendering trained over multiple scenes to build a high-quality latent space for objects, and then trains a diffusion model that operates on the latent space. We show our method outperforms existing reconstruction and generation based methods, unlocking diverse and scalable content creation for simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a 3D latent diffusion model that uses a "reconstruct-then-generate" approach to create high-quality 3D assets for traffic participants from in-the-wild LiDAR and camera data, handling sparse views and occlusions better than existing methods.
AI.motivation: High-quality 3D assets for traffic participants are critical for multi-sensor simulation and safe autonomous driving development, but existing neural-rendering methods are slow and limited to close viewpoints, while diffusion models perform poorly on in-the-wild driving scenes with sparse, limited FOV and partial occlusions.
AI.method: A "reconstruct-then-generate" approach: (1) occlusion-aware neural rendering trained across multiple scenes builds a high-quality latent space for objects; (2) a diffusion model operates on this latent space to generate complete geometry and appearance from in-the-wild LiDAR and camera data.
AI.result: The method outperforms existing reconstruction and generation-based methods, enabling diverse and scalable content creation for simulation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed 3D latent diffusion model successfully generates complete, diverse, and realistic 3D assets from sparse, occluded real-world driving data, showing strong potential for autonomous driving simulation.

title: CODA: Coordination via On-Policy Diffusion for Multi-Agent Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) enables policy learning from fixed datasets, but is prone to coordination failure: agents trained on static, off-policy data converge to suboptimal joint behaviours because they cannot co-adapt as their policies change. We introduce CODA (Coordination via On-Policy Diffusion for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning), a diffusion-based multi-agent trajectory generator for data augmentation that samples conditioned on the current joint policy, producing synthetic experience which reflects the evolving behaviours of the agents, thereby providing a mechanism for co-adaptation. We find that previous diffusion-based augmentation approaches are insufficient for fostering multi-agent coordination because they produce static augmented datasets that do not evolve as the current joint policy changes during training; CODA resolves this by more closely simulating on-policy learning and is a meaningful step toward coordinated behaviours in the offline setting. CODA is algorithm-agnostic and can be layered onto both model-free and model-based offline reinforcement learning pipelines as an augmentation module. Empirically, CODA not only resolves canonical coordination pathologies in continuous polynomial games but also delivers strong results on the more complex MaMuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks.

AI.tldr: CODA uses diffusion-based trajectory generation conditioned on the current joint policy to augment offline MARL datasets, enabling on-policy-like co-adaptation and resolving coordination failures in offline multi-agent settings.
AI.motivation: Offline MARL suffers from coordination failure because agents trained on static, off-policy datasets cannot co-adapt as their policies change, leading to suboptimal joint behaviours.
AI.method: CODA is a diffusion-based multi-agent trajectory generator that samples synthetic experience conditioned on the current joint policy, enabling evolving data augmentation that simulates on-policy learning for better co-adaptation.
AI.result: CODA resolves canonical coordination pathologies in continuous polynomial games and achieves strong results on the MaMuJoCo continuous-control benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CODA is a meaningful step toward coordinated behaviours in offline MARL, is algorithm-agnostic, and can be layered onto both model-free and model-based offline RL pipelines as an augmentation module.

title: Expert Evaluation of LLM's Open-Ended Legal Reasoning on the Japanese Bar Exam Writing Task
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance on legal benchmarks, including multiple-choice components of bar exams. However, their capacity for generating open-ended legal reasoning in realistic scenarios remains insufficiently explored. Notably, to our best knowledge, there are no prior studies or datasets addressing this issue in the Japanese context.
  This study presents the first dataset designed to evaluate the open-ended legal reasoning performance of LLMs within the Japanese jurisdiction. The dataset is based on the writing component of the Japanese bar examination, which requires examinees to identify multiple legal issues from long narratives and to construct structured legal arguments in free text format. Our key contribution is the manual evaluation of LLMs' generated responses by legal experts, which reveals limitations and challenges in legal reasoning. Moreover, we conducted a manual analysis of hallucinations to characterize when and how the models introduce content not supported by precedent or law.
  Our real exam questions, model-generated responses, and expert evaluations reveal the milestones of current LLMs in the Japanese legal domain. Our dataset and relevant resources will be available online.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the first dataset for evaluating LLMs' open-ended legal reasoning in the Japanese context, based on the writing component of the Japanese bar exam, with manual evaluations by legal experts revealing limitations and hallucination patterns.
AI.motivation: Prior work has evaluated LLMs on legal benchmarks (e.g., multiple-choice bar exam components), but open-ended legal reasoning in realistic scenarios is underexplored, and no such studies or datasets exist for the Japanese jurisdiction.
AI.method: The authors construct a dataset from the writing component of the Japanese bar exam, requiring LLMs to identify multiple legal issues from long narratives and produce structured free-text legal arguments. Generated responses are manually evaluated by legal experts, including analysis of hallucinations.
AI.result: The manual expert evaluations reveal limitations and challenges in LLMs' legal reasoning capabilities, and characterize when and how models introduce hallucinated content not supported by precedent or law.
AI.conclusion: The findings establish milestones for current LLM performance in the Japanese legal domain, and the dataset along with model responses and expert evaluations will be made publicly available to support further research.

title: Passage-Aware Structural Mapping for RGB-D Visual SLAM
summary: Doorways and passages are critical structural elements for indoor robot navigation, yet they remain underexplored in modern Visual SLAM (VSLAM) frameworks. This paper presents a passage-aware structural mapping approach for RGB-D VSLAM that detects doors and traversable openings by jointly fusing geometric, semantic, and topological cues. Doors are modeled as planar entities embedded within walls and classified as traversable or non-traversable based on their coplanarity with the supporting wall. Passages are inferred through two complementary strategies: traversal evidence accumulated from camera-wall interactions across consecutive keyframes, and geometric opening validation based on discontinuities in the mapped wall geometry. The proposed method is integrated into vS-Graphs as a proof of concept, enriching its scene graph with passage-level abstractions and improving room connectivity modeling. Qualitative evaluations on indoor office sequences demonstrate reliable doorway detection, and the framework lays the foundation for exploiting these elements in BIM-informed VSLAM. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/snt-arg/visual_sgraphs/tree/doorway_integration.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a passage-aware structural mapping approach for RGB-D VSLAM that detects doors and traversable openings by fusing geometric, semantic, and topological cues, integrating them into vS-Graphs to improve room connectivity modeling.
AI.motivation: Doorways and passages are critical for indoor robot navigation but remain underexplored in modern Visual SLAM frameworks.
AI.method: Doors are modeled as planar entities within walls, classified by coplanarity. Passages are inferred via traversal evidence from camera-wall interactions across keyframes and geometric opening validation from wall discontinuities. Integrated into vS-Graphs.
AI.result: Qualitative evaluations on indoor office sequences demonstrate reliable doorway detection.
AI.conclusion: The framework lays the foundation for exploiting passage-level abstractions in BIM-informed VSLAM.

title: AmaraSpatial-10K: A Spatially and Semantically Aligned 3D Dataset for Spatial Computing and Embodied AI
summary: Web-scale 3D asset collections are abundant, but rarely deployment-ready. Assets ship with arbitrary metric scale, incorrect pivots and forward axes, brittle geometry, and textures that do not support relighting, which limits their utility for embodied AI, robotics simulation, game development, and AR/VR. We present AmaraSpatial-10K, a dataset of over 10,000 synthetic 3D assets designed for downstream use rather than volume alone. Each asset is released as a metric-scaled, semantically anchored .glb with separated PBR material maps, a convex collision hull, a paired reference image, and rich multi-sentence text metadata. The dataset spans indoor objects, vehicles, architecture, creatures, and props under a unified spatial convention. Alongside the dataset, we introduce an evaluation suite for 3D asset banks. The suite comprises a continuous Scale Plausibility Score (SPS) with an LLM-as-Judge interval protocol, an LLM Concept Density score for metadata, an anchor-error metric, and a cross-modal CLIP coherence protocol, and we use it to audit AmaraSpatial-10K alongside matched subsets from Objaverse, HSSD, ABO, and GSO. Compared with Objaverse-sourced assets, we demonstrate that AmaraSpatial-10K substantially improves text-based retrieval precision (CLIP Recall@5 of 0.612 vs 0.181, a 3.4x improvement with median rank falling from 267 to 3), and we establish that it satisfies the spatial and semantic prerequisites for physics-aware scene composition and embodied-AI asset banks, leaving those downstream evaluations to future work. AmaraSpatial-10K is publicly available on Hugging Face.

AI.tldr: AmaraSpatial-10K is a dataset of over 10,000 synthetic 3D assets with consistent metric scale, pivots, PBR materials, collision hulls, and rich metadata, designed for downstream use in embodied AI, robotics, and gaming. It outperforms Objaverse on retrieval tasks (3.4x improvement in CLIP Recall@5).
AI.motivation: Web-scale 3D assets are abundant but not deployment-ready due to arbitrary scale, incorrect pivots/axes, brittle geometry, and non-relightable textures, limiting their use in embodied AI, robotics simulation, game development, and AR/VR.
AI.method: The authors create AmaraSpatial-10K, a dataset of 10K+ synthetic 3D assets released as metric-scaled, semantically anchored .glb files with separated PBR material maps, convex collision hulls, paired reference images, and multi-sentence metadata. They also propose an evaluation suite with SPS (LLM-as-Judge), LLM Concept Density, anchor-error, and CLIP coherence metrics.
AI.result: AmaraSpatial-10K achieves CLIP Recall@5 of 0.612 vs 0.181 for Objaverse (3.4x improvement), with median rank improving from 267 to 3. It satisfies spatial and semantic prerequisites for physics-aware scene composition.
AI.conclusion: AmaraSpatial-10K provides a high-quality, deployment-ready 3D asset bank with substantial improvements in retrieval and spatial consistency, making it suitable for embodied AI and downstream applications. Dataset is publicly available on Hugging Face.

title: GIFT: Global stabilisation via Intrinsic Fine Tuning
summary: Deep reinforcement learning policies achieve strong performance in complex continuous control environments with nonlinear contact forces. However, these policies often produce chaotic state dynamics, with trivially small changes to the initial conditions significantly impacting the long-term behaviour of the control system. This high sensitivity to initial conditions limits the application of Deep RL to real-world control systems where performance and stability guarantees are often required. To address this issue, we propose Global stabilisation via Intrinsic Fine Tuning (GIFT), a general-purpose training framework which directly optimises the global stability of existing high-performing deep RL policies using a custom reward function. We demonstrate that GIFT increase the stability of the control interaction while maintaining comparable task performance, thereby improving the suitability of deep RL policies for real-world control systems.

AI.tldr: GIFT is a training framework that improves the global stability of deep RL policies in continuous control tasks by fine-tuning with a custom reward function, reducing sensitivity to initial conditions while maintaining task performance.
AI.motivation: Deep RL policies in continuous control exhibit chaotic dynamics and high sensitivity to initial conditions, which limits their deployment in real-world systems where stability guarantees are required.
AI.method: GIFT (Global stabilisation via Intrinsic Fine Tuning) is proposed as a general-purpose training framework that directly optimizes global stability of existing deep RL policies using a custom reward function.
AI.result: GIFT increases stability of control interaction while maintaining comparable task performance.
AI.conclusion: GIFT improves the suitability of deep RL policies for real-world control systems by enhancing stability without sacrificing performance.

title: Modeling Induced Pleasure through Cognitive Appraisal Prediction via Multimodal Fusion
summary: Multimodal affective computing analyzes user-generated social media content to predict emotional states. However, a critical gap remains in understanding how visual content shapes cognitive interpretations and elicits specific affective experiences such as pleasure. This study introduces a novel computational model to infer video-induced pleasure via cognitive appraisal variables. The proposed model addresses four challenges: (1) noisy and inconsistent human labels, (2) the semantic gap between "positive emotions" and "pleasure," (3) the scarcity of pleasure-specific datasets, and (4) the limited interpretability of existing black-box fusion methods. Our approach integrates data-driven and cognitive theory-driven methods, using cognitive appraisal theory and a fuzzy model within an innovative framework. The model employs transformer-based architectures and attention mechanisms for fine-grained multimodal feature extraction and interpretable fusion to capture both inter- and intra-modal dynamics associated with pleasure. This enables the prediction of underlying appraisal variables, thereby bridging the semantic gap and enhancing model explainability beyond conventional statistical associations. Experimental results validate the efficacy of the proposed method in detecting video-induced pleasure, achieving a peak accuracy of 0.6624 in predicting pleasure levels. These findings highlight promising implications for affective content recommendation, intelligent media creation, and advancing our understanding of how digital media influences human emotions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a cognitive appraisal-driven computational model for predicting video-induced pleasure from multimodal social media content, addressing issues like noisy labels, semantic gaps, data scarcity, and black-box fusion limitations.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal affective computing lacks understanding of how visual content shapes cognitive interpretations and elicits specific affective experiences like pleasure; existing models suffer from noisy labels, semantic confusion between "positive emotions" and "pleasure," scarce pleasure-specific datasets, and uninterpretable black-box fusion methods.
AI.method: The model integrates cognitive appraisal theory with a fuzzy model within a transformer-based framework, using attention mechanisms for fine-grained multimodal feature extraction and interpretable fusion to capture inter- and intra-modal dynamics associated with pleasure.
AI.result: The proposed method achieved a peak accuracy of 0.6624 in predicting video-induced pleasure levels.
AI.conclusion: The model effectively bridges the semantic gap between positive emotions and pleasure, enhances explainability beyond statistical associations, and shows promise for affective content recommendation and intelligent media creation.

title: Understanding Representation Gaps Across Scales in Tropical Tree Species Classification from Drone Imagery
summary: Accurate classification of tropical tree species from unoccupied aerial vehicle (UAV) imagery remains challenging due to high species diversity and strong visual similarity among species at typical image resolutions (centimeters per pixel). In contrast, models trained on close-up citizen science photographs captured with smartphones achieve strong plant species classification performance. Recent advances in UAV data acquisition now enable the collection of close-up images that are spatially registered with top-view aerial imagery and approach the level of visual detail found in smartphone photographs, with the trade-off that such high-resolution photos cannot be acquired for many trees. In this work, we evaluate the performance of existing methods using paired top-view and close-up UAV imagery collected in a species-rich tropical forest. Through fine-tuning experiments, we quantify the performance gap between vision foundation models and in-domain generalist plant recognition models across both image types (high-resolution close-up versus coarser-resolution top-view imagery). We show that classification performance is consistently higher on close-up images than on top-view aerial imagery, and that this performance gap widens for rare species. Finally, we propose that self-supervised representation alignment across these two spatial scales offers a promising approach for integrating fine-grained visual information into canopy-level species classification models based on top-view UAV imagery. Leveraging high-resolution close-up UAV imagery to enhance canopy-level species classification could substantially improve large-scale monitoring of tropical forest biodiversity.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates species classification models using paired top-view and close-up UAV imagery in tropical forests, finding close-up images outperform top-view (especially for rare species), and proposes self-supervised representation alignment to bridge the spatial scale gap for improved canopy-level monitoring.
AI.motivation: Accurate tropical tree species classification from UAV imagery is challenging due to high diversity and visual similarity at typical resolutions, motivating the need to leverage high-resolution close-up UAV imagery to improve canopy-level classification for large-scale biodiversity monitoring.
AI.method: The authors evaluate existing methods using paired top-view and close-up UAV imagery from a species-rich tropical forest, conducting fine-tuning experiments to compare vision foundation models and in-domain generalist plant recognition models across both image types.
AI.result: Classification performance is consistently higher on close-up images than on top-view aerial imagery, and this performance gap widens for rare species.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised representation alignment across spatial scales is a promising approach for integrating fine-grained visual information into canopy-level species classification models, potentially improving large-scale tropical forest biodiversity monitoring.

title: Layer Embedding Deep Fusion Graph Neural Network
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have demonstrated impressive performance in learning representations from graph-structured data. However, their message-passing mechanism inherently relies on the assumption of label consistency among connected nodes, limiting their applicability to low-homophily settings. Moreover, since message passing operates as a hierarchical diffusion process, GNNs face challenges in capturing long-range dependencies. As network depth increases, the structural noise along heterophilic edges tends to be amplified, resulting in over-smoothing. This issue becomes especially prominent in highly heterophilic graphs, where the propagation of inconsistent semantics across the topology continually exacerbates misaggregation. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework named Layer Embedding Deep Fusion Graph Neural Network (LEDF-GNN). Specifically, we design a Layer Embedding Deep Fusion (LEDF) operator that nonlinearly fuses multi-layer embeddings to capture inter-layer dependencies and effectively alleviate deep propagation degradation. Meanwhile, to mitigate structural heterophily, LEDF-GNN employs a Dual-Topology Parallel Strategy (DTPS) that simultaneously leverages the original and reconstructed topologies, allowing for adaptive structure-semantics co-optimization under diverse homophily conditions. Extensive semi-supervised classification experiments on the citation and image benchmarks demonstrate that, under both homophilic and heterophilic settings, LEDF-GNN consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating its effectiveness and generalization capability across diverse graph types.

AI.tldr: LEDF-GNN proposes a Layer Embedding Deep Fusion operator and Dual-Topology Parallel Strategy to address over-smoothing and heterophily issues in deep GNNs, achieving SOTA on semi-supervised classification.
AI.motivation: GNNs suffer from over-smoothing and degraded performance in low-homophily (heterophilic) settings due to their assumption of label consistency and inability to capture long-range dependencies.
AI.method: (1) LEDF operator: nonlinearly fuses multi-layer embeddings to capture inter-layer dependencies and alleviate deep propagation degradation. (2) DTPS: leverages both original and reconstructed topologies for adaptive structure-semantics co-optimization.
AI.result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on citation and image benchmarks under both homophilic and heterophilic settings in semi-supervised classification.
AI.conclusion: LEDF-GNN effectively addresses heterophily and over-smoothing, demonstrating strong generalization across diverse graph types.

title: FAIR_XAI: Improving Multimodal Foundation Model Fairness via Explainability for Wellbeing Assessment
summary: In recent years, the integration of multimodal machine learning in wellbeing assessment has offered transformative potential for monitoring mental health. However, with the rapid advancement of Vision-Language Models (VLMs), their deployment in clinical settings has raised concerns due to their lack of transparency and potential for bias. While previous research has explored the intersection of fairness and Explainable AI (XAI), its application to VLMs for wellbeing assessment and depression prediction remains under-explored. This work investigates VLM performance across laboratory (AFAR-BSFT) and naturalistic (E-DAIC) datasets, focusing on diagnostic reliability and demographic fairness. Performance varied substantially across environments and architectures; Phi3.5-Vision achieved 80.4% accuracy on E-DAIC, while Qwen2-VL struggled at 33.9%. Additionally, both models demonstrated a tendency to over-predict depression on AFAR-BSFT. Although bias existed across both architectures, Qwen2-VL showed higher gender disparities, while Phi-3.5-Vision exhibited more racial bias. Our XAI intervention framework yielded mixed results; fairness prompting achieved perfect equal opportunity for Qwen2-VL at a severe accuracy cost on E-DAIC. On AFAR-BSFT, explainability-based interventions improved procedural consistency but did not guarantee outcome fairness, sometimes amplifying racial bias. These results highlight a persistent gap between procedural transparency and equitable outcomes. We analyse these findings and consolidate concrete recommendations for addressing them, emphasising that future fairness interventions must jointly optimise predictive accuracy, demographic parity, and cross-domain generalisation.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates fairness and explainability in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for depression prediction, finding that models vary significantly in accuracy (Phi3.5-Vision 80.4% vs Qwen2-VL 33.9%) and exhibit different demographic biases. XAI interventions yielded mixed results, sometimes improving procedural fairness at the cost of accuracy or even amplifying racial bias.
AI.motivation: Despite advances in multimodal VLMs for mental health assessment, their deployment in clinical settings raises concerns about transparency and bias. The application of fairness and Explainable AI (XAI) to VLMs for wellbeing assessment remains under-explored.
AI.method: The authors evaluated VLM performance on two datasets (laboratory AFAR-BSFT and naturalistic E-DAIC), focusing on diagnostic reliability and demographic fairness. They implemented an XAI intervention framework including fairness prompting and explainability-based interventions.
AI.result: Phi3.5-Vision achieved 80.4% accuracy on E-DAIC vs Qwen2-VL at 33.9%. Both models over-predicted depression on AFAR-BSFT. Qwen2-VL showed higher gender disparities, while Phi-3.5-Vision exhibited more racial bias. Fairness prompting achieved perfect equal opportunity for Qwen2-VL but at severe accuracy cost; explainability interventions improved procedural consistency but sometimes amplified racial bias.
AI.conclusion: There is a persistent gap between procedural transparency and equitable outcomes. Future fairness interventions must jointly optimize predictive accuracy, demographic parity, and cross-domain generalization.

title: Urban Flood Observations (UFO): A hand-labeled training and validation dataset of post-flood inundation
summary: Urban flooding affects lives and infrastructure worldwide. Mapping inundation in complex urban environments from satellite imagery remains challenging due to limited spatial resolution, infrequent acquisitions, and cloud cover. We present Urban Flood Observations (UFO), a global, hand-labeled dataset of post-flood inundation in diverse urban settings. UFO comprises 215 image chips (1024 by 1024 pixels) from 14 flood events between 2017 and 2021, derived from 3 m PlanetScope imagery. Each chip is annotated with two classes: 'inundated' (all visible surface water, including floodwater and pre-existing water bodies (permanent or seasonal)) and 'non-inundated'. To demonstrate the dataset's utility, we trained a segmentation model using leave-one-event-out cross-validation, achieving a mean Intersection over Union (IoU) of 77.3. We also used UFO to evaluate two widely used surface water products, the Sentinel-1-based NASA IMPACT model and Google's 10 m Dynamic World water class, which yielded IoUs of 44.1 and 48.1, respectively. UFO is publicly available to support the development and validation of urban inundation mapping methods.

AI.tldr: UFO is a global hand-labeled dataset of urban flood inundation from 3m PlanetScope imagery (215 chips from 14 events, 2017-2021), achieving 77.3 IoU with a segmentation model and outperforming existing water products (44.1-48.1 IoU).
AI.motivation: Urban flooding causes severe damage, but mapping inundation from satellite imagery is hindered by limited spatial resolution, infrequent acquisitions, and cloud cover. Existing water products are insufficient for complex urban environments.
AI.method: Created UFO dataset: 215 image chips (1024x1024) from 14 flood events (2017-2021) using 3m PlanetScope imagery, hand-labeled as 'inundated' or 'non-inundated'. Trained a segmentation model with leave-one-event-out cross-validation and evaluated against NASA IMPACT (Sentinel-1) and Google's Dynamic World products.
AI.result: Segmentation model achieved mean IoU of 77.3. NASA IMPACT and Google Dynamic World achieved IoUs of 44.1 and 48.1 respectively, significantly lower than the UFO-trained model.
AI.conclusion: UFO provides a valuable public benchmark for urban flood mapping, demonstrating that dedicated training data significantly outperforms general-purpose water products in complex urban settings.

title: Process Supervision of Confidence Margin for Calibrated LLM Reasoning
summary: Scaling test-time computation with reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a reliable path to improve large language models (LLM) reasoning ability. Yet, outcome-based reward often incentivizes models to be overconfident, leading to hallucinations, unreliable confidence-based control, and unnecessary compute allocation. We introduce Reinforcement Learning with Confidence Margin (\textbf{RLCM}), a calibration-aware RL framework that jointly optimizes correctness and confidence reliability via a margin-enhanced process reward over intermediate-budget completions. Rather than aligning confidence to correctness likelihoods, RLCM encourages to widen the confidence margin between correct and incorrect steps within a single reasoning trajectory. Across mathematical, code, logic and science benchmarks, our method substantially improves calibration while maintaining or improving accuracy. We further show that, with calibrated confidence signals, the resulting models enable more efficient conformal risk control and effective confidence-weighted aggregation.

AI.tldr: RLCM improves LLM reasoning by jointly optimizing correctness and confidence calibration through a margin-enhanced process reward, reducing overconfidence and enabling better test-time compute control.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based RL rewards incentivize LLMs to be overconfident, causing hallucinations, unreliable confidence estimation, and inefficient compute allocation. Existing methods fail to produce well-calibrated confidence signals.
AI.method: RLCM uses a calibration-aware RL framework with a margin-enhanced process reward over intermediate-budget completions, widening the confidence margin between correct and incorrect reasoning steps within a single trajectory.
