AI.result: Across math, code, logic, and science benchmarks, RLCM improves calibration while maintaining or improving accuracy. It also enables more efficient conformal risk control and confidence-weighted aggregation.
AI.conclusion: Calibrated confidence from RLCM enables more reliable and efficient test-time compute allocation without sacrificing reasoning performance.

title: Domain-Filtered Knowledge Graphs from Sparse Autoencoder Features
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) extract millions of interpretable features from a language model, but flat feature inventories aren't very useful on their own. Domain concepts get mixed with generic and weakly grounded features, while related ideas are scattered across many units, and there's no way to understand relationships between features. We address this by first constructing a strict domain-specific concept universe from a large SAE inventory using contrastive activations and a multi-stage filtering process. Next, we build two aligned graph views on the filtered set: a co-occurrence graph for corpus-level conceptual structure, organized at multiple levels of granularity, and a transcoder-based mechanism graph that links source-layer and target-layer features through sparse latent pathways. Automated edge labeling then turns these graph views into readable knowledge graphs rather than unlabeled layouts. In a case study on a biology textbook, these graphs recover coherent chapter and subchapter-level structure, reveal concepts that bridge neighboring topics, and transform messy sentence-level activity containing thousands of features into compact, readable views that illustrate the model's local activity. Taken together, this reframes a flat SAE inventory as an internal knowledge graph that converts feature-level interpretability into a global map of model knowledge and enables audits of reasoning faithfulness.

AI.tldr: This paper transforms sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature inventories into structured knowledge graphs by filtering domain-specific concepts, building co-occurrence and mechanism graphs, and adding automated edge labels, enabling global interpretability of model knowledge.
AI.motivation: Flat SAE feature inventories mix domain concepts with generic/weakly grounded features and scatter related ideas across many units, lacking relational structure. The authors aim to organize these features into readable, hierarchical knowledge graphs that reveal conceptual relationships and model reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Construct a domain-specific concept universe from a large SAE inventory using contrastive activations and multi-stage filtering. 2) Build two aligned graph views: a co-occurrence graph (corpus-level conceptual structure at multiple granularities) and a transcoder-based mechanism graph (linking source-layer to target-layer features via sparse pathways). 3) Apply automated edge labeling to convert unlabeled layouts into readable knowledge graphs.
AI.result: In a biology textbook case study, the graphs recover coherent chapter/subchapter-level structure, reveal bridging concepts between topics, and transform messy sentence-level feature activity into compact, readable views of local model activity.
AI.conclusion: Reframing flat SAE inventories as internal knowledge graphs converts feature-level interpretability into a global map of model knowledge and enables audits of reasoning faithfulness.

title: From Pixels to Explanations: Interpretable Diabetic Retinopathy Grading with CNN-Transformer Ensembles, Visual Explainability and Vision-Language Models
summary: The quality of diabetic retinopathy (DR) screening relies on the ability to correctly grade severity; however, many deep-learning (DL) classifiers cannot be easily interpreted in the clinical context. This study presents a methodology that combines strong discriminative models with multimodal explanations, converting retinal pixels into clinically interpretable outputs. Using the APTOS 2019 benchmark, we evaluated six representative CNN- and transformer-based backbones under a controlled protocol with stratified five-fold cross-validation. We then compared ensembling strategies (hard voting, weighted soft voting, stacking) and investigated a hybrid class-level fusion variant to exploit grade-specific advantages. For interpretability, we produced Grad-CAM++ visual attribution maps and short textual rationales using vision-language models (VLMs) conditioned on the fundus image and classifier outputs under conservative prompting constraints. Modern CNN backbones (ResNet-50 and ConvNeXt-Tiny) provided the strongest single-model baselines, with cross-validated QWK up to 0.919 and 0.914, respectively. Ensembling improved ordinal agreement, and weighted soft voting was the most consistent across folds (QWK 0.934 +/- 0.017). Hybrid class-level fusion was competitive but did not yield a statistically reliable improvement over standard fusion in paired fold comparisons (Holm-adjusted p >= 1.000). For explanation quality, Grad-CAM++ offered plausible but coarse localization, and VLM rationales were generally grade-consistent. Quantitatively, VLM variants showed a trade-off between clinical completeness and template-level semantic similarity (coverage 0.700 vs. BERTScore 0.072), while image-text alignment was comparable (CLIPScore approximately 0.34).

AI.tldr: This paper presents a methodology for interpretable diabetic retinopathy grading using deep learning, combining CNN/transformer backbones with ensembling strategies and multimodal explanations (Grad-CAM++ visual maps and VLM-generated textual rationales), achieving high QWK scores (up to 0.934) but revealing trade-offs in explanation quality.
AI.motivation: DR screening quality depends on correct severity grading, but deep-learning classifiers are often hard to interpret in clinical contexts. The authors aim to bridge this gap by combining strong discriminative models with clinically interpretable multimodal outputs.
AI.method: Evaluated 6 CNN- and transformer-based backbones on APTOS 2019 with stratified 5-fold CV; compared ensembling strategies (hard voting, weighted soft voting, stacking) and a hybrid class-level fusion variant; generated Grad-CAM++ visual attributions and VLM-based textual rationales under conservative prompting.
AI.result: ResNet-50 (QWK 0.919) and ConvNeXt-Tiny (0.914) were best single models; weighted soft voting achieved best ensemble performance (QWK 0.934±0.017); hybrid fusion showed no statistically reliable improvement; Grad-CAM++ provided plausible but coarse localization; VLM rationales showed trade-off between clinical completeness (coverage 0.700) and semantic similarity (BERTScore 0.072).
AI.conclusion: Ensembling improves ordinal agreement for DR grading, but explanation quality remains a challenge with trade-offs between localization precision, clinical completeness, and semantic fidelity, suggesting further work is needed for truly clinically interpretable AI.

title: TEMPO: Transformers for Temporal Disease Progression from Cross-Sectional Data
comment: 31 pages; Published at Conference on Health, Inference, and Learning (CHIL) 2026
summary: Event-Based Models (EBMs) infer biomarker progression from cross-sectional data but typically only as ordinal sequences and rely on rigid model assumptions. We propose \textsc{Tempo}, a Transformer architecture that learns both ordinal and continuous event sequences through simulation-based supervised learning. \textsc{Tempo} uses two Transformer modules: one treats biomarkers as tokens to infer event sequencing; the other treats patients as tokens, representing each by their per-biomarker abnormality profile, to infer patients' disease stages. On synthetic benchmarks, \textsc{Tempo} reduces normalized Kendall's Tau distance by 52.89\% and staging MAE by 25.33\% compared to state-of-the-art SA-EBM, with larger reductions in high-dimensional settings (58.88\% and 61.10\%). Applied to ADNI, \textsc{Tempo} recovers a biologically plausible Alzheimer's progression: early medial temporal atrophy, followed by amyloid accumulation and cognitive decline, and late-stage tau pathology with terminal acceleration of global neurodegeneration -- broadly consistent with established disease models. \textsc{Tempo} also eliminates the need to derive custom inference algorithms and enables rapid empirical comparison of generative hypotheses.

AI.tldr: Tempo is a Transformer-based model that learns both ordinal and continuous biomarker progression sequences from cross-sectional data via simulation-based learning, outperforming existing methods and recovering biologically plausible Alzheimer's progression patterns.
AI.motivation: Event-Based Models (EBMs) can only infer ordinal sequences and rely on rigid model assumptions; the authors aim to overcome these limitations by learning both ordinal and continuous event sequences.
AI.method: Tempo uses two Transformer modules: one treating biomarkers as tokens for event sequencing, another treating patients as tokens with per-biomarker abnormality profiles for disease staging, trained via simulation-based supervised learning.
AI.result: On synthetic benchmarks, Tempo reduces normalized Kendall's Tau distance by 52.89% and staging MAE by 25.33% over SA-EBM, with larger gains in high-dimensional settings (58.88% and 61.10%). Applied to ADNI, it recovers a biologically plausible Alzheimer's progression.
AI.conclusion: Tempo eliminates the need for custom inference algorithms, enables rapid comparison of generative hypotheses, and achieves state-of-the-art performance in both ordinal and continuous biomarker progression modeling.

title: ClawTrace: Cost-Aware Tracing for LLM Agent Skill Distillation
summary: Skill-distillation pipelines learn reusable rules from LLM agent trajectories, but they lack a key signal: how much each step costs. Without per-step cost, a pipeline cannot distinguish adding a missing step to fix a bug from removing an expensive step that never affected the outcome. We introduce ClawTrace, an agent tracing platform that records every LLM call, tool use, and sub-agent spawn during an agent session and compiles each session into a TraceCard: a compact YAML summary with per-step USD cost, token counts, and redundancy flags. Built on ClawTrace, CostCraft is a distillation pipeline that reads TraceCards and produces three types of skill patches. Preserve patches keep behaviors that led to success. Prune patches remove expensive steps that did not matter, each backed by a counterfactual argument against a named high-cost step. Repair patches fix failures grounded in oracle evidence. Ablations on 30 held-out SpreadsheetBench tasks show that both cost attribution and prune patches independently reduce quality regressions. When the same skill is applied to 30 unrelated SkillsBench tasks, an unexpected asymmetry emerges: prune rules transferred across benchmarks and cut median cost by 32%, while preserve rules, trained on benchmark-specific conventions, caused regressions on new task types. We release ClawTrace and TraceCards as open infrastructure for cost-aware agent research.

AI.tldr: ClawTrace is a platform that records per-step costs of LLM agent sessions into TraceCards, and CostCraft uses these to produce three types of skill patches (preserve, prune, repair). Prune patches reduce costs by 32% and transfer across benchmarks, while preserve patches cause regressions on new tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing skill-distillation pipelines lack per-step cost signals, making them unable to distinguish between adding a missing step (fix) and removing an expensive but unnecessary step (optimization).
AI.method: ClawTrace records every LLM call, tool use, and sub-agent spawn with per-step USD cost, token counts, and redundancy flags into TraceCards. CostCraft reads these and produces three skill patch types: Preserve (keep successful behaviors), Prune (remove expensive unnecessary steps with counterfactual justification), and Repair (fix failures with oracle evidence).
AI.result: On 30 SpreadsheetBench tasks, cost attribution and prune patches independently reduce quality regressions. On 30 SkillsBench tasks, prune rules transfer across benchmarks cutting median cost by 32%, while preserve rules cause regressions on new task types.
AI.conclusion: Cost-aware distillation improves efficiency and transferability; ClawTrace and TraceCards are released as open infrastructure for cost-aware agent research.

title: Toward Real-World Adoption of Portrait Relighting via Hybrid Domain Knowledge Fusion
summary: The real-world adoption of portrait relighting is hindered by dataset domain gaps, camera sensitivity, and computational costs. We address these challenges with Hybrid Domain Knowledge Fusion, a paradigm that fuses the specialized strengths of synthetic, One-Light-at-A-Time (OLAT), and real-world datasets into a compact model. Our approach features specialized prior models hardened by domain-aware adaptation, followed by augmented knowledge distillation into a lightweight student model with multi-domain expertise. Our method demonstrates a 6x to 240x inference speedup while maintaining state-of-the-art (SOTA) visual quality in the experiments. Additionally, we construct a massive, high-fidelity synthetic dataset with diverse ground-truth intrinsics to support our training pipeline.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Hybrid Domain Knowledge Fusion, a method that combines synthetic, OLAT, and real-world datasets for portrait relighting, achieving 6-240x speedup with SOTA quality through domain-aware adaptation and knowledge distillation into a lightweight model.
AI.motivation: Portrait relighting faces challenges from dataset domain gaps, camera sensitivity differences, and high computational costs that hinder real-world adoption.
AI.method: Hybrid Domain Knowledge Fusion: (1) Specialized prior models hardened by domain-aware adaptation across synthetic, OLAT, and real-world datasets, (2) Augmented knowledge distillation into a lightweight student model, (3) Construction of a massive high-fidelity synthetic dataset with diverse ground-truth intrinsics.
AI.result: Achieves 6x to 240x inference speedup while maintaining state-of-the-art visual quality.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid domain fusion paradigm effectively overcomes domain gaps and computational bottlenecks, enabling practical real-world portrait relighting with a compact model.

title: When Context Sticks: Studying Interference in In-Context Learning
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables. Code available at: https://github.com/nilsvselte/icl-context-stickiness
summary: This paper investigates context stickiness in in-context learning (ICL), a phenomenon where earlier examples in a prompt interfere with a transformer's ability to adapt to later tasks. Using synthetic regression tasks over linear and quadratic functions, we examine how models trained under sequential, mixed, and random curricula handle abrupt task switches during inference. By sweeping over structured combinations of misleading linear examples followed by recovery quadratic examples, we quantify how prior context biases prediction error and how quickly models realign. Our results show strong evidence of persistent interference: more preceding linear examples reliably degrade quadratic predictions, while additional quadratic examples reduce error but with diminishing returns. We further find that training curricula significantly modulate resilience, with sequential training on the target function class yielding the fastest recovery, and surprisingly, random training producing the least robust behavior.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how earlier examples in a prompt (context stickiness) interfere with transformer ICL performance on task switches, finding that more misleading examples degrade predictions, recovery is nonlinear, and sequential training curricula yield the fastest adaptation.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify how prior context biases transformer ICL abilities when tasks shift, and how training curricula modulate this interference.
AI.method: Synthetic regression tasks over linear and quadratic functions with structured prompts containing misleading linear examples followed by recovery quadratic examples, comparing models trained under sequential, mixed, and random curricula.
AI.result: More preceding linear examples reliably degrade quadratic predictions; additional quadratic examples reduce error with diminishing returns; sequential training yields fastest recovery; random training produces least robust behavior.
AI.conclusion: Context stickiness is a persistent issue in ICL, and training curriculum design significantly affects model robustness to task switches.

title: Does Machine Unlearning Preserve Clinical Safety? A Risk Analysis for Medical Image Classification
summary: The application of Deep Learning in medical diagnosis must balance patient safety with compliance with data protection regulations. Machine Unlearning enables the selective removal of training data from deployed models. However, most methods are validated primarily through efficiency and privacy-oriented metrics, with limited attention to clinically asymmetric error costs. In this work, we investigate how unlearning affects clinical risk in binary medical image classification. We show that standard unlearning strategies (Fine-Tuning, Random Labeling, and SalUn) may reduce test utility while increasing false-negative rates, thereby amplifying clinical risk. To mitigate this, we propose SalUn-CRA (Clinical Risk-Aware), a variant of SalUn that replaces random relabeling with entropy-based forgetting for malignant samples in the forget set, preventing the model from learning harmful benign associations. We evaluate on DermaMNIST and PathMNIST medical image datasets under 20% and 50% data removal. Using Global Risk metrics with asymmetric costs, SalUn-CRA achieves lower or comparable clinical risk to full retraining while preserving unlearning effectiveness. These results suggest that clinical risk should be an integral component of unlearning validation in medical systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes SalUn-CRA, a clinical risk-aware machine unlearning method for medical image classification that reduces false-negative rates and achieves comparable clinical risk to full retraining, demonstrating that clinical risk should be a key validation metric in medical unlearning.
AI.motivation: Standard machine unlearning methods prioritize efficiency and privacy but ignore clinically asymmetric error costs (e.g., false negatives are more harmful than false positives in medical diagnosis), potentially amplifying clinical risk.
AI.method: SalUn-CRA modifies the SalUn unlearning method by replacing random relabeling with entropy-based forgetting specifically for malignant samples in the forget set, preventing harmful benign associations from being learned.
AI.result: On DermaMNIST and PathMNIST datasets under 20% and 50% data removal, SalUn-CRA achieves lower or comparable clinical risk (using Global Risk metrics with asymmetric costs) compared to full retraining, while maintaining unlearning effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Clinical risk should be an integral component of unlearning validation in medical systems, as standard unlearning methods may reduce utility while increasing false-negative rates and amplifying clinical risk.

title: Keypoint-based Dynamic Object 6-DoF Pose Tracking via Event Camera
comment: Accepted to 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Accurate 6-DoF pose estimation of objects is critical for robots to perform precise manipulation tasks. However, for dynamic object pose estimation, conventional camera-based approaches face several major challenges, such as motion blur, sensor noise, and low-light limitation. To address these issues, we employ event cameras, whose high dynamic range and low latency offer a promising solution. Furthermore, we propose a keypoint-based detection and tracking approach for dynamic object pose estimation. Firstly, a keypoint detection network is constructed to extract keypoints from the time surface generated by the event stream. Subsequently, the polarity and spatial coordinates of the events are leveraged, and the event density in the vicinity of each keypoint is utilized to achieve continuous keypoint tracking. Finally, a hash mapping is established between the 2D keypoints and the 3D model keypoints, and the EPnP algorithm is employed to estimate the 6-DoF pose. Experimental results demonstrate that, whether in simulated or real event environments, the proposed method outperforms the event-based state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and robustness.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a keypoint-based detection and tracking method using event cameras for accurate 6-DoF pose estimation of dynamic objects, outperforming existing event-based methods in accuracy and robustness.
AI.motivation: Conventional camera-based approaches struggle with dynamic object pose estimation due to motion blur, sensor noise, and low-light limitations. Event cameras offer high dynamic range and low latency, motivating their use.
AI.method: A keypoint detection network extracts keypoints from the time surface of event streams. Polarity and spatial coordinates of events, plus event density near each keypoint, enable continuous tracking. 2D-3D keypoint correspondences via hash mapping feed into EPnP for 6-DoF pose estimation.
AI.result: In both simulated and real event environments, the proposed method achieves superior accuracy and robustness compared to event-based state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: Event cameras combined with the proposed keypoint-based tracking approach effectively address dynamic object pose estimation challenges, outperforming existing methods.

title: INSIGHT: Indoor Scene Intelligence from Geometric-Semantic Hierarchy Transfer for Public~Safety
summary: Indoor environments lack the spatial intelligence infrastructure that GPS provides outdoors; first responders arriving at unfamiliar buildings typically have no machine-readable map of safety equipment. Prior work on 3D semantic segmentation for public safety identified two barriers: scarcity of labeled indoor training data and poor recognition of small safety-critical features by native point-cloud methods. This paper presents INSIGHT, a zero-target-domain-annotation pipeline that projects 2D image understanding into 3D metric space via registered RGB-D data. Two interchangeable vision stacks share a common 3D back end: a SAM3 foundation-model stack for text-prompted segmentation, and a traditional CV stack (open-set detection, VQA, OCR) whose intermediate outputs are independently inspectable. Evaluated on all seven subareas of Stanford 2D-3D-S (70{,}496 images), the pipeline produces Pointcept-schema-compatible labeled point clouds and ISO~19164-compliant scene graphs with ${\sim}10^{4}{\times}$ compression; role-filtered payloads transmit in ${<}15$\,s at 1\,Mbps over FirstNet Band~14. We report per-point labeling accuracy on 7 shared classes, detection sensitivity for 15 safety-critical classes absent from public 3D benchmarks alongside code-capped deployable estimates, and inter-pipeline complementarity, demonstrating that 2D-to-3D semantic transfer addresses the labeled-data bottleneck while scene graphs provide building intelligence compact enough for field deployment.

AI.tldr: INSIGHT is a zero-annotation pipeline that transfers 2D image understanding to 3D point clouds for indoor spatial intelligence, using SAM3 and traditional CV stacks, producing compact scene graphs for first responders.
AI.motivation: Indoor environments lack GPS-like spatial intelligence; first responders have no machine-readable maps of safety equipment. Prior work faced barriers of scarce labeled indoor training data and poor recognition of small safety-critical features by native point-cloud methods.
AI.method: A zero-target-domain-annotation pipeline that projects 2D image understanding into 3D metric space via registered RGB-D data, using two interchangeable vision stacks (SAM3 foundation model and traditional CV stack) with a shared 3D back end, producing Pointcept-schema-compatible point clouds and ISO 19164-compliant scene graphs.
AI.result: Evaluated on Stanford 2D-3D-S (70,496 images), achieves ~10^4× compression via scene graphs; role-filtered payloads transmit in <15s at 1Mbps over FirstNet Band 14. Reports per-point labeling accuracy on 7 shared classes, detection sensitivity for 15 safety-critical classes absent from public 3D benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: 2D-to-3D semantic transfer addresses the labeled-data bottleneck; scene graphs provide building intelligence compact enough for field deployment.

title: V-GRPO: Online Reinforcement Learning for Denoising Generative Models Is Easier than You Think
summary: Aligning denoising generative models with human preferences or verifiable rewards remains a key challenge. While policy-gradient online reinforcement learning (RL) offers a principled post-training framework, its direct application is hindered by the intractable likelihoods of these models. Prior work therefore either optimizes an induced Markov decision process (MDP) over sampling trajectories, which is stable but inefficient, or uses likelihood surrogates based on the diffusion evidence lower bound (ELBO), which have so far underperformed on visual generation. Our key insight is that the ELBO-based approach can, in fact, be made both stable and efficient. By reducing surrogate variance and controlling gradient steps, we show that this approach can beat MDP-based methods. To this end, we introduce Variational GRPO (V-GRPO), a method that integrates ELBO-based surrogates with the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm, alongside a set of simple yet essential techniques. Our method is easy to implement, aligns with pretraining objectives, and avoids the limitations of MDP-based methods. V-GRPO achieves state-of-the-art performance in text-to-image synthesis, while delivering a $2\times$ speedup over MixGRPO and a $3\times$ speedup over DiffusionNFT.

AI.tldr: V-GRPO integrates ELBO-based surrogate objectives with GRPO algorithm to achieve efficient and stable alignment of diffusion models with rewards, outperforming MDP-based methods in text-to-image synthesis with 2-3x speedup.
AI.motivation: Aligning denoising generative models with human preferences/rewards is challenging because policy-gradient RL is hindered by intractable likelihoods. Existing MDP-based methods are stable but inefficient, while ELBO-based surrogates have underperformed on visual generation.
AI.method: V-GRPO combines ELBO-based surrogate objectives with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), reducing surrogate variance and controlling gradient steps to make the ELBO-based approach both stable and efficient.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance in text-to-image synthesis; 2x speedup over MixGRPO and 3x speedup over DiffusionNFT.
AI.conclusion: ELBO-based approaches can be made stable and efficient, outperforming MDP-based methods. V-GRPO is easy to implement, aligns with pretraining objectives, and avoids limitations of MDP-based methods.

title: Time-Series Forecasting in Safety-Critical Environments: An EU-AI-Act-Compliant Open-Source Package / Zeitreihenprognose in sicherheitskritischen Umgebungen: Ein KI-VO-konformes Open-Source-Paket
comment: Bilingual twin paper: English version first, German original below (91 pages total). Single shared bibliography
summary: With spotforecast2-safe we present an integrated Compliance-by-Design approach to Python-based point forecasting of time series in safety-critical environments. A review of the relevant open-source tooling shows that existing compliance solutions operate consistently outside of the library to be used - e.g. as scanners, templates, or runtime layers. spotforecast2-safe takes the inverse approach and anchors the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (the EU AI Act, in German: KI-VO), of IEC 61508, of the ISA/IEC 62443 standards series, and of the Cyber Resilience Act within the library: in application-programming-interface contracts, persistence formats, and continuous-integration gates. The approach is operationalised by four non-negotiable code-development rules (zero dead code, deterministic processing, fail-safe handling, minimal dependencies) together with the corresponding process rules (model card, executable docstrings, CI workflows, Common-Platform-Enumeration (CPE) identifier, REUSE-conformant licensing, release pipeline). Interactive visualisation, hyperparameter tuning and automated machine learning (AutoML), as well as deep-learning and large-language-model backends are deliberately excluded, because each of these components either enlarges the attack surface, introduces non-determinism, or impairs reproducibility. A bidirectional traceability matrix maps every regulatory provision onto the corresponding mechanism in the code; an end-to-end example of European-market electricity generation, transmission, and consumption forecasting demonstrates the application. The package is open-source and available under Affero General Public License (AGPL) 3.0-or-later.

AI.tldr: spotforecast2-safe presents a Compliance-by-Design approach for time-series forecasting in safety-critical environments, embedding regulatory requirements (EU AI Act, IEC 61508, ISA/IEC 62443, Cyber Resilience Act) directly into library contracts, persistence formats, and CI gates via four non-negotiable code rules and corresponding process rules, while excluding non-deterministic components like deep learning and LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing compliance tools operate externally (scanners, templates, runtime layers) rather than being embedded within the library itself, leaving gaps for safety-critical forecasting applications.
AI.method: Four non-negotiable code-development rules (zero dead code, deterministic processing, fail-safe handling, minimal dependencies) plus process rules (model cards, executable docstrings, CI workflows, CPE identifiers, REUSE licensing, release pipeline), with a bidirectional traceability matrix linking regulations to code mechanisms.
AI.result: An open-source Python package (AGPL 3.0) demonstrated via European electricity market forecasting, excluding deep learning/AutoML/LLMs to avoid attack surface enlargement and non-determinism.
AI.conclusion: Embedding regulatory compliance directly into library design (Compliance-by-Design) is a viable alternative to external compliance tooling for safety-critical time-series forecasting.

title: Learning to Identify Out-of-Distribution Objects for 3D LiDAR Anomaly Segmentation
comment: This paper has been accepted at the 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR)
summary: Understanding the surrounding environment is fundamental in autonomous driving and robotic perception. Distinguishing between known classes and previously unseen objects is crucial in real-world environments, as done in Anomaly Segmentation. However, research in the 3D field remains limited, with most existing approaches applying post-processing techniques from 2D vision. To cover this lack, we propose a new efficient approach that directly operates in the feature space, modeling the feature distribution of inlier classes to constrain anomalous samples. Moreover, the only publicly available 3D LiDAR anomaly segmentation dataset contains simple scenarios, with few anomaly instances, and exhibits a severe domain gap due to its sensor resolution. To bridge this gap, we introduce a set of mixed real-synthetic datasets for 3D LiDAR anomaly segmentation, built upon established semantic segmentation benchmarks, with multiple out-of-distribution objects and diverse, complex environments. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art and competitive results on the existing real-world dataset and the newly introduced mixed datasets, respectively, validating the effectiveness of our method and the utility of the proposed datasets. Code and datasets are available at https://simom0.github.io/lido-page/.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a feature-space method for 3D LiDAR anomaly segmentation and introduces new mixed real-synthetic datasets to address limitations in existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing 3D anomaly segmentation research is limited, relying on 2D post-processing techniques; available datasets are simple, contain few anomalies, and suffer from domain gaps due to sensor resolution differences.
AI.method: An efficient approach operating directly in feature space that models the feature distribution of inlier classes to constrain anomalous samples, plus the introduction of mixed real-synthetic datasets built on established semantic segmentation benchmarks.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on existing real-world datasets and competitive results on the newly introduced mixed datasets.
AI.conclusion: Validates effectiveness of the proposed method and utility of the new datasets; code and datasets are publicly available.

title: Transferable Physical-World Adversarial Patches Against Object Detection in Autonomous Driving
summary: Deep learning drives major advances in autonomous driving (AD), where object detectors are central to perception. However, adversarial attacks pose significant threats to the reliability and safety of these systems, with physical adversarial patches representing a particularly potent form of attack. Physical adversarial patch attacks pose severe risks but are usually crafted for a single model, yielding poor transferability to unseen detectors. We propose AdvAD, a transfer-based physical attack against object detection in autonomous driving. Instead of targeting a specific detector, AdvAD optimizes adversarial patches over multiple detection models in a unified framework, encouraging the learned perturbations to capture shared vulnerabilities across architectures. The optimization process adaptively balances model contributions and enforces robustness to physical variations. It further employs data augmentation and geometric transformations to maintain patch effectiveness under diverse physical conditions. Experiments in both digital and real-world settings show that AdvAD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) attacks in performance and transferability.

AI.tldr: AdvAD is a transfer-based physical adversarial patch attack that optimizes perturbations across multiple detection models to improve attack transferability against object detectors in autonomous driving.
AI.motivation: Physical adversarial patches pose severe risks to autonomous driving systems, but existing attacks are crafted for a single model and have poor transferability to unseen detectors.
AI.method: AdvAD optimizes adversarial patches over multiple detection models in a unified framework, adaptively balancing model contributions, enforcing robustness to physical variations, and using data augmentation and geometric transformations.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms SOTA attacks in both performance and transferability across digital and real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: AdvAD demonstrates that multi-model optimization enhances transferability of physical adversarial patches, making attacks more practical and dangerous for real-world autonomous driving.

title: Domain-Adapted Fine-Tuning of ECG Foundation Models for Multi-Label Structural Heart Disease Screening
summary: Transthoracic echocardiography is the reference standard for confirming structural heart disease (SHD), but first-line screening is limited by cost, workflow burden, and specialist availability. We evaluated whether open pretrained electrocardiogram (ECG) foundation models can support echo-confirmed multi-label SHD detection using the public EchoNext Mini-Model benchmark. Six echocardiography-derived abnormalities were targeted: reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, increased left ventricular wall thickness, aortic stenosis, mitral regurgitation, tricuspid regurgitation, and right ventricular systolic dysfunction. Under a common pipeline, we compared engineered ECG features with gradient boosting, end-to-end waveform learning from scratch, and transfer from open ECG foundation models. We then applied in-domain self-supervised adaptation of an ECG foundation model (ECG-FM) on EchoNext waveforms followed by selective supervised fine-tuning, and evaluated trade-offs between discrimination and adaptation cost. Adapted ECG-FM models achieved the best overall performance: peak macro-AUROC 0.8509 and macro-AUPRC 0.4297, while a parameter-efficient operating point preserved AUROC (0.8501) and attained the highest fixed-threshold macro-F1 0.3691. Late fusion with covariates did not improve threshold-independent discrimination, and evaluated LoRA, alternative backbones, and mixture-of-foundations strategies did not surpass the best adapted single-backbone models. These results indicate that for ECG-based case finding and echocardiography triage, combining target-domain self-supervised adaptation with selective supervised updating of a pretrained ECG backbone is the most effective transfer strategy.

AI.tldr: This study evaluates open ECG foundation models for detecting structural heart disease using a public benchmark. Adapted ECG foundation models with self-supervised learning outperform other methods, achieving the best discrimination and efficiency trade-offs for echocardiography triage.
AI.motivation: Standard screening for structural heart disease via echocardiography is limited by cost, workflow burden, and specialist availability. The authors aim to evaluate whether open pretrained ECG foundation models can serve as an effective first-line screening tool.
AI.method: The study uses the EchoNext Mini-Model benchmark targeting six echo-derived abnormalities. They compare engineered features with gradient boosting, end-to-end waveform learning, and transfer from open ECG foundation models. The best approach combines in-domain self-supervised adaptation of an ECG foundation model (ECG-FM) followed by selective supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: Adapted ECG-FM models achieved peak macro-AUROC 0.8509 and macro-AUPRC 0.4297. A parameter-efficient version preserved AUROC (0.8501) and achieved the highest macro-F1 of 0.3691. Late fusion, LoRA, alternative backbones, and mixture-of-foundations strategies did not surpass the best single-backbone models.
AI.conclusion: For ECG-based case finding and echocardiography triage, combining target-domain self-supervised adaptation with selective supervised updating of a pretrained ECG backbone is the most effective transfer strategy.

title: ZenBrain: A Neuroscience-Inspired 7-Layer Memory Architecture for Autonomous AI Systems
comment: Pre-print of NeurIPS 2026 main-track submission. Earliest preprint version on Zenodo 31 March 2026 (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19353664); cross-posted to TDCommons (dpubs_series/9683, 1 April 2026). Six Zenodo revisions and three TDCommons revisions through 9 April 2026 (Zenodo concept DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19353663). 41 pages, 22 tables, 2 figures
summary:
Despite a century of empirical memory research, existing AI agent memory systems rely on system-engineering metaphors (virtual-memory paging, flat LLM storage, Zettelkasten notes), none integrating principles of consolidation, forgetting, and reconsolidation.
  We present ZenBrain, a multi-layer memory architecture integrating fifteen neuroscience models. It implements seven memory layers (working, short-term, episodic, semantic, procedural, core, cross-context) orchestrated by nine foundational algorithms (Two-Factor Synaptic Model, vmPFC-coupled FSRS, Simulation-Selection sleep, Bayesian confidence, and five more) plus six new Predictive Memory Architecture (PMA) components: a four-channel NeuromodulatorEngine, prediction-error-gated ReconsolidationEngine, TripleCopyMemory with divergent decay, four-dimensional PriorityMap with amygdala fast-path, StabilityProtector (NogoA/HDAC3 analogue), and MetacognitiveMonitor for bias detection.
  The 15-algorithm ablation reveals a cooperative survival network: under stress, 9 of 15 algorithms become individually critical (delta-Q up to -93.7%, Wilcoxon, 10 seeds, alpha=0.005). Simulation-Selection sleep achieves 37% stability improvement (p<0.005) with 47.4% storage reduction. TripleCopyMemory retains S(t)=0.912 at 30 days; PriorityMap reaches NDCG@10=0.997.
  Multi-layer routing beats a flat single-layer baseline by 20.7% F1 on LoCoMo (p<0.005) and 19.5% on MemoryArena (p=0.015). On LongMemEval-500, ZenBrain holds the highest mean rank on all 12 system-judge cells (4 systems x 3 LLM judges), three-judge mean J=0.545 vs letta=0.485, a-mem=0.414, mem0=0.394; all 9 pair-wise contrasts clear Bonferroni (alpha=0.05/18, min p=6.2e-31, d in [0.18, 0.52]). Under LongMemEval's binary judge, ZenBrain reaches 91.3% of oracle accuracy at 1/106th the per-query token budget. Open-source with 11,589 automated test cases.

AI.tldr: ZenBrain is a multi-layer AI memory architecture integrating 15 neuroscience models and 9 algorithms, outperforming baselines (Letta, a-mem, mem0) by 19.5-20.7% F1 on memory benchmarks, achieving 91.3% of oracle accuracy at 1/106th token cost, with open-source release and 11,589 test cases.
AI.motivation: Existing AI agent memory systems rely on system-engineering metaphors (virtual-memory paging, flat LLM storage, Zettelkasten notes) and none integrate neuroscience principles of consolidation, forgetting, and reconsolidation, despite a century of empirical memory research.
AI.method: A 7-layer memory architecture (working, short-term, episodic, semantic, procedural, core, cross-context) orchestrated by 9 foundational algorithms (Two-Factor Synaptic Model, vmPFC-coupled FSRS, Simulation-Selection sleep, Bayesian confidence, etc.) plus 6 new Predictive Memory Architecture components (NeuromodulatorEngine, ReconsolidationEngine, TripleCopyMemory, PriorityMap, StabilityProtector, MetacognitiveMonitor).
AI.result: Multi-layer routing beats flat single-layer by 20.7% F1 on LoCoMo and 19.5% on MemoryArena. On LongMemEval-500, three-judge mean J=0.545 vs Letta=0.485, a-mem=0.414, mem0=0.394; all 9 pairwise contrasts pass Bonferroni. 91.3% of oracle accuracy at 1/106th token budget. TripleCopyMemory retains S(t)=0.912 at 30 days.
AI.conclusion: Neuroscience-inspired memory architectures significantly outperform engineering-based baselines; a cooperative survival network exists among algorithms where 9/15 become individually critical under stress; the approach is open-sourced with comprehensive testing.

title: ARETE: Attention-based Rasterized Encoding for Topology Estimation using HSV-transformed Crowdsourced Vehicle Fleet Data
summary: The continuous advancement of autonomous driving (AD) introduces challenges across multiple disciplines to ensure safe and efficient driving. One such challenge is the generation of High-Definition (HD) maps, which must remain up to date and highly accurate for downstream automotive tasks. One promising approach is the use of crowdsourced data from a vehicle fleet, representing road topology and lane-level features. This work focuses on the generation of centerlines and lane dividers from crowdsourced vehicle trajectories. We adopt a Detection Transformer (DETR)-based approach, where a rasterized representation of vehicle trajectories is used as input to predict vectorized lane representations. Each lane consists of a centerline with an associated direction and corresponding lane dividers that are geometrically constrained by the centerline. Our method includes the extraction of local tiles, from which crowdsourced vehicle trajectories are aggregated. Each tile undergoes a transformation into a rasterized representation encoding both the presence and direction of each trajectory, enabling the prediction of vectorized directed lanes. Experiments are conducted on an internal dataset as well as on the public datasets nuScenes and nuPlan.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a DETR-based approach for generating HD map lane elements (centerlines and lane dividers) from crowdsourced vehicle trajectories, using rasterized trajectory representations and achieving results on internal and public datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of keeping HD maps up-to-date and highly accurate for autonomous driving by leveraging crowdsourced vehicle trajectory data instead of expensive manual mapping.
AI.method: A DETR-based architecture that takes rasterized tiles of aggregated vehicle trajectories (encoding presence and direction) as input to predict vectorized lanes, each consisting of a centerline with direction and geometrically constrained lane dividers.
AI.result: Experiments conducted on an internal dataset, nuScenes, and nuPlan datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: The DETR-based crowdsourced trajectory approach enables scalable and automated HD map lane generation without reliance on expensive sensor suites or manual annotation.

title: Learning from Imperfect Text Guidance: Robust Long-Tail Visual Recognition with High-Noise Label
summary: Real-world data often exhibit long-tailed distributions with numerous noisy labels, substantially degrading the performance of deep models. While prior research has made progress in addressing this combined challenge, it overlooks the severe label-image mismatch inherent to high-noise settings, thereby limiting their effectiveness. Given that observed labels, though mismatched with images, still retain category information, we propose employing auxiliary text information from labels to address label-image inconsistencies in long-tailed noisy data. Specifically, we leverage the intrinsic cross-modal alignment in pre-trained visual-language models to correct the label-image inconsistencies. This supervisory signal, referred to as Weak Teacher Supervision (WTS), is unaffected by label noise and data distribution biases, albeit exhibits limited accuracy. Therefore, the activation of WTS is determined by evaluating the discrepancy between text-predicted labels and observed labels. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance of WTS across synthetic and real-world datasets, particularly under high-noise conditions. The source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/WTS-0F3C.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Weak Teacher Supervision (WTS), which uses pre-trained vision-language models to correct label-image inconsistencies in long-tailed noisy data, achieving superior performance especially under high-noise conditions.
AI.motivation: Real-world long-tailed data with noisy labels degrades deep model performance; prior work overlooks severe label-image mismatch in high-noise settings.
AI.method: Leverages pre-trained vision-language models' cross-modal alignment to correct label-image inconsistencies. WTS is unaffected by label noise/data biases but has limited accuracy, so it's activated only when text-predicted labels and observed labels show significant discrepancy.
AI.result: Superior performance demonstrated on both synthetic and real-world datasets, especially under high-noise conditions.
AI.conclusion: WTS effectively addresses long-tailed noisy label problems by leveraging auxiliary text information from labels via vision-language models, outperforming existing methods in high-noise scenarios.

title: Approximating Uniform Random Rotations by Two-Block Structured Hadamard Rotations in High Dimensions
summary:
Uniform random rotations are a useful primitive in applications such as fast Johnson-Lindenstrauss embeddings, kernel approximation, communication-efficient learning, and recent AI compression pipelines, but they are computationally expensive to generate and apply in high dimensions. A common practical replacement is repeated structured random rotations built from Walsh-Hadamard transforms and random sign diagonals.
  Applying the structured random rotation twice has been shown empirically to be useful, but the supporting theory is still limited. In this paper we study the approximation quality achieved when using this two-block structured Hadamard rotation. Our results are both positive and negative. On the positive side, we prove that every fixed coordinate of the two-block transform converges uniformly, over all inputs, to the corresponding coordinate of a uniformly rotated vector, with an explicit Kolmogorov-distance bound of order $d^{-1/5}$. On the negative side, we prove an explicit lower bound on the Wasserstein distance between the full vector distributions, showing that the two-block transform is not a globally accurate surrogate for a uniform random rotation in the worst case. For the extremal input used in the lower bound, we also prove a matching asymptotic upper bound, showing that the lower-bound scale is sharp for that input.
  Taken together, the results identify a clear separation between one-dimensional marginal behavior, where approximation improves with dimension, and full high-dimensional geometry, where a nonvanishing discrepancy remains. This provides a partial theoretical explanation for the empirical success of structured Hadamard rotations in some algorithms, while also clarifying the limitations of treating them as drop-in replacements for true uniform random rotations.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the two-block structured Hadamard rotation, proving that its one-dimensional marginals converge to a uniform random rotation (Kolmogorov distance ~ d^{-1/5}), but the full high-dimensional distribution has a nonvanishing Wasserstein gap, showing it is not a globally accurate surrogate.
AI.motivation: Structured random rotations (e.g., using Walsh-Hadamard transforms) are widely used in practice as faster alternatives to uniform random rotations for ML applications like Johnson-Lindenstrauss embeddings and kernel approximation, but their theoretical guarantees remain limited, especially for the empirically popular two-block variant.
AI.method: The paper provides theoretical analysis using Kolmogorov distance for marginal convergence and Wasserstein distance for joint distribution discrepancy. It derives explicit bounds: an upper bound of order d^{-1/5} for coordinate-wise convergence and matching lower/upper bounds for the full vector Wasserstein distance.
AI.result: Positive: Every fixed coordinate of the two-block transform converges uniformly to a uniformly rotated coordinate with rate d^{-1/5}. Negative: The full vector distribution has a nonvanishing Wasserstein distance from a true uniform rotation in the worst case, with a sharp asymptotic bound for the extremal input.
AI.conclusion: The two-block structured Hadamard rotation works well for low-dimensional marginal tasks but fails as a global replacement for uniform random rotations in high dimensions, explaining its empirical success in some algorithms while highlighting its limitations.

title: MarketBench: Evaluating AI Agents as Market Participants
summary: Markets are a promising way to coordinate AI agent activity for similar reasons to those used to justify markets more broadly. In order to effectively participate in markets, agents need to have informative signals of their own ability to successfully complete a task and the cost of doing so. We propose MarketBench, a benchmark for assessing whether AI agents have these capabilities. We use a 93-task subset of SWE-bench Lite, a software engineering benchmark, with six recently released LLMs as a demonstration. These LLMs are miscalibrated on both success probability and token usage, and auctions built from these self-reports diverge from a full-information allocation. A follow-up intervention where we add information about capabilities from prior experiments to the context improves calibration, but only modestly narrows the gap to a full-information benchmark. We also document the performance of a market-based scaffolding with these LLMs. Our results point to self-assessment as a key bottleneck for market-style coordination of AI agents.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MarketBench, a benchmark to evaluate whether AI agents can accurately self-assess their capabilities (success probability and cost) for market participation. Testing on SWE-bench Lite with six LLMs, they find agents are miscalibrated on both dimensions, leading to suboptimal market allocations. Adding prior performance information helps calibration but doesn't fully close the gap to optimal allocation.
AI.motivation: Markets could effectively coordinate AI agents, but agents need reliable self-assessment signals (ability to complete tasks and associated costs). Current LLMs lack this capability, creating a bottleneck.
AI.method: Proposes MarketBench benchmark using a 93-task subset of SWE-bench Lite; evaluates six recent LLMs on their self-reported success probability and token usage calibration; compares market allocations from self-reports vs. full-information baseline; tests an intervention adding prior experiment info to context.
AI.result: LLMs are miscalibrated on both success probability and token usage; auctions based on self-reports diverge from full-information allocation; adding prior capability information improves calibration but only modestly narrows the gap.
AI.conclusion: Self-assessment is a key bottleneck for market-style coordination of AI agents; improving calibration remains an open challenge.

title: An Automatic Ground Collision Avoidance System with Reinforcement Learning
summary: This article evaluates an artificial intelligence (AI)-based Automatic Ground Collision Avoidance System (AGCAS) designed for advanced jet trainers to enhance operational effectiveness. In the continuously evolving field of aerospace engineering, the integration of AI is crucial for advancing operations with improved timing constraints and efficiency. Our study explores the design process of an AI-driven AGCAS, specifically tailored for advanced jet trainers, focusing on addressing the AGCAS problem within a limited observation space. The system utilizes line-of-sight queries on a terrain server to ensure precise and efficient collision avoidance. This approach aims to significantly improve the safety and operational capabilities of advanced jet trainers.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an AI-based Automatic Ground Collision Avoidance System (AGCAS) for advanced jet trainers, using line-of-sight queries on a terrain server to enhance safety and operational effectiveness within a limited observation space.
AI.motivation: To improve operational effectiveness and safety of advanced jet trainers by integrating AI for automated ground collision avoidance, addressing timing and efficiency constraints in aerospace operations.
AI.method: The system employs an AI-driven design that uses line-of-sight queries on a terrain server to achieve precise collision avoidance within a limited observation space.
AI.result: The AI-based AGCAS is expected to significantly improve safety and operational capabilities of advanced jet trainers, though specific quantitative results are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Integrating AI into AGCAS for advanced jet trainers is a promising approach to enhance operational effectiveness and safety in aerospace engineering.

title: CNN-ViT Fusion with Adaptive Attention Gate for Brain Tumor MRI Classification: A Hybrid Deep Learning Model
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, submitted as conference paper
summary: Early detection and classifying brain tumors using Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) images is highly important but difficult to extract in medical images. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are good at capturing both local texture and spatial information whereas Vision Transformers (ViTs) are good at capturing long-range global dependencies. We propose a new hybrid architecture that combines a SqueezeNet-style CNN branch with a MobileViT-style global transformer branch, through an Adaptive Attention Gate mechanism, in this paper. The gate learns dynamically per-sample, per-feature weights to weight the contribution of each branch, allowing context-sensitive merging of local and global representations. The proposed model has a test accuracy of 97.60, a precision of 97.30, a recall of 97.50, an F1-score of 97.40, and a macro-average area under the curve (AUC) of 0.9946 with a trained and evaluated on the Brain Tumor MRI Dataset (Kaggle). These scores are higher than single CNN and ViT baselines, and current competitive fusion methods, showing that dynamic feature weighting is an effective way to classify medical images.

AI.tldr: A hybrid CNN-ViT model with an Adaptive Attention Gate dynamically fuses local and global features for brain tumor classification, achieving 97.60% accuracy on a Kaggle MRI dataset, outperforming single-model and existing fusion baselines.
AI.motivation: CNNs capture local texture and spatial details well, while ViTs capture long-range global dependencies; combining both could improve brain tumor detection, but existing fusion methods lack dynamic, per-sample feature weighting.
AI.method: A hybrid architecture with a SqueezeNet-style CNN branch and a MobileViT-style transformer branch, fused via an Adaptive Attention Gate that learns per-sample, per-feature weights to dynamically balance local and global representations.
AI.result: Test accuracy 97.60%, precision 97.30%, recall 97.50%, F1-score 97.40%, macro-average AUC 0.9946 on the Brain Tumor MRI Dataset (Kaggle).
AI.conclusion: Dynamic feature weighting via an adaptive attention gate effectively fuses local and global features, outperforming single CNN/ViT models and existing fusion methods for medical image classification.

title: Evolve: A Persistent Knowledge Lifecycle for Small Language Models
comment: 35 pages, 1 figure. Code and evaluation data: https://gitlab.com/dikran.hovagimian/evolve
summary:
Evolve pairs a small local language model with a persistent, teacher-compiled knowledge store -- refined through sleep consolidation and usage-driven refresh -- to deliver substantial accuracy gains over the model's parametric baseline while amortizing teacher costs through cross-query knowledge reuse. Rather than retrieving document fragments at query time, Evolve constructs a store of semantically coherent sections compiled by teacher models at natural conceptual boundaries; new sections are staged on acquisition, consolidated offline through teacher-mediated merging, and refreshed inline when expired. A 2B-parameter local model handles classification and generation; large teacher models are invoked only for knowledge operations.
  Across 750 benchmark queries spanning custom specialist questions, NaturalQuestions, and TriviaQA, the 2B model augmented by Evolve improves from 20-33% baseline accuracy to 60-84% (+40-52pp) while reducing teacher invocations by over 50% through reuse. Post-consolidation compresses the knowledge store by 31-33.5% across three independent benchmarks while preserving accuracy; section-based retrieval outperforms chunk-based retrieval by 5-9pp across every lifecycle condition. The architecture supports two generation modes over the same lifecycle -- suppress (strict section-only grounding, auditable) and augment (section-supplemented responses).

AI.tldr: Evolve enhances a 2B-parameter local LM with a teacher-compiled knowledge store refined via sleep consolidation and usage-driven refresh, boosting accuracy from 20-33% to 60-84% (+40-52pp) across 750 queries while reducing teacher invocations by over 50% through cross-query reuse. Post-consolidation compresses the store by 31-33.5% and section-based retrieval outperforms chunk-based by 5-9pp.
AI.motivation: To overcome the accuracy limitations of small local language models without incurring high costs of large teacher model invocation at every query, by building a reusable, consolidated knowledge store.
AI.method: Evolve pairs a 2B-parameter local LM with a persistent knowledge store of semantically coherent sections compiled by large teacher models. New sections are staged on acquisition, consolidated offline via teacher-mediated merging, and refreshed inline when expired. Teacher models are invoked only for knowledge operations (not per-query). Supports two generation modes: suppress (section-only grounding) and augment (section-supplemented responses).
AI.result: On 750 benchmark queries (specialist, NaturalQuestions, TriviaQA), accuracy improved from 20-33% baseline to 60-84% (+40-52pp). Teacher invocations reduced by >50% via reuse. Post-consolidation compressed store by 31-33.5% without accuracy loss. Section-based retrieval outperformed chunk-based retrieval by 5-9pp across all lifecycle conditions.
AI.conclusion: Evolve demonstrates that a small local model augmented with a teacher-compiled, consolidated knowledge store can achieve substantial accuracy gains over its parametric baseline while efficiently amortizing teacher costs, with section-based retrieval and consolidation providing both performance and storage benefits.

title: LLM-Augmented Traffic Signal Control with LSTM-Based Traffic State Prediction and Safety-Constrained Decision Support
summary: Traffic signal control is a critical task in intelligent transportation systems, yet conventional fixed-time and rule-based methods often struggle to adapt to dynamic traffic demand and provide limited decision interpretability. This study proposes an LLM-augmented traffic signal control framework that integrates LSTM-based short-term traffic state prediction, predictive phase selection, structured large language model reasoning, and safety-constrained action filtering. The LSTM module forecasts future queue length, waiting time, vehicle count, and lane occupancy based on recent intersection-level observations. A predictive controller then generates candidate signal actions, while the LLM module evaluates these actions using structured traffic-state inputs and produces congestion diagnoses, phase adjustment recommendations, and natural-language explanations. To ensure operational reliability, all LLM-generated recommendations are validated by a safety filter before execution. Simulation-based experiments in SUMO compare the proposed method with fixed-time control, rule-based control, and an LSTM-based predictive baseline under balanced demand, directional peak demand, and sudden surge scenarios. The results indicate that the proposed framework improves traffic efficiency, especially under dynamic and non-recurrent traffic conditions, while maintaining zero constraint violations after safety filtering. Overall, this study demonstrates that LLMs can enhance traffic signal control when used as constrained reasoning and decision-support modules rather than direct low-level controllers. Keywords: Intelligent Transportation Systems; Traffic Signal Control; Large Language Models; LSTM; Traffic State Prediction; Decision Support; Safety-Constrained Control; SUMO Simulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an LLM-augmented traffic signal control framework combining LSTM prediction, predictive phase selection, structured LLM reasoning, and safety-constrained filtering. Experiments in SUMO show improved efficiency under dynamic traffic conditions with zero safety violations.
AI.motivation: Conventional fixed-time and rule-based traffic signal control methods struggle to adapt to dynamic traffic demand and offer limited decision interpretability.
AI.method: An LSTM module predicts traffic states (queue length, waiting time, vehicle count, lane occupancy). A predictive controller generates candidate actions. An LLM evaluates these actions with structured inputs, producing congestion diagnoses, phase adjustment recommendations, and natural-language explanations. A safety filter validates all LLM outputs before execution.
AI.result: The framework improves traffic efficiency, especially under dynamic and non-recurrent conditions, while maintaining zero constraint violations after safety filtering.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can effectively enhance traffic signal control when used as constrained reasoning and decision-support modules rather than direct low-level controllers.

title: UpstreamQA: A Modular Framework for Explicit Reasoning on Video Question Answering Tasks
summary: Video Question Answering (VideoQA) demands models that jointly reason over spatial, temporal, and linguistic cues. However, the task's inherent complexity often requires multi-step reasoning that current large multimodal models (LMMs) perform implicitly, leaving their internal decision process opaque. In contrast, large reasoning models (LRMs) explicitly generate intermediate logical steps that enhance interpretability and can improve multi-hop reasoning accuracy. Yet, these models are not designed for native video understanding, as they typically rely on static frame sampling. We propose UpstreamQA, a modular framework that disentangles and evaluates core video reasoning components through explicit upstream reasoning modules. Specifically, we employ multimodal LRMs to perform object identification and scene context generation before passing enriched reasoning traces to downstream LMMs for VideoQA. We evaluate UpstreamQA on the OpenEQA and NExTQA datasets using two LRMs (o4-mini, Gemini 2.5 Pro) and two LMMs (GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash). Our results demonstrate that introducing explicit reasoning can significantly boost performance and interpretability of downstream VideoQA, but can also lead to performance degradation when baseline performance is sufficiently high. Overall, UpstreamQA offers a principled framework for combining explicit reasoning and multimodal understanding, advancing both performance and diagnostic transparency in VideoQA in several scenarios.

AI.tldr: UpstreamQA is a modular framework that enhances VideoQA by using large reasoning models (LRMs) to perform explicit upstream reasoning (object identification, scene context) before passing enriched traces to downstream large multimodal models (LMMs), improving interpretability and performance in some cases but sometimes degrading results when baselines are already high.
AI.motivation: Current VideoQA models (LMMs) perform multi-step reasoning implicitly, making their internal decision process opaque and hard to interpret; meanwhile, LRMs generate explicit intermediate steps but are not designed for native video understanding (relying on static frames). The authors aim to combine the strengths of both.
AI.method: Proposes UpstreamQA, a modular framework that first uses multimodal LRMs (o4-mini, Gemini 2.5 Pro) for explicit upstream reasoning (object identification, scene context generation), then passes these enriched reasoning traces to downstream LMMs (GPT-4o, Gemini 2.5 Flash) for final VideoQA.
AI.result: Evaluated on OpenEQA and NExTQA datasets. Explicit reasoning boosts performance and interpretability in many scenarios, but can cause degradation when baseline performance is already sufficiently high.
AI.conclusion: UpstreamQA provides a principled framework combining explicit reasoning and multimodal understanding, advancing both performance and diagnostic transparency in VideoQA across several scenarios.

title: When Does Removing LayerNorm Help? Activation Bounding as a Regime-Dependent Implicit Regularizer
comment: 28 pages, 7 figures, includes appendices. Code and artifacts: https://github.com/lucky-verma/dyt-composition-study
summary: Dynamic Tanh (DyT) removes LayerNorm by bounding activations with a learned tanh(alpha x). We show that this bounding is a regime-dependent implicit regularizer, not a uniformly beneficial replacement. Across GPT-2-family models spanning 64M to 3.78B parameters and 1M to 118M tokens, with Llama and ViT cross-checks, DyT improves validation loss by 27.3% at 64M/1M but worsens it by 18.8% at 64M/118M; the 1M benefit vanishes with capacity (+1.7% at 3.78B), while the 118M penalty reaches +27.9%. The mechanism is measurable: 49% of DyT activations saturate at 1M versus 23% at 118M, and a 500-step saturation heuristic classifies DyT's sign with 75% raw in-sample accuracy on the 12-cell GPT-2 calibration set (AUC 0.75; 64% when adding Scale 5 stress cells), correctly labels 3/3 Llama checks, but only reaches 50% raw leave-one-scale-out accuracy. Three interventions support the bounding explanation: HardTanh reproduces the regime pattern, increasing alpha at 118M monotonically reduces DyT's penalty, and vanilla+dropout(p=0.5) matches DyT's data-rich loss. We also localize Llama-DyT collapse to SwiGLU gating, where saturation separates collapse from convergence in a 3-seed component ablation (r=0.94). Scope: all experiments are compute-limited (T/P < 1.84), below Chinchilla-optimal training.

AI.tldr: Dynamic Tanh (DyT) acts as a regime-dependent implicit regularizer—helpful in low-data, small-model settings but harmful in data-rich or large-model regimes due to activation saturation—contradicting claims of a uniformly beneficial LayerNorm replacement.
AI.motivation: To critically examine the claim that DyT universally replaces LayerNorm effectively, by characterizing its behavior across diverse training regimes (model size, token count) and identifying when and why it helps or hurts.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation across GPT-2 (64M–3.78B, 1M–118M tokens), Llama, and ViT models; activation saturation analysis; saturation heuristic classifier; HardTanh, alpha scaling, dropout, and SwiGLU ablation interventions.
AI.result: DyT improves loss by 27.3% at 64M/1M but worsens by 18.8% at 64M/118M; 1M benefit vanishes at 3.78B (+1.7%). 49% vs 23% activation saturation correlates with regime. Saturation heuristic achieves 75% raw accuracy (AUC 0.75). Interventions confirm bounding mechanism; Llama-DyT collapse localizes to SwiGLU gating (r=0.94).
AI.conclusion: DyT is a regime-dependent regularizer, not a universal LayerNorm replacement; its effectiveness depends on saturation levels determined by data/model size, with practical guidance limited to compute-limited training (T/P < 1.84).

title: Agentic AI platforms for autonomous training and rule induction of human-human and virus-human protein-protein interactions
summary: We instruct an AI agent to construct two separate agentic AI platforms: one for autonomous training of predictive ML models for human-human and virus-human PPI, and the other for inducing explicit general rules governing human-human and virus-human PPI. The first agentic AI platform for autonomous training of predictive ML models for PPI is designed to consist of five AI agents that handle autonomous data collection, data verification, feature embedding, model design, and training and validation on three-way protein-disjoint cross-fold datasets. For human-human and human-virus PPIs, the final three-way protein-disjoint ensemble achieves an accuracy of 87.3% and 86.5%, respectively. For cross-checking and interpretability purposes, the second agentic AI platform is designed to replace ML predictions with human-readable rules derived from protein embeddings, physicochemical autocovariance descriptors, compartment annotations, pathway-domain overlap, and graph contexts. For human-human PPI, it is defined by a two-rule induction, whereas human-virus is induced by a more complex set of weighted rules. The rules induced by the second agentic platform align with the SHAP-identified features from the predictive ML models built by the first agentic platform. Taken together, our work demonstrates the agentic AI's ability to orchestrate from data planning to execution, and from rule induction to explanation in ML, opening the door to various applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents two agentic AI platforms: one autonomously trains predictive ML models for human-human and virus-human protein-protein interactions (PPI) achieving ~87% accuracy, and another induces explicit human-readable rules for interpretability, showing alignment between the two approaches.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate agentic AI's ability to autonomously orchestrate ML workflows from data collection to rule induction and explanation, specifically for predicting and interpreting protein-protein interactions (PPI).
AI.method: Two agentic AI platforms were built: (1) five AI agents handling autonomous data collection, verification, feature embedding, model design, and training/validation on three-way protein-disjoint cross-fold datasets; (2) a second platform inducing human-readable rules from protein embeddings, physicochemical descriptors, compartment annotations, pathway-domain overlap, and graph contexts.
AI.result: Human-human PPI accuracy: 87.3%; human-virus PPI accuracy: 86.5%. Human-human PPI defined by two-rule induction; human-virus by a more complex set of weighted rules. Rules aligned with SHAP-identified features from the ML models.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI can effectively orchestrate end-to-end ML pipelines from data planning to execution, and from rule induction to explanation, offering interpretable and accurate PPI prediction.

title: BSViT: A Burst Spiking Vision Transformer for Expressive and Efficient Visual Representation Learning
summary: Spiking Vision Transformers (S-ViTs) offer a promising framework for energy-efficient visual learning. However, existing designs remain limited by two fundamental issues: the restricted information capacity of binary spike coding and the dense token interactions introduced by global self-attention. To address these challenges, this work proposes BSViT, a burst spiking-driven Vision Transformer featuring a Dual-Channel Burst Spiking Self-Attention (DBSSA) mechanism. DBSSA encodes queries with binary spikes and keys with burst spikes to enhance representational capacity. The value pathway adopts dual excitatory and inhibitory binary channels, enabling signed modulation and richer spike interactions. Importantly, the entire attention operation preserves addition-only computation, ensuring compatibility with energy-efficient neuromorphic hardware. To further reduce spike activity and incorporate spatial priors, a patch adjacency masking strategy is introduced to restrict attention to local neighborhoods, resulting in structure-aware sparsity and reduced computational overhead. In addition, burst spike coding is systematically integrated across the network to increase spike-level representational capacity beyond conventional binary spiking. Extensive experiments on both static and event-based vision benchmarks demonstrate that BSViT consistently outperforms existing spiking Transformers in accuracy while maintaining competitive energy efficiency.

AI.tldr: BSViT introduces burst spiking (multi-spike) and dual-channel excitatory/inhibitory attention to improve S-ViTs' capacity and efficiency, plus patch adjacency masking for local sparsity.
AI.motivation: Existing S-ViTs suffer from (1) limited information capacity of binary spikes and (2) dense token interactions from global self-attention.
AI.method: Proposes DBSSA: queries use binary spikes, keys use burst spikes, values use dual excitatory/inhibitory binary channels; patch adjacency masking restricts attention to local neighborhoods; burst spike coding is integrated network-wide.
AI.result: BSViT consistently outperforms existing spiking Transformers in accuracy on static and event-based benchmarks while maintaining competitive energy efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Burst spike coding and signed dual-channel attention significantly boost S-ViT performance without breaking addition-only neuromorphic compatibility.

title: Machine learning models for estimating counterfactuals in a single-arm inflammatory bowel disease study
summary: Single-arm trials accelerate study timelines by reducing the number of patients that must be recruited for a concurrent control group. However, these designs require an alternative comparator to estimate treatment effects. One approach is to construct a virtual control arm using a machine learning (ML) model trained on external control data to predict the counterfactual outcomes of the treatment arm. Our aim in this study was to leverage virtual controls by developing and evaluating ML-based counterfactual outcome models trained on IFX-treated patients to predict 1-year steroid-free clinical remission (SFCR ) and a composite of C-reactive protein remission plus steroid-free clinical remission (CRP-SFCR) for ADA-treated pediatric Crohn's disease patients, and to compare the resulting IFX-versus-ADA treatment effect estimates with those obtained using propensity score matching to external controls. Five ML models were used to train counterfactual models on the observed IFX cohort data. The resulting models were used to predict the counterfactual outcomes for the ADA arm patients. LGBM yields the best OR closest to the propensity score matched reference, and all 95% CI results align with the conclusion from the reference study that no statistical difference in the primary and secondary outcomes has been observed between the patients treated with ADA or IFX. Our study supports virtual controls as a viable and effective substitute for expensive, lengthy or unethical patient recruitment in an inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) trial. The developed gradient boosted prediction model can be used as a pretrained model to generate IFX counterfactual predictions in future studies, pending external validation and assessment of transportability.

AI.tldr: This study developed ML-based virtual control arms using IFX-treated patient data to predict counterfactual outcomes for ADA-treated pediatric Crohn's disease patients, finding that virtual controls can effectively substitute for traditional control groups in IBD trials.
AI.motivation: Single-arm trials can accelerate study timelines, but require alternative comparators. Virtual control arms using ML models trained on external data offer a solution to reduce the need for concurrent control groups and expensive/lengthy patient recruitment.
AI.method: Five ML models (including LGBM) were trained on observed IFX-treated patient data to predict counterfactual outcomes (1-year SFCR and CRP-SFCR) for ADA-treated pediatric Crohn's disease patients. Treatment effect estimates were compared to those from propensity score matching with external controls.
AI.result: LGBM produced odds ratios closest to the propensity score matched reference. All 95% confidence intervals aligned with the reference conclusion that no statistical difference in outcomes was observed between ADA and IFX treatment groups.
AI.conclusion: Virtual controls are a viable and effective substitute for traditional control groups in IBD trials, and the gradient boosted model can serve as a pretrained model for future IFX counterfactual predictions pending external validation.

title: GAMED.AI: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Educational Game Generation
summary: We introduce GameDAI, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that transforms instructor-provided questions into fully playable, pedagogically grounded educational games validated through formal mechanic contracts. Built on phase-based LangGraph sub-graphs, deterministic Quality Gates, and structured Pydantic schemas, GameDAI supports two template families encompassing 15 interaction mechanics across spatial reasoning, procedural execution, and higher-order Bloom's Taxonomy objectives.
