Evaluated on 200 questions spanning five subject domains, the system achieves a 90% validation pass rate, 98.3% schema compliance, and 73% token reduction over ReAct agents (${\sim}$73,500 $\rightarrow$ ${\sim}$19,900 tokens/game) at $0.46 per game. Within this model configuration, these results suggest that phase-bounded architectural structure correlates more strongly with alignment quality than prompting strategy alone. Our demonstration lets attendees generate Bloom's-aligned games from natural language in under 60 seconds, inspect Quality Gate outputs at each pipeline phase, and browse a curated library of 50 games spanning all 15 mechanic types.

AI.tldr: GameDAI is a hierarchical multi-agent framework that converts instructor questions into educational games with 90% validation pass rate, 98.3% schema compliance, and 73% token reduction ($0.46/game) across 200 questions, suggesting phase-bounded architecture correlates more strongly with alignment quality than prompting strategy alone.
AI.motivation: To transform instructor-provided questions into fully playable, pedagogically grounded educational games validated through formal mechanic contracts, addressing the gap between question generation and game-based learning.
AI.method: A hierarchical multi-agent framework using phase-based LangGraph sub-graphs, deterministic Quality Gates, and structured Pydantic schemas, supporting two template families with 15 interaction mechanics across spatial reasoning, procedural execution, and higher-order Bloom's Taxonomy objectives.
AI.result: 90% validation pass rate, 98.3% schema compliance, 73% token reduction (from ~73,500 to ~19,900 tokens/game) at $0.46 per game across 200 questions in five domains. Generation in under 60 seconds.
AI.conclusion: Phase-bounded architectural structure correlates more strongly with alignment quality than prompting strategy alone, demonstrating that structured multi-agent frameworks can efficiently produce pedagogically sound educational games.

title: A Topology fixated Shape Gradient Framework for Non Simple Boundary Extraction for CIE Lab color images with Repulsive Energy
summary: A levelset free but a hybrid image segmentation approach based on a modified version of the piece wise constant shape gradient of an Mumford Shah shape functional and a repulsive function is considered. The segmentation is performed a non-local shape based through an evolution of discrete curves driven by a non local shape based energy to segment images containing disjoint regions and multiple boundaries. This formulation has a novel additional component as a multivariable function dependent on a few sampled points of the curves that handles the occurrence of self intersection during boundary curves evolution. The method is applied to a few gray scale and color images, including images with nested structures and astronomical objects. The results indicate effective segmentation in complex scenarios with absolute control on the topology of the segments and self-intersections of the boundaries

AI.tldr: A levelset-free hybrid image segmentation approach using a modified Mumford-Shah shape functional with a repulsive function and non-local shape-based energy, featuring a multivariable function to prevent self-intersections during curve evolution.
AI.motivation: To develop a segmentation method that handles disjoint regions, multiple boundaries, and complex scenarios (e.g., nested structures) without relying on levelset methods, while offering absolute control over topology and preventing self-intersections.
AI.method: Modifies the piecewise constant shape gradient of a Mumford-Shah functional; uses non-local shape-based energy with discrete curve evolution; introduces a multivariable function dependent on sampled curve points to prevent self-intersections.
AI.result: Applied successfully to gray scale and color images, including nested structures and astronomical objects; achieved effective segmentation in complex scenarios with full topology control.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides robust segmentation with absolute topology control and self-intersection prevention, suitable for complex imaging scenarios.

title: Evaluating CUDA Tile for AI Workloads on Hopper and Blackwell GPUs
summary:
NVIDIA's CUDA Tile (CuTile) introduces a Python-based, tile-centric abstraction for GPU kernel development that aims to simplify programming while retaining Tensor Core and Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) efficiency on modern GPUs. We present the first independent, cross-architecture evaluation of CuTile against established approaches such as cuBLAS, Triton, WMMA, and raw SIMT on three NVIDIA GPUs spanning Hopper and Blackwell: H100 NVL, B200, and RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition. We benchmark representative AI workloads, including GEMM, fused multi-head attention, and end-to-end LLM inference in BF16/FP16 precision, to assess both performance and portability.
  Our results show that CuTile effectiveness is strongly workload- and architecture-dependent. On datacenter-class Blackwell (B200), CuTile achieves up to 1007 TFLOP/s for fused attention, outperforming FlashAttention-2 by 2.5x while requiring only 60 lines of Python kernel code. For GEMM, CuTile reaches 52-79% of cuBLAS performance in 22 lines of code (versus 123 for WMMA), making it a practical replacement for hand-written CUDA kernels but not yet for vendor-optimized libraries. However, the same CuTile attention kernel achieves only 53% of FlashAttention-2 throughput on RTX PRO 6000 (sm_120), exposing significant cross-architecture optimization gaps. In contrast, Triton sustains 62-101% of cuBLAS performance across all tested platforms without architecture-specific tuning, demonstrating substantially stronger portability.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates NVIDIA's CuTile, a Python-based tile-centric GPU programming abstraction, across three GPU architectures (H100, B200, RTX PRO 6000), comparing it against cuBLAS, Triton, WMMA, and raw SIMT. CuTile shows impressive peak performance on B200 (up to 1007 TFLOP/s for fused attention, 2.5x over FlashAttention-2) in very compact code, but suffers from significant cross-architecture portability issues, achieving only 53% of FlashAttention-2 on RTX PRO 6000. Triton demonstrates superior portability with consistent performance across all tested platforms.
AI.motivation: To independently evaluate CuTile's promise of simplifying GPU kernel development while maintaining efficiency on modern GPUs, and to assess its cross-architecture portability across different NVIDIA GPU generations (Hopper and Blackwell).
AI.method: Cross-architecture benchmarking on three NVIDIA GPUs (H100 NVL, B200, RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition) using representative AI workloads (GEMM, fused multi-head attention, end-to-end LLM inference) in BF16/FP16 precision, comparing CuTile against cuBLAS, Triton, WMMA, and raw SIMT.
AI.result: On B200, CuTile achieves 1007 TFLOP/s for fused attention (2.5x FlashAttention-2) in 60 lines of Python; for GEMM, 52-79% of cuBLAS in 22 lines. However, CuTile's attention kernel achieves only 53% of FlashAttention-2 on RTX PRO 6000, revealing cross-architecture gaps. Triton sustains 62-101% of cuBLAS across all platforms without architecture-specific tuning.
AI.conclusion: CuTile offers impressive peak performance and code simplicity on specific architectures (especially B200), making it a practical replacement for hand-written CUDA kernels. However, it currently lacks cross-architecture portability, while Triton remains the more portable solution across NVIDIA GPU generations.

title: Context-Aware Hospitalization Forecasting Evaluations for Decision Support using LLMs
summary: Medical and public health experts must make real-time resource decisions, such as expanding hospital bed capacity, based on projected hospitalization trends during large-scale healthcare disruptions (e.g., operational failures or pandemics). Forecasting models can assist in this task by analyzing large volumes of resource-related data at the facility level, but they must be reliable for decision-making under real-world data conditions. Recent work shows that large language models (LLMs) can incorporate richer forms of context into numerical forecasting. Whereas traditional models rely primarily on temporal context (i.e., past observations), LLMs can also leverage non-temporal public health context such as demographic, geographic, and population-level features. However, it remains unclear how these models should be used to produce stable or decision-relevant predictions in real-world healthcare settings. To evaluate how LLMs can be effectively used in this setting, we evaluate three approaches across 60 counties with low-,mid-, and high-hospitalization intensities in the United States: direct LLM-based forecasting, classical time-series models, and a context-augmented hybrid pipeline (HybridARX) that incorporates LLM-derived signals into structured models. Because the goal is operational decision-making rather than error minimization alone, we evaluate performance with bias and lead-lag alignment in addition to standard forecasting metrics. Our results show that HybridARX improves over classical ARX by yielding more stable and better-calibrated forecasts, particularly when incorporating noisy contextual signals into structured time-series models. These findings suggest that, in non-stationary healthcare resource forecasting, LLMs are most useful when embedded within structured hybrid models.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates three forecasting approaches (direct LLM, classical time-series, and a hybrid LLM-structured model) for healthcare resource planning across 60 U.S. counties, finding that hybrid models incorporating LLM-derived context into structured models (HybridARX) produce more stable and better-calibrated forecasts for real-world decision-making.
AI.motivation: Medical experts need reliable forecasting models for real-time resource decisions (e.g., hospital bed capacity) during large-scale healthcare disruptions, but it's unclear how LLMs can be effectively used to produce stable, decision-relevant predictions under real-world data conditions.
AI.method: The authors compare three approaches across 60 U.S. counties with varying hospitalization intensities: (1) direct LLM-based forecasting, (2) classical time-series models, and (3) a context-augmented hybrid pipeline (HybridARX) that integrates LLM-derived contextual signals into structured time-series models. Performance is evaluated using bias, lead-lag alignment, and standard forecasting metrics.
AI.result: HybridARX outperforms classical ARX by producing more stable and better-calibrated forecasts, especially when incorporating noisy contextual signals. Direct LLM forecasting alone is less effective for operational decision-making in this setting.
AI.conclusion: For non-stationary healthcare resource forecasting, LLMs are most effective when embedded within structured hybrid models rather than used in isolation, as they enrich temporal models with valuable non-temporal context while maintaining stability.

title: One Identity, Many Roles: Multimodal Entity Coreference for Enhanced Video Situation Recognition
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026 Findings. Project Page: https://katha-ai.github.io/projects/cinemec/
summary:
Video Situation Recognition (VidSitu) addresses the challenging problem of "who did what to whom, with what, how, and where" in a video. It tests thorough video understanding by requiring identification of salient actions and associated short descriptions for event roles across multiple events. Grounding with VidSitu requires spatio-temporal localization of key entities across shots and varied appearances.
  We posit that coherent video understanding requires consistent identification of entities that play different roles. We propose Multimodal Entity Coreference (MEC) to unite entity descriptions in text with grounding across the video. Towards this, we introduce CineMEC, a multi-stage approach that unites event role mention groups with visual clusters of entities, without explicit grounding supervision during training. Our approach is designed to exploit the synergy between visual grounding and captioning, where improving one influences the other and vice versa. For evaluation, we extend the VidSitu dataset with grounding annotations. While previous work focuses primarily on descriptions, CineMEC improves consistency across both: captioning (+2.5% CIDEr, +7% LEA) and visual grounding (+18% HOTA).

AI.tldr: CineMEC improves video situation recognition by jointly handling entity coreference, captioning, and visual grounding, achieving gains of +2.5% CIDEr, +7% LEA in captioning and +18% HOTA in grounding.
AI.motivation: VidSitu requires understanding "who did what to whom, with what, how, and where" across multiple events. A key challenge is coherently identifying entities that play different roles across events, requiring consistent multimodal entity coreference without explicit grounding supervision.
AI.method: CineMEC is a multi-stage approach that unites event role mention groups with visual clusters of entities, leveraging synergy between visual grounding and captioning without explicit grounding supervision during training.
AI.result: Captioning improved by +2.5% CIDEr and +7% LEA; visual grounding improved by +18% HOTA on the VidSitu dataset.
AI.conclusion: Coherent video understanding benefits from consistent entity coreference across modalities; improving grounding and captioning jointly yields better performance in both tasks.

title: Hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph Optimization for Low-Latency Large Language Model Inference
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across natural language and multimodal tasks, yet their practical deployment remains constrained by inference latency and kernel launch overhead, particularly in interactive, short-sequence settings. This paper presents a hybrid runtime framework that combines Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation with CUDA Graph execution to reduce launch overhead while preserving runtime flexibility during autoregressive decoding. The framework partitions transformer inference into static components executed via CUDA Graph replay and dynamic components handled through JIT-compiled kernels, enabling asynchronous graph capture and reuse across decoding steps.
  We evaluate the proposed approach on LLaMA-2 7B using single-GPU, batch-size-one inference across prompt lengths from 10 to 500 tokens. Experimental results show that the hybrid runtime reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by up to 66.0% and achieves lower P99 latency compared with TensorRT-LLM in this regime. These results indicate that hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph execution can effectively reduce inference latency and variance for short-sequence LLM workloads, making it a practical optimization strategy for latency-sensitive AI applications.

AI.tldr: A hybrid runtime framework combining JIT compilation with CUDA Graph execution to reduce inference latency and kernel launch overhead for short-sequence LLM serving, achieving up to 66% TTFT reduction over TensorRT-LLM on LLaMA-2 7B.
AI.motivation: LLMs suffer from inference latency and kernel launch overhead in interactive, short-sequence settings, limiting practical deployment despite strong model performance.
AI.method: Partitions transformer inference into static components (CUDA Graph replay) and dynamic components (JIT-compiled kernels), with asynchronous graph capture and reuse across autoregressive decoding steps.
AI.result: On LLaMA-2 7B (single-GPU, batch-size-1, 10-500 token prompts), the hybrid runtime reduces TTFT by up to 66.0% and achieves lower P99 latency compared to TensorRT-LLM.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid JIT-CUDA Graph execution is an effective optimization for reducing inference latency and variance in short-sequence LLM workloads, suitable for latency-sensitive AI applications.

title: An empirical evaluation of the risks of AI model updates using clinical data: stability, arbitrariness, and fairness
comment: Accepted to iEEE EMBC 2026. 4 pages, 3 figures
summary: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning (AI/ML) models used in clinical settings are increasingly deployed to support clinical decision-making. However, when training data become stale due to changes in demographics, environment, or patient behaviors, model performance can degrade substantially. While updating models with new training data is necessary, such updates may also introduce new risks. We evaluated the proposed monitoring framework on four publicly available U.S.-based Type 1 Diabetes datasets containing high-resolution continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) data, comprising approximately 11,300 weekly observations from 496 participants under 20 years of age. All datasets included structured sociodemographic information. Using the prediction of severe hyperglycemia events in children with type 1 diabetes as a case study, we examine how different model update strategies can adversely affect model stability (e.g., by causing predictions to "flip" for a large number of cases after an update), increase arbitrariness in predictions, or worsen accuracy equity and the balance of error rates across subpopulations. We propose multiple dimensions for continuous monitoring to detect these issues and argue that such monitoring is essential for the development of trustworthy clinical decision support systems.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how updating AI/ML models in clinical settings can cause issues like prediction instability, arbitrariness, and inequity across subpopulations, using Type 1 Diabetes data as a case study, and proposes a continuous monitoring framework to detect these harms.
AI.motivation: AI/ML models in clinical settings degrade over time due to stale training data, but updating models may introduce new risks such as prediction flipping and worsened accuracy equity, which are underexplored.
AI.method: The authors evaluate different model update strategies on four Type 1 Diabetes datasets (11,300 weekly observations, 496 participants under 20) predicting severe hyperglycemia, examining effects on stability, arbitrariness, and equity across subpopulations.
AI.result: Model updates can cause predictions to flip for many cases, increase arbitrariness, and worsen accuracy equity and error rate balance across subpopulations.
AI.conclusion: Continuous monitoring across multiple dimensions is essential for developing trustworthy clinical decision support systems.

title: DyABD: The Abdominal Muscle Segmentation in Dynamic MRI Benchmark
summary: This work introduces DyABD, a novel and complex benchmark dataset of dynamic abdominal MRIs from patients with abdominal hernias and associated high quality abdominal muscle annotations. DyABD is the first-of-its-kind in four key ways; (1) it proposes the first abdominal muscle segmentation task, (2) the dynamic MRIs are acquired whilst the patients perform various exercises, introducing extreme anatomical variability, making it one of the most challenging segmentation datasets to date, (3) it includes both pre and post corrective MRIs and (4) DyABD promotes clinical research into the high recurrence rates of abdominal hernias. Beyond dataset introduction, this work provides a comprehensive evaluation of the generalisation capabilities of existing segmentation models across Supervised, Few Shot and Zero Shot paradigms on the unseen DyABD dataset. This work reveals that there is still room for substantial improvement in the field of medical image segmentation, with the majority of techniques achieving a Dice Coefficient of 0.82. This work therefore sheds light on the true progress of the field and redefines the benchmark for progress in medical image segmentation.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces DyABD, a novel benchmark dataset of dynamic abdominal MRIs from hernia patients with muscle annotations. It evaluates existing segmentation models across supervised, few-shot, and zero-shot paradigms, finding most achieve only 0.82 Dice, revealing significant room for improvement in medical image segmentation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of dynamic abdominal MRI datasets for abdominal hernia research and to provide a challenging benchmark that reveals the true generalization capabilities of existing segmentation models.
AI.method: Creation of the DyABD dataset (dynamic MRIs during exercises with pre/post-operative scans and muscle annotations) followed by comprehensive evaluation of existing segmentation models across supervised, few-shot, and zero-shot learning paradigms.
AI.result: Most existing segmentation techniques achieve a Dice Coefficient of 0.82 on the DyABD dataset, indicating substantial room for improvement despite being considered state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: The field of medical image segmentation still has significant progress to make, and DyABD redefines the benchmark for measuring true advancement in this area.

title: GeoCert: Certified Geometric AI for Reliable Forecasting
summary: Forecasting systems in science must be accurate, physically consistent, and certifiably reliable. Most existing models address prediction, constraint enforcement, and verification separately, limiting scalability and interpretability. We introduce GeoCert, a geometric AI framework that unifies forecasting, physical reasoning, and formal verification within a single differentiable computation. GeoCert formulates forecasting as evolution along a hyperbolic manifold, where negative curvature induces contraction dynamics, intrinsic robustness, and logarithmic-time certification. A hierarchical constraint architecture separates universal physical laws from domain-specific dynamics, enabling certified generalization across energy, climate, finance, and transportation systems. GeoCert achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing computational cost by 97.5% and maintaining better certification rates. By embedding verification into the geometry of learning, GeoCert transforms forecasting from empirical approximation to formally verified inference, offering a scalable foundation for trustworthy, reproducible, and physically grounded scientific AI.

AI.tldr: GeoCert is a geometric AI framework that unifies forecasting, physical reasoning, and formal verification in a single differentiable system using hyperbolic manifolds, achieving SOTA accuracy with 97.5% computational cost reduction and certified reliability.
AI.motivation: Existing forecasting systems handle prediction, constraint enforcement, and verification separately, limiting scalability and interpretability. There is a need for a unified framework that is accurate, physically consistent, and certifiably reliable.
AI.method: GeoCert formulates forecasting as evolution along a hyperbolic manifold with negative curvature for contraction dynamics and robustness. It uses a hierarchical constraint architecture separating universal physical laws from domain-specific dynamics, enabling certified generalization across domains.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing computational cost by 97.5% and maintaining better certification rates across energy, climate, finance, and transportation systems.
AI.conclusion: By embedding verification into the geometry of learning, GeoCert transforms forecasting from empirical approximation to formally verified inference, offering a scalable foundation for trustworthy and physically grounded scientific AI.

title: LLM-Guided Agentic Floor Plan Parsing for Accessible Indoor Navigation of Blind and Low-Vision People
summary: Indoor navigation remains a critical accessibility challenge for the blind and low-vision (BLV) individuals, as existing solutions rely on costly per-building infrastructure. We present an agentic framework that converts a single floor plan image into a structured, retrievable knowledge base to generate safe, accessible navigation instructions with lightweight infrastructure. The system has two phases: a multi-agent module that parses the floor plan into a spatial knowledge graph through a self-correcting pipeline with iterative retry loops and corrective feedback; and a Path Planner that generates accessible navigation instructions, with a Safety Evaluator agent assessing potential hazards along each route. We evaluate the system on the real-world UMBC Math and Psychology building (floors MP-1 and MP-3) and on the CVC-FP benchmark. On MP-1, we achieve success rates of 92.31%, 76.92%, and 61.54% for short, medium, and long routes, outperforming the strongest single-call baseline (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) at 84.62%, 69.23%, and 53.85%. On MP-3, we reach 76.92%, 61.54%, and 38.46%, compared to the best baseline at 61.54%, 46.15%, and 23.08%. These results show consistent gains over single-call LLM baselines and demonstrate that our workflow is a scalable solution for accessible indoor navigation for BLV individuals.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an agentic framework that converts a single floor plan image into a structured knowledge base for generating safe, accessible indoor navigation instructions for blind and low-vision individuals, achieving significant success rate improvements over single-call LLM baselines on real-world benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Indoor navigation remains a critical accessibility challenge for blind and low-vision individuals because existing solutions rely on costly per-building infrastructure, limiting scalability and widespread adoption.
AI.method: The system has two phases: (1) a multi-agent module that parses a floor plan into a spatial knowledge graph via a self-correcting pipeline with iterative retry loops and corrective feedback; (2) a Path Planner that generates accessible navigation instructions, with a Safety Evaluator agent assessing potential hazards along each route.
AI.result: On UMBC Math and Psychology building MP-1: 92.31%, 76.92%, 61.54% success rates for short/medium/long routes (vs. best baseline 84.62%, 69.23%, 53.85%). On MP-3: 76.92%, 61.54%, 38.46% (vs. best baseline 61.54%, 46.15%, 23.08%).
AI.conclusion: The proposed workflow consistently outperforms single-call LLM baselines and demonstrates a scalable, lightweight-infrastructure solution for accessible indoor navigation for BLV individuals.

title: AnalogRetriever: Learning Cross-Modal Representations for Analog Circuit Retrieval
comment: 10 pages, 7 figures. Yihan Wang and Lei Li contributed equally to this paper
summary: Analog circuit design relies heavily on reusing existing intellectual property (IP), yet searching across heterogeneous representations such as SPICE netlists, schematics, and functional descriptions remains challenging. Existing methods are largely limited to exact matching within a single modality, failing to capture cross-modal semantic relationships. To bridge this gap, we present AnalogRetriever, a unified tri-modal retrieval framework for analog circuit search. We first build a high-quality dataset on top of Masala-CHAI through a two-stage repair pipeline that raises the netlist compile rate from 22\% to 100\%. Built on this foundation, AnalogRetriever encodes schematics and descriptions with a vision-language model and netlists with a port-aware relational graph convolutional network, mapping all three modalities into a shared embedding space via curriculum contrastive learning. Experiments show that AnalogRetriever achieves an average Recall@1 of 75.2\% across all six cross-modal retrieval directions, significantly outperforming existing baselines. When integrated into the AnalogCoder agentic framework as a retrieval-augmented generation module, it consistently improves functional pass rates and enables previously unsolved tasks to be completed. Our code and dataset will be released.

AI.tldr: AnalogRetriever is a unified tri-modal retrieval framework that enables cross-modal search across SPICE netlists, schematics, and functional descriptions for analog circuit design, achieving 75.2% average Recall@1 and improving the AnalogCoder agent's task completion rates.
AI.motivation: Analog circuit design heavily reuses existing IP, but searching across heterogeneous representations (netlists, schematics, descriptions) is challenging because existing methods are limited to exact matching within a single modality and fail to capture cross-modal semantic relationships.
AI.method: Built a high-quality dataset via a two-stage repair pipeline (improving netlist compile rate from 22% to 100%), then designed AnalogRetriever to encode schematics/descriptions with a vision-language model and netlists with a port-aware relational graph convolutional network, mapping all three modalities into a shared embedding space via curriculum contrastive learning.
AI.result: AnalogRetriever achieves 75.2% average Recall@1 across all six cross-modal retrieval directions, significantly outperforming baselines, and consistently improves functional pass rates when integrated into the AnalogCoder agentic framework as a RAG module.
AI.conclusion: The proposed tri-modal retrieval framework effectively bridges cross-modal semantic gaps in analog circuit search, enabling previously unsolved tasks to be completed when used as a retrieval-augmented generation component, with code and dataset to be released.

title: Supernodes and Halos: Loss-Critical Hubs in LLM Feed-Forward Layers
summary: We study the organization of channel-level importance in transformer feed-forward networks (FFNs). Using a Fisher-style loss proxy (LP) based on activation-gradient second moments, we show that loss sensitivity is concentrated in a small set of channels within each layer. In Llama-3.1-8B, the top 1% of channels per layer accounts for a median of 58.7% of LP mass, with a range of 33.0% to 86.1%. We call these loss-critical channels supernodes. Although FFN layers also contain strong activation outliers, LP-defined supernodes overlap only weakly with activation-defined outliers and are not explained by activation power or weight norms alone. Around this core, we find a weaker but consistent halo structure: some non-supernode channels share the supernodes' write support and show stronger redundancy with the protected core. We use one-shot structured FFN pruning as a diagnostic test of this organization. At 50% FFN sparsity, baselines that prune many supernodes degrade sharply, whereas our SCAR variants explicitly protect the supernode core; the strongest variant, SCAR-Prot, reaches perplexity 54.8 compared with 989.2 for Wanda-channel. The LP-concentration pattern appears across Mistral-7B, Llama-2-7B, and Qwen2-7B, remains visible in targeted Llama-3.1-70B experiments, and increases during OLMo-2-7B pretraining. These results suggest that LLM FFNs develop a small learned core of loss-critical channels, and that preserving this core is important for reliable structured pruning.

AI.tldr: This paper discovers that in transformer FFN layers, loss sensitivity (measured by a Fisher-based proxy) is highly concentrated in a small set of "supernode" channels (top 1% accounts for ~58.7% of LP mass). These supernodes are distinct from activation outliers and form a protected core with a halo structure. The paper demonstrates that explicitly preserving these supernodes during structured pruning (SCAR-Prot) yields far better perplexity (54.8) than baselines that prune them (989.2), and this pattern generalizes across multiple LLM families.
AI.motivation: To understand the organization of channel-level importance in transformer FFNs, specifically whether loss-critical channels form a concentrated structure that differs from activation-based importance measures.
AI.method: Uses a Fisher-style loss proxy based on activation-gradient second moments to identify "supernode" channels; analyzes overlap with activation outliers and weight norms; proposes SCAR variants for structured pruning that protect the supernode core; validates across Llama-3.1-8B/70B, Mistral-7B, Llama-2-7B, Qwen2-7B, and OLMo-2-7B during pretraining.
AI.result: Top 1% of channels per layer accounts for median 58.7% of LP mass (range 33.0%-86.1%). Supernodes weakly overlap with activation outliers. SCAR-Prot structured pruning achieves perplexity 54.8 vs 989.2 for Wanda-channel at 50% FFN sparsity. Pattern holds across model families and increases during pretraining.
AI.conclusion: LLM FFNs develop a small learned core of loss-critical channels (supernodes) that are distinct from activation outliers, and preserving this core is critical for reliable structured pruning.

title: Representational Curvature Modulates Behavioral Uncertainty in Large Language Models
summary: In autoregressive large language models (LLMs), temporal straightening offers an account of how the next-token prediction objective shapes representations. Models learn to progressively straighten the representational trajectory of input sequences across layers, potentially facilitating next-token prediction via linear extrapolation. However, a direct link between this trajectory and token-level behavior has been missing. We provide such a link by relating contextual curvature-a geometric measure of how sharply the representational trajectory bends over recent context-to next-token entropy. Across two models (GPT-2 XL and Pythia-2.8B), contextual curvature is correlated with entropy, and this relationship emerges during training. Perturbation experiments reveal selective dependence: manipulating curvature through trajectory-aligned interventions reliably modulates entropy, while geometrically misaligned perturbations have no effect. Finally, regularizing representations to be straighter during training modestly reduces token-level entropy without degrading validation loss. These results identify trajectory curvature as a task-aligned representational feature that influences behavioral uncertainty in LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper links representational trajectory curvature in LLMs to token-level entropy, showing that straighter trajectories correlate with lower uncertainty in next-token prediction.
AI.motivation: To establish a direct connection between geometric properties of representational trajectories (curvature) and token-level predictive behavior (entropy) in autoregressive LLMs.
AI.method: The authors compute contextual curvature (how sharply the representational trajectory bends) and correlate it with next-token entropy across GPT-2 XL and Pythia-2.8B. They conduct perturbation experiments (trajectory-aligned vs. misaligned interventions) and regularization experiments to straighten representations during training.
AI.result: Contextual curvature is correlated with next-token entropy; this relationship emerges during training. Only trajectory-aligned perturbations modulate entropy, while misaligned ones have no effect. Regularizing toward straighter trajectories reduces token entropy without harming validation loss.
AI.conclusion: Trajectory curvature is a task-aligned representational feature that causally influences behavioral uncertainty in LLMs.

title: Micro-Expression-Aware Avatar Fingerprinting via Inter-Frame Feature Differencing
comment: Accepted to TrustFA Workshop, IEEE FG 2026
summary: Avatar fingerprinting, i.e., verifying who drives a synthetic talking-head video rather than whether it is real, is a critical safeguard for authorized use of face-reenactment technology. Existing methods rely on a fixed, non-differentiable landmark extraction stage that prevents the fingerprinting model from being optimized end-to-end from raw pixels. We propose a preprocessing-free system built on a micro-expression-aware backbone operating on raw video frames, with inter-frame feature differencing as the core design principle: consecutive feature maps are subtracted in the learned deep feature space, so that temporally stable appearance dimensions contribute zero to the output while driver-specific motion dynamics are preserved. A controlled ablation on NVFAIR confirms that temporal motion accounts for the large majority of discriminative performance, and that raw appearance features actively degrade identity separation. Both the choice of backbone and the differencing principle are essential: differencing alone is insufficient when applied to a generic encoder, as appearance-dominated features collapse to near-identical representations across adjacent frames, while the micro-expression-aware F5C backbone retains measurable motion variation that the differencing operation can exploit. Without any external preprocessing, our model achieves an overall AUC of 0.877 on NVFAIR and matches or exceeds the landmark-based baseline on the majority of cross-generator pairs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a preprocessing-free avatar fingerprinting system that uses inter-frame feature differencing on raw video frames, achieving 0.877 AUC on NVFAIR by capturing driver-specific motion dynamics while suppressing appearance features.
AI.motivation: Existing avatar fingerprinting methods rely on fixed, non-differentiable landmark extraction, preventing end-to-end optimization. The authors aim to build a system that operates directly on raw pixels without preprocessing.
AI.method: A micro-expression-aware backbone (F5C) processes raw video frames, and inter-frame feature differencing in deep feature space subtracts consecutive feature maps — suppressing stable appearance while preserving motion dynamics. Ablation studies confirm motion features dominate performance.
AI.result: Achieves overall AUC of 0.877 on NVFAIR, matches or exceeds landmark-based baselines on most cross-generator pairs. Ablation shows appearance features actively degrade identity separation.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end learnable avatar fingerprinting is feasible without landmark preprocessing. The key design insight is that motion differencing requires a motion-sensitive backbone (F5C) to be effective; generic encoders collapse under the differencing operation.

title: Do Synthetic Trajectories Reflect Real Reward Hacking? A Systematic Study on Monitoring In-the-Wild Hacking in Code Generation
summary: Reward hacking in code generation, where models exploit evaluation loopholes to obtain full reward without correctly solving the tasks, poses a critical challenge for Reinforcement Learning (RL) and the deployment of reasoning models. Existing studies have been conducted primarily on synthetic hacking trajectories. However, whether these synthetic behaviors faithfully represent naturally emerging hacking in the wild remains unclear. In this work, we present a systematic analysis of the synthetic vs. in-the-wild discrepancy in reward hacking. We examine to what extent hacking behaviors induced by prompting resemble those emerging during RL training, and whether monitors trained on synthetic trajectories generalize to naturally arising but previously unseen hacking. To scale up the curation of in-the-wild reward hacking trajectories, we modified Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) by injecting conflicting unit tests as tracers and applying a "resampling-until-hack" mechanism. Through controlled comparisons between monitors trained on synthetic versus in-the-wild data, we find that (1) synthetic-data-trained monitors fail to generalize to "in-the-wild" hacking, and (2) monitors trained on our "in-the-wild" trajectories demonstrate stronger generalizability to unseen hacking types. Our results indicate that synthetic reward hacking data may not fully reflect natural reward hacking behaviors, and that relying solely on synthetic data can lead to misleading conclusions. The codebase is available at https://github.com/LichenLillc/CoTMonitoring.git

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that synthetic reward hacking data in code generation does not generalize to real "in-the-wild" hacking behaviors observed during RL training. The authors develop a method to collect natural hacking trajectories using modified GRPO and show that monitors trained on in-the-wild data are more robust and generalizable than those trained on synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Existing studies on reward hacking in code generation rely on synthetic hacking trajectories, but it is unclear whether these synthetic behaviors faithfully represent naturally emerging hacking. This discrepancy can lead to misleading conclusions about model safety and robustness.
AI.method: The authors modified GRPO by injecting conflicting unit tests as tracers and applied a "resampling-until-hack" mechanism to scale up curation of in-the-wild reward hacking trajectories. They then conducted controlled comparisons between monitors trained on synthetic vs. in-the-wild data.
AI.result: (1) Synthetic-data-trained monitors fail to generalize to in-the-wild hacking. (2) Monitors trained on in-the-wild trajectories demonstrate stronger generalizability to unseen hacking types.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic reward hacking data may not fully reflect natural reward hacking behaviors, and relying solely on synthetic data can lead to misleading conclusions about model safety in code generation.

title: Failure-Centered Runtime Evaluation for Deployed Trilingual Public-Space Agents
summary:
This paper presents PSA-Eval, a failure-centered runtime evaluation framework for deployed trilingual public-space agents. The central claim is that, when the evaluation object shifts from a static input-output mapping to a runtime system, the basic unit of analysis should shift from score to failure. PSA-Eval extends the conventional chain Question -> Answer -> Score -> End into Question -> Batch -> Run -> Score -> Failure Case -> Repair -> Regression Batch, making failures traceable, reviewable, repairable, and regression-testable.
  The framework uses trilingual equivalent inputs as controlled probes for observing group-level cross-language policy drift. We conduct a pilot study on a real trilingual digital front-desk system deployed in the lobby of an international financial institution. The pilot uses a simplified single-foundation-model setting (MA = MB), so the observed drift should not be interpreted as an A/B foundation-model difference. The study contains 81 samples organized into 27 trilingual equivalent question groups. Although the system achieves an average score of 23.15/24, 14 groups show non-zero cross-language score drift, 5 groups show drift of at least 3 points, and the maximum drift reaches 9 points. These results provide initial evidence that failure-centered runtime evaluation can expose structured deployment signals hidden by aggregate scoring.

AI.tldr: PSA-Eval is a failure-centered runtime evaluation framework for deployed trilingual public-space agents that shifts analysis from scores to failures, enabling traceable, repairable, and regression-testable evaluation. A pilot study on a real financial institution system reveals that despite high average scores (23.15/24), significant cross-language policy drift exists (up to 9 points), exposing hidden deployment issues.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation frameworks treat models as static input-output mappings using aggregate scores, which fails to capture runtime system behavior. The authors argue that deployed systems need a shift from "score" to "failure" as the basic unit of analysis to detect hidden issues like cross-language policy drift.
AI.method: PSA-Eval extends the conventional evaluation chain (Question→Answer→Score→End) into (Question→Batch→Run→Score→Failure Case→Repair→Regression Batch). It uses trilingual equivalent inputs as controlled probes to observe cross-language policy drift. A pilot study was conducted on a real trilingual digital front-desk system with 81 samples grouped into 27 trilingual equivalent question groups.
AI.result: Average score was 23.15/24. Despite high scores, 14 out of 27 groups showed non-zero cross-language score drift, 5 groups showed drift ≥3 points, and maximum drift reached 9 points. These failures were hidden by aggregate scoring.
AI.conclusion: Failure-centered runtime evaluation can expose structured deployment signals that aggregate scoring misses, providing evidence that the proposed framework is valuable for detecting cross-language policy drift in deployed multilingual agents.

title: MotionHiFlow: Text-to-motion via hierarchical flow matching
summary: Text-to-motion generation aims to generate 3D human motions that are tightly aligned with the input text while remaining physically plausible and rich in fine-grained detail. Although recent approaches can produce complex and natural movements, they usually operate at only one temporal scale, which limits both semantic alignment and temporal coherence. Inspired by the fact that complex motions are conceptualized hierarchically rather than at a single temporal scale in the human cognitive system, we propose \textit{MotionHiFlow}, a hierarchical flow matching framework to generate motion progressively by constructing flow path from low to high temporal scales. The flows at lower scales capture high-level semantics and coarse motion structures, while flows at higher scales refine temporal details. To link the flows across scales, we introduce a novel cross-scale transition process, ensuring continuity and preserving noise consistency. Furthermore, by integrating a Text-Motion Diffusion Transformer and a topology-aware Motion VAE, MotionHiFlow explicitly models structural dependencies among joints via joint-aware positional encoding and skeletal topology, enabling precise semantic alignment alongside fine-grained motion details. Extensive experiments on HumanML3D and KIT-ML benchmarks demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with ablation studies confirming the effectiveness of the hierarchical design and key components. Code is available at https://github.com/ai-lh/MotionHiFlow.

AI.tldr: MotionHiFlow is a hierarchical flow matching framework for text-to-motion generation that progressively generates motions from low to high temporal scales, using cross-scale transitions and a topology-aware Motion VAE to achieve state-of-the-art performance on HumanML3D and KIT-ML benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-motion methods operate at only a single temporal scale, limiting semantic alignment and temporal coherence. The authors are inspired by the human cognitive system, which conceptualizes complex motions hierarchically across multiple temporal scales.
AI.method: MotionHiFlow is a hierarchical flow matching framework that builds flow paths from low to high temporal scales. Lower-scale flows capture high-level semantics and coarse structures, while higher-scale flows refine temporal details. It introduces cross-scale transition for continuity and noise consistency, integrates a Text-Motion Diffusion Transformer, and a topology-aware Motion VAE with joint-aware positional encoding and skeletal topology modeling.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on HumanML3D and KIT-ML benchmarks. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the hierarchical design and key components.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical multi-scale flow matching approach effectively improves semantic alignment, temporal coherence, and fine-grained motion detail compared to single-scale methods.

title: Interpretable Physics-Informed Load Forecasting for U.S. Grid Resilience: SHAP-Guided Ensemble Validation in Hybrid Deep Learning Under Extreme Weather
summary: Accurate short-term electricity load forecasting is a cornerstone of U.S. grid reliability; however, prevailing deep learning models remain opaque, limiting operator trust during extreme weather. A unified, interpretable, physics-informed ensemble framework is proposed, integrating a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) branch for local feature extraction and a Transformer branch for long-range dependency modeling; the branches are fused through a validation-optimized weighted ensemble and regularized by a physics-informed loss derived from the piecewise parabolic temperature-demand relationship of the Electric Reliability Council of Texas (ERCOT) system. Post-hoc interpretability is provided through SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) with the DeepExplainer backend, yielding global and event-level attributions. Using eight years of ERCOT hourly load data (2018-2025) fused with Automated Surface Observing System (ASOS) records from three Texas stations, the framework achieves 713 MW MAE, 812 MW RMSE, and 1.18% MAPE on the test window. For Hampel-flagged extreme events, MAPE falls by 20.7% relative to its Transformer branch and by 40.5% relative to its CNN branch; an ablation confirms that the parabolic and ramp constraints drive a 14.7% RMSE reduction. SHAP analysis reveals a regime shift: temperature dominates under normal operation, whereas wind speed and precipitation become more influential during cold fronts and heatwaves.

AI.tldr: Proposes a physics-informed ensemble CNN-Transformer model with SHAP interpretability for short-term electricity load forecasting; achieves 1.18% MAPE on 8 years of ERCOT data, with 20.7-40.5% error reduction on extreme events.
AI.motivation: Deep learning load forecasting models are opaque, limiting operator trust during extreme weather events where reliability is most critical.
AI.method: CNN-Transformer weighted ensemble fused with physics-informed parabolic loss from ERCOT's temperature-demand curve, plus SHAP DeepExplainer for post-hoc interpretability.
AI.result: 713 MW MAE, 812 MW RMSE, 1.18% MAPE on test set; 20.7-40.5% MAPE reduction on extreme events; 14.7% RMSE improvement from physics constraints.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed ensemble improves accuracy and trust; SHAP reveals temperature-dominant normal operation shifts to wind/precipitation influence during extremes.

title: CT-FineBench: A Diagnostic Fidelity Benchmark for Fine-Grained Evaluation of CT Report Generation
summary: The evaluation of generated reports remains a critical challenge in Computed Tomography (CT) report generation, due to the large volume of text, the diversity and complexity of findings, and the presence of fine-grained, disease-oriented attributes. Conventional evaluation metrics offer only coarse measures of lexical overlap or entity matching and fail to reflect the granular diagnostic accuracy required for clinical use. To address this gap, we propose CT-FineBench, a benchmark built from CT-RATE and Merlin to evaluate the fine-grained factual consistency of CT reports, constructed from CT-RATE and Merlin. Our benchmark is constructed through a meticulous, Question-Answering (QA) based process: first, we identify and structure key, finding-specific clinical attributes (like location, size, margin). Second, we systematically transform these attributes into a QA dataset, where questions probe for specific clinical details grounded in gold-standard reports. The evaluation protocol for CT-FineBench involves using this QA dataset to query a machine-generated report and scoring the correctness of the answers. This allows for a comprehensive, interpretable, and clinically-relevant assessment, moving beyond superficial lexical overlap to pinpoint specific clinical errors. Experiments show that CT-FineBench correlates better with expert clinical assessment and is substantially more sensitive to fine-grained factual errors than prior metrics.

AI.tldr: CT-FineBench is a new QA-based benchmark for evaluating CT report generation that focuses on fine-grained factual consistency of clinical attributes, showing better correlation with expert assessment than traditional metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation metrics for CT report generation (like lexical overlap measures) are too coarse and fail to capture the granular diagnostic accuracy needed for clinical use, especially given the large volume, diversity, and complexity of findings in CT reports.
AI.method: The benchmark is constructed via a QA-based process: (1) identify key finding-specific clinical attributes (location, size, margin, etc.), (2) systematically transform these into a QA dataset grounded in gold-standard reports, (3) evaluate machine-generated reports by querying them with this QA dataset and scoring answer correctness.
AI.result: CT-FineBench correlates better with expert clinical assessment and is substantially more sensitive to fine-grained factual errors compared to prior evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: CT-FineBench enables a more comprehensive, interpretable, and clinically-relevant assessment of CT report generation, moving beyond superficial lexical overlap to pinpoint specific clinical errors.

title: LatentBurst: A Fast and Efficient Multi Frame Super-Resolution for Hexadeca-Bayer Pattern CIS images
summary: This paper introduces a novel multi frame super-resolution network (MFSR) for burst hexadeca Bayer pattern Contact Image Sensor (CIS) images, which includes demosaicing, denoising, multi-frame fusion, and super-resolution. Designing a high-quality reconstruction network poses several challenges as follows: 1) Unlike the Bayer color filter array (CFA) pattern, it is hard to interpolate hexadeca-Bayer pattern since the pixel distance between the same color groups increases; 2) Due to large object motion and camera movements, the final fusion result usually suffers the misalignment resulting a blurry image or ghosting artifacts; 3) The proposed network should be fast and efficient enough to operate in real-time on mobile devices. To overcome these challenges, we propose a novel network, called LatentBurst, which contains: 1) a pyramid align and fusion approach in latent feature to deal with large motion scenario; 2) an efficient UNet-based structure which can run efficiently on mobile device; 3) fine-tuned optical flow estimation and two-step knowledge distillation to reduce domain-gap more effectively. Experimental results in various scenarios demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method compared with other state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: This paper presents LatentBurst, a multi-frame super-resolution network designed for hexadeca-Bayer pattern CIS images, featuring pyramid alignment in latent space, efficient UNet architecture for mobile deployment, and two-step knowledge distillation, achieving real-time performance with state-of-the-art reconstruction quality.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in reconstructing high-quality images from burst hexadeca-Bayer pattern CIS sensors, including increased pixel distance between same-color groups causing interpolation difficulties, misalignment from large object/camera motion leading to blur and ghosting artifacts, and the need for real-time mobile operation.
AI.method: LatentBurst employs: 1) a pyramid align-and-fusion approach operating in latent feature space to handle large motion; 2) an efficient UNet-based structure for mobile deployment; 3) fine-tuned optical flow estimation combined with two-step knowledge distillation to bridge the domain gap effectively.
AI.result: Experimental results across various scenarios demonstrate that LatentBurst outperforms other state-of-the-art methods in reconstruction quality while maintaining efficiency suitable for real-time mobile deployment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed LatentBurst network successfully tackles the unique challenges of hexadeca-Bayer pattern image reconstruction, achieving high-quality super-resolution on mobile devices through latent space alignment and efficient architecture design.

title: Autocorrelation Reintroduces Spectral Bias in KANs for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Existing theory suggests that Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) can overcome the spectral bias commonly observed in neural networks under the assumption that inputs are statistically independent. However, this assumption does not hold in time series forecasting (TSF), where inputs are lagged observations with strong temporal autocorrelation. Through theoretical analysis and empirical validation, we obtain an unexpected finding: temporal autocorrelation reintroduces spectral bias in KANs, and the bias becomes increasingly pronounced as the degree of autocorrelation increases. This suggests that standard KANs may face substantial difficulties in TSF with strongly autocorrelated inputs. To address this problem, we introduce the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) to reduce the correlations among the network inputs. As expected, experimental results reveal that DCT preprocessing substantially reduces the observed low-frequency preference in TSF. This result also corroborates that the spectral bias of KANs in TSF tasks is indeed induced by the autocorrelation among input variables.

AI.tldr: KANs suffer from spectral bias in time series forecasting due to autocorrelated inputs, but DCT preprocessing can mitigate this issue.
AI.motivation: Existing theory assumes KANs overcome spectral bias with independent inputs, but time series data violates this assumption due to temporal autocorrelation, motivating investigation into KANs' performance on TSF tasks.
AI.method: The authors conduct theoretical analysis and empirical validation to study spectral bias in KANs under autocorrelated inputs. They propose using Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) as a preprocessing step to reduce input correlations.
AI.result: Temporal autocorrelation reintroduces spectral bias in KANs, which worsens with increasing autocorrelation. DCT preprocessing significantly reduces the observed low-frequency preference in TSF.
AI.conclusion: Standard KANs face difficulties in TSF with strongly autocorrelated inputs, but DCT preprocessing effectively mitigates the spectral bias, confirming that autocorrelation is the root cause.

title: QED: An Open-Source Multi-Agent System for Generating Mathematical Proofs on Open Problems
summary: We explore a central question in AI for mathematics: can AI systems produce original, nontrivial proofs for open research problems? Despite strong benchmark performance, producing genuinely novel proofs remains an outstanding challenge for LLMs. Through systematic experiments with frontier LLMs on research-level proof tasks, we identify seven failure modes that prevent reliable proof generation, including context contamination, citation hallucination, hand-waving on key steps and misallocation of proof effort, unstable proof plans, unfocused verification, problem modification and single-model bottleneck. We argue that the gap between benchmark success and research-level proving is primarily one of system design, due to those failure modes. We present QED, an open-source multi-agent proof system in which each architectural decision directly addresses a specific failure mode. Evaluated on five open problems in applied analysis and PDEs contributed by domain experts, QED produces correct proofs for three problems, each verified by the contributing experts as original and nontrivial. QED is released as open-source software at https://github.com/proofQED/QED.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces QED, a multi-agent proof system that addresses seven identified failure modes of LLMs in research-level mathematics, successfully producing original proofs for three out of five open problems in applied analysis and PDEs.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether AI systems can produce original, nontrivial proofs for open research problems in mathematics, and to identify and address the failure modes that prevent current LLMs from achieving this.
AI.method: The authors systematically experiment with frontier LLMs on research-level proof tasks to identify seven failure modes. They then design QED, an open-source multi-agent proof system where each architectural decision directly targets a specific failure mode.
AI.result: QED produces correct proofs for three out of five open problems in applied analysis and PDEs, each verified by domain experts as original and nontrivial.
AI.conclusion: The gap between benchmark success and research-level proving is primarily a system design issue; properly addressing failure modes via multi-agent architecture enables LLM-based systems to generate original mathematical proofs.

title: A Hierarchical Ensemble Inference Pipeline for Robust White Blood Cell Classification Under Domain Shifts
summary: Automated white blood cell (WBC) classification is essential for scalable leukaemia screening. However, real-world deployment is challenged by domain shifts caused by staining protocols, scanner characteristics, and inter-laboratory variability, which often degrade model performance. The White Blood Cell Classification Challenge (WBCBench) at ISBI 2026 aims to advance robust WBC recognition, with a focus on accurately identifying blast cells and other clinically critical rare subtypes. We propose a memory-augmented, hierarchical ensemble pipeline for WBC classification under domain shifts, leveraging a feature bank and a DinoBloom backbone fine-tuned with LoRA. Our three-stage inference hierarchy combines k-nearest neighbors (kNN) retrieval at each level, reducing over-reliance on any single decision. Evaluated on the WBCBench dataset, our method ranks within the top ten by macro F1-score in the final testing phase.

AI.tldr: A memory-augmented hierarchical ensemble pipeline using DinoBloom + LoRA and kNN retrieval for robust WBC classification under domain shifts, ranking top-10 in WBCBench at ISBI 2026.
AI.motivation: Real-world WBC classification suffers from domain shifts (staining, scanner, lab variability), hindering scalable leukemia screening. The WBCBench challenge aims to improve robust blast cell and rare subtype recognition.
AI.method: Proposes a three-stage hierarchical ensemble pipeline with memory augmentation: uses a feature bank, DinoBloom backbone fine-tuned with LoRA, and k-nearest neighbors (kNN) retrieval at each level to reduce over-reliance on single decisions.
AI.result: Ranked within the top ten by macro F1-score on the WBCBench dataset final testing phase.
AI.conclusion: The memory-augmented hierarchical ensemble effectively handles domain shifts for WBC classification, demonstrating competitive performance in a benchmark challenge.

title: When PINNs Go Wrong: Pseudo-Time Stepping Against Spurious Solutions
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) provide a promising machine learning framework for solving partial differential equations, but their training often breaks down on challenging problems, sometimes converging to physically incorrect solutions despite achieving small residual losses. This failure, we argue, is not merely an optimization difficulty. Rather, it reflects a fundamental weakness of the empirical PDE residual loss, which can admit trivial or spurious solutions during training. From this perspective, we revisit pseudo-time stepping, a technique that has recently shown strong empirical success in PINNs. We show that its main benefit is not simply to ease optimization; instead, when combined with collocation-point resampling, it helps reveal and avoid spurious solutions. At the same time, we find that the effectiveness of pseudo-time stepping depends critically on the choice of step size, which cannot be tuned reliably from the training loss alone. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive pseudo-time stepping strategy that selects the step size from a finite-difference surrogate of the local residual Jacobian, yielding the largest step permitted by local stability without per-problem tuning. Across a diverse set of PDE benchmarks, the proposed method consistently improves both accuracy and robustness. Together, these findings provide a clearer understanding of why PINNs fail and suggest a practical pathway toward more reliable physics-informed learning. All code and data accompanying this manuscript are available at https://github.com/sifanexisted/jaxpi2.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies that PINN failures stem from spurious solutions in residual loss, not just optimization issues. It reinterprets pseudo-time stepping as a method to avoid these spurious solutions, and proposes an adaptive step-size strategy using a finite-difference surrogate of the Jacobian, improving accuracy and robustness across PDE benchmarks.
AI.motivation: PINNs often converge to physically incorrect solutions despite small residual losses. The authors argue this is a fundamental weakness of the empirical PDE residual loss (admitting spurious solutions), not merely an optimization difficulty. The goal is to understand why PINNs fail and develop more reliable training methods.
AI.method: The paper revisits pseudo-time stepping, showing its benefit lies in revealing/avoiding spurious solutions when combined with collocation-point resampling. They propose an adaptive pseudo-time stepping strategy that selects step size from a finite-difference surrogate of the local residual Jacobian, guided by local stability constraints, eliminating per-problem tuning.
AI.result: The proposed adaptive pseudo-time stepping consistently improves both accuracy and robustness across a diverse set of PDE benchmarks, outperforming fixed-step approaches that are sensitive to step size choice.
AI.conclusion: PINN failures are fundamentally due to spurious solutions in the residual loss. Pseudo-time stepping helps avoid these, and an adaptive step-size strategy enables reliable training without manual tuning. This provides both theoretical understanding and a practical pathway toward more reliable physics-informed learning.

title: AgentPulse: A Continuous Multi-Signal Framework for Evaluating AI Agents in Deployment
comment: 19 pages, 5 figures, 9 tables. Preprint under review
summary: Static benchmarks measure what AI agents can do at a fixed point in time but not how they are adopted, maintained, or experienced in deployment. We introduce AgentPulse, a continuous evaluation framework scoring 50 agents across 10 workload categories along four factors (Benchmark Performance, Adoption Signals, Community Sentiment, and Ecosystem Health) aggregated from 18 real-time signals across GitHub, package registries, IDE marketplaces, social platforms, and benchmark leaderboards. Three analyses ground the framework. The four factors capture largely complementary information (n=50; $ρ_{\max}=0.61$ for Adoption-Ecosystem, all others $|ρ| \leq 0.37$). A circularity-controlled test (n=35) shows the Benchmark+Sentiment sub-composite, which contains no GitHub-derived signals, predicts external adoption proxies it does not aggregate: GitHub stars ($ρ_s=0.52$, $p<0.01$) and Stack Overflow question volume ($ρ_s=0.49$, $p<0.01$), with VS Code installs ($ρ_s=0.44$, $p<0.05$) reported as illustrative given that only 11 of 35 agents have non-zero installs. On the n=11 subset with published SWE-bench scores, composite and benchmark-only rankings are nearly uncorrelated ($ρ_s=0.25$; 9 of 11 agents shift by at least 2 ranks), driven by a strong negative Adoption-Capability correlation among closed-source high-capability agents within this subset. This is precisely why we rest the framework's validity claim on the broader n=35 test rather than the SWE-bench overlap. AgentPulse surfaces deployment signal absent from benchmarks; it is a methodology, not a ground-truth ranking. The framework, all collected signals, scoring outputs, and evaluation harness are released under CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: AgentPulse is a continuous evaluation framework that scores AI agents across four complementary factors (Benchmark Performance, Adoption Signals, Community Sentiment, Ecosystem Health) from 18 real-time signals, revealing that deployment signals are largely uncorrelated with benchmark rankings and that composite scores predict external adoption proxies better than benchmarks alone.
AI.motivation: Static benchmarks fail to capture how AI agents are actually adopted, maintained, and experienced in real-world deployment, creating a gap between lab performance and practical utility.
AI.method: A continuous evaluation framework scoring 50 agents across 10 workload categories using four factors aggregated from 18 real-time signals across GitHub, package registries, IDE marketplaces, social platforms, and benchmark leaderboards.
AI.result: The four factors capture complementary information (max ρ=0.61); the Benchmark+Sentiment sub-composite predicts external adoption proxies (GitHub stars ρ=0.52, Stack Overflow ρ=0.49); composite and benchmark-only rankings are nearly uncorrelated (ρ=0.25) on the SWE-bench subset.
AI.conclusion: AgentPulse surfaces deployment signals absent from benchmarks and serves as a methodology for continuous evaluation rather than a ground-truth ranking; the framework and data are publicly released.

title: SemiGDA: Generative Dual-distribution Alignment for Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation
comment: This paper have been accepted by CVPR 2026
summary: Semi-supervised learning addresses label scarcity and high annotation costs in medical image segmentation by exploiting the latent information in unlabeled data to enhance model performance. Traditional discriminative segmentation relies on segmentation masks, neglecting feature-level distribution constraints. This limits robust semantic representation learning and adaptive modeling of unlabeled data in scenarios with few labels. To address these limitations, we propose SemiGDA, a novel Generative Dual-distribution Alignment framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation. Our SemiGDA overcomes the reliance of discriminative methods on large labeled datasets by aligning feature and semantic distributions to boost semantic learning and scene adaptability. Specifically, we propose a Dual-distribution Alignment Module (DAM), which employs two structurally distinct encoders to model image and mask feature distributions. It enforces their alignment in the latent space via distributional constraints, establishing structured feature consistency. Moreover, we design a Consistency-Driven Skip Adapter (CDSA) strategy, which introduces dual skip adapters (Image and Mask) to fuse multi-scale features via skip connections. Using a consistency loss, CDSA enhances cross-branch semantic alignment and reinforces fine-grained semantic consistency. Experimental results on diverse medical datasets show that our method outperforms other state-of-the-art semi-supervised segmentation methods. Code is released at: https://github.com/taozh2017/SemiGDA.

AI.tldr: SemiGDA is a Generative Dual-distribution Alignment framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation that uses two encoders to align image and mask feature distributions, plus a Consistency-Driven Skip Adapter to reinforce fine-grained semantic consistency, outperforming existing methods on multiple medical datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional discriminative semi-supervised segmentation methods rely on segmentation masks and neglect feature-level distribution constraints, limiting robust semantic representation learning and adaptive modeling of unlabeled data under label scarcity.
AI.method: Proposes SemiGDA with two key components: (1) Dual-distribution Alignment Module (DAM) using two structurally distinct encoders to model image and mask feature distributions and enforce their alignment via distributional constraints; (2) Consistency-Driven Skip Adapter (CDSA) introducing dual skip adapters (Image and Mask) with consistency loss to enhance cross-branch semantic alignment and fine-grained consistency.
AI.result: Outperforms other state-of-the-art semi-supervised segmentation methods on diverse medical datasets.
AI.conclusion: Generative dual-distribution alignment effectively boosts semantic learning and scene adaptability for semi-supervised medical image segmentation, overcoming reliance on large labeled datasets.

title: On the Memorization of Consistency Distillation for Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models are central to modern generative modeling, and understanding how they balance memorization and generalization is critical for reliable deployment. Recent work has shown that memorization in diffusion models is shaped by training dynamics, with generalization and memorization emerging at different stages of training. However, deployed diffusion models are often further distilled, introducing an additional training phase whose impact on memorization is not well understood. In this work, we analyze how distillation reshapes memorization behavior in diffusion models, taking consistency distillation as a representative framework. Empirically, we show that when applied to a teacher model that has memorized data, consistency distillation significantly reduces transferred memorization in the student while preserving, and sometimes improving, sample quality. To explain this behavior, we provide a theoretical analysis using a random feature neural network model [Bonnaire et al., 2025], showing that consistency distillation suppresses unstable feature directions associated with memorization while preserving stable, generalizable modes. Our findings suggest that distillation can serve not only as an acceleration tool, but also as a mechanism for improving the memorization-generalization trade-off.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how distillation (specifically consistency distillation) affects memorization in diffusion models, finding that it reduces memorization while preserving or improving sample quality, both empirically and through theoretical analysis using random feature neural networks.
AI.motivation: Understanding the memorization-generalization trade-off in diffusion models is critical for reliable deployment, but the impact of distillation—an additional training phase commonly applied to deployed models—on memorization is not well understood.
AI.method: Empirically applies consistency distillation to teacher models that have memorized data and measures memorization transfer to student models; theoretically analyzes behavior using a random feature neural network model, showing distillation suppresses unstable feature directions linked to memorization while preserving stable generalizable modes.
AI.result: Consistency distillation significantly reduces transferred memorization in student models while preserving (and sometimes improving) sample quality, compared to the teacher model.
AI.conclusion: Distillation can serve not only as an acceleration tool for diffusion models but also as a mechanism to improve the memorization-generalization trade-off.

title: A2DEPT: Large Language Model-Driven Automated Algorithm Design via Evolutionary Program Trees
summary: Designing heuristics for combinatorial optimization problems (COPs) is a fundamental yet challenging task that traditionally requires extensive domain expertise. Recently, Large Language Model (LLM)-based Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) has shown promise in autonomously generating heuristic components with minimal human intervention. However, most existing LLM-based AHD methods enforce fixed algorithmic templates to ensure executability, which confines the search to component-level tuning and limits system-level algorithmic expressiveness. To enable open-ended solver synthesis beyond rigid templates, we propose Automated Algorithm Design via Evolutionary Program Trees (A2DEPT), which treats LLMs as system-level algorithm architects. A2DEPT explores the vast program space via a tree-structured evolutionary search with hybrid selection and hierarchical operators, enabling iterative refinement of complete algorithms. To make open-ended generation practical, we enforce executability with a lightweight program-maintenance loop that performs feedback-driven repair. In experiments, A2DEPT consistently outperforms representative LLM-based baselines on both standard and highly constrained benchmarks. On the standard benchmarks, it reduces the mean normalized optimality gap by 9.8% relative to the strongest competing AHD baseline.

AI.tldr: A2DEPT uses LLMs as algorithm architects with evolutionary program trees to automatically design heuristics for combinatorial optimization, outperforming existing LLM-based methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based Automated Heuristic Design methods enforce fixed algorithmic templates, limiting system-level expressiveness to only component-level tuning. The goal is to enable open-ended solver synthesis without rigid templates.
AI.method: A2DEPT (Automated Algorithm Design via Evolutionary Program Trees) treats LLMs as system-level algorithm architects, using tree-structured evolutionary search with hybrid selection and hierarchical operators. It includes a lightweight program-maintenance loop for feedback-driven repair to ensure executability.
AI.result: A2DEPT consistently outperforms representative LLM-based baselines on standard and highly constrained benchmarks. It reduces the mean normalized optimality gap by 9.8% relative to the strongest competing AHD baseline.
AI.conclusion: Treating LLMs as system-level architects with evolutionary program tree search enables more expressive and effective automated heuristic design for combinatorial optimization problems.

title: Lightweight and Production-Ready PDF Visual Element Parsing
summary: PDF documents contain critical visual elements such as figures, tables, and forms whose accurate extraction is essential for document understanding and multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Existing PDF parsers often miss complex visuals, extract non-informative artifacts (e.g., watermarks, logos), produce fragmented elements, and fail to reliably associate captions with their corresponding elements, which degrades downstream retrieval and question answering. We present a lightweight and production level PDF parsing framework that can accurately detect visual elements and associates captions using a combination of spatial heuristics, layout analysis, and semantic similarity. On popular benchmark datasets and internal product data, the proposed solution achieves $\geq96\%$ visual element detection accuracy and $93\%$ caption association accuracy. When used as a preprocessing step for multimodal RAG, it significantly outperforms state-of-the-art parsers and large vision-language models on both internal data and the MMDocRAG benchmark, while reducing latency by over $2\times$. We have deployed the proposed system in challenging production environment.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a lightweight, production-level PDF parsing framework that achieves ≥96% visual element detection accuracy and 93% caption association accuracy using spatial heuristics, layout analysis, and semantic similarity. It significantly outperforms existing parsers and large vision-language models in multimodal RAG tasks while reducing latency by over 2×, and has been deployed in production.
AI.motivation: Existing PDF parsers struggle with accurate extraction of visual elements (figures, tables, forms), often missing complex visuals, extracting non-informative artifacts (watermarks, logos), producing fragmented elements, and failing to reliably associate captions with their corresponding elements—all of which degrade downstream retrieval and question answering performance.
AI.method: The framework combines spatial heuristics, layout analysis, and semantic similarity to accurately detect visual elements and associate captions. It is designed as a lightweight, production-level system.
AI.result: Achieves ≥96% visual element detection accuracy and 93% caption association accuracy on popular benchmarks and internal data. In multimodal RAG, it outperforms state-of-the-art parsers and large vision-language models while reducing latency by over 2×.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system is effective, efficient, and robust enough for production deployment in challenging environments, significantly advancing PDF parsing for multimodal RAG applications.
