Depth-wise analysis reveals that 26.92% of queries resolve within three seconds (2.45s mean latency) via single-step routing (d=0: 37.9% Subset Accuracy, 74.8% Micro-F1), while token consumption scales by 4.9x across depths, validating complexity-aware resource allocation and establishing Pareto-optimal balance across accuracy, latency, and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Tree-of-Retrieval (Adaptive ToR) is a complexity-aware retrieval architecture that dynamically configures retrieval topology based on query complexity, achieving 9.7% accuracy improvement over fixed-depth baselines while reducing latency by 37.6% and LLM invocations by 43.0% on the NLU++ benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-intent NLU retrieval systems trade off between accuracy (single-step retrieval compromises recall) and efficiency (fixed-depth hierarchical decomposition introduces excessive latency regardless of query complexity).
AI.method: Adaptive ToR consists of four components: (1) Query Tree Classifier using a Query Complexity Index for routing; (2) Tree-Based Retrieval recursively decomposing complex queries; (3) Adaptive Pruning Module with two-stage filtering to suppress exponential growth; (4) Retrieval Reranking Layer with deduplicator-first pipeline and global LLM rescoring.
AI.result: 29.07% Subset Accuracy, 71.79% Micro-F1 (9.7% relative improvement), 37.6% latency reduction, 43.0% fewer LLM invocations, 9.8% less token consumption on NLU++ benchmark (2,693 multi-intent queries).
AI.conclusion: Complexity-aware resource allocation via adaptive retrieval topology establishes a Pareto-optimal balance across accuracy, latency, and computational efficiency, with 26.92% of queries resolved within 2.45s via single-step routing.

title: Hierarchical Spatio-Channel Clustering for Efficient Model Compression in Medical Image Analysis
summary: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have become increasingly difficult to deploy in resource-constrained environments due to their large memory and computational requirements. Although low-rank compression methods can reduce this burden, most existing approaches compress spatial and channel redundancy independently and therefore do not fully exploit the localised structure within convolutional feature maps. This paper proposes a hierarchical spatio-channel low-rank compression framework for CNNs that exploits redundancy across spatial regions and channel activations. Unlike conventional methods, which apply a uniform decomposition across an entire layer, the proposed approach first partitions feature maps into spatial regions, then groups channels according to their co-activation patterns within each region, and finally applies rank-adaptive SVD to each resulting spatio-channel cluster. The method is evaluated on an AlexNet-based brain tumour MRI classification model and compared with Global SVD and Tucker decomposition under \(3\times\) and \(6\times\) compression budgets. Our method outperforms both baselines, reducing FLOPs from \(8.21\,\mathrm{G}\) to \(1.55\,\mathrm{G}\) (\(81.1\%\) reduction), achieving a \(1.38\times\) inference speed-up, and increasing classification accuracy from \(87.76\%\) to \(89.80\%\). The method also improves the macro \(F_1\)-score and performance on challenging classes such as meningioma. A hyper-parameter trade-off analysis demonstrates that the framework provides Pareto-optimal configurations, enabling control over the balance between compression and predictive performance. Moderate clustering with adaptive rank selection yields strong results. Bootstrap standard errors are reported for all classification metrics.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a hierarchical spatio-channel low-rank compression framework for CNNs that partitions feature maps into spatial regions, groups channels by co-activation patterns, and applies rank-adaptive SVD to each cluster. On an AlexNet-based brain tumor MRI classifier, it achieves 81.1% FLOPs reduction, 1.38× speed-up, and accuracy improvement from 87.76% to 89.80%, outperforming Global SVD and Tucker decomposition.
AI.motivation: CNNs are difficult to deploy in resource-constrained environments due to large memory/computation requirements. Existing low-rank compression methods compress spatial and channel redundancy independently, failing to fully exploit localized structure within convolutional feature maps.
AI.method: A hierarchical spatio-channel low-rank compression framework: (1) partition feature maps into spatial regions, (2) group channels by co-activation patterns within each region, (3) apply rank-adaptive SVD to each spatio-channel cluster. Evaluated on AlexNet-based brain tumor MRI classification.
AI.result: Outperforms Global SVD and Tucker decomposition at 3× and 6× compression budgets. Reduces FLOPs from 8.21G to 1.55G (81.1% reduction), achieves 1.38× inference speed-up, improves accuracy from 87.76% to 89.80%, and improves macro F1-score especially on meningioma class.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical spatio-channel framework provides Pareto-optimal configurations balancing compression and predictive performance. Moderate clustering with adaptive rank selection yields strong results, and the method is effective for resource-constrained deployment of CNNs.

title: Agentic Fusion of Large Atomic and Language Models to Accelerate Materials Discovery
summary: The discovery of novel materials is critical for global energy and quantum technology transitions. While deep learning has fundamentally reshaped this landscape, existing predictive or generative models typically operate in isolation, lacking the autonomous orchestration required to execute the full discovery process. Here we present ElementsClaw, an agentic framework for materials discovery that synergizes Large Atomic Models (LAMs) with Large Language Models (LLMs). In response to varied human requirements, ElementsClaw dynamically orchestrates a suite of LAM tools finetuned from our proposed model Elements for atomic-scale numerical computation, while leveraging LLMs for high-level semantic reasoning. This shift moves AI-driven materials science from isolated processes toward integrated and human interactive discovery. In the demanding domain of superconductors, our agentic system guides the experimental synthesis of four new superconductors, including Zr3ScRe8 with a transition temperature of 6.8 K and HfZrRe4 at 6.7 K. At scale, ElementsClaw screens more than 2.4 million stable crystals within only 28 GPU hours, identifying 68,000 high-confidence superconducting candidates and vastly expanding the known superconducting space. These results demonstrate how our agent accelerates materials discovery with high physical fidelity.

AI.tldr: ElementsClaw is an agentic framework combining Large Atomic Models (LAMs) with Large Language Models (LLMs) to autonomously orchestrate materials discovery, successfully synthesizing four new superconductors and screening 2.4 million crystals in 28 GPU hours.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning models for materials discovery operate in isolation and lack autonomous orchestration to execute the full discovery process, limiting their practical impact.
AI.method: ElementsClaw synergizes Large Atomic Models (LAMs) finetuned from a proposed model called Elements for numerical computation with LLMs for semantic reasoning, dynamically orchestrating tools in response to human requirements.
AI.result: Guided synthesis of four new superconductors (Zr3ScRe8 at 6.8 K, HfZrRe4 at 6.7 K, and two others); screened 2.4M+ stable crystals in 28 GPU hours; identified 68,000 high-confidence superconducting candidates.
AI.conclusion: The agentic framework accelerates materials discovery with high physical fidelity, moving AI-driven materials science from isolated processes toward integrated, human-interactive discovery.

title: Generative Design of a Gas Turbine Combustor Using Invertible Neural Networks
summary: The need to burn 100% H2 in high efficient gas turbines featuring low NOx combustion in premix mode require the complete redesign of the combustion system to ensure stable operation without any flashback. Since all engine frames featuring a power range from 4 MW up to 600 MW are affected, a huge design effort is expected. To reduce this effort, especially to transfer knowledge between the different engine classes, generative design methods using latest AI technology will provide promising potential. In this work, this challenge is approached utilizing the current advances in generative artificial intelligence. We train an Invertible Neural Network (INN) on an expandable database of geometrically parameterized combustor designs with simulated performance labels. Utilizing the INN in its inverse direction, multiple design proposals are generated which fulfill specified performance labels.

AI.tldr: This paper uses Invertible Neural Networks to generate multiple combustion system designs that meet specific performance targets for 100% H2 gas turbines, reducing redesign efforts across different engine classes.
AI.motivation: Burning 100% H2 in gas turbines requires complete redesign of combustion systems to prevent flashback, which demands huge design efforts across all engine power ranges (4MW to 600MW).
AI.method: Train an Invertible Neural Network (INN) on a database of geometrically parameterized combustor designs with simulated performance labels, then use the INN in inverse direction to generate design proposals meeting specified performance criteria.
AI.result: Multiple design proposals are successfully generated that fulfill specified performance labels using the INN's inverse capability.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI methods like INNs show promising potential to reduce design effort and transfer knowledge between different engine classes for H2 combustion systems.

title: Necessary and sufficient conditions for universality of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks
summary: We analyze the universal approximation property of Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) in terms of their edge functions. If these functions are all affine, then universality clearly fails. How many non-affine functions are needed, in addition to affine ones, to ensure universality? We show that a single one suffices. More precisely, we prove that deep KANs in which all edge functions are either affine or equal to a fixed continuous function $σ$ are dense in $C(K)$ for every compact set $K\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ if and only if $σ$ is non-affine. In contrast, for KANs with exactly two hidden layers, universality holds if and only if $σ$ is nonpolynomial. We further show that the full class of affine functions is not required; it can be replaced by a finite set without affecting universality. In particular, in the nonpolynomial case, a fixed family of five affine functions suffices when the depth is arbitrary. More generally, for every continuous non-affine function $σ$, there exists a finite affine family $A_σ$ such that deep KANs with edge functions in $A_σ\cup\{σ\}$ remain universal. We also prove that KANs with the spline-based edge parameterization introduced by Liu et al.~\cite{Liu2024} are universal approximators in the classical sense, even when the spline degree and knot sequence are fixed in advance.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) can achieve universal approximation with just a single non-affine edge function, and that affine functions can be reduced to a finite set without losing universality.
AI.motivation: To understand the minimal conditions for universal approximation in KANs, specifically how many non-affine edge functions are needed beyond affine ones, and whether the full class of affine functions is necessary.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis proving necessary and sufficient conditions for universality in deep KANs and two-hidden-layer KANs, based on whether the non-affine function σ is affine, non-affine, or nonpolynomial, and characterizing minimal affine families.
AI.result: (1) Deep KANs are universal iff σ is non-affine. (2) Two-hidden-layer KANs are universal iff σ is nonpolynomial. (3) A finite set of five affine functions suffices for deep KANs. (4) Spline-based KANs are universal even with fixed degree and knots.
AI.conclusion: Universality in KANs requires surprisingly few non-affine and affine components, and practical spline-based KANs retain classical universal approximation guarantees.

title: Certified geometric robustness -- Super-DeepG
comment: ICCPS / HSCC 2026, CPS IoT Week, May 2026, Saint Malo (Palais du Grand Large), France
summary: Safety-critical applications are required to perform as expected in normal operations. Image processing functions are often required to be insensitive to small geometric perturbations such as rotation, scaling, shearing or translation. This paper addresses the formal verification of neural networks against geometric perturbations on their image dataset. Our method Super-DeepG improves the reasoning used in linear relaxation techniques and Lipschitz optimization, and provides an implementation that leverages GPU hardware. By doing so, Super-DeepG achieves both precision and computational efficiency of robustness certification, to an extent that outperforms prior work. Super-DeepG is shared as an open-source tool on GitHub.

AI.tldr: Super-DeepG improves formal verification of neural network robustness against geometric image perturbations using enhanced linear relaxation, Lipschitz optimization, and GPU acceleration, outperforming prior work in precision and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical applications require neural networks to be robust against small geometric perturbations (rotation, scaling, shearing, translation) in image processing, but existing verification methods lack efficiency and precision.
AI.method: Super-DeepG enhances linear relaxation techniques and Lipschitz optimization for formal verification, with a GPU-based implementation to improve both precision and computational efficiency.
AI.result: Super-DeepG achieves superior precision and computational efficiency in robustness certification compared to prior work.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively verifies neural network robustness against geometric perturbations, and the tool is shared as open-source on GitHub.

title: Breaking the Resource Wall: Geometry-Guided Sequence Modeling for Efficient Semantic Segmentation
comment: 15 pages, 20 figures. Code will be released
summary: High-performance semantic segmentation has achieved significant progress in recent years, often driven by increasingly large backbones and higher computational budgets. While effective, such approaches introduce substantial computational overhead and limit accessibility under constrained hardware settings. In this paper, we propose DGM-Net (Directional Geometric Mamba Network), an efficient architecture that improves modeling capability through structural design rather than increasing model capacity. We introduce Directional Geometric Mamba (G-Mamba), a linear-complexity O(N) operator as an alternative to conventional context modeling modules such as ASPP and PPM. To further enhance structural awareness in state space model (SSM)-based modeling, we design the DGM-Module, which extracts centripetal flow fields and topological skeletons to guide the scanning process and improve boundary preservation. Without relying on large-scale pretraining or heavy backbone scaling, DGM-Net achieves 80.8% mIoU within 28k iterations, 82.3% mIoU on Cityscapes test set, and 45.24% mIoU on ADE20K. In addition, the model maintains stable performance under constrained hardware settings (e.g., batch size of 2 on 8GB VRAM), highlighting its efficiency and practicality. These results demonstrate that incorporating geometric guidance into SSM-based architectures provides an effective and resource-efficient direction for semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: DGM-Net is an efficient semantic segmentation architecture replacing heavy backbones with a Directional Geometric Mamba operator (O(N) complexity) and DGM-Module that uses centripetal flow and topological skeletons for boundary preservation. It achieves strong results (80.8% mIoU in 28k iterations, 82.3% on Cityscapes, 45.24% on ADE20K) while running on constrained hardware (8GB VRAM, batch size 2).
AI.motivation: High-performance semantic segmentation relies on large backbones and high computational budgets, creating accessibility issues on constrained hardware. The authors aim to improve modeling capability through better structural design rather than scaling model capacity.
AI.method: 1) Directional Geometric Mamba (G-Mamba): a linear-complexity O(N) operator replacing heavy context modules like ASPP/PPM. 2) DGM-Module: extracts centripetal flow fields and topological skeletons to guide SSM scanning and enhance boundary preservation.
AI.result: 80.8% mIoU within 28k iterations, 82.3% mIoU on Cityscapes test set, 45.24% mIoU on ADE20K. Stable performance under constrained hardware (batch size 2 on 8GB VRAM).
AI.conclusion: Incorporating geometric guidance into SSM-based architectures provides an effective and resource-efficient direction for semantic segmentation.

title: WISE-FM:Operation-Aware, Engineering-Informed Foundation Model for Multi-Task Well Design
summary: Deploying machine learning models across diverse well portfolios requires generalisation to wells with design parameters outside the training distribution. Current data-driven approaches to virtual flow metering (VFM) and bottomhole estimation typically treat each well independently or ignore the influence of well design on operational behaviour. We present WISE (Well Intelligence and Systems Engineering Foundation Model), a design-aware, physics-informed multi-task model that integrates three complementary mechanisms: Feature-wise Linear Modulation (FiLM) and cross-modal attention to condition operational embeddings on well design parameters; multi-task learning for simultaneous prediction of flow rates, bottomhole conditions, and flow regime classification; and structural mass conservation with soft physics constraints derived from well engineering principles. Evaluation on the ManyWells benchmark (2000 simulated wells, $10^6$ data points) demonstrates that design-aware models reduce VFM prediction error by up to $13\times$ compared to design-unaware baselines, and that physics constraints reduce negative flow predictions by 65%. Flow regime classification achieves 97.7% bottomhole accuracy, providing continuous well integrity monitoring without additional sensors. The methodology transfers to real operational data from five Equinor Volve producers (oil rate $R^2 = 0.89$, bottomhole pressure $R^2 = 0.98$, water rate $R^2 = 0.97$). The trained model additionally serves as a fast surrogate for integrity-aware well design optimisation over a 24-dimensional design space, with more than $1000\times$ speedup over drift-flux simulations. These results demonstrate that design awareness, physics enforcement, and multi-task learning are essential and complementary ingredients for foundation models intended to operate across large well portfolios.

AI.tldr: WISE is a design-aware, physics-informed multi-task foundation model for virtual flow metering across diverse well portfolios, achieving up to 13× error reduction, 65% fewer negative flows, 97.7% flow regime accuracy, and 1000× speedup over traditional simulations.
AI.motivation: Current data-driven VFM methods treat wells independently or ignore well design parameters, leading to poor generalization across diverse well portfolios with out-of-distribution designs.
AI.method: WISE integrates FiLM and cross-modal attention for design-aware conditioning, multi-task learning for simultaneous predictions, and soft physics constraints for mass conservation.
AI.result: Design-aware models reduce VFM error 13×; physics constraints cut negative flows 65%; flow regime accuracy 97.7%; real-world transfer: oil R²=0.89, BHP R²=0.98, water R²=0.97; 1000× speedup for design optimization.
AI.conclusion: Design awareness, physics enforcement, and multi-task learning are essential and complementary for foundation models operating across large well portfolios.

title: Aligning with Your Own Voice: Self-Corrected Preference Learning for Hallucination Mitigation in LVLMs
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) frequently suffer from hallucinations. Existing preference learning-based approaches largely rely on proprietary models to construct preference datasets. We identify that this reliance introduces a distributional mismatch between the proprietary and target models that hinders efficient alignment. To address this, we propose Alignment via VErified Self-correction DPO (AVES-DPO), a framework that aligns LVLMs using in-distribution data derived from the model's intrinsic knowledge. Our approach employs a consensus-based verification mechanism to diagnose diverse hallucinations and guides the model to self-correct, thereby generating preference pairs strictly compatible with its internal distribution. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AVES-DPO surpasses existing baselines in hallucination mitigation while requiring only 5.2k samples.

AI.tldr: AVES-DPO uses self-correction and consensus-based verification to reduce LVLM hallucinations with only 5.2k samples, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing preference learning for LVLMs relies on proprietary models, causing distribution mismatch that hinders efficient hallucination alignment.
AI.method: Consensus-based verification mechanism diagnoses hallucinations, then guides the model to self-correct, generating in-distribution preference pairs for DPO alignment.
AI.result: AVES-DPO surpasses existing baselines in hallucination mitigation using only 5.2k samples.
AI.conclusion: In-distribution preference data from self-correction effectively reduces hallucinations without relying on external proprietary models.

title: Learn&Drop: Fast Learning of CNNs based on Layer Dropping
comment: Preprint. Paper accepted to Springer Neural Computing and Applications
summary: This paper proposes a new method to improve the training efficiency of deep convolutional neural networks. During training, the method evaluates scores to measure how much each layer's parameters change and whether the layer will continue learning or not. Based on these scores, the network is scaled down such that the number of parameters to be learned is reduced, yielding a speed up in training. Unlike state-of-the-art methods that try to compress the network to be used in the inference phase or to limit the number of operations performed in the backpropagation phase, the proposed method is novel in that it focuses on reducing the number of operations performed by the network in the forward propagation during training. The proposed training strategy has been validated on two widely used architecture families: VGG and ResNet. Experiments on MNIST, CIFAR-10 and Imagenette show that, with the proposed method, the training time of the models is more than halved without significantly impacting accuracy. The FLOPs reduction in the forward propagation during training ranges from 17.83\% for VGG-11 to 83.74\% for ResNet-152. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed technique in speeding up learning of CNNs. The technique will be especially useful in applications where fine-tuning or online training of convolutional models is required, for instance because data arrive sequentially.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method to speed up CNN training by scoring layer parameter changes and selectively scaling down the network during training, reducing forward propagation operations. The technique halves training time on VGG and ResNet across MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Imagenette with minimal accuracy loss, achieving up to 83.74% FLOPs reduction for ResNet-152.
AI.motivation: To accelerate the training phase of deep CNNs by reducing forward propagation operations, unlike existing methods that focus on compressing networks for inference or limiting backward propagation operations.
AI.method: The method evaluates scores during training to measure layer parameter changes and predict whether a layer will continue learning. Based on these scores, the network is dynamically scaled down during training, reducing the number of parameters learned and thus speeding up forward propagation.
AI.result: Training time is more than halved across VGG and ResNet architectures on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Imagenette without significant accuracy loss. FLOPs reduction in forward propagation ranges from 17.83% (VGG-11) to 83.74% (ResNet-152).
AI.conclusion: The proposed technique effectively speeds up CNN training, particularly useful for fine-tuning or online training scenarios where data arrives sequentially.

title: A General Representation-Based Approach to Multi-Source Domain Adaptation
summary: A central problem in unsupervised domain adaptation is determining what to transfer from labeled source domains to an unlabeled target domain. To handle high-dimensional observations (e.g., images), a line of approaches use deep learning to learn latent representations of the observations, which facilitate knowledge transfer in the latent space. However, existing approaches often rely on restrictive assumptions to establish identifiability of the joint distribution in the target domain, such as independent latent variables or invariant label distributions, limiting their real-world applicability. In this work, we propose a general domain adaptation framework that learns compact latent representations to capture distribution shifts relative to the prediction task and address the fundamental question of what representations should be learned and transferred. Notably, we first demonstrate that learning representations based on all the predictive information, i.e., the label's Markov blanket in terms of the learned representations, is often underspecified in general settings. Instead, we show that, interestingly, general domain adaptation can be achieved by partitioning the representations of Markov blanket into those of the label's parents, children, and spouses. Moreover, its identifiability guarantee can be established. Building on these theoretical insights, we develop a practical, nonparametric approach for domain adaptation in a general setting, which can handle different types of distribution shifts.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a general unsupervised domain adaptation framework that identifies and transfers specific latent representations (label's parents, children, and spouses in the Markov blanket) rather than all predictive information, providing identifiability guarantees and a practical nonparametric method.
AI.motivation: Existing unsupervised domain adaptation methods often rely on restrictive assumptions (e.g., independent latent variables or invariant label distributions) to establish identifiability, limiting real-world applicability. The authors aim to address what representations should be learned and transferred in a more general setting.
AI.method: The paper first shows that learning representations based on all predictive information (the label's Markov blanket) is underspecified. Then it proves that partitioning the Markov blanket into label's parents, children, and spouses enables general domain adaptation with identifiability guarantees. A nonparametric approach is developed based on these theoretical insights.
AI.result: The proposed framework provides identifiability guarantees for domain adaptation in a general setting, handling different types of distribution shifts without restrictive assumptions on latent variable independence or label distribution invariance.
AI.conclusion: General unsupervised domain adaptation can be achieved by focusing on specific causal substructures (parents, children, spouses) within the Markov blanket rather than all predictive information, providing both theoretical guarantees and practical applicability.

title: PhysNote: Self-Knowledge Notes for Evolvable Physical Reasoning in Vision-Language Model
comment: 11 pages. Accepted by ICLR 2026 Workshop ES-Reasoning
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on textbook-style physics problems, yet they frequently fail when confronted with dynamic real-world scenarios that require temporal consistency and causal reasoning across frames. We identify two fundamental challenges underlying these failures: (1) spatio-temporal identity drift, where objects lose their physical identity across successive frames and break causal chains, and (2) volatility of inference-time insights, where a model may occasionally produce correct physical reasoning but never consolidates it for future reuse. To address these challenges, we propose PhysNote, an agentic framework that enables VLMs to externalize and refine physical knowledge through self-generated "Knowledge Notes." PhysNote stabilizes dynamic perception through spatio-temporal canonicalization, organizes self-generated insights into a hierarchical knowledge repository, and drives an iterative reasoning loop that grounds hypotheses in visual evidence before consolidating verified knowledge. Experiments on PhysBench demonstrate that PhysNote achieves 56.68% overall accuracy, a 4.96% improvement over the best multi-agent baseline, with consistent gains across all four physical reasoning domains.

AI.tldr: PhysNote is an agentic framework that improves VLMs' physical reasoning in dynamic visual scenarios by using self-generated "Knowledge Notes" to stabilize object identities across frames and consolidate inference insights, achieving 56.68% accuracy on PhysBench with a 4.96% improvement over baselines.
AI.motivation: VLMs perform well on textbook physics but fail in dynamic real-world scenarios due to spatio-temporal identity drift (objects losing identity across frames) and volatility of inference-time insights (occasional correct reasoning not being reused).
AI.method: PhysNote externalizes and refines physical knowledge through self-generated "Knowledge Notes," stabilizes perception via spatio-temporal canonicalization, organizes insights into a hierarchical knowledge repository, and uses an iterative reasoning loop that grounds hypotheses in visual evidence before consolidating verified knowledge.
AI.result: 56.68% overall accuracy on PhysBench, a 4.96% improvement over the best multi-agent baseline, with consistent gains across all four physical reasoning domains.
AI.conclusion: Externalizing and consolidating physical knowledge through structured note-taking and iterative verification significantly improves VLMs' dynamic physical reasoning capabilities.

title: PushupBench: Your VLM is not good at counting pushups
summary: Large vision-language models (VLMs) can recognize \textit{what} happens in video but fail to count \textit{how many} times. We introduce \textbf{PushupBench}, 446 long-form clips (avg. 36.7s) for evaluating repetition counting. The best frontier model achieves 42.1\% exact accuracy; open-source 4B models score $\sim$6\%, matching supervised baselines. We show that accuracy alone misleads -- weaker models exploit the modal count rather than reason temporally. Fine-tuning on counting with 1k samples transfers to general video understanding: MVBench (+2.15), PerceptionTest (+1.88), TVBench (+4.54), suggesting counting is a proxy for broader temporal reasoning.PushupBench incorporated in \texttt{lmms-eval} (https://github.com/EvolvingLMMs-Lab/lmms-eval/pull/1262) and hosted on (pushupbench.com/)

AI.tldr: PushupBench is a new benchmark of 446 long-form video clips for evaluating repetition counting in VLMs. The best model achieves only 42.1% exact accuracy, and accuracy alone is misleading as weaker models exploit modal counts. Fine-tuning on counting (1k samples) improves general video understanding benchmarks, suggesting counting is a proxy for temporal reasoning.
AI.motivation: Large vision-language models (VLMs) fail to count repetitive actions in videos, revealing a gap in temporal reasoning capabilities. The authors aim to benchmark and highlight this deficiency.
AI.method: The authors introduce PushupBench, a benchmark of 446 long-form video clips (avg. 36.7s). They evaluate frontier VLMs and open-source models, and fine-tune on counting tasks to measure transfer to general video understanding benchmarks (MVBench, PerceptionTest, TVBench).
AI.result: Best frontier model achieves 42.1% exact accuracy. Open-source 4B models score ~6%. Weak models exploit the modal count rather than reason temporally. Fine-tuning on counting (1k samples) transfers to MVBench (+2.15), PerceptionTest (+1.88), TVBench (+4.54).
AI.conclusion: Counting repetitive actions is a challenging but useful proxy for broader temporal reasoning in VLMs. Accuracy alone is misleading; counting ability improves general video understanding performance.

title: ELSA: Exact Linear-Scan Attention for Fast and Memory-Light Vision Transformers
summary: Existing attention accelerators often trade exact softmax semantics, depend on fused Tensor Core kernels, or incur sequential depth that limits FP32 throughput on long sequences. We present \textbf{ELSA}, an algorithmic reformulation of online softmax attention that (i)~preserves exact softmax semantics in real arithmetic with a \emph{provable} $\mathcal{O}(u\log n)$ FP32 relative error bound; (ii)~casts the online softmax update as a prefix scan over an associative monoid $(m,S,W)$, yielding $O(n)$ extra memory and $O(\log n)$ parallel depth; and (iii)~is Tensor-Core independent, implemented in Triton and CUDA C++, and deployable as a \emph{drop-in replacement} requiring no retraining or weight modification. Unlike FlashAttention-2/3, which rely on HMMA/GMMA Tensor Core instructions and provide no compatible FP32 path, ELSA operates identically on A100s and resource-constrained edge devices such as Jetson TX2 -- making it the only hardware-agnostic exact-attention kernel that reduces parallel depth to $O(\log n)$ at full precision. On A100 FP32 benchmarks (1K--16K tokens), ELSA delivers $1.3$--$3.5\times$ speedup over memory-efficient SDPA and $1.97$--$2.27\times$ on BERT; on Jetson TX2, ELSA achieves $1.5$--$1.6\times$ over Math (64--900 tokens), with $17.8$--$20.2\%$ throughput gains under LLaMA-13B offloading at $\ge$32K. In FP16, ELSA approaches hardware-fused baselines at long sequences while retaining full FP32 capability, offering a unified kernel for high-precision inference across platforms. Our code and implementation are available at https://github.com/ming053l/ELSA.

AI.tldr: ELSA reformulates online softmax attention using an associative prefix scan monoid (m,S,W) to achieve exact softmax semantics, O(log n) parallel depth, and hardware-agnostic performance without Tensor Cores, delivering 1.3-3.5× speedup over SDPA on A100 FP32 and 1.5-1.6× on Jetson TX2.
AI.motivation: Existing attention accelerators either trade exact softmax semantics, depend on fused Tensor Core kernels (limiting deployment), or incur sequential depth that limits FP32 throughput on long sequences. ELSA aims to provide a hardware-agnostic, exact-softmax attention kernel with reduced parallel depth.
AI.method: ELSA reformulates online softmax attention as a prefix scan over an associative monoid (m,S,W), where m is the running maximum, S is the sum of exponentials, and W is the weighted sum. This casting enables O(log n) parallel depth via prefix scan operations, preserves exact softmax semantics with a provable O(u log n) FP32 relative error bound, and is implemented in Triton/CUDA C++ without Tensor Core dependencies.
AI.result: On A100 FP32 (1K-16K tokens): 1.3-3.5× speedup over SDPA, 1.97-2.27× on BERT. On Jetson TX2: 1.5-1.6× over Math (64-900 tokens), 17.8-20.2% throughput gains under LLaMA-13B offloading at ≥32K. FP16 approaches hardware-fused baselines at long sequences.
AI.conclusion: ELSA is a unified, hardware-agnostic exact-attention kernel that reduces parallel depth to O(log n) at full precision, works on both A100s and edge devices like Jetson TX2, and requires no retraining or weight modification, making it suitable for high-precision inference across diverse platforms.

title: Agentic clinical reasoning over longitudinal myeloma records: a retrospective evaluation against expert consensus
summary: Multiple myeloma is managed through sequential lines of therapy over years to decades, with each decision depending on cumulative disease history distributed across dozens to hundreds of heterogeneous clinical documents. Whether LLM-based systems can synthesise this evidence at a level approaching expert agreement has not been established. A retrospective evaluation was conducted on longitudinal clinical records of 811 myeloma patients treated at a tertiary centre (2001-2026), covering 44,962 documents and 1,334,677 laboratory values, with external validation on MIMIC-IV. An agentic reasoning system was compared against single-pass retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), iterative RAG, and full-context input on 469 patient-question pairs from 48 templates at three complexity levels. Reference labels came from double annotation by four oncologists with senior haematologist adjudication. Iterative RAG and full-context input converged on a shared ceiling (75.4% vs 75.8%, p = 1.00). The agentic system reached 79.6% concordance (95% CI 76.4-82.8), exceeding both baselines (+3.8 and +4.2 pp; p = 0.006 and 0.007). Gains rose with question complexity, reaching +9.4 pp on criteria-based synthesis (p = 0.032), and with record length, reaching +13.5 pp in the top decile (n = 10). The system error rate (12.2%) was comparable to expert disagreement (13.6%), but severity was inverted: 57.8% of system errors were clinically significant versus 18.8% of expert disagreements. Agentic reasoning was the only approach to exceed the shared ceiling, with gains concentrated on the most complex questions and longest records. The greater clinical consequence of residual system errors indicates that prospective evaluation in routine care is required before these findings translate into patient benefit.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based agentic reasoning system achieved 79.6% concordance with expert oncologists in synthesizing longitudinal clinical records of 811 multiple myeloma patients, outperforming RAG baselines by ~4 percentage points, especially on complex questions and long records, though system errors were more clinically severe than expert disagreements.
AI.motivation: To determine whether LLM-based systems can synthesize distributed, heterogeneous clinical evidence from decades of multiple myeloma treatment records at a level approaching expert agreement, which has not been previously established.
AI.method: Retrospective evaluation of 811 myeloma patients (44,962 documents, 1,334,677 lab values) comparing agentic reasoning against single-pass RAG, iterative RAG, and full-context input on 469 patient-question pairs across 48 templates at three complexity levels, with double annotation by four oncologists and senior haematologist adjudication as reference.
AI.result: Agentic reasoning reached 79.6% concordance, exceeding iterative RAG (75.4%) and full-context (75.8%) baselines. Gains increased with question complexity (+9.4 pp on criteria-based synthesis) and record length (+13.5 pp in top decile). System error rate (12.2%) approximated expert disagreement (13.6%), but 57.8% of system errors were clinically significant vs. 18.8% for experts.
AI.conclusion: Agentic reasoning is the only approach exceeding the shared ceiling of simpler methods, with gains concentrated on complex/long-record cases. However, the higher clinical severity of system errors necessitates prospective evaluation in routine care before clinical translation.

title: A Heterogeneous Two-Stream Framework for Video Action Recognition with Comparative Fusion Analysis
summary: Most two-stream action recognition networks apply the same convolutional backbone to both RGB and optical flow streams, ignoring the fact that the two modalities have fundamentally different structural properties. Optical flow captures fine-grained motion patterns, while RGB frames carry rich appearance and scene context - treating them identically discards this distinction. We propose DualStreamHybrid, a heterogeneous two-stream architecture that assigns each stream a backbone suited to its input: a pretrained ViT-Tiny/16 for RGB frames, and a MobileNetV2 trained from scratch on a 20-channel stacked optical flow representation. A learned projection layer maps the two differently-sized feature vectors to a common dimensionality before fusion, enabling the two streams to interact without forcing architectural symmetry. We design five fusion strategies within a unified framework - late fusion, concatenation, cross-attention, weighted fusion, and gated fusion - and evaluate them on UCF11 (1,600 videos, 11 classes) and UCF50 (6,681 videos, 50 classes) to study how fusion behaviour scales with dataset size. On UCF11, cross-attention achieves 98.12% test accuracy, outperforming the RGB-only ViT-Tiny baseline of 95.94%, which suggests that explicit inter-modal attention is particularly effective on smaller, less complex datasets. On UCF50, weighted fusion reaches 96.86% and proves the most consistent strategy across both benchmarks. The learned stream weights reveal an interesting pattern: UCF11 sees near-equal modality contribution (RGB: 0.507, flow: 0.493), while UCF50 favours the RGB stream slightly more (RGB: 0.554, flow: 0.446) - arguably reflecting the larger and more visually diverse action space. Taken together, these results suggest that even a lightweight motion stream meaningfully complements a strong appearance encoder, and that the optimal fusion strategy depends on dataset scale.

AI.tldr: DualStreamHybrid proposes a heterogeneous two-stream architecture using ViT-Tiny for RGB and MobileNetV2 for optical flow, with learned projection and five fusion strategies. Cross-attention achieves 98.12% on UCF11, while weighted fusion reaches 96.86% on UCF50, showing dataset scale affects optimal fusion choice.
AI.motivation: Existing two-stream action recognition networks use identical backbones for RGB and optical flow, ignoring their fundamentally different structural properties (fine-grained motion vs. rich appearance). This work aims to properly distinguish and exploit these modalities.
AI.method: A heterogeneous two-stream architecture: pretrained ViT-Tiny/16 for RGB, MobileNetV2 trained from scratch on 20-channel stacked optical flow. A learned projection layer unifies feature dimensions, and five fusion strategies (late fusion, concatenation, cross-attention, weighted fusion, gated fusion) are evaluated within a unified framework.
AI.result: On UCF11, cross-attention achieves 98.12% (vs. RGB-only 95.94%). On UCF50, weighted fusion reaches 96.86%. Stream weights show near-equal contribution on UCF11 (RGB:0.507, flow:0.493), but RGB slightly favored on UCF50 (RGB:0.554, flow:0.446).
AI.conclusion: A lightweight motion stream meaningfully complements a strong appearance encoder. The optimal fusion strategy depends on dataset scale: cross-attention excels on smaller datasets, while weighted fusion is more consistent across both benchmarks.

title: Causal Representation Learning from General Environments under Nonparametric Mixing
comment: Accepted to AISTATS 2025. This is a slightly revised version of the published paper
summary: Causal representation learning aims to recover the latent causal variables and their causal relations, typically represented by directed acyclic graphs (DAGs), from low-level observations such as image pixels. A prevailing line of research exploits multiple environments, which assume how data distributions change, including single-node interventions, coupled interventions, or hard interventions, or parametric constraints on the mixing function or the latent causal model, such as linearity. Despite the novelty and elegance of the results, they are often violated in real problems. Accordingly, we formalize a set of desiderata for causal representation learning that applies to a broader class of environments, referred to as general environments. Interestingly, we show that one can fully recover the latent DAG and identify the latent variables up to minor indeterminacies under a nonparametric mixing function and nonlinear latent causal models, such as additive (Gaussian) noise models or heteroscedastic noise models, by properly leveraging sufficient change conditions on the causal mechanisms up to third-order derivatives. These represent, to our knowledge, the first results to fully recover the latent DAG from general environments under nonparametric mixing. Notably, our results match or improve upon many existing works, but require less restrictive assumptions about changing environments.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that under nonparametric mixing and nonlinear latent causal models, one can fully recover latent DAGs and identify variables from general environments using sufficient change conditions up to third-order derivatives, requiring less restrictive assumptions than prior work.
AI.motivation: Existing causal representation learning methods rely on restrictive assumptions (e.g., specific intervention types, linearity) that are often violated in real-world problems. The authors aim to formalize a broader class of "general environments" that relaxes these constraints.
AI.method: The authors formalize a set of desiderata for general environments and leverage sufficient change conditions on causal mechanisms up to third-order derivatives to recover latent DAGs under nonparametric mixing and nonlinear latent causal models (e.g., additive noise models, heteroscedastic noise models).
AI.result: The paper achieves full recovery of latent DAGs and identification of latent variables (up to minor indeterminacies) under nonparametric mixing functions and nonlinear latent causal models from general environments. These are the first results of their kind.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach matches or improves upon many existing works while requiring less restrictive assumptions about changing environments, offering a more practical framework for causal representation learning in real-world settings.

title: MIMIC: A Generative Multimodal Foundation Model for Biomolecules
summary: Biological function emerges from coupled constraints across sequence, structure, regulation, evolution, and cellular context, yet most foundation models in biology are trained within one modality or for a fixed forward task. We present MIMIC, a generative multimodal foundation model trained on our newly curated and aligned dataset, LORE, linking nucleic acid, protein, evolutionary, structural, regulatory, and semantic/contextual modalities within partially observed biomolecular states. MIMIC uses a split-track encoder-decoder architecture to condition on arbitrary subsets of observed modalities and reconstruct or generate missing components of molecular state across the genome, transcriptome, and proteome. Multimodal conditioning consistently improves MIMIC's sequence reconstruction relative to sequence-only inputs, while its learned representations enable state-of-the-art performance on RNA and protein downstream tasks. MIMIC achieves state-of-the-art splicing prediction, and its joint generative formulation enables isoform-aware inference that further improves performance. Beyond prediction, the same generative framework supports constrained design. For RNA, MIMIC identifies corrective edits in a clinically relevant HBB splice-disrupting mutation without reverting it by using evolutionary and structural signals. For proteins, jointly conditioning on shape and surface chemistry of PD-L1 and hACE2 binding sites produces diverse, high-confidence sequences with strong in silico support for target binding. Finally, MIMIC uses experimental context as semantic conditioning to model assay-dependent RNA chemical probing, rather than treating context as a fixed output. Together, these results position MIMIC's aligned multimodal generative modeling as a strong foundation for unifying representation learning, conditional prediction, and constrained biomolecular design within a single model.

AI.tldr: MIMIC is a generative multimodal foundation model trained on LORE, an aligned dataset linking nucleic acid, protein, evolutionary, structural, regulatory, and semantic modalities. Using a split-track encoder-decoder, it conditions on arbitrary observed modalities to reconstruct missing molecular components, achieving SOTA on RNA/protein tasks, splicing prediction, and enabling constrained design (e.g., corrective RNA edits, protein binding site design).
AI.motivation: Biological function emerges from coupled constraints across multiple modalities (sequence, structure, regulation, evolution, cellular context), yet most foundation models are trained within one modality or for fixed forward tasks. The authors aim to unify these modalities in a single generative model.
AI.method: MIMIC uses a split-track encoder-decoder architecture trained on LORE, a curated aligned multimodal dataset. The model conditions on arbitrary subsets of observed modalities (nucleic acid, protein, evolutionary, structural, regulatory, semantic/contextual) to reconstruct or generate missing components of molecular state across genome, transcriptome, and proteome.
AI.result: Multimodal conditioning improves sequence reconstruction over sequence-only inputs. SOTA on RNA and protein downstream tasks, including SOTA splicing prediction with isoform-aware inference. For design: identifies corrective edits in HBB splice-disrupting mutation without reversion; generates diverse sequences for PD-L1 and hACE2 binding sites with strong in silico binding support. Models assay-dependent RNA chemical probing via semantic conditioning.
AI.conclusion: MIMIC's aligned multimodal generative modeling provides a strong foundation for unifying representation learning, conditional prediction, and constrained biomolecular design within a single model.

title: Enhanced Privacy and Communication Efficiency in Non-IID Federated Learning with Adaptive Quantization and Differential Privacy
comment: Published in IEEE Access, Vol. 13, 2025. DOI: 10.1109/ACCESS.2025.3554138 Github: https://github.com/eardic/FL_DPQS
summary: Federated learning (FL) is a distributed machine learning method where multiple devices collaboratively train a model under the management of a central server without sharing underlying data. One of the key challenges of FL is the communication bottleneck caused by variations in connection speed and bandwidth across devices. Therefore, it is essential to reduce the size of transmitted data during training. Additionally, there is a potential risk of exposing sensitive information through the model or gradient analysis during training. To address both privacy and communication efficiency, we combine differential privacy (DP) and adaptive quantization methods. We use Laplacian-based DP to preserve privacy, which is relatively underexplored in FL and offers tighter privacy guarantees than Gaussian-based DP. We propose a simple and efficient global bit-length scheduler using round-based cosine annealing, along with a client-based scheduler that dynamically adapts based on client contribution estimated through dataset entropy analysis. We evaluate our approach through extensive experiments on CIFAR10, MNIST, and medical imaging datasets, using non-IID data distributions across varying client counts, bit-length schedulers, and privacy budgets. The results show that our adaptive quantization methods reduce total communicated data by up to 52.64% for MNIST, 45.06% for CIFAR10, and 31% to 37% for medical imaging datasets compared to 32-bit float training while maintaining competitive model accuracy and ensuring robust privacy through differential privacy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes combining Laplacian-based differential privacy with adaptive quantization (using round-based cosine annealing and client-based dataset entropy scheduling) to simultaneously improve communication efficiency and privacy in federated learning, achieving up to 52.64% data reduction on MNIST while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address two key challenges in federated learning: the communication bottleneck caused by varying device bandwidths, and the privacy risk of exposing sensitive information through model/gradient analysis.
AI.method: Combines Laplacian-based differential privacy (offering tighter guarantees than Gaussian DP) with adaptive quantization using a global round-based cosine annealing scheduler and a client-based scheduler that adapts based on dataset entropy analysis of client contributions.
AI.result: Achieved up to 52.64% (MNIST), 45.06% (CIFAR10), and 31-37% (medical imaging) reduction in total communicated data compared to 32-bit float training, while maintaining competitive accuracy and ensuring robust privacy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive quantization with Laplacian DP effectively reduces communication costs and preserves privacy in FL across diverse datasets and non-IID settings.

title: Reparameterization through Coverings and Topological Weight Priors
summary: We generalise the reparameterization trick applied in variational autoencoders (VAEs) letting these have latent spaces of non-trivial topology - i.e. that of base manifolds covered with other ones, on which some technique for RT is available. That is possible since covering maps are measurable - moreover, in case of particular measure preservation property holding for the covering, one can establish an inequality on KL-divergence between pushforward (PF) densities on the base latent manifold, making the KL-term of VAE's ELBO analytically tractable, despite the topological non-triviality of the supporting latent manifold. Our development follows a route close but somewhat alternative to reparameterization on Lie groups, the latest proposal for which is to reparameterize PFs of normal densities from the Lie algebra - "through" the exponential map, seen by us as sometimes a particular case of what we propose to call reparameterization through a covering. Covering maps need not be global diffeomorphisms (although Lie-exp maps, in general, need not either, but, to date only smooth ones were considered in this context, to the best of our knowledge), which makes many non-trivial topologies tamable to our proposed technique, that we detail on a particular such example. We demonstrate the working of our approach by constructing a VAE with the latent space of Klein bottle (not a Lie group) topology, which we call KleinVAE, successfully learning an appropriate artificial dataset. We discuss potential applicability of such topology-informed generative models as weight priors in Bayesian learning, particularly for convolutional vision models, where said manifold was peculiarly shown to have some relevance.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the reparameterization trick in VAEs to latent spaces with non-trivial topologies by using covering maps, enabling KL-divergence computation without requiring global diffeomorphisms. They demonstrate this with a Klein bottle latent space (KleinVAE) and discuss applications in Bayesian learning for convolutional models.
AI.motivation: Standard VAEs assume latent spaces with trivial topology (e.g., Euclidean). Many real-world data distributions have non-trivial topological structure, and the authors aim to enable VAEs to learn such structures without sacrificing tractability of the ELBO KL-term.
AI.method: The method uses covering maps (measurable functions) from a simpler space to the target latent manifold. When the covering map preserves a measure property, the KL-divergence between pushforward densities on the base manifold becomes analytically tractable. This generalizes Lie-group-based reparameterization, as covering maps need not be global diffeomorphisms.
AI.result: The authors construct KleinVAE, a VAE with a Klein bottle latent space (a non-Lie-group topology), and successfully learn an appropriate artificial dataset, demonstrating that non-trivial topologies are indeed tamable via their covering map approach.
AI.conclusion: The covering map reparameterization trick opens up VAEs to latent spaces of non-trivial topology. Potential applications include weight priors in Bayesian learning for convolutional vision models, where the Klein bottle topology has shown some relevance.

title: Beyond the Attention Stability Boundary: Agentic Self-Synthesizing Reasoning Protocols
summary: As LLM agents transition to autonomous digital coworkers, maintaining deterministic goal-directedness in non-linear multi-turn conversations emerged as an architectural bottleneck. We identify and formalize a systemic failure mode termed the Attention Latch in decoder-only autoregressive Transformers. This phenomenon, a behavioral manifestation of Information Over-squashing, occurs when the cumulative probabilistic weight of historical context overrides mid-task updates, causing agents to remain anchored to obsolete constraints despite explicit contradictory instructions. We propose Self-Synthesizing Reasoning Protocols (SSRP), a metacognitive framework that implements a discrete separation between high-level architectural planning (Architect) and turn-by-turn procedural execution (Executive). We evaluate SSRP across 9K trajectories using the MultiWOZ 2.2 dataset and the Aggregate Pivot Accuracy (APA), a novel metric we validate by mapping its scores to the U-shaped 'Lost in the Middle' curve. We present 3 experimental tiers: a shallow recency-based retrieval pilot, a high-entropy SOP, and a semantic hijacked 3-hop Multi-Fact Synthesis task. Our results empirically locate the Attention Stability Boundary, where stateless Vanilla ReAct baselines for GPT 5.4 collapse to 0.1% success while SSRP achieves a 715X Resilience Lift. We demonstrate statistically significant gains across Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.6 and DeepSeek V3.2. Audits confirm SSRP necessity by proving attentional lapse via a recursive reflexion baseline (100% success); decoupling the latch from positional bias through equidistant stress testing (90% accuracy); and formalizing SSRP via the Information Bottleneck principle and granularity ablations. Procedural Integrity audit (98.8% adherence) reveals a Grounding Paradox where high-stability models fail by refusing to hallucinate under retrieval-reasoning contamination.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies the "Attention Latch" failure mode in LLM agents—where models get stuck on outdated context—and proposes Self-Synthesizing Reasoning Protocols (SSRP), a metacognitive framework separating high-level planning from execution. SSRP achieves 715X resilience improvement over baselines in complex multi-turn tasks.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle to maintain goal-directed behavior in non-linear multi-turn conversations due to an architectural bottleneck in decoder-only Transformers, where historical probabilistic weight overrides new instructions.
AI.method: Proposes SSRP, a metacognitive framework with discrete separation between Architect (high-level planning) and Executive (turn-by-turn execution). Evaluated using MultiWOZ 2.2 dataset across 9K trajectories with the novel Aggregate Pivot Accuracy (APA) metric.
AI.result: Vanilla ReAct baselines (GPT 5.4) collapse to 0.1% success while SSRP achieves 715X Resilience Lift. Statistically significant gains across Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and DeepSeek V3.2. Procedural Integrity audit at 98.8% adherence.
AI.conclusion: SSRP effectively decouples the Attention Latch from positional bias; reveals a Grounding Paradox where high-stability models fail by refusing to hallucinate under retrieval-reasoning contamination.

title: Sphere-Depth: A Benchmark for Depth Estimation Methods with Varying Spherical Camera Orientations
summary: Reliable depth estimation from spherical images is crucial for 360° vision in robotic navigation and immersive scene understanding. However, the onboard spherical camera can experience unintentional pose variations in real-world robotic platforms that, along with the geometric distortions inherent in equirectangular projections, significantly impact the effectiveness of depth estimation. To study this issue, a novel public benchmark, called Sphere-Depth, is introduced to systematically evaluate the robustness of monocular depth estimation models from equirectangular images in a reproducible way. Camera pose perturbations are simulated and used to assess the performance of a popular perspective-based model, Depth Anything, and of spherical-aware models such as Depth Anywhere, ACDNet, Bifuse++, and SliceNet. Furthermore, to ensure meaningful evaluation across models, a depth calibration-based error protocol is proposed to convert predicted relative depth values into metric depth values using supervised learned scaling factors for each model. Experiments show that even models explicitly designed to process spherical images exhibit substantial performance degradation when variations in the camera pose are observed with respect to the canonical pose. The full benchmark, evaluation protocol, and dataset splits are made publicly available at: https://github.com/sgazzeh/Sphere_depth

AI.tldr: Sphere-Depth benchmark evaluates robustness of monocular depth estimation models on spherical images under camera pose perturbations, showing significant performance degradation even for spherical-aware models.
AI.motivation: To study how unintentional camera pose variations and geometric distortions in equirectangular projections affect depth estimation from spherical cameras in real-world robotic platforms.
AI.method: Introduces Sphere-Depth benchmark with simulated camera pose perturbations, evaluates perspective-based (Depth Anything) and spherical-aware models (Depth Anywhere, ACDNet, Bifuse++, SliceNet), and proposes a depth calibration-based error protocol using supervised learned scaling factors for fair comparison.
AI.result: Even spherical-aware models exhibit substantial performance degradation when camera pose varies from the canonical pose.
AI.conclusion: Camera pose variations significantly impact depth estimation on spherical images, and the benchmark provides a reproducible evaluation framework for future research.

title: Symmetric Equilibrium Propagation for Thermodynamic Diffusion Training
summary: The reverse process in score-based diffusion models is formally equivalent to overdamped Langevin dynamics in a time-dependent energy landscape. In our prior work we showed that a bilinearly-coupled analog substrate can physically realize this dynamics at a projected three-to-four orders of magnitude energy advantage over digital inference by replacing dense skip connections with low-rank inter-module couplings. Whether the \emph{training} loop can be closed on the same substrate -- without routing gradients through an external digital accelerator -- has remained open. We resolve this affirmatively: Equilibrium Propagation applied directly to the bilinear energy yields an unbiased estimator of the denoising score-matching gradient in the zero-nudge limit. For finite nudging we derive a sharp bias bound controlled solely by substrate stiffness, local curvature, and the norm of the loss-gradient signal, with a bilinear-specific corollary showing that one dominant bias term vanishes identically for coupling-parameter updates. Symmetric nudging further upgrades the leading bias from $ \mathcal{O}(β) $ to $ \mathcal{O}(β^2) $ at negligible extra cost. Under realistic finite-relaxation budgets this upgrade is essential, as one-sided EqProp produces anti-correlated gradients while symmetric EqProp yields well-aligned updates. Bias-variance analysis determines the optimal operating point, and end-to-end physical-unit accounting projects a $ 10^3$-$10^4\times $ energy advantage per training step over a matched GPU baseline. Symmetric bilinear EqProp is the first local, readout-only training rule that preserves the low-rank coupling enabling scalable thermodynamic diffusion models.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that Equilibrium Propagation applied to bilinear-coupled substrates can train score-based diffusion models locally and energy-efficiently, achieving 10^3-10^4× energy advantage per training step over GPUs, by proving unbiased gradient estimation in the zero-nudge limit and introducing symmetric nudging to reduce bias from O(β) to O(β²).
AI.motivation: Prior work demonstrated that bilinearly-coupled analog substrates can physically realize score-based diffusion model inference with massive energy savings, but whether training could be done on the same substrate without external digital accelerators remained an open question.
AI.method: The authors apply Equilibrium Propagation directly to the bilinear energy function, deriving the unbiased score-matching gradient estimator in the zero-nudge limit. They analyze finite-nudge bias bounds, introduce symmetric nudging to reduce bias from O(β) to O(β²), and conduct bias-variance analysis to find optimal operating points.
AI.result: Symmetric bilinear EqProp yields unbiased gradient estimates (zero-nudge limit), reduces bias to O(β²) with symmetric nudging, produces well-aligned (not anti-correlated) gradients under finite relaxation budgets, and projects 10^3-10^4× energy advantage per training step over GPU baselines.
AI.conclusion: Symmetric bilinear EqProp is the first local, readout-only training rule that preserves low-rank coupling, enabling scalable thermodynamic diffusion model training on analog substrates without external digital accelerators.

title: Interoceptive machine framework: Toward interoception-inspired regulatory architectures in artificial intelligence
summary: This review proposes an integrative framework grounded on interoception and embodied AI-termed the interoceptive machine framework-that translates biologically inspired principles of internal-state regulation into computational architectures for adaptive autonomy. Interoception, conceived as the monitoring, integration, and regulation of internal signals, has proven relevant for understanding adaptive behavior in biological systems. The proposed framework organizes interoceptive contributions into three functional principles: homeostatic, allostatic, and enactive, each associated with distinct computational roles: internal viability regulation, anticipatory uncertainty-based re-evaluation, and active data generation through interaction. These principles are not intended as direct neurophysiological mappings, but as abstractions that inform the design of artificial agents with improved self-regulation and context-sensitive behavior. By embedding internal state variables and regulatory loops within these principles, AI systems can achieve more robust decision-making, calibrated uncertainty handling, and adaptive interaction strategies, particularly in uncertain and dynamic environments. This approach provides a concrete and testable pathway toward agents capable of functionally grounded self-regulation, with direct implications for human-computer interaction and assistive technologies. Ultimately, the interoceptive machine framework offers a unifying perspective on how internal-state regulation can enhance autonomy, adaptivity, and robustness in embodied AI systems

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an "interoceptive machine framework" that translates biological interoception principles (monitoring internal body states) into computational architectures for embodied AI, organized around three functional principles: homeostatic, allostatic, and enactive.
AI.motivation: To address the need for improved self-regulation and context-sensitive behavior in AI systems, particularly in uncertain and dynamic environments, by drawing inspiration from biological interoception mechanisms.
AI.method: The paper develops an integrative framework based on interoception principles (homeostatic, allostatic, enactive) that serve as computational abstractions—not direct neurophysiological mappings—for embedding internal state variables and regulatory loops into AI architectures.
AI.result: The framework enables AI systems to achieve more robust decision-making, calibrated uncertainty handling, and adaptive interaction strategies in uncertain and dynamic environments.
AI.conclusion: The interoceptive machine framework offers a unifying perspective on how internal-state regulation can enhance autonomy, adaptivity, and robustness in embodied AI, with direct implications for human-computer interaction and assistive technologies.

title: Knee-xRAI: An Explainable AI Framework for Automatic Kellgren-Lawrence Grading of Knee Osteoarthritis
summary: Radiographic grading of knee osteoarthritis (KOA) with the Kellgren-Lawrence (KL) system is limited by inter-reader variability and the opacity of current deep learning approaches, which predict KL grades directly from images without decomposing structural features. We present Knee-xRAI, a modular framework that independently quantifies the three cardinal radiographic features of KOA (joint space narrowing [JSN], osteophytes, and subchondral sclerosis) and integrates them into an explainable KL grade classification. The pipeline combines U-Net++ segmentation for contour-based JSN measurement, an SE-ResNet-50 network for per-site osteophyte grading (OARSI scale), and a hybrid texture-CNN classifier for binary sclerosis quantification. The resulting 50-dimensional structured feature vector feeds two complementary classification paths. An XGBoost path supports SHAP-based feature attribution. A ConvNeXt hybrid path combines the structured vector with a full-image encoder for enhanced predictive performance. Evaluated on 8,260 radiographs from an OAI-derived dataset, the JSN module achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.8909 and an mJSW intraclass correlation of 0.8674 against manual annotations. The ConvNeXt hybrid path reached a test quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.8436 and AUC of 0.9017. The transparent XGBoost path achieved a test QWK of 0.6294 with full feature-level audit capability. Ablation confirmed JSN as the dominant predictor (QWK = 0.6103 alone), with osteophyte features providing consistent incremental gain (+0.0183) and sclerosis contributing marginally. Inference-time ablation of Path B confirmed the structured pathway contributes materially beyond the image encoder, with QWK drops of 0.098 (feature zeroing) and 0.284 (feature-image permutation). Knee-xRAI explicitly quantifies all three KL-defining radiographic features within a single auditable pipeline.

AI.tldr: Knee-xRAI is a modular, explainable deep learning framework for knee osteoarthritis grading that independently quantifies joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and subchondral sclerosis, then integrates them into an auditable KL grade classification with strong performance (QWK 0.8436, AUC 0.9017).
AI.motivation: Current KL grading suffers from inter-reader variability and deep learning opacity—models predict grades directly without decomposing the underlying radiographic features (JSN, osteophytes, sclerosis), limiting interpretability and clinical trust.
AI.method: A modular pipeline: U-Net++ for contour-based JSN measurement, SE-ResNet-50 for per-site osteophyte grading (OARSI), and a hybrid texture-CNN for sclerosis. The resulting 50D feature vector feeds two paths: an XGBoost path (SHAP-explainable) and a ConvNeXt hybrid path that also encodes the full image.
AI.result: On 8,260 OAI radiographs: JSN module Dice=0.8909, mJSW ICC=0.8674. ConvNeXt hybrid path QWK=0.8436, AUC=0.9017. XGBoost path QWK=0.6294 with full auditability. Ablation confirmed JSN as dominant predictor; osteophytes gave +0.0183 gain; sclerosis minimal.
AI.conclusion: Knee-xRAI explicitly quantifies all three KL-defining features within a single auditable pipeline, balancing high predictive performance with full explainability, addressing both opacity and variability limitations of prior approaches.

title: JigsawRL: Assembling RL Pipelines for Efficient LLM Post-Training
summary: We present JigsawRL, a cost-efficient framework that explores Pipeline Multiplexing as a new dimension of RL parallelism. JigsawRL decomposes each pipeline into a Sub-Stage Graph that exposes the intra-stage and inter-worker imbalance hidden by stage-level systems. On this abstraction, JigsawRL resolves multiplexing interference through dynamic resource allocation, eliminates fragmented utilization by migrating long-tail rollouts across workers, and formulates their coordination as a graph scheduling problem solved with a look-ahead heuristic. On 4-64 H100/A100 GPUs across different agentic RL pipelines and models, JigsawRL achieves up to 1.85x throughput over Verl on synchronous RL, 1.54x over StreamRL and AReaL on asynchronous RL, and supports heterogeneous pipelines with moderate latency trade-off.

AI.tldr: JigsawRL introduces Pipeline Multiplexing for RL parallelism, achieving up to 1.85x throughput over Verl and 1.54x over StreamRL/AReaL on 4-64 GPUs by decomposing pipelines into sub-stage graphs, resolving multiplexing interference, and migrating long-tail rollouts.
AI.motivation: Existing RL parallelism frameworks suffer from intra-stage and inter-worker imbalance hidden by stage-level systems, leading to fragmented utilization and multiplexing interference in large-scale training.
AI.method: JigsawRL decomposes pipelines into Sub-Stage Graphs, uses dynamic resource allocation to resolve multiplexing interference, migrates long-tail rollouts to eliminate fragmented utilization, and formulates coordination as a graph scheduling problem solved via look-ahead heuristic.
AI.result: Achieves up to 1.85x throughput over Verl on synchronous RL, 1.54x over StreamRL and AReaL on asynchronous RL, validated on 4-64 H100/A100 GPUs across various agentic RL pipelines and models.
AI.conclusion: Pipeline Multiplexing is a new and effective dimension of RL parallelism; JigsawRL supports heterogeneous pipelines with moderate latency trade-off while significantly improving throughput.

title: STELLAR-E: a Synthetic, Tailored, End-to-end LLM Application Rigorous Evaluator
summary: The increasing reliance on Large Language Models (LLMs) across diverse sectors highlights the need for robust domain-specific and language-specific evaluation datasets; however, the collection of such datasets is challenging due to privacy concerns, regulatory restrictions, and the time cost for manual creation. Existing automated benchmarking methods are often limited by relying on pre-existing data, poor scalability, single-domain focus, and lack of multilingual support. We present STELLAR-E - a fully automated system to generate high-quality synthetic datasets of custom size, using minimal human inputs without depending on existing datasets. The system is structured in two stages: (1) We modify the TGRT Self-Instruct framework to create a synthetic data engine that enables controllable, custom synthetic dataset generation, and (2) an evaluation pipeline incorporating statistical and LLM-based metrics to assess the applicability of the synthetic dataset for LLM-based application evaluations. The synthetic datasets reach an average difference of +5.7% in terms of LLM-as-a-judge scores against existing language-specific benchmarks, demonstrating comparable quality for comprehensive assessment of big and small LLMs. While real datasets remain slightly more challenging for LLMs especially for smaller models, this work establishes a scalable and domain-adaptable benchmarking framework that supports fair evaluation of LLM applications, offering a faster alternative to manual approaches and enabling high-efficiency automated quality assurance cycles.

AI.tldr: STELLAR-E is a fully automated system that generates high-quality synthetic evaluation datasets for LLMs using minimal human input, achieving comparable quality to existing benchmarks with an average score difference of +5.7%.
AI.motivation: The need for robust domain-specific and language-specific evaluation datasets for LLMs is growing, but manual collection is hindered by privacy concerns, regulations, and time costs. Existing automated methods suffer from poor scalability, single-domain focus, and lack of multilingual support.
AI.method: A two-stage system: (1) modifies the TGRT Self-Instruct framework to create a controllable synthetic data engine, and (2) an evaluation pipeline using statistical and LLM-based metrics to assess synthetic dataset applicability.
AI.result: Synthetic datasets achieve an average difference of +5.7% in LLM-as-a-judge scores against existing language-specific benchmarks, demonstrating comparable quality for evaluating both large and small LLMs.
AI.conclusion: STELLAR-E offers a scalable, domain-adaptable benchmarking framework as a faster alternative to manual approaches, enabling high-efficiency automated quality assurance cycles for LLM evaluation, though real datasets remain slightly more challenging for smaller models.

title: Resource-Constrained UAV-Based Weed Detection for Site-Specific Management on Edge Devices
summary: Weeds compete with crops for light, water, and nutrients, reducing yield and crop quality. Efficient weed detection is essential for site-specific weed management (SSWM). Although deep learning models have been deployed on UAV-based edge systems, a systematic understanding of how different model architectures perform under real-world resource constraints is still lacking. To address this gap, this study proposes a deployment-oriented framework for real-time UAV-based weed detection on resource-constrained edge platforms. The framework integrates UAV data acquisition, model development, and on-device inference, with a focus on balancing detection accuracy and computational efficiency. A diverse set of state-of-the-art object detection models is evaluated, including convolution-based YOLO models (v8-v12) and transformer-based RT-DETR models (v1-v2). Experiments on three edge devices (Jetson Orin Nano, Jetson AGX Xavier, and Jetson AGX Orin) demonstrate clear trade-offs between accuracy and inference latency across models and hardware configurations. Results show that high-capacity models achieve up to 86.9% mAP50 but suffer from high latency, limiting real-time deployment. In contrast, lightweight models achieve 66%-71% mAP50 with significantly lower latency, enabling real-time performance. Among all models, RT-DETRv2-R50-M achieves competitive accuracy (79% mAP50) with improved efficiency, while YOLOv10n provides the fastest inference speed. YOLOv11s and RT-DETRv2-R50-M offer the best balance between accuracy and speed, making them strong candidates for real-time UAV deployment.

AI.tldr: This study proposes a deployment-oriented framework for real-time UAV-based weed detection on resource-constrained edge devices, evaluating YOLO (v8-v12) and RT-DETR (v1-v2) models on Jetson platforms, finding that YOLOv11s and RT-DETRv2-R50-M offer the best accuracy-speed trade-off.
AI.motivation: Weeds compete with crops for resources, reducing yield and quality. Efficient weed detection is needed for site-specific weed management, but a systematic understanding of how different deep learning models perform under real-world resource constraints on UAV edge systems is lacking.
