AI.motivation: Existing studies on emergent intelligence rely on empirical observations without a rigorous theoretical framework. This work aims to fill this gap by providing a mathematical formalization.
AI.method: The authors introduce a performance function E(N,P,K) and frame emergence as the limit of this function. They employ nonlinear Lipschitz operator theory to prove necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of a "limit architecture," and derive scaling laws using Lipschitz operators and covering numbers.
AI.result: 1) Emergence depends on training steps, data size, and model architecture (especially basic blocks). 2) The critical condition Lip(T)=1 governs emergence. 3) Emergence is determined by an infinite-dimensional system but realizable via finite-dimensional architectures.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a rigorous theoretical foundation for emergent intelligence, confirming existing empirical findings and offering new insights into scaling laws and architectural design.

title: BVI-Mamba: Video Enhancement Using a Visual State-Space Model for Low-Light and Underwater Environments
summary: Videos captured in low-light and underwater conditions often suffer from distortions such as noise, low contrast, color imbalance, and blur. These issues not only limit visibility but also degrade automatic tasks like detection. Post-processing is typically required but can be time-consuming. AI-based tools for video enhancement also demand significantly more computational resources compared to image-based methods. This paper introduces a novel framework, Visual Mamba, designed to reduce memory usage and computational time by leveraging the Visual State Space (VSS) model. The framework consists of two modules: (i) a feature alignment module, where spatio-temporal displacement between input frames is registered in the feature space, and (ii) an enhancement module, where noise removal and brightness adjustment are performed using a UNet-like architecture, with all convolutional layers replaced by VSS blocks. Experimental results show that the Visual Mamba technique outperforms Transformer and convolution-based models in both low-light and underwater video enhancement tasks. Code is available on line at https://github.com/russellllaputa/BVI-Mamba.

AI.tldr: Visual Mamba is a video enhancement framework using Visual State Space (VSS) models to reduce memory and computation costs while outperforming Transformer/CNN methods in low-light and underwater video enhancement.
AI.motivation: Low-light and underwater videos suffer from noise, low contrast, color imbalance, and blur. Existing AI enhancement tools are computationally expensive and time-consuming, especially for video (vs. images).
AI.method: The framework has two modules: (1) a feature alignment module that registers spatio-temporal displacements between frames; (2) an enhancement module using a UNet-like architecture with all convolutional layers replaced by Visual State Space (VSS) blocks for noise removal and brightness adjustment.
AI.result: Visual Mamba outperforms Transformer and convolution-based models in both low-light and underwater video enhancement tasks, with reduced memory usage and computational time.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Visual Mamba framework effectively addresses video enhancement challenges by leveraging VSS models, offering superior performance with lower computational demands than existing approaches.

title: AgenticCache: Cache-Driven Asynchronous Planning for Embodied AI Agents
summary: Embodied AI agents increasingly rely on large language models (LLMs) for planning, yet per-step LLM calls impose severe latency and cost. In this paper, we show that embodied tasks exhibit strong plan locality, where the next plan is largely predictable from the current one. Building on this, we introduce AgenticCache, a planning framework that reuses cached plans to avoid per-step LLM calls. In AgenticCache, each agent queries a runtime cache of frequent plan transitions, while a background Cache Updater asynchronously calls the LLM to validate and refine cached entries. Across four multi-agent embodied benchmarks, AgenticCache improves task success rate by 22% on average across 12 configurations (4 benchmarks x 3 models), reduces simulation latency by 65%, and lowers token usage by 50%. Cache-based plan reuse thus offers a practical path to low-latency, low-cost embodied agents. Code is available at https://github.com/hojoonleokim/MLSys26_AgenticCache.

AI.tldr: AgenticCache reuses cached plan transitions in embodied AI agents to avoid per-step LLM calls, improving success rate by 22%, reducing latency by 65%, and cutting token usage by 50% across 12 configurations.
AI.motivation: Per-step LLM calls in embodied AI agents cause severe latency and high cost, necessitating a more efficient planning approach.
AI.method: AgenticCache leverages plan locality in embodied tasks by caching frequent plan transitions. A runtime cache is queried per step, while a background Cache Updater asynchronously calls the LLM to validate and refine cached entries.
AI.result: Across four multi-agent embodied benchmarks (12 configurations: 4 benchmarks x 3 models), AgenticCache improves task success rate by 22%, reduces simulation latency by 65%, and lowers token usage by 50% on average.
AI.conclusion: Cache-based plan reuse is a practical approach to achieving low-latency, low-cost embodied agents without sacrificing performance.

title: SolarFCD: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Solar Fault Classification in Photovoltaic Systems
summary: The increasing global deployment of solar photovoltaic (PV) systems needs robust, scalable, and automated inspection technologies capable of detecting a wide range of panel flaws under a variety of operating situations. The lack of large-scale, multi-modal, publicly available annotated datasets is a major obstacle preventing advancement in this field. We introduce SolarFCD, an extensive dataset of solar panel defects created by methodically combining and reconciling three publicly accessible datasets covering two imaging modalities: RGB/Drone images and Thermal Infrared. The dataset consist of 4,435 images arranged under four unified defect classes such as: healthy images, Surface Obstruction, structural fault, and electrical fault. The dataset was divided into training, validation, and test splits at an 80:10:10 ratio through methodical label mapping, near-duplicate removal, and targeted augmentation of minority classes. Sixteen classification architectures from five design families were trained and assessed on the dataset to provide repeatable benchmark baselines. With an accuracy of 86.68%, precision of 88.65%, recall of 88.62%, and F1-score of 88.17%, ResNet101V2 performed the best overall. Per-class results showed balanced detection across all four defect categories within a narrow performance band of less than 1.2 percentage points. To promote open and repeatable research in automated PV inspection and solar energy operations and maintenance, the dataset, annotation files, and baseline code are made openly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SolarFCD, a unified multi-modal dataset of 4,435 solar panel images merging RGB/Drone and Thermal Infrared modalities across four defect classes. Benchmarking 16 classification models shows ResNet101V2 achieves best performance (86.68% accuracy, 88.17% F1-score). Dataset and code are publicly released.
AI.motivation: Current advancement in automated solar panel defect detection is hindered by the lack of large-scale, multi-modal, publicly available annotated datasets.
AI.method: Combined and reconciled three publicly accessible datasets (RGB/Drone + Thermal Infrared), applied label mapping, near-duplicate removal, minority class augmentation, and split 80:10:10. Sixteen classification architectures from five design families were benchmarked.
AI.result: ResNet101V2 achieved best overall performance: accuracy 86.68%, precision 88.65%, recall 88.62%, F1-score 88.17%; per-class performance within 1.2% band.
AI.conclusion: SolarFCD enables open and reproducible research in automated PV inspection; dataset and code are publicly available to advance solar energy operations and maintenance.

title: End-to-End Learning for Partially-Observed Time Series with PyPOTS
summary: Partially-observed time series (POTS) is ubiquitous in real-world applications, yet most existing toolchains separate missing-value handling from downstream learning, which limits reproducibility and overall performance. This tutorial introduces PyPOTS, an open-source Python ecosystem for end-to-end data mining and machine learning on POTS. We present practical workflows spanning missingness simulation, data preprocessing, model training, and evaluation across core tasks, including imputation, forecasting, classification, clustering, and anomaly detection. The tutorial consists of two parts: Part I emphasizes hands-on application for practitioners through unified APIs and benchmark-oriented experiments. Part II targets developers and researchers, focusing on extending PyPOTS with custom models, domain-specific constraints, and contribution-ready engineering practices. Participants will gain both conceptual understanding and implementation experience for building robust, transparent, and reusable POTS pipelines in research and production settings. PyPOTS is publicly available at https://github.com/WenjieDu/PyPOTS

AI.tldr: PyPOTS is an open-source Python ecosystem for end-to-end data mining and machine learning on partially-observed time series, covering imputation, forecasting, classification, clustering, and anomaly detection with unified APIs.
AI.motivation: Existing toolchains separate missing-value handling from downstream learning, limiting reproducibility and overall performance for partially-observed time series (POTS).
AI.method: A two-part tutorial: Part I focuses on hands-on application via unified APIs and benchmark experiments for practitioners; Part II targets developers/researchers on extending PyPOTS with custom models, domain constraints, and contribution-ready practices.
AI.result: PyPOTS provides complete workflows including missingness simulation, data preprocessing, model training, and evaluation across five core tasks (imputation, forecasting, classification, clustering, anomaly detection).
AI.conclusion: Participants gain both conceptual understanding and implementation experience for building robust, transparent, and reusable POTS pipelines in research and production.

title: HAC: Parameter-Efficient Hyperbolic Adaptation of CLIP for Zero-Shot VQA
comment: This is the preprint version of the paper. The final version has been accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026)
summary: Recent advances in representation learning have shown that hyperbolic geometry can offer a more expressive alternative to the Euclidean embeddings used in CLIP models, capturing hierarchical structures and leading to better-organized representations. However, current hyperbolic CLIP variants are trained entirely from scratch, which is computationally expensive and resource-intensive. In this work, we propose HAC (Hyperbolic Adaptation of CLIP), a parameter-efficient framework that enables pretrained CLIP models to transition into hyperbolic space via lightweight fine-tuning. We apply HAC to Visual Question Answering (VQA), where models must interpret visual elements and align them with textual queries. Notably, HAC's training is performed on a dataset with no overlap with any VQA benchmark, resulting in a strict zero-shot evaluation paradigm that underscores HAC's task-agnostic adaptability. We evaluate HAC across a diverse suite of VQA benchmarks spanning General, Reasoning, and OCR categories. Both HAC-S (small) and HAC-B (medium) consistently surpass Euclidean baselines and prior hyperbolic approaches, with HAC-B delivering up to a +1.9 point average improvement over CLIP-B on reasoning-intensive tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/fdibiton/HAC

AI.tldr: HAC introduces a parameter-efficient framework to adapt pretrained Euclidean CLIP models into hyperbolic space via lightweight fine-tuning, achieving superior zero-shot VQA performance, especially on reasoning-intensive tasks, without training on any VQA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Hyperbolic geometry can capture hierarchical structures better than Euclidean space for representation learning, but existing hyperbolic CLIP variants require training from scratch, which is computationally expensive and resource-intensive.
AI.method: HAC (Hyperbolic Adaptation of CLIP) is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework that transitions pretrained CLIP models into hyperbolic space with minimal additional parameters, trained on a dataset with no overlap with any VQA benchmark to enable strict zero-shot evaluation.
AI.result: HAC-S and HAC-B consistently outperform Euclidean baselines and prior hyperbolic approaches; HAC-B achieves up to +1.9 point average improvement over CLIP-B on reasoning-intensive VQA tasks across General, Reasoning, and OCR categories.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight hyperbolic adaptation of pretrained CLIP is an effective, task-agnostic strategy for improving VQA performance without expensive training from scratch or benchmark-specific supervision.

title: Deploy DINO with Many-to-Many Association
summary: Motivated by the limited generalization of supervised image matching models to unseen image domains, we explore the zero-shot deployment of DINO features for this task. The generalist visual representation extracted from DINO has inherent ambiguity when used to match feature points among semantically similar instances, prompting us to adopt a many-to-many (m-to-m) matching paradigm. However, the existing robust mechanism under m-to-m data association is computationally heavy, which requires finding a maximum-cardinality matching in the inlier association graph for each parameter evaluation. To address this inefficiency, we introduce a novel likelihood perspective, which interprets the existing method as a zeroth-order approximation of otherwise intractable likelihood calculation,and inspires us to propose a faster and finer-grained robust mechanism, termed as Harmonic Consensus Maximization (HCM). Take camera pose estimation as an exemplifying downstream task, we demonstrate that general-purpose visual features, used out of the box without any adaptation, can compete with specialized matching models on out-of-distribution datasets when mated with m-to-m association and the HCM mechanism.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Harmonic Consensus Maximization (HCM), a fast robust mechanism for many-to-many matching of DINO features in zero-shot image matching, demonstrating competitive performance on out-of-distribution pose estimation without any domain adaptation.
AI.motivation: Supervised image matching models generalize poorly to unseen image domains, motivating the need for zero-shot deployment using general-purpose DINO features.
AI.method: The authors reinterpret existing robust m-to-m matching as a zeroth-order likelihood approximation and propose Harmonic Consensus Maximization (HCM) for faster, finer-grained robust matching.
AI.result: DINO features with m-to-m matching and HCM compete with specialized matching models on out-of-distribution camera pose estimation datasets without any adaptation.
AI.conclusion: General-purpose visual features (DINO) combined with many-to-many association and HCM can rival specialized models in zero-shot cross-domain matching tasks.

title: FreeScale: Distributed Training for Sequence Recommendation Models with Minimal Scaling Cost
comment: 14 pages, 11 figures. Accepted to the 9th MLSys Conference, Bellevue, WA, USA, 2026
summary: Modern industrial Deep Learning Recommendation Models typically extract user preferences through the analysis of sequential interaction histories, subsequently generating predictions based on these derived interests. The inherent heterogeneity in data characteristics frequently result in substantial under-utilization of computational resources during large-scale training, primarily due to computational bubbles caused by severe stragglers and slow blocking communications. This paper introduces FreeScale, a solution designed to (1) mitigate the straggler problem through meticulously load balanced input samples (2) minimize the blocking communication by overlapping prioritized embedding communications with computations (3) resolve the GPU resource competition during computation and communication overlapping by communicating through SM-Free techniques. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that FreeScale achieves up to 90.3% reduction in computational bubbles when applied to real-world workloads running on 256 H100 GPUs.

AI.tldr: FreeScale reduces computational bubbles by up to 90.3% on 256 H100 GPUs through load-balanced inputs, overlapped embedding communication, and SM-Free techniques for deep learning recommendation models.
AI.motivation: Modern Deep Learning Recommendation Models suffer from severe computational bubbles during large-scale training due to heterogeneous data characteristics causing stragglers and slow blocking communications, leading to substantial GPU resource under-utilization.
AI.method: FreeScale introduces three key techniques: (1) load-balanced input samples to mitigate stragglers, (2) overlapping prioritized embedding communications with computations to minimize blocking communication, and (3) SM-Free (Streaming Multiprocessor-free) techniques to resolve GPU resource competition during computation-communication overlap.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world workloads running on 256 H100 GPUs shows FreeScale achieves up to 90.3% reduction in computational bubbles.
AI.conclusion: FreeScale effectively addresses computational inefficiencies in large-scale recommendation model training by tackling stragglers, blocking communication, and resource contention, yielding significant performance improvements.

title: Learning to Decipher from Pixels -- A Case Study of Copiale
summary: Historical encrypted manuscripts require both paleographic interpretation of cipher symbols and cryptanalytic recovery of plaintext. Most existing computational workflows rely on a transcription-first paradigm, in which handwritten symbols are transcribed prior to decipherment. This intermediate step is labor-intensive, error-prone, and not always aligned with the goal of direct plaintext recovery. We propose an end-to-end, transcription-free approach that directly maps handwritten cipher images to plaintext. Using the Copiale cipher as a case study, we introduce the first text-line-level dataset pairing cipher images with German plaintext. We show that pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by cipher-specific fine-tuning substantially improves decipherment accuracy. Our results demonstrate that transcription-free image-to-plaintext decipherment is both feasible and effective for historical substitution ciphers, offering a simplified and scalable alternative to traditional pipelines. https://github.com/leitro/Decipher-from-Pixels-Copiale

AI.tldr: This paper proposes and demonstrates an end-to-end, transcription-free approach that directly maps handwritten cipher images to plaintext, using the Copiale cipher as a case study. Pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by cipher-specific fine-tuning improves accuracy, offering a scalable alternative to traditional pipelines.
AI.motivation: Traditional decipherment of historical encrypted manuscripts relies on a transcription-first paradigm—transcribing handwritten cipher symbols before decipherment—which is labor-intensive, error-prone, and misaligned with plaintext recovery.
AI.method: An end-to-end image-to-plaintext model that bypasses symbol transcription. The first text-line-level dataset pairing Copiale cipher images with German plaintext was created. Pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by cipher-specific fine-tuning was employed.
AI.result: Pretraining on generic handwriting data followed by cipher-specific fine-tuning substantially improves decipherment accuracy. The transcription-free approach is both feasible and effective for historical substitution ciphers.
AI.conclusion: Transcription-free image-to-plaintext decipherment offers a simplified and scalable alternative to traditional pipelines for historical substitution ciphers.

title: Explaining Temporal Graph Predictions With Shapley Values
summary: Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) have become increasingly popular in recent years due to their superior predictive performance by combining both spatial and temporal information. However, how these models utilize the information to make predictions is rather unexplored, leading to potentially faulty or biased models. This work introduces two novel model-agnostic explainers for local explanations of TGNNs based on Shapley and Owen values. The first method, an event-level (edge-level) Shapley explainer, applies the KernelSHAP algorithm to estimate contribution scores for individual temporal events, providing interpretable descriptions for model behavior. The second, a feature-level Shapley explainer, extends this framework by decomposing event-level Shapley values into Owen values, and thereby uncovers hierarchical dependencies of the event and its features. The explainers outperform SOTA explainers on different metrics and datasets. Additionally, the Feature Explainer reveals a faulty extraction of actual timestamps of a commonly used TGAT implementation, helping to further understand performance drops on very sparse explanations.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes two novel model-agnostic explainers (event-level and feature-level) for Temporal Graph Neural Networks using Shapley and Owen values, which outperform existing methods and reveal implementation flaws in common TGNN models.
AI.motivation: Despite the popularity of TGNNs, how these models utilize spatial and temporal information for predictions remains unexplored, potentially leading to faulty or biased models.
AI.method: Two explainers: (1) Event-level Shapley explainer using KernelSHAP for temporal event contributions; (2) Feature-level Shapley explainer extending to Owen values to uncover hierarchical feature dependencies.
AI.result: Both explainers outperform SOTA methods on various metrics and datasets. The Feature Explainer identified a faulty timestamp extraction in a common TGAT implementation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed explainers provide interpretability for TGNNs, revealing model biases and implementation errors, contributing to more reliable temporal graph analysis.

title: Reading in the Dark: Low-light Scene Text Recognition
summary: Accurate text recognition in low-light environments is essential for intelligent systems in applications ranging from autonomous vehicles to smart surveillance. However, challenges such as poor illumination and noise interference remain underexplored. To address this gap, we introduce LSTR, a large-scale Low-light Scene Text Recognition dataset comprising 11,273 low-light images generated from well-lit datasets (ICDAR2015, IIIT5K, and WordArt), along with ESTR, which includes 60 real nighttime street-scene images in English and Spanish for exclusive evaluation. We explore two solution strategies: (1) employing Optical Character Recognition (OCR) models with fine-tuning and LoRA-based fine-tuning and (2) a joint training strategy that integrates a low-light image enhancement (LLIE) module with an OCR model. In particular, we propose a novel re-render LLIE (RLLIE) module, which demonstrates improved performance on real-world data. Through extensive experimentation, we analyze various training strategies and address a key research question: \emph{How bright is bright enough for effective scene text recognition?} Our results indicate that standalone LLIE or OCR models perform inadequately under low-light conditions, highlighting the advantages of specialized, jointly trained text-centric approaches. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive benchmark to support future research in robust low-light scene text recognition. https://huggingface.co/datasets/lumimusta/Low-light_Scene_Text_Dataset.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LSTR (11,273 low-light images) and ESTR (60 real nighttime scenes) datasets for low-light scene text recognition, proposes a re-render LLIE (RLLIE) module for joint training with OCR, and benchmarks various strategies to determine optimal brightness levels for text recognition.
AI.motivation: Accurate text recognition in low-light environments is critical for autonomous vehicles and smart surveillance, yet challenges like poor illumination and noise interference remain underexplored. No large-scale dedicated dataset or comprehensive benchmark existed for this problem.
AI.method: (1) Created LSTR dataset (11,273 low-light images from ICDAR2015, IIIT5K, WordArt) and ESTR (60 real nighttime street images in English/Spanish). (2) Explored two strategies: OCR with fine-tuning/LoRA, and joint training integrating LLIE + OCR. (3) Proposed a novel re-render LLIE (RLLIE) module for improved real-world performance.
AI.result: Standalone LLIE or OCR models perform poorly under low-light conditions. Jointly trained text-centric approaches, especially with the proposed RLLIE module, show significant improvements. Real-world data benefits more from the re-render approach.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, jointly trained text-centric models are essential for low-light scene text recognition. The LSTR and ESTR datasets, along with comprehensive benchmarks, provide a foundation for future research. The RLLIE module offers a promising direction for handling real-world low-light conditions.

title: Meta-Ensemble Learning with Diverse Data Splits for Improved Respiratory Sound Classification
summary: Training reliable respiratory sound classification models remains challenging due to the limited size and subject diversity of datasets. Ensemble methods can improve robustness, but when base models are trained on identical data, models tend to overfit and produce highly correlated predictions, thereby reducing the effectiveness of ensembling. In this work, we investigate a meta-ensemble learning methodology that enhances prediction diversity by training base models on diverse data splits and combining their outputs through a trained meta-model. Specifically, we train base models on the ICBHI dataset using two data split settings: fixed 80-20% split and five-fold cross-validation split, under two data granularity settings: patient- and sample-level. The resulting diversity in base model predictions enables the meta-model to better generalize. Our approach achieves new state-of-the-art performance on the ICBHI benchmark, reaching a Score of 66.49% and showing improved generalization on two out-of-distribution datasets, indicating its potential applicability to real-world clinical data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a meta-ensemble learning method for respiratory sound classification that trains base models on diverse data splits and combines them via a meta-model, achieving new SOTA on ICBHI (66.49% Score) with improved generalization on out-of-distribution datasets.
AI.motivation: Respiratory sound classification models suffer from limited dataset size and subject diversity. Ensemble methods help but fail when base models are trained on identical data due to correlated predictions and overfitting.
AI.method: A meta-ensemble learning approach: base models are trained on different data splits (fixed 80-20% and 5-fold CV) at two granularity levels (patient-level and sample-level), creating prediction diversity, then a meta-model is trained to combine their outputs.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art Score of 66.49% on ICBHI benchmark and demonstrates improved generalization on two out-of-distribution datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed meta-ensemble with diverse data splits effectively enhances prediction diversity and generalization, showing potential for real-world clinical respiratory sound classification.

title: Do Protective Perturbations Really Protect Portrait Privacy under Real-world Image Transformations?
summary: Proactive defense methods protect portrait images from unauthorized editing or talking face generation (TFG) by introducing pixel-level protective perturbations, and have already attracted increasing attention for privacy protection. In real-world scenarios, images inevitably undergo various transformations during cross-device display and dissemination--such as scale transformations and color compression--that directly alter pixel values. However, it remains unclear whether such pixel-level modifications affect the effectiveness of existing proactive defense methods that rely on pixel-level perturbations. To solve this problem, we conduct a systematic evaluation of representative proactive defenses under image transformation. The evaluated methods are selected to span different generation architectures such as diffusion and GAN-based models, as well as defense scopes covering both portrait and natural images, and are assessed using both qualitative and quantitative metrics for subjective and objective comparison. Experimental results indicate that defense methods based on pixel-level perturbations struggle to withstand common image transformations, posing a risk of defense failure in real-world applications. To further highlight this risk, we propose a simple yet effective purification framework by leveraging the vulnerabilities induced by real-world image transformations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method can efficiently remove protective perturbations with low computational cost, highlighting previously overlooked risks to the research community.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates existing proactive defense methods (pixel-level perturbations) for portrait privacy protection against common image transformations (scaling, compression), finding them vulnerable. The authors propose a simple, efficient purification framework to remove these perturbations, highlighting overlooked real-world risks.
AI.motivation: Real-world images undergo transformations (scaling, color compression) during cross-device display/dissemination, which alter pixel values. It is unclear whether these changes affect the effectiveness of existing pixel-level proactive defense methods.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of representative proactive defenses (diffusion and GAN-based models, covering portrait and natural images) under image transformations using qualitative and quantitative metrics. A simple purification framework is proposed to exploit vulnerabilities induced by real-world transformations.
AI.result: Defense methods based on pixel-level perturbations fail to withstand common image transformations. The proposed purification framework can efficiently remove protective perturbations with low computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Existing pixel-level proactive defenses are vulnerable in real-world applications; the proposed purification method highlights previously overlooked risks, urging the research community to consider real-world image transformations.

title: Fed-DLoRA: Efficient Wireless Federated Learning with Dynamic Low-Rank Adaptation
comment: 11 pages, 7 figures. Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: Federated learning (FL) offers a promising distributed learning paradigm for internet of vehicles (IoV) applications. However, it faces challenges from communication overhead and dynamic environments. Model compression techniques reduce computing and communication burden yet create trade-offs between compression ratios and vehicle participation strategies. In this paper, we propose a lightweight FL algorithm named federated learning with dynamic low-rank adaptation (Fed-DLoRA), which is combined with low-rank adaptation (LoRA) to effectively reduce parameters and communication costs while enhancing training efficiency. The convergence analysis of Fed-DLoRA is conducted through stochastic gradient descent optimization coupled with singular value decomposition. This analysis establishes the theoretical relationships among LoRA rank, vehicular scheduling strategies and the model's convergence characteristics. Building on these insights, we formulate a joint optimization problem aimed at maximizing system performance. To address this problem, we propose an adaptive rank, bandwidth and vehicle selection (ARBVS) algorithm that integrates enumeration with greedy optimization strategies. The algorithm provides efficient rank selection and resource scheduling strategies for each FL communication round, thereby achieving effective performance improvements for the FL system. Experimental results demonstrate that Fed-DLoRA achieves superior performance compared to conventional federated learning approaches, exhibiting enhanced accuracy, faster convergence, and improved communication efficiency.

AI.tldr: Fed-DLoRA combines LoRA with FL to reduce communication costs in IoV, jointly optimizes rank/bandwidth/vehicle selection via ARBVS, and achieves better accuracy and convergence.
AI.motivation: FL in IoV suffers from communication overhead and dynamic environments; model compression creates trade-offs between compression ratios and vehicle participation strategies.
AI.method: Proposes Fed-DLoRA using LoRA to reduce parameters; conducts convergence analysis via SGD + SVD; formulates joint optimization; introduces ARBVS (enumeration + greedy) for adaptive rank, bandwidth, and vehicle selection.
AI.result: Fed-DLoRA achieves superior accuracy, faster convergence, and better communication efficiency compared to conventional FL approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed joint optimization and dynamic low-rank adaptation effectively improve FL system performance in IoV environments.

title: A Pose-only Geometric Constraint for Multi-Camera Pose Adjustment
summary: Multi-camera systems offer rich observation capabilities for visual navigation and 3D scene reconstruction; however, the resulting feature redundancy often compromises computational efficiency. This challenge is particularly pronounced during bundle adjustment, where the non-linear optimization of both system poses and scene points incurs substantial computational overhead. To address this challenge, this paper introduces a pose-only geometric constraint for multi-camera systems and proposes a corresponding pose adjustment algorithm. Specifically, we use generalized camera model to establish a unified representation of the multi-camera system. Building upon this model, we formulate the multi-camera pose-only constraint, which implicitly represents a 3D scene point using two base observations and their associated poses, thereby achieving a pose-only representation of the projection geometry. Subsequently, we introduce a multi-camera pose adjustment algorithm that eliminates 3D points from the parameter space, thereby achieving efficient and focused pose optimization. Experimental results on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed algorithm outperforms baseline bundle adjustment methods in computational efficiency, while maintaining or even improving pose estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a pose-only geometric constraint and adjustment algorithm for multi-camera systems that eliminates 3D scene points from the parameter space, improving computational efficiency in bundle adjustment while maintaining or improving pose estimation accuracy.
AI.motivation: Multi-camera systems suffer from feature redundancy and high computational overhead during bundle adjustment due to non-linear optimization of both system poses and scene points.
AI.method: The authors use a generalized camera model for unified representation, formulate a multi-camera pose-only constraint that implicitly represents 3D points using two base observations and their associated poses, and introduce a pose adjustment algorithm that removes 3D points from the parameter space.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show the proposed algorithm outperforms baseline bundle adjustment methods in computational efficiency while maintaining or improving pose estimation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The pose-only constraint and adjustment algorithm effectively addresses efficiency challenges in multi-camera bundle adjustment by eliminating 3D points from optimization.

title: Leveraging Human Feedback for Semantically-Relevant Skill Discovery
comment: Accepted at the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026)
summary: Unsupervised skill discovery in reinforcement learning aims to intrinsically motivate agents to discover diverse and useful behaviours. However, unconstrained approaches can produce unsafe, unethical, or misaligned behaviours. To mitigate these risks and improve the practical desireability of discovered skills, recent work grounds the discovery process by leveraging human preference feedback. However, preference-based approaches are feedback-inefficient and inherently ill-equipped to deal with skill spaces composed of a variety of different skills such as running, jumping, walking, etc. To overcome this limitation, we introduce semantic labelling, a novel and feedback-efficient approach that leverages human cognitive strengths to identify and label semantically meaningful behaviours. Based on semantic labelling, we propose Semantically Relevant Skill Discovery (SRSD), a novel human-in-the-loop approach that collects semantic labels from human feedback and learns a reward function to encourage skills to be more semantically diverse and relevant. Through our experiments in a 2D navigation environment and four locomotion environments, we demonstrate that SRSD can improve semantic diversity and discover relevant behaviours while scaling effectively to a large variety of behaviours.

AI.tldr: SRSD is a human-in-the-loop RL method that uses semantic labeling from human feedback to discover diverse, safe, and relevant behaviors more efficiently than preference-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised skill discovery can produce unsafe or misaligned behaviors; preference-based human feedback is inefficient and struggles with diverse skill spaces (e.g., running, jumping, walking).
AI.method: Proposes semantic labeling (leveraging human cognitive strengths to label meaningful behaviors) and SRSD (human-in-the-loop approach collecting semantic labels, learning a reward function to encourage semantically diverse and relevant skills).
AI.result: In 2D navigation and four locomotion environments, SRSD improves semantic diversity and discovers relevant behaviors, scaling effectively to a wide variety of behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Semantic labeling is a feedback-efficient alternative to preference-based methods for grounding skill discovery toward safe and desirable behaviors.

title: Weakly Supervised Multicenter Nancy Index Scoring in Ulcerative Colitis Using Foundation Models
summary:
Histologic assessment of ulcerative colitis (UC) activity is an important endpoint in clinical trials and routine care, but manual grading with indices such as the Nancy histological index (NHI) is time-consuming and prone to observer variability. While computational pathology methods can automate scoring, many approaches depend on dense region-level annotations, which are costly to obtain, particularly in heterogeneous, multicenter cohorts.
  We propose a weakly supervised multiple instance learning (MIL) approach for whole-slide images that learns from case- and slide-level NHI labels, leveraging foundation models. Our method targets clinically relevant endpoints, including neutrophilic activity and derived Nancy-low/high groupings, enabling full five-grade NHI prediction.
  On a multicenter dataset of H&E-stained colon biopsies from three hospitals (2019-2025), we evaluate multiple foundation model encoders and aggregation strategies. We find that foundation model choice and resolution substantially affect performance, with Virchow2 providing the most consistent gains, and that a simple ensembling rule improves five-grade NHI prediction compared to a hierarchical gating baseline.
  Overall, our results demonstrate that weakly supervised MIL with modern foundation-model representations can provide robust, interpretable UC histology activity assessment in realistic multicenter settings.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a weakly supervised multiple instance learning (MIL) approach using foundation models to automatically score the Nancy histological index (NHI) from whole-slide images of UC biopsies, achieving robust performance on a multicenter dataset without costly dense annotations.
AI.motivation: Manual histologic assessment of UC activity via NHI is time-consuming, subjective, and suffers from observer variability. Existing computational methods require expensive dense region-level annotations, which are impractical for large, heterogeneous multicenter cohorts.
AI.method: A weakly supervised MIL framework that learns from case- and slide-level NHI labels using foundation model encoders (e.g., Virchow2) and various aggregation strategies. It predicts full five-grade NHI and derived binary endpoints (neutrophilic activity, Nancy-low/high groupings).
AI.result: On a multicenter dataset (3 hospitals, 2019-2025), Virchow2 foundation model provided the most consistent performance gains. Simple ensembling outperformed a hierarchical gating baseline for five-grade NHI prediction. Foundation model choice and resolution significantly impacted performance.
AI.conclusion: Weakly supervised MIL with modern foundation-model representations can deliver robust, interpretable automated UC histology activity assessment in realistic multicenter settings, reducing the need for costly fine-grained annotations.

title: Machine-Learning-Based Classification of Radio Frequency Building Loss
comment: Accepted as a short paper in International Conference on Telecommunications (ICT) 2026
summary: Accurate modeling of outdoor-to-indoor (O2I) and indoor-to-indoor (I2I) signal loss is important for improving indoor wireless network performance in dense urban areas. Traditional on-site measurements are expensive, time-consuming, and difficult to conduct across wide regions. Real-world datasets also tend to be noisy and imbalanced, which makes signal loss prediction challenging. This study presents a machine learning framework for classifying radio frequency (RF) building loss. The framework combines passively collected, crowdsourced user equipment (UE) data from 3GPP-compliant networks with public building information. We evaluated Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and a voting classifier using both supervised (SL) and semi-supervised learning (SSL). Compared to SL-only inference, the proposed SL and SSL framework improved both prediction accuracy and confidence under identical data constraints, achieving up to 12.6% relative accuracy gain for O2I loss and 3.4% for I2I loss, while reducing prediction entropy by up to 8.4%. Among the evaluated models, SSL XGBoost provided the most confident O2I loss classification, whereas SSL LightGBM achieved the best performance for I2I loss. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach provides a practical, data-driven alternative to traditional models, with promising potential to support better network planning and indoor coverage optimization.

AI.tldr: A machine learning framework combining supervised and semi-supervised learning with crowdsourced UE data and public building info to classify O2I and I2I signal loss, achieving up to 12.6% accuracy gain over supervised-only methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional on-site O2I/I2I signal loss measurements are expensive, time-consuming, and hard to scale. Real-world datasets are noisy and imbalanced, making accurate prediction challenging for indoor network planning.
AI.method: Combines passively collected crowdsourced UE data with public building information. Evaluates Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and voting classifier under both supervised learning (SL) and semi-supervised learning (SSL) frameworks.
AI.result: SL+SSL framework achieved up to 12.6% relative accuracy gain for O2I loss and 3.4% for I2I loss, with up to 8.4% reduction in prediction entropy. SSL XGBoost best for O2I; SSL LightGBM best for I2I.
AI.conclusion: The proposed data-driven approach offers a practical alternative to traditional models, with strong potential for improving network planning and indoor coverage optimization.

title: Progressive Approximation in Deep Residual Networks: Theory and Validation
summary: The Universal Approximation Theorem (UAT) guarantees universal function approximation but does not explain how residual models distribute approximation across layers. We reframe residual networks as a layer-wise approximation process that builds an approximation trajectory from input to target, and prove the existence of progressive trajectories where error decreases monotonically with depth. It reveals that residual networks can implement structured, step-by-step refinement rather than end-to-end (E2E) black-box mapping. Building on this, we propose Layer-wise Progressive Approximation (LPA), a theoretically grounded training principle that explicitly aligns each layer with its residual target to realize such trajectories. LPA is architecture-agnostic: we observe progressive behavior in residual FNNs, ResNets, and Transformers across tasks including complex surface fitting, image classification, and NLP with LLMs for generation and classification. Crucially, this enables ``train once, use $N$ models": a single network yields useful predictions at every depth, supporting efficient shallow inference without retraining. Our work unifies approximation theory with practical deep learning, providing a new lens on representation learning and a flexible framework for multi-depth deployment. The source code will be released unpon acceptance at https://(open\_upon\_acceptance).

AI.tldr: This paper reframes residual networks as layer-wise approximation processes (not just black-box mappers), proves error decreases monotonically with depth, proposes Layer-wise Progressive Approximation (LPA) training principle, and demonstrates "train once, use N models" efficiency across architectures.
AI.motivation: The Universal Approximation Theorem guarantees function approximation but doesn't explain how residual networks distribute approximation across layers. The authors aim to bridge this gap between approximation theory and practical deep learning.
AI.method: 1) Theoretically proves existence of progressive trajectories with monotonic error decrease. 2) Proposes Layer-wise Progressive Approximation (LPA) — a training principle aligning each layer with its residual target. 3) Empirically validates on residual FNNs, ResNets, and Transformers across diverse tasks.
AI.result: Residual networks exhibit structured, step-by-step refinement. LPA enables "train once, use N models" — a single network provides useful predictions at every depth, supporting efficient shallow inference without retraining.
AI.conclusion: This work unifies approximation theory with practice, offering a new understanding of representation learning and a flexible framework for multi-depth deployment across architectures.

title: Caries DETR: Tooth Structure-aware Prior and Lesion-aware Dynamic Loss Refinement for DETR Based Caries Detection
summary: As dental caries appear as subtle, low-contrast lesions in intraoral imaging, existing deep learning models face significant challenges in the early detection of caries. While recent Transformer-based detectors have shown promising results in natural images, they often fail to capture the domain-specific anatomical priors crucial for dental caries detection. In this paper, we propose Caries-DETR, a specialized Transformer framework for caries detection in intraoral images. A Tooth Structure-aware Query Initialization (TSQI) is designed, leveraging large-scale intraoral photograph pre-training and a structure perception branch (SPB) to integrate high-frequency structural priors, guiding the model to focus on anatomically significant lesion areas. Furthermore, we design a Lesion-aware Dynamic Loss Refinement (LDLR) to implement quality-driven hard mining through adaptive loss reweighting based on lesion size, anatomical relevance, and prediction quality, optimizing detection for subtle lesions. Extensive experiments on two public datasets (i.e., AlphaDent and DentalAI) demonstrate that Caries-DETR achieves a state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods and exhibits good generalization and robustness. Code and data at https://github.com/XuefenLiu-SZU/Caries-DETR}{https://github.com/XuefenLiu-SZU/Caries-DETR.

AI.tldr: Caries-DETR is a specialized Transformer framework for dental caries detection that uses tooth structure-aware query initialization and lesion-aware dynamic loss refinement to achieve state-of-the-art performance on intraoral images.
AI.motivation: Dental caries appear as subtle, low-contrast lesions in intraoral imaging, making early detection challenging. Existing deep learning models (including Transformer-based detectors) fail to capture domain-specific anatomical priors crucial for dental caries detection.
AI.method: 1) Tooth Structure-aware Query Initialization (TSQI) with large-scale pre-training and a structure perception branch (SPB) for high-frequency structural priors. 2) Lesion-aware Dynamic Loss Refinement (LDLR) for quality-driven hard mining via adaptive loss reweighting based on lesion size, anatomical relevance, and prediction quality.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on two public datasets (AlphaDent and DentalAI), with good generalization and robustness compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Caries-DETR effectively addresses early dental caries detection challenges by incorporating anatomical priors and adaptive loss refinement, demonstrating superior performance and generalization.

title: Meta-Aligner: Bidirectional Preference-Policy Optimization for Multi-Objective LLMs Alignment
summary: Multi-Objective Alignment aims to align Large Language Models (LLMs) with diverse and often conflicting human values by optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously. Existing methods predominantly rely on static preference weight construction strategies. However, rigidly aligning to fixed targets discards valuable intermediate information, as training responses inherently embody valid preference trade-offs even when deviating from the target. To address this limitation, we propose Meal, i.e., MEta ALigner, a bi-level meta-learning framework enabling bidirectional optimization between preferences and policy responses, generating instructive dynamic preferences for steadier training. Specifically, we introduce a preference-weight-net as a meta-learner to generate adaptive preference weights based on input prompts and update the preference weights as learnable parameters, while the LLM policy acts as a base-learner optimizing response generation conditioned on these preferences with rejection sampling strategy. Extensive empirical results demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance on several multi-objective benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of the dynamic bidirectional preference-policy optimization framework.

AI.tldr: Meal is a bi-level meta-learning framework that dynamically adjusts preference weights during multi-objective LLM alignment, outperforming static preference methods.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-objective alignment methods use static preference weights, discarding valuable intermediate information from training responses that embody valid trade-offs even when deviating from fixed targets.
AI.method: Meal uses a bi-level meta-learning framework: a preference-weight-net (meta-learner) generates adaptive preference weights based on input prompts, while the LLM policy (base-learner) optimizes responses conditioned on these weights using rejection sampling.
AI.result: Superior performance on several multi-objective benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of the dynamic bidirectional preference-policy optimization framework.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic bidirectional preference-policy optimization via meta-learning is more effective than static preference weight strategies for multi-objective LLM alignment.

title: Zoom In, Reason Out: Efficient Far-field Anomaly Detection in Expressway Surveillance Videos via Focused VLM Reasoning Guided by Bayesian Inference
summary: Expressway video anomaly detection is essential for safety management. However, identifying anomalies across diverse scenes remains challenging, particularly for far-field targets exhibiting subtle abnormal vehicle motions. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate strong semantic reasoning capabilities, processing global frames causes attention dilution for these far-field objects and incurs prohibitive computational costs. To address these issues, we propose VIBES, an asynchronous collaborative framework utilizing VLMs guided by Bayesian inference. Specifically, to overcome poor generalization across varying expressway environments, we introduce an online Bayesian inference module. This module continuously evaluates vehicle trajectories to dynamically update the probabilistic boundaries of normal driving behaviors, serving as an asynchronous trigger to precisely localize anomalies in space and time. Instead of processing the continuous video stream, the VLM processes only the localized visual regions indicated by the trigger. This targeted visual input prevents attention dilution and enables accurate semantic reasoning. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that VIBES improves detection accuracy for far-field anomalies and reduces computational overhead, achieving high real-time efficiency and explainability while demonstrating generalization across diverse expressway conditions.

AI.tldr: VIBES is an asynchronous collaborative framework that uses Bayesian inference to trigger a Vision-Language Model to focus only on localized anomalous regions in expressway videos, improving far-field anomaly detection accuracy while reducing computational costs.
AI.motivation: Expressway video anomaly detection faces challenges with far-field targets showing subtle abnormal motions. Existing VLMs suffer from attention dilution when processing global frames and incur high computational costs. Additionally, poor generalization across diverse expressway environments remains a problem.
AI.method: An asynchronous collaborative framework (VIBES) that combines: (1) an online Bayesian inference module that continuously evaluates vehicle trajectories to dynamically update probabilistic boundaries of normal driving behaviors, serving as an asynchronous trigger to localize anomalies in space and time; (2) a VLM that processes only the localized visual regions indicated by the trigger, preventing attention dilution.
AI.result: Improved detection accuracy for far-field anomalies, reduced computational overhead, high real-time efficiency, and explainability. Demonstrates generalization across diverse expressway conditions.
AI.conclusion: VIBES effectively addresses the limitations of using VLMs for expressway anomaly detection by avoiding global frame processing and leveraging Bayesian-triggered localized analysis, achieving efficiency and generalization.

title: CMGL: Confidence-guided Multi-omics Graph Learning for Cancer Subtype Classification
comment: 24 pages, 15 figures, 13 tables, 2 algorithms (main paper + supplementary materials)
summary:
Motivation: Multi-omics integration can improve cancer subtyping, but modality informativeness and noise vary across cancer types and patients. Existing graph-based methods optimize modality weights jointly with the classification objective and therefore lack independent reliability estimates, so low-quality omics distort patient similarity graphs and amplify noise through message passing.
  Results: We propose CMGL, a two-stage framework that estimates per-sample modality reliability through evidential deep learning and uses the frozen confidence scores to guide cross-omics fusion and graph construction. On four MLOmics cancer-subtype tasks and the 32-class pan-cancer task, CMGL consistently improves over the strongest baseline, surpassing it by 4.03% in average accuracy on the four single-cancer tasks. Its representations recover the PAM50 intrinsic subtypes of breast invasive carcinoma (BRCA), and the BRCA-trained model transfers without fine-tuning to kidney renal clear cell carcinoma (KIRC), stratifying patients into prognostically distinct groups.

AI.tldr: CMGL is a two-stage framework that estimates per-sample modality reliability via evidential deep learning, then uses frozen confidence scores to guide cross-omics fusion and graph construction, improving cancer subtyping across multiple tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based multi-omics integration methods optimize modality weights jointly with classification, lacking independent reliability estimates, causing noisy omics to distort patient similarity graphs and amplify noise through message passing.
AI.method: CMGL uses a two-stage framework: first, evidential deep learning estimates per-sample modality reliability; second, frozen confidence scores guide cross-omics fusion and graph construction.
AI.result: CMGL surpasses the strongest baseline by 4.03% in average accuracy on four single-cancer tasks, recovers PAM50 intrinsic subtypes in BRCA, and transfers to KIRC without fine-tuning to stratify patients into prognostically distinct groups.
AI.conclusion: CMGL provides a robust, interpretable, and transferable approach for multi-omics cancer subtyping by decoupling reliability estimation from classification objectives.

title: ESIA: An Energy-Based Spatiotemporal Interaction-Aware Framework for Pedestrian Intention Prediction
summary: Recent advances in autonomous driving have motivated research on pedestrian intention prediction, which aims to infer future crossing decisions and actions by modeling temporal dynamics, social interactions, and environmental context. However, existing studies remain constrained by oversimplified multi-agent interaction patterns, opaque reasoning logic, and a lack of global consistency in behavioral predictions, which compromise both robustness and interpretability. In this work, we propose ESIA (Energy-based Spatiotemporal Interaction-Aware framework), a novel Conditional Random Field (CRF)-based paradigm. We cast the intention prediction task as a structured prediction problem over a unified graph-based representation, treating pedestrians and the environment as spatiotemporal nodes. To characterize their distinct roles, we assign unary potentials to nodes to capture individual intentions, and pairwise potentials to edges to encode social and environmental interactions. These potentials are integrated into a unified global energy function to ensure scene-level consistency across behavioral predictions. To further constrain inference without ground-truth supervision, we introduce structural consistency terms to penalize logical contradictions. This optimization is efficiently solved via a novel Unary-Seeded Simulated Annealing (U-SSA) algorithm, which leverages high-confidence unary priors to rapidly converge to a high-quality solution. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that ESIA achieves state-of-the-art performance with improved interpretability over existing methods.

AI.tldr: ESIA proposes a CRF-based framework for pedestrian intention prediction that models spatiotemporal interactions via energy functions and optimizes with a Unary-Seeded Simulated Annealing algorithm, achieving SOTA results with better interpretability.
AI.motivation: Existing pedestrian intention prediction methods suffer from oversimplified multi-agent interactions, opaque reasoning logic, and lack of global consistency in behavioral predictions, compromising robustness and interpretability.
AI.method: ESIA formulates intention prediction as a structured CRF problem over a unified graph: unary potentials capture individual intentions, pairwise potentials encode social/environmental interactions, integrated into a global energy function for scene-level consistency. Structural consistency terms penalize logical contradictions. Optimization uses a Unary-Seeded Simulated Annealing (U-SSA) algorithm leveraging high-confidence priors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show ESIA achieves state-of-the-art performance with improved interpretability over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The energy-based CRF paradigm with structural consistency constraints and efficient U-SSA optimization effectively addresses key limitations in pedestrian intention prediction, offering both higher accuracy and better interpretability.

title: IMPA-Net: Meteorology-Aware Multi-Scale Attention and Dynamic Loss for Extreme Convective Radar Nowcasting
summary: Short-range prediction of convective precipitation from weather radar observations is essential for severe weather warnings. However, deep learning models trained with pixel-wise error metrics tend to produce overly smooth forecasts that suppress intense echoes critical for hazard detection. This issue is exacerbated by insufficient multi-scale feature interaction and suboptimal fusion of heterogeneous geophysical inputs. We propose IMPA-Net (Integrated Multi-scale Predictive Attention Network), a deterministic 0-2 hour nowcasting framework that addresses these limitations through meteorologically-informed designs at the input, architecture, and loss function levels. A parameter-free Spatial Mixer reorganizes heterogeneous input channels at the mesoscale-$γ$ neighborhood (~2 km) via deterministic channel permutation, providing a structured cross-field prior. An integrated multi-scale predictive attention module serves as the spatiotemporal translator, capturing dynamics from mesoscale-$β$ to mesoscale-$γ$ scales. A Meteorologically-Aware Dynamic Loss employs three-level asymmetric weighting -- adapting across training epochs, storm intensity, and forecast lead time -- to counteract regression-to-the-mean. Evaluated against seven baselines on a multi-source radar dataset over eastern China, IMPA-Net raises the Heidke Skill Score at $\geq$45 dBZ from 0.049 (SimVP baseline) to 0.143 under matched settings. Relative to pySTEPS, it provides a better trade-off between severe-event detection and false-alarm control. Spectral analysis confirms preserved energy across mesoscale bands where competing methods show progressive smoothing. These improvements are shown within a single domain and convective regime; generalizability to other orographic and climatic regions remains to be tested.

AI.tldr: IMPA-Net is a deep learning nowcasting framework for 0-2 hour convective precipitation prediction that uses meteorologically-informed input reorganization (Spatial Mixer), multi-scale spatiotemporal attention, and a dynamic asymmetric loss function to overcome the over-smoothing problem common in existing models, achieving significantly better detection of intense storms (45+ dBZ) compared to seven baselines on eastern China radar data.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning models for short-term convective precipitation nowcasting produce overly smooth forecasts that suppress intense radar echoes critical for severe weather warnings, due to pixel-wise error metrics, insufficient multi-scale feature interaction, and suboptimal fusion of heterogeneous geophysical inputs.
AI.method: IMPA-Net introduces three key components: (1) a parameter-free Spatial Mixer that reorganizes heterogeneous input channels via deterministic permutation at mesoscale-γ neighborhoods; (2) an integrated multi-scale predictive attention module for spatiotemporal translation across mesoscale-β to mesoscale-γ scales; and (3) a Meteorologically-Aware Dynamic Loss with three-level asymmetric weighting adapting across training epochs, storm intensity, and forecast lead time.
AI.result: On a multi-source radar dataset over eastern China, IMPA-Net raised the Heidke Skill Score at ≥45 dBZ from 0.049 (SimVP baseline) to 0.143 under matched settings. It outperformed pySTEPS in severe-event detection vs. false-alarm trade-off, and spectral analysis confirmed preserved energy across mesoscale bands where competing methods showed progressive smoothing.
AI.conclusion: IMPA-Net provides meteorologically-informed improvements in convective nowcasting, but generalizability to other orographic and climatic regions remains to be tested.

title: DynProto: Dynamic Prototype Evolution for Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Recent studies show that using potential out-of-distribution (OOD) labels from large corpora as auxiliary information can improve OOD detection in vision-language models (VLMs). However, these methods often fail when real-world OOD samples fall outside the predefined OOD label set. To address this limitation, we propose DynProto, a novel approach that learns OOD prototypes dynamically during testing using only in-distribution (ID) information. DynProto is inspired by a key observation: OOD samples predicted as the same ID class tend to cluster in the feature space. With this insight, we leverage easy-to-detect OOD samples as ``anchors'' to find their harder-to-detect, similar counterparts. To this end, DynProto introduces two modules: \textbf{Coarse OOD Pattern Capturing Module} caches OOD patterns that are easily confused with each ID class during testing, and \textbf{Fine-grained OOD Pattern Refinement Module} subsequently clusters these patterns within each cache and aggregates them into representative OOD prototypes. By measuring similarity to ID and dynamic OOD prototypes, DynProto enables accurate OOD detection. DynProto significantly outperforms prior methods across multiple benchmarks. On ImageNet OOD benchmark, DynProto reduces FPR95 by 11.60\% and improves AUROC by 4.70\%. Moreover, the framework is architecture-agnostic and can be integrated into various backbones.

AI.tldr: DynProto dynamically learns OOD prototypes during testing using only ID information, leveraging easy-to-detect OOD samples as anchors to find harder-to-detect counterparts, achieving state-of-the-art OOD detection for vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Existing methods that use predefined OOD labels from large corpora fail when real-world OOD samples fall outside the predefined label set. The authors aim to address this limitation by learning OOD prototypes dynamically without needing predefined OOD labels.
AI.method: DynProto has two modules: (1) Coarse OOD Pattern Capturing Module - caches OOD patterns easily confused with each ID class during testing; (2) Fine-grained OOD Pattern Refinement Module - clusters patterns within each cache and aggregates them into representative OOD prototypes. OOD detection is done by measuring similarity to both ID and dynamic OOD prototypes.
AI.result: On ImageNet OOD benchmark, DynProto reduces FPR95 by 11.60% and improves AUROC by 4.70%. It significantly outperforms prior methods across multiple benchmarks and is architecture-agnostic.
AI.conclusion: DynProto provides a more robust OOD detection approach that doesn't rely on predefined OOD labels, works dynamically during testing, and can be integrated into various backbone architectures.

title: GeoEdit: Local Frames for Fast, Training-Free On-Manifold Editing in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models are a leading paradigm for data generation, but training-free editing typically re-runs the full denoising trajectory for every edit strength, making iterative refinement expensive. To address this issue, we instead edit near the data manifold, where small local updates can replace repeated re-synthesis. To enable this, we estimate a local manifold tangent space directly from perturbed samples and prove that this sample-based estimator closely approximates the true tangent. Building on this guarantee, we devise a Jacobian-free algorithm that constructs a tangent frame via small perturbations to the initial noise and alternates small tangent moves with diffusion-based projections. Updates within this frame follow principled on-manifold directions while suppressing off-manifold drift, enabling fine-grained edits without full re-diffusion or additional training. Edit strength is controlled by the number of steps for rapid, continuous adjustments that preserve fidelity and plug into existing samplers. Empirically, the resulting tangent directions yield smooth, semantic unsupervised traversals and effective CLIP-guided optimization, demonstrating practical interactive continuous editing.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a training-free editing method for diffusion models that edits near the data manifold using a Jacobian-free tangent space estimator, enabling fine-grained, continuous control without full re-denoising.
AI.motivation: Training-free editing in diffusion models is expensive because it requires re-running the full denoising trajectory for every edit strength, making iterative refinement computationally prohibitive.
AI.method: Estimates local manifold tangent space from perturbed samples with theoretical guarantees, constructs a tangent frame via small noise perturbations, and alternates tangent moves with diffusion-based projections for on-manifold edits without Jacobian computation or re-diffusion.
AI.result: The tangent directions yield smooth, semantic unsupervised traversals and effective CLIP-guided optimization, enabling practical interactive continuous editing while preserving fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables efficient, fine-grained, and controllable editing in diffusion models by working directly on the data manifold, eliminating the need for full re-synthesis or additional training.

title: Edit Where You Mean: Region-Aware Adapter Injection for Mask-Free Local Image Editing
summary: Large diffusion transformers (DiTs) follow global editing instructions well but consistently leak local edits into unrelated regions, because joint-attention architectures offer no explicit channel telling the network where to apply the edit. We introduce REDEdit, a co-trained, instruction- and region-aware adapter framework that retrofits a frozen DiT into a precise local editor without modifying its backbone weights. A lightweight Block Adapter at every transformer block injects a structured condition stream that factorizes what to edit (instruction semantics) from where to edit (spatial mask); a learned SpatialGate routes the adapter signal selectively into the edit region while keeping the rest of the image near-identical to the source; and a Region-Aware Loss focuses the training objective on the changing pixels. Because these components make the backbone's internal representation mask-aware end-to-end, a thin MaskPredictor head trained jointly with the editor can ground the edit region directly from the instruction and source image eliminating any user-mask requirement at deployment. We evaluate on two complementary benchmarks: MagicBrush (paired ground-truth targets) to measure pixel-level preservation and edit accuracy, and Emu-Edit Test (no ground-truth images, 9 diverse edit categories) to stress-test instruction following and generalization across edit types. On both, REDEdit achieves state-of-the-art results, simultaneously outperforming mask-free and oracle-mask baselines. A seven-variant ablation cleanly isolates the contribution of each component.

AI.tldr: REDEdit is a lightweight adapter for frozen diffusion transformers that enables precise local image editing without leaking changes, using block adapters, spatial gating, and region-aware loss. It achieves SOTA on MagicBrush and Emu-Edit benchmarks and can optionally predict edit masks from text alone.
AI.motivation: Existing large diffusion transformers (DiTs) with joint-attention architectures fail at local editing because they cannot distinguish where to apply edits, causing unintended modifications to unrelated image regions.
AI.method: REDEdit retrofits a frozen DiT with: (1) Block Adapters injecting structured condition streams separating instruction semantics from spatial masks, (2) SpatialGate routing adapter signals only into edit regions, (3) Region-Aware Loss focusing training on changing pixels, and (4) an optional MaskPredictor for mask-free deployment.
AI.result: State-of-the-art results on MagicBrush (pixel-level preservation/edit accuracy) and Emu-Edit Test (9 diverse edit categories), outperforming both mask-free and oracle-mask baselines. A 7-variant ablation confirms each component's contribution.
AI.conclusion: REDEdit demonstrates that precise, local image editing in DiTs is achievable without modifying backbone weights, by explicitly factorizing edit semantics from spatial location through a co-trained adapter framework.

title: BitRL: Reinforcement Learning with 1-bit Quantized Language Models for Resource-Constrained Edge Deployment
comment: 6pages, 1 Figure, IEEE International Conference of Frontiers of Engineering and Emerging Technologies 2026
summary:
The deployment of intelligent reinforcement learning (RL) agents on resource-constrained edge devices remains a fundamental challenge due to the substantial memory, computational, and energy requirements of modern deep learning systems. While large language models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful architectures for decision-making agents, their multi-billion parameter scale confines them to cloud-based deployment, raising concerns about latency, privacy, and connectivity dependence.
  We introduce BitRL, a framework for building RL agents using 1-bit quantized language models that enables practical on-device learning and inference under severe resource constraints. Leveraging the BitNet b1.58 architecture with ternary weights (-1, 0, +1) and an optimized inference stack, BitRL achieves 10-16x memory reduction and 3-5x energy efficiency improvements over full-precision baselines while maintaining 85-98 percent of task performance across benchmarks.
  We provide theoretical analysis of quantization as structured parameter perturbation, derive convergence bounds for quantized policy gradients under frozen-backbone architectures, and identify the exploration-stability trade-off in extreme quantization. Our framework systematically integrates 1-bit quantized language models with reinforcement learning for edge deployment and demonstrates effectiveness on commodity hardware.

AI.tldr: BitRL introduces a framework for building RL agents using 1-bit quantized language models (BitNet b1.58 with ternary weights), achieving 10-16x memory reduction and 3-5x energy savings while maintaining 85-98% of task performance, enabling practical on-device learning and inference for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Deploying RL agents on resource-constrained edge devices is challenging due to high memory, compute, and energy demands of modern deep learning systems. LLMs offer powerful decision-making but are confined to cloud deployment due to multi-billion parameter scale, raising latency, privacy, and connectivity issues.
AI.method: BitRL uses 1-bit quantized language models (BitNet b1.58 with ternary weights -1, 0, +1) with an optimized inference stack. It incorporates theoretical analysis of quantization as structured parameter perturbation, deriving convergence bounds for quantized policy gradients under frozen-backbone architectures, and identifies the exploration-stability trade-off in extreme quantization.
AI.result: 10-16x memory reduction and 3-5x energy efficiency improvements over full-precision baselines while maintaining 85-98% of task performance across benchmarks, demonstrated on commodity hardware.
AI.conclusion: BitRL systematically integrates 1-bit quantized LLMs with RL for edge deployment, offering a practical solution for on-device learning and inference under severe resource constraints while maintaining competitive performance.

title: From Noisy Historical Maps to Time-Series Oil Palm Mapping Without Annotation in Malaysia and Indonesia (2020-2024)
summary: Accurate monitoring of oil palm plantations is critical for balancing economic development with environmental conservation in Southeast Asia. However, existing plantation maps often suffer from low spatial resolution and a lack of recent temporal coverage, impeding effective surveillance of rapid land-use changes. In this study, we propose a deep learning framework to generate 10-meter resolution oil palm plantation maps for Indonesia and Malaysia from 2020 to 2024, utilizing Sentinel-2 imagery without requiring new manual annotations. To address the resolution mismatch between coarse 100-meter historical labels and 10-meter imagery, we employ a U-Net architecture optimized with Determinant-based Mutual Information (DMI). This approach effectively mitigates the influence of label noise. We validated our method against 2,058 manually verified points, achieving overall accuracies of 70.64%, 63.53%, and 60.06% for the years 2020, 2022, and 2024, respectively. Our comprehensive analysis reveals that oil palm coverage in the region peaked in 2022 before experiencing a decline in 2024. Furthermore, land cover transition analysis highlights a concerning trajectory of plantation expansion into flooded vegetation areas, despite a general stabilization in rotations with other crop types. These high-resolution maps provide essential data for monitoring sustainability commitments and deforestation dynamics in the region, and the generated datasets are made publicly available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17768444.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a deep learning framework (U-Net with DMI) that generates 10-meter resolution oil palm plantation maps for Indonesia and Malaysia from 2020 to 2024 using Sentinel-2 imagery without new manual annotations, achieving accuracies of 60-70% and revealing that oil palm coverage peaked in 2022 before declining, with expansion into flooded vegetation areas.
AI.motivation: Existing oil palm plantation maps suffer from low spatial resolution and outdated temporal coverage, hindering effective monitoring of rapid land-use changes in Southeast Asia where balancing economic development and environmental conservation is critical.
AI.method: The authors propose a U-Net architecture optimized with Determinant-based Mutual Information (DMI) to address the resolution mismatch between coarse 100-meter historical labels and 10-meter Sentinel-2 imagery, enabling training without new manual annotations.
AI.result: Validated against 2,058 manually verified points, the method achieved overall accuracies of 70.64% (2020), 63.53% (2022), and 60.06% (2024). Analysis shows oil palm coverage peaked in 2022 then declined, with concerning expansion into flooded vegetation areas.
AI.conclusion: These high-resolution maps provide essential data for monitoring sustainability commitments and deforestation dynamics, and the datasets are publicly available for further research and policy applications.

title: Model-Free Inference of Investor Preferences: A Relative Entropy IRL Approach
summary: We present a framework using Relative Entropy Inverse Reinforcement Learning (RE-IRL) to recover investor reward functions from observed investment actions and market conditions.
