Unlike traditional IRL algorithms, RE-IRL is employed to account for environments where transition probabilities are unknown or inaccessible. To address the challenge of data sparsity, we utilize a $K$-nearest neighbor approach to estimate the observed behavior policy. Furthermore, we propose a statistical testing framework to evaluate the validity and robustness of the estimated results.

AI.tldr: This paper applies RE-IRL with K-nearest neighbor estimation and statistical testing to recover investor reward functions from observed actions and market data.
AI.motivation: To recover investor reward functions from observed actions when transition probabilities are unknown, addressing data sparsity challenges.
AI.method: Uses Relative Entropy IRL (RE-IRL) to handle unknown transition probabilities, K-nearest neighbor for behavior policy estimation, and a statistical testing framework for result robustness.
AI.result: A framework capable of recovering reward functions under unknown transition dynamics with robustness validation.
AI.conclusion: RE-IRL combined with KNN estimation and statistical testing effectively recovers investor reward functions in data-sparse environments with unknown transitions.

title: ClawMark: A Living-World Benchmark for Multi-Turn, Multi-Day, Multimodal Coworker Agents
summary: Language-model agents are increasingly used as persistent coworkers that assist users across multiple working days. During such workflows, the surrounding environment may change independently of the agent: new emails arrive, calendar entries shift, knowledge-base records are updated, and evidence appears across images, scanned PDFs, audio, video, and spreadsheets. Existing benchmarks do not adequately evaluate this setting because they typically run within a single static episode and remain largely text-centric. We introduce \bench{}, a benchmark for coworker agents built around multi-turn multi-day tasks, a stateful sandboxed service environment whose state evolves between turns, and rule-based verification. The current release contains 100 tasks across 13 professional scenarios, executed against five stateful sandboxed services (filesystem, email, calendar, knowledge base, spreadsheet) and scored by 1537 deterministic Python checkers over post-execution service state; no LLM-as-judge is invoked during scoring. We benchmark seven frontier agent systems. The strongest model reaches 75.8 weighted score, but the best strict Task Success is only 20.0\%, indicating that partial progress is common while complete end-to-end workflow completion remains rare. Turn-level analysis shows that performance drops after the first exogenous environment update, highlighting adaptation to changing state as a key open challenge. We release the benchmark, evaluation harness, and construction pipeline to support reproducible coworker-agent evaluation.

AI.tldr: Introduces Bench, a benchmark for evaluating language-model coworker agents in multi-turn, multi-day, stateful environments with dynamic changes (emails, calendar updates, etc.), finding that even the best agent achieves only 20% strict task success.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks evaluate agents within single static episodes and remain text-centric, failing to capture the realistic setting where agents work persistently across days while the environment changes independently (new emails, calendar shifts, updated knowledge-base records, multimodal evidence).
AI.method: Built 100 tasks across 13 professional scenarios with five stateful sandboxed services (filesystem, email, calendar, knowledge base, spreadsheet), scored by 1537 deterministic Python checkers on post-execution service state without LLM-as-judge. Tasks involve multi-turn multi-day workflows with exogenous environment updates.
AI.result: Best model achieves 75.8 weighted score but only 20.0% strict Task Success; partial progress is common while end-to-end completion is rare. Performance drops after the first exogenous environment update, highlighting adaptation challenges.
AI.conclusion: Adaptation to changing environment state remains a key open challenge for agent systems; benchmark, evaluation harness, and pipeline are released publicly for reproducible evaluation.

title: Latent-Hysteresis Graph ODEs: Modeling Coupled Topology-Feature Evolution via Continuous Phase Transitions
summary: Graph neural ordinary differential equations (Graph ODEs) extend graph learning from discrete message-passing layers to continuous-time representation flows. While it supports adaptive long-range propagation, we show that Graph ODEs with strictly positive irreducible mixing operators face an inherent \emph{monostability trap}: in the long-time regime, information leakage is unavoidable and the dynamics converge to a single global consensus attractor. We propose the \textbf{Hysteresis Graph ODE (HGODE)}, which couples feature evolution with a latent topological potential driven by a learned pairwise force. A double-well edge potential and bipolarized gate allow edge states to polarize into connected or insulated phases while preserving differentiability. We provide asymptotic analysis of the collapse mechanism and the proposed hysteretic topology dynamics, and validate HGODE on theory-driven synthetic diagnostics and real-world graph benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Graph ODEs suffer from a monostability trap causing information leakage to a global consensus. HGODE solves this by introducing hysteresis dynamics with a double-well edge potential and bipolarized gate, preserving differentiation and enabling polarized long-range propagation.
AI.motivation: Graph ODEs with positive irreducible mixing operators converge to a single global consensus attractor over long time, causing unavoidable information leakage. This monostability trap limits their ability to preserve distinct node representations.
AI.method: HGODE couples feature evolution with a latent topological potential driven by learned pairwise forces. It uses a double-well edge potential and bipolarized gate to polarize edge states into connected/insulated phases while preserving differentiability.
AI.result: Asymptotic analysis of collapse mechanism and hysteretic topology dynamics is provided; validation on synthetic diagnostics and real-world graph benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: HGODE overcomes the monostability trap in Graph ODEs by introducing hysteresis in edge topology, enabling long-range propagation without information collapse.

title: MIRAGE: A Micro-Interaction Relational Architecture for Grounded Exploration in Multi-Figure Artworks
comment: 14 pages (11 pages main text), 6 figures, 1 table
summary:
Appreciating multi-figure paintings requires understanding how characters relate through subtle cues like gaze alignment, gesture, and spatial arrangement. We present MIRAGE, an evidence-centric framework designed to scaffold the exploration of these "micro-interactions" in multi-figure artworks. While such cues are essential for deep narrative appreciation, they are often distributed across complex scenes and difficult for viewers to systematically identify. Existing vision-language models (VLMs) frequently fail to provide reliable assistance, offering ungrounded interpretations that lack traceable visual evidence.
  MIRAGE addresses this by constructing a structured intermediate representation capturing identities, pose cues, and gaze hypotheses. However, the challenge extends beyond extracting these cues to coordinating them during interpretation. Without an explicit mechanism to organize and reconcile relational evidence, models often collapse multiple interaction hypotheses into a single unstable or weakly grounded narrative, even when low-level signals are available. This representation allows users to verify how high-level interpretations are anchored in low-level visual facts.
  By separating spatial grounding from narrative generation, MIRAGE enables users to inspect and reason about figure-to-figure relationships through a verifiable evidence layer. We evaluate MIRAGE against painting-only VLM baselines using a blind assessment protocol. Results show that MIRAGE significantly improves identity consistency, reduces relational hallucinations, and increases the coverage of subtle interactions. These findings suggest that structured grounding can serve as a critical interaction control layer, providing the necessary scaffolding for a more reliable, transparent, and human-led understanding of complex visual narratives.

AI.tldr: MIRAGE is an evidence-centric framework that uses structured intermediate representations (identities, poses, gaze) to scaffold the analysis of micro-interactions in multi-figure paintings, improving identity consistency and reducing hallucinations compared to standard VLMs.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs fail to reliably analyze subtle relational cues (gaze, gesture, spatial arrangement) in multi-figure artworks, often producing ungrounded interpretations without traceable visual evidence.
AI.method: MIRAGE constructs a structured intermediate representation capturing identities, pose cues, and gaze hypotheses, then separates spatial grounding from narrative generation to allow verifiable evidence-based interpretation.
AI.result: Blind evaluation against painting-only VLM baselines shows MIRAGE significantly improves identity consistency, reduces relational hallucinations, and increases coverage of subtle interactions.
AI.conclusion: Structured grounding serves as a critical interaction control layer, enabling more reliable, transparent, and human-led understanding of complex visual narratives in multi-figure artworks.

title: SolarTformer: A Transformer Based Deep Learning Approach for Short Term Solar Power Forecasting
summary: Accurate forecasting of solar power output is essential for efficient integration of renewable energy into the grid. In this study, an attention-based deep learning model, inspired by transformer architecture, is used for short-term solar power forecasting. Our proposed model, "SolarTformer", is designed to predict solar power output from meteorological data. Unlike traditional models, SolarTformer leverages self-attention mechanisms to effectively capture temporal dependencies and spatial variability in solar irradiance. In addition, the proposed methodology includes feeding power station-specific metadata into the model, which helps to generalize between power stations located at different locations and with different panel configurations and in different seasons. Our experiments demonstrate that SolarTformer significantly outperforms previous models on the same data set. In particular, the model exhibits strong performance on both clear and cloudy days, indicating high robustness and generalizability. These findings highlight the potential of attention-based architectures in enhancing the accuracy of solar forecasting, contributing to a more reliable management of renewable energy.

AI.tldr: SolarTformer, an attention-based deep learning model inspired by transformer architecture, achieves superior short-term solar power forecasting by capturing temporal and spatial dependencies in solar irradiance while incorporating station-specific metadata for better generalization.
AI.motivation: Accurate solar power forecasting is essential for integrating renewable energy into the grid efficiently.
AI.method: An attention-based deep learning model (SolarTformer) leveraging self-attention mechanisms and incorporating power station-specific metadata (location, panel configuration, season).
AI.result: SolarTformer significantly outperforms previous models on the same dataset and shows strong performance on both clear and cloudy days.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based architectures have high potential for enhancing solar forecasting accuracy and supporting reliable renewable energy management.

title: MuSS: A Large-Scale Dataset and Cinematic Narrative Benchmark for Multi-Shot Subject-to-Video Generation
summary: While video foundation models excel at single-shot generation, real-world cinematic storytelling inherently relies on complex multi-shot sequencing. Further progress is constrained by the absence of datasets that address three core challenges: authentic narrative logic, spatiotemporal text-video alignment conflicts, and the "copy-paste" dilemma prevalent in Subject-to-Video (S2V) generation. To bridge this gap, we introduce MuSS, a large-scale, dual-track dataset tailored for multi-shot video and S2V generation. Sourced from over 3,000 movies, MuSS explicitly supports both complex montage transitions and subject-centric narratives. To construct this dataset, we pioneer a progressive captioning pipeline that eliminates contextual conflicts by ensuring local shot-level accuracy before enforcing global narrative coherence. Crucially, we implement a cross-shot matching mechanism to fundamentally eradicate the S2V copy-paste shortcut. Alongside the dataset, we propose the Cinematic Narrative Benchmark, featuring a visual-logic-driven paradigm and a novel Anti-Copy-Paste Variance (ACP-Var) metric to rigorously assess continuous storytelling and 3D structural consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that while current baselines struggle with continuous narrative logic or degenerate into trivial 2D sticker generators, our MuSS-augmented model achieves state-of-the-art narrative effectiveness and cross-shot identity preservation.

AI.tldr: MuSS is a large-scale, dual-track dataset from 3,000+ movies for multi-shot video and Subject-to-Video generation, featuring a progressive captioning pipeline, cross-shot matching to prevent copy-paste shortcuts, and a Cinematic Narrative Benchmark with ACP-Var metric.
AI.motivation: Current video foundation models excel at single-shot generation but fail at multi-shot storytelling due to the absence of datasets addressing authentic narrative logic, spatiotemporal text-video conflicts, and the copy-paste dilemma in Subject-to-Video generation.
AI.method: Constructed MuSS dataset from 3,000+ movies with a progressive captioning pipeline ensuring local shot accuracy before global coherence, plus a cross-shot matching mechanism to eliminate S2V copy-paste shortcuts. Proposes Cinematic Narrative Benchmark with visual-logic-driven paradigm and ACP-Var metric.
AI.result: Current baselines struggle with continuous narrative logic or degenerate into 2D sticker generators; MuSS-augmented model achieves state-of-the-art narrative effectiveness and cross-shot identity preservation.
AI.conclusion: MuSS effectively bridges the gap in multi-shot video generation by providing authentic narrative data and evaluation metrics, enabling models to produce coherent cinematic storytelling while preserving subject identity across shots.

title: Self-Abstraction Learning for Effective and Stable Training of Deep Neural Networks
comment: Submitted to IEEE Access. Under review
summary: Training large-scale deep neural networks effectively and stably is essential for applying deep learning across various fields. However, conventional methods, which rely on training a single large network, often encounter challenges such as gradient vanishing, overfitting and unstable learning. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Self-Abstraction Learning (SAL), a hierarchical framework. In SAL, networks are arranged by structural complexity, where the simplest topmost network is trained first and its hidden and output layers serve as guidance for the successively more complex networks below. This top-down sequential guidance effectively mitigates optimization issues, enabling stable training of deep architectures. Various experiments across MLP, CNN, and RNN architectures demonstrate that SAL consistently outperforms conventional methods, ensuring robust generalization even in data-scarce and complex network regimes.

AI.tldr: Self-Abstraction Learning (SAL) is a hierarchical framework that trains networks from simplest (top) to most complex (bottom), using simpler networks to guide deeper ones, improving stability and performance across MLP, CNN, and RNN architectures.
AI.motivation: Conventional training of single large deep networks suffers from gradient vanishing, overfitting, and unstable learning, especially in data-scarce or complex network regimes.
AI.method: SAL arranges networks by structural complexity, trains the simplest topmost network first, then uses its hidden and output layers as guidance for successively more complex networks below in a top-down sequential manner.
AI.result: SAL consistently outperforms conventional methods across MLP, CNN, and RNN architectures, ensuring robust generalization even with limited data or complex networks.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical top-down guidance in SAL effectively mitigates optimization issues, enabling stable and effective training of deep architectures.

title: VitaminP: cross-modal learning enables whole-cell segmentation from routine histology
comment: 44 pages, 10 figures. Code and models available
summary: Accurate whole-cell and nuclear segmentation is essential for precision pathology and spatial omics, yet routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining provides limited cytoplasmic contrast, restricting analyses to nuclei. Multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) facilitates precise whole-cell delineation but remains constrained by cost and accessibility. We introduce VitaminP, a cross-modal learning framework enabling whole cell segmentation from H&E images. By learning from paired H&E-mIF data, VitaminP transfers molecular boundary information from mIF to overcome cytoplasmic contrast in H&E, establishing cross-modal supervision as a general strategy for recovering missing biological structure. We train VitaminP on 14 public datasets covering 34 cancer types and over 7 million instances, integrating publicly available labels with extensive annotations generated in this study, forming one of the largest resources for segmentation. VitaminP outperforms four state-of-the-art methods and generalizes to unseen datasets, including an in-house dataset spanning 24 rare cancer types. We further developed VitaminPScope, an open-source platform providing an interface for scalable inference and enabling broad adoption.

AI.tldr: VitaminP is a cross-modal learning framework that uses paired H&E and multiplex immunofluorescence data to enable whole-cell segmentation from H&E images, overcoming the cytoplasmic contrast limitations of standard H&E staining. It was trained on 14 datasets covering 34 cancer types and over 7 million instances, outperforms four state-of-the-art methods, and is deployed via the open-source platform VitaminPScope.
AI.motivation: Whole-cell and nuclear segmentation is critical for precision pathology and spatial omics, but H&E staining lacks cytoplasmic contrast, restricting analyses to nuclei. Multiplex immunofluorescence (mIF) enables whole-cell delineation but is costly and inaccessible. The authors aim to transfer molecular boundary information from mIF to H&E to recover missing biological structure.
AI.method: VitaminP uses a cross-modal learning framework trained on paired H&E-mIF data to learn molecular boundary information from mIF and apply it to H&E images, overcoming the cytoplasmic contrast limitation. The model was trained on 14 public datasets covering 34 cancer types and over 7 million instances, integrating public labels with extensive new annotations.
AI.result: VitaminP outperforms four state-of-the-art methods in whole-cell segmentation from H&E images and generalizes to unseen datasets, including an in-house dataset of 24 rare cancer types.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal supervision is a generalizable strategy for recovering missing biological structure in H&E images. The open-source platform VitaminPScope enables scalable inference and broad adoption of the method.

title: Mitigating Error Amplification in Fast Adversarial Training
summary: Fast Adversarial Training (FAT) has proven effective in enhancing model robustness by encouraging networks to learn perturbation-invariant representations. However, FAT often suffers from catastrophic overfitting (CO), where the model overfits to the training attack and fails to generalize to unseen ones. Moreover, robustness oriented optimization typically leads to notable performance degradation on clean inputs, and such degradation becomes increasingly severe as the perturbation budget grows. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of how guidance strength affects model performance by modulating perturbation and supervision levels across distinct confidence groups. The findings reveal that low confidence samples are the primary contributors to CO and the robustness accuracy trade off. Building on this insight, we propose a Distribution-aware Dynamic Guidance (DDG) strategy that dynamically adjusts both the perturbation budget and supervision signal. Specifically, DDG scales the perturbation magnitude according to the sample confidence at the ground truth class, thereby guiding samples toward consistent decision boundaries while mitigating the influence of learning spurious correlations. Simultaneously, it dynamically adjusts the supervision signal based on the prediction state of each sample, preventing overemphasis on incorrect signals. To alleviate potential gradient instability arising from dynamic guidance, we further design a weighted regularization constraint. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that DDG effectively alleviates both CO and the robustness accuracy trade off.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Distribution-aware Dynamic Guidance (DDG), a strategy that dynamically adjusts perturbation budgets and supervision signals based on sample confidence to mitigate catastrophic overfitting and the robustness-accuracy trade-off in Fast Adversarial Training.
AI.motivation: FAT suffers from catastrophic overfitting (CO) and robustness-accuracy trade-off, where model overfits to training attacks and clean accuracy degrades especially under larger perturbation budgets. The authors want to understand how guidance strength affects performance across different confidence groups.
AI.method: DDG dynamically scales perturbation magnitude based on sample confidence at the ground truth class, adjusts supervision signals according to each sample's prediction state, and applies weighted regularization to prevent gradient instability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show DDG effectively alleviates both catastrophic overfitting and the robustness-accuracy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Low confidence samples are the primary contributors to CO and robustness-accuracy degradation. Dynamic guidance tailored to sample confidence can effectively address these issues.

title: Bringing a Personal Point of View: Evaluating Dynamic 3D Gaussian Splatting for Egocentric Scene Reconstruction
comment: Accepted at the EgoVis Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Egocentric video provides a unique view into human perception and interaction, with growing relevance for augmented reality, robotics, and assistive technologies. However, rapid camera motion and complex scene dynamics pose major challenges for 3D reconstruction from this perspective. While 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has become a state-of-the-art method for efficient, high-quality novel view synthesis, variants, that focus on reconstructing dynamic scenes from monocular video are rarely evaluated on egocentric video. It remains unclear whether existing models generalize to this setting or if egocentric-specific solutions are needed. In this work, we evaluate dynamic monocular 3DGS models on egocentric and exocentric video using paired ego-exo recordings from the EgoExo4D dataset. We find that reconstruction quality is consistently lower in egocentric views. Analysis reveals that the difference in reconstruction quality, measured in peak signal-to-noise ratio, stems from the reconstruction of static, not dynamic, content. Our findings underscore current limitations and motivate the development of egocentric-specific approaches, while also highlighting the value of separately evaluating static and dynamic regions of a video.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates dynamic monocular 3D Gaussian Splatting models on egocentric vs. exocentric video using EgoExo4D dataset, finding egocentric views have consistently lower reconstruction quality, primarily due to static content reconstruction issues, highlighting the need for egocentric-specific approaches.
AI.motivation: Egocentric video is increasingly important for AR, robotics, and assistive tech, but existing dynamic 3DGS models are rarely evaluated on this challenging domain with rapid camera motion and complex dynamics. It's unclear if current models generalize or if egocentric-specific solutions are needed.
AI.method: The authors evaluate dynamic monocular 3DGS models on both egocentric and exocentric video using paired ego-exo recordings from the EgoExo4D dataset, and analyze reconstruction quality via PSNR, separately examining static and dynamic content.
AI.result: Reconstruction quality (PSNR) is consistently lower in egocentric views compared to exocentric views. The quality gap is primarily driven by differences in reconstructing static content, not dynamic content.
AI.conclusion: Current dynamic monocular 3DGS models underperform on egocentric video, motivating the development of egocentric-specific approaches. The paper also highlights the importance of separately evaluating static and dynamic regions when assessing reconstruction quality.

title: Perfecting Aircraft Maneuvers with Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper evaluates an advanced jet trainer's utilization of artificial intelligence (AI)-based aircraft aerobatic maneuvers with the intention of developing an AI-assisted pilot training module for specific aircraft maneuvers. A multitude of aircraft maneuvers have been simulated using reinforcement learning (RL) agents, which will serve as a training tool for future pilots.

AI.tldr: This paper explores the use of AI-based aerobatic maneuvers in an advanced jet trainer, using reinforcement learning to simulate maneuvers for an AI-assisted pilot training module.
AI.motivation: To develop an AI-assisted pilot training module that can help train future pilots more effectively for specific aircraft maneuvers.
AI.method: Simulating aircraft maneuvers using reinforcement learning (RL) agents.
AI.result: A multitude of aircraft maneuvers have been successfully simulated using RL agents.
AI.conclusion: The simulated maneuvers serve as a viable training tool for future pilots.

title: ShredBench: Evaluating the Semantic Reasoning Capabilities of Multimodal LLMs in Document Reconstruction
comment: ACL 2026 Findings. Code available at https://github.com/ythere-y/ShredBench
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable performance in Visually Rich Document Understanding (VRDU) tasks, but their capabilities are mainly evaluated on pristine, well-structured document images. We consider content restoration from shredded fragments, a challenging VRDU setting that requires integrating visual pattern recognition with semantic reasoning under significant content discontinuities. To facilitate systematic evaluation of complex VRDU tasks, we introduce ShredBench, a benchmark supported by an automated generation pipeline that renders fragmented documents directly from Markdown. The proposed pipeline ensures evaluation validity by allowing the flexible integration of latest or unseen textual sources to prevent training data contamination. ShredBench assesses four scenarios (English, Chinese, Code, Table) with three fragmentation granularities (8, 12, 16 pieces). Empirical evaluations on state-of-the-art MLLMs reveal a significant performance gap: The method is effective on intact documents; however, once the document is shredded, restoration becomes a significant challenge, with NED dropping sharply as fragmentation increases. Our findings highlight that current MLLMs lack the fine-grained cross-modal reasoning required to bridge visual discontinuities, identifying a critical gap in robust VRDU research.

AI.tldr: ShredBench is a benchmark for evaluating MLLMs on content restoration from shredded document fragments, revealing a significant performance gap compared to intact document processing.
AI.motivation: Current MLLMs are evaluated mainly on pristine documents; the authors aim to assess performance under realistic, fragmented conditions where visual discontinuities require cross-modal reasoning.
AI.method: An automated pipeline generates fragmented documents from Markdown, supporting four scenarios (English, Chinese, Code, Table) with three fragmentation granularities (8, 12, 16 pieces), while preventing data contamination.
AI.result: SOTA MLLMs perform well on intact documents but struggle significantly with shredded fragments; NED (Normalized Edit Distance) drops sharply as fragmentation increases.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs lack fine-grained cross-modal reasoning to bridge visual discontinuities, identifying a critical gap in robust VRDU research.

title: Unveiling the Backdoor Mechanism Hidden Behind Catastrophic Overfitting in Fast Adversarial Training
summary: Fast Adversarial Training (FAT) has attracted significant attention due to its efficiency in enhancing neural network robustness against adversarial attacks. However, FAT is prone to catastrophic overfitting (CO), wherein models overfit to the specific attack used during training and fail to generalize to others. While existing methods introduce diverse hypotheses and propose various strategies to mitigate CO, a systematic and intuitive explanation of CO remains absent. In this work, we innovatively interpret CO through the lens of backdoor. Through validations on pathway division, diverse feature predictions, and universal class distinguishable triggers in CO, we conceptualize CO as a weak trigger variant of unlearnable tasks, unifying CO, backdoor attacks, and unlearnable tasks under a common theoretical framework. Guided by this, we leverage several backdoor inspired strategies to mitigate CO: (i) Recalibrate CO affected model parameters using vanilla fine tuning, linear probing, or reinitialization-based techniques; (ii) Introduce a weight outlier suppression constraint to regulate abnormal deviations in model weights. Extensive experiments support our interpretation of CO and show the efficacy of the proposed mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper interprets catastrophic overfitting (CO) in Fast Adversarial Training through the lens of backdoor attacks, unifying CO, backdoor attacks, and unlearnable tasks under a common framework, and proposes backdoor-inspired mitigation strategies.
AI.motivation: Existing methods lack a systematic and intuitive explanation for why catastrophic overfitting occurs in Fast Adversarial Training despite proposing various mitigation strategies.
AI.method: The authors (1) validate CO as a weak trigger variant of unlearnable tasks through pathway division, feature predictions, and trigger analysis; (2) propose backdoor-inspired mitigation strategies: vanilla fine-tuning, linear probing, reinitialization-based techniques, and a weight outlier suppression constraint.
AI.result: Extensive experiments support the interpretation of CO as a backdoor-like phenomenon and demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Catastrophic overfitting can be understood as a weak trigger variant of unlearnable tasks, and backdoor-inspired strategies effectively mitigate CO in Fast Adversarial Training.

title: Mapping License Plate Recoverability Under Extreme Viewing Angles for Oppor-tunistic Urban Sensing
summary: Urban environments contain many imaging sensors built for specific purposes, including ATM, body-worn, CCTV, and dashboard cameras. Under the opportunistic sensing paradigm, these sensors can be repurposed for secondary inference tasks such as license plate recognition. Yet objects of interest in such imagery are often noisy, low-resolution, and captured from extreme viewpoints. Recent advances in AI-based restoration can recover use-ful information even from severely degraded images. A central challenge is determining which distortion parame-ters allow reliable recovery and which lead to inference failure. This paper introduces recoverability maps, a task-agnostic method for quantifying this boundary. The method combines a dense synthetic sweep of degrada-tion parameters with two summary measures: boundary area-under-curve, which estimates the recoverable frac-tion of the parameter space, and a reliability score, which captures the frequency and severity of failures within that region. We demonstrate the method on license plate recognition from highly angled views under realistic camera artifacts. Several restoration architectures are trained and evaluated, including U-Net, Restormer, Pix2Pix, and SR3 diffusion. The best model recovers about 93% of the parameter space. Similar results across models sug-gest that sensing geometry, rather than architecture, sets the limit of recovery.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces recoverability maps, a task-agnostic method to quantify when degraded images can be successfully restored for secondary inference tasks, demonstrated on license plate recognition from extreme angles.
AI.motivation: Opportunistic sensing repurposes existing cameras (ATMs, body-worn, CCTV) for tasks like license plate recognition, but images are often noisy, low-resolution, and from extreme viewpoints. A key challenge is determining which distortion parameters allow reliable recovery versus lead to failure.
AI.method: A dense synthetic sweep of degradation parameters is combined with two summary measures: (1) boundary area-under-curve (recoverable fraction of parameter space), and (2) reliability score (frequency/severity of failures). Multiple restoration architectures (U-Net, Restormer, Pix2Pix, SR3 diffusion) are trained and evaluated.
AI.result: The best model recovers about 93% of the parameter space. Similar results across different architectures indicate that sensing geometry (not architecture) sets the fundamental limit of recovery.
AI.conclusion: Recoverability maps provide a principled, task-agnostic method to quantify the boundary between recoverable and non-recoverable image degradations, with implications for designing robust opportunistic sensing systems.

title: Diffusion Templates: A Unified Plugin Framework for Controllable Diffusion
summary: Controllable diffusion methods have substantially expanded the practical utility of diffusion models, but they are typically developed as isolated, backbone-specific systems with incompatible training pipelines, parameter formats, and runtime hooks. This fragmentation makes it difficult to reuse infrastructure across tasks, transfer capabilities across backbones, or compose multiple controls within a single generation pipeline. We present Diffusion Templates, a unified and open plugin framework that decouples base-model inference from controllable capability injection. The framework is organized around three components: Template models that map arbitrary task-specific inputs to an intermediate capability representation, a Template cache that functions as a standardized interface for capability injection, and a Template pipeline that loads, merges, and injects one or more Template caches into the base diffusion runtime. Because the interface is defined at the systems level rather than tied to a specific control architecture, heterogeneous capability carriers such as KV-Cache and LoRA can be supported under the same abstraction. Based on this design, we build a diverse model zoo spanning structural control, brightness adjustment, color adjustment, image editing, super-resolution, sharpness enhancement, aesthetic alignment, content reference, local inpainting, and age control. These case studies show that Diffusion Templates can unify a broad range of controllable generation tasks while preserving modularity, composability, and practical extensibility across rapidly evolving diffusion backbones. All resources will be open sourced, including code, models, and datasets.

AI.tldr: Diffusion Templates is a unified plugin framework that decouples base diffusion model inference from controllable capability injection, supporting composable, backbone-agnostic controls via a standardized Template cache interface.
AI.motivation: Existing controllable diffusion methods are fragmented as isolated, backbone-specific systems with incompatible training pipelines and runtime hooks, making reuse, transfer, and composition difficult.
AI.method: A three-component framework: Template models (map task inputs to intermediate representations), Template cache (standardized interface for capability injection), and Template pipeline (loads, merges, and injects caches into base diffusion runtime). Supports heterogeneous carriers like KV-Cache and LoRA under one abstraction.
AI.result: Built a diverse model zoo spanning 10 tasks (structural control, brightness/color adjustment, image editing, super-resolution, sharpness, aesthetic alignment, content reference, inpainting, age control), demonstrating unified support with modularity and composability.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion Templates effectively unifies a broad range of controllable generation tasks while preserving modularity, composability, and extensibility across evolving backbones; all resources will be open-sourced.

title: Focus on What Matters: Two-Stage ROI-Aware Refinement for Anatomy-Preserving Fetal Ultrasound Reconstruction
comment: 18 pages, 7 figures, multiple tables. Preprint submitted to arXiv
summary: Measurement-critical ultrasound tasks often depend on a small anatomical region, making global reconstruction metrics an unreliable proxy for clinical fidelity. We propose an ROI-aware representation learning framework and instantiate it for first-trimester nuchal translucency (NT) screening under multi-hospital domain shift. A two-phase convolutional autoencoder (CAE) first learns a globally faithful 128-D latent code via MS-SSIM, then refines the NT ROI using intensity (L1) and normalized Sobel-edge constraints. To combine these heterogeneous objectives without manual tuning, we initialize loss weights via gradient-based calibration from per-term gradient magnitudes. Under strict hospital-wise evaluation with one hospital held out, ROI refinement improves both global and measurement-relevant quality: on the standard dev split it increases PSNR by +0.27 dB (val) and +0.29 dB (held-out test), reduces ROI MAE by 8.87% (val) and 6.43% (held-out test), and reduces ROI Edge-MAE by 11.10% on source hospitals and 4.90% on the unseen hospital. Beyond reconstruction, frozen-latent probes provide additional evidence of generalization: hospital provenance becomes less confidently predictable on the unseen site (0.556 to 0.541 max-softmax; 0.684 to 0.688 entropy) while OOD detection remains strong across site-held-out protocols (Mahalanobis AUROC up to 0.9956, with modest KNN gains in challenging splits). The same ROI-aware refinement principle is anatomy-agnostic and can be adopted for other fetal biometry targets (e.g., crown-rump length (CRL), nasal bone (NB)) and broader medical imaging settings where small ROIs dominate clinical decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an ROI-aware representation learning framework for ultrasound, applied to nuchal translucency screening, using a two-phase convolutional autoencoder with gradient-based loss weighting. It improves global and ROI-specific quality metrics while enhancing generalization across hospital domains.
AI.motivation: Global reconstruction metrics are unreliable for clinical fidelity in measurement-critical ultrasound tasks where decisions depend on small anatomical regions. Manual tuning of heterogeneous loss objectives is also impractical.
AI.method: A two-phase convolutional autoencoder: first learns globally faithful 128-D latent code via MS-SSIM, then refines NT ROI using L1 and normalized Sobel-edge constraints. Loss weights are initialized via gradient-based calibration from per-term gradient magnitudes.
AI.result: ROI refinement improves PSNR (+0.27/0.29 dB), reduces ROI MAE (8.87%/6.43%), reduces ROI Edge-MAE (11.10%/4.90%). Frozen-latent probes show improved generalization (lower max-softmax confidence on unseen sites) while maintaining strong OOD detection (Mahalanobis AUROC up to 0.9956).
AI.conclusion: The ROI-aware refinement principle is anatomy-agnostic and applicable to other fetal biometry targets (CRL, NB) and broader medical imaging where small ROIs drive clinical decisions.

title: An Aircraft Upset Recovery System with Reinforcement Learning
summary: This article explores the progress made in the creation of a pilot activated recovery system (PARS) for advanced jet trainers that utilizes artificial intelligence (AI) in an effort to enhance operational efficiency. The PARS model employs an advanced reinforcement learning (RL) architecture, incorporating a cutting-edge soft-actor critic (SAC) model and hyper-parameter optimization methods. Negative-g punishments and other handcrafted features remarked upon by control engineers and domain experts regarding PARS are also taken into account by the system. When evaluated by them, the AI model's behavior is deemed more desirable than that of conventional control methods.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an AI-driven Pilot Activated Recovery System (PARS) for advanced jet trainers, using a Soft Actor-Critic reinforcement learning model with hyper-parameter optimization and expert-informed features like negative-g punishments, which outperforms conventional control methods.
AI.motivation: To enhance operational efficiency of pilot recovery systems in advanced jet trainers by leveraging AI to improve upon conventional control methods.
AI.method: Develops a PARS using a Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning architecture with hyper-parameter optimization, incorporating negative-g punishments and handcrafted features from domain experts.
AI.result: The AI model's behavior was deemed more desirable than conventional control methods when evaluated by control engineers and domain experts.
AI.conclusion: AI-enhanced reinforcement learning (SAC) offers superior performance for pilot activated recovery systems compared to traditional control approaches.

title: Latent Inter-Frame Pruning: A Training-Free Method Bridging Traditional Video Compression and Modern Diffusion Transformers for Efficient Generation
summary: Video generation, while capable of generating realistic videos, is computationally expensive and slow, prohibiting real-time applications. In this paper, we observe that video latents encoded via an autoencoder under the Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) framework contain redundancy along the temporal axis. Analogous to how traditional video compression algorithms avoid transmitting redundant frame data, we propose the Latent Inter-frame Pruning framework to prune (skip the re-computation of) duplicated latent patches, thereby reducing computational burden and increasing throughput. However, direct pruning results in visual artifacts due to the discrepancy between full-sequence training and pruned inference. To resolve these artifacts, we propose an Attention Recovery mechanism to bridge the train-inference gap. With our proposed method, we increase video editing throughput by 1.44$\times$, achieving 12.44 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 6000 while maintaining video quality. We hope our work inspires further research into integrating traditional video compression methods with modern video generation pipelines. This work is a preliminary work on Training-free Latent Inter-Frame Pruning with Attention Recovery.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Latent Inter-frame Pruning to skip redundant temporal latent patches in video LDMs, plus Attention Recovery to fix train-inference mismatch, yielding 1.44× throughput gains (12.44 FPS on RTX 6000) without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Video generation under LDM is too slow for real-time use; temporally duplicated latent patches waste computation.
AI.method: (1) Latent Inter-frame Pruning: skip re-computation of temporally redundant latent patches, inspired by video compression. (2) Attention Recovery mechanism: bridge the gap between full-sequence training and pruned inference to avoid visual artifacts.
AI.result: 1.44× throughput increase for video editing, reaching 12.44 FPS on an NVIDIA RTX 6000 while maintaining video quality.
AI.conclusion: Integrating traditional video compression ideas (inter-frame redundancy removal) into modern video LDMs is promising for real-time applications; this preliminary work encourages further exploration.

title: DPRM: A Plug-in Doob h transform-induced Token-Ordering Module for Diffusion Language Models
summary:
Diffusion language models generate without a fixed left-to-right order, making token ordering a central algorithmic choice: which tokens should be revealed, retained, revised or verified at each step? Existing systems mainly use random masking or confidence-driven ordering. Random masking creates train--test mismatch, while confidence-only rules are efficient but can be myopic and suppress useful exploration.
  We introduce DPRM (Doob h-transform Process Reward Model), a plug-in token-ordering module for diffusion language models. DPRM keeps the host architecture, denoising objective and supervision unchanged, and changes only the ordering policy. It starts from confidence-driven progressive ordering and gradually shifts to Doob h transform Process Reward guided ordering through online estimates.
  We characterize the exact DPRM policy as a reward-tilted Gibbs reveal law, prove O(1/N) convergence of the stagewise Soft-BoN approximation, and show that the online bucketized controller tracks the exact DPRM score at empirical-Bernstein rates. Under tractable optimization assumptions, DPRM also yields a sample-complexity advantage over random and confidence-only ordering.
  DPRM improves over confidence-based baselines in pretraining, post-training, test-time scaling, and single-cell masked diffusion, with particularly strong gains on harder reasoning subsets. In protein, molecular generation and DNA design, the effect is more multi-objective: ordering-aware variants significantly improve selected structural or fragment-constrained metrics while not uniformly dominating the host baseline on every quality metric. These results identify token ordering as a fundamental control axis in diffusion language models and establish DPRM as a general-purpose module for improving it. Code is available at https://github.com/DakeBU/DPRM-DLLM.

AI.tldr: DPRM is a plug-in token-ordering module for diffusion language models that replaces random/confidence-only ordering with a Doob h-transform Process Reward guided policy, improving performance across text, protein, molecular, and DNA generation tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion language models rely on random masking (causing train-test mismatch) or confidence-driven ordering (myopic, suppresses exploration). The paper argues token ordering is a central but underexplored algorithmic choice.
AI.method: DPRM starts from confidence-driven progressive ordering and gradually shifts to Doob h-transform Process Reward Model guided ordering via online estimates. It derives a reward-tilted Gibbs reveal law, proves O(1/N) convergence of stagewise Soft-BoN approximation, and uses an online bucketized controller with empirical-Bernstein rate guarantees.
AI.result: DPRM outperforms confidence-based baselines in pretraining, post-training, test-time scaling, and single-cell masked diffusion, especially on harder reasoning subsets. In protein, molecule, and DNA domains, it improves selected structural/fragment metrics.
AI.conclusion: Token ordering is a fundamental control axis in diffusion language models, and DPRM is a general-purpose plug-in module for improving it across diverse domains and settings.

title: Exploring Audio Hallucination in Egocentric Video Understanding
summary: Egocentric videos provide a distinctive setting in which sound serves as crucial cues to understand user activities and surroundings, particularly when visual information is unstable or occluded due to continuous camera movement. State-of-the-art large audio-visual language models (AV-LLMs) can generate multimodal descriptions. However, we show in this work that they are prone to audio hallucinations, often inferring sounds from visual cues that are visible but not heard. We present a systematic and automatic evaluation framework for analyzing audio hallucinations in egocentric video through a targeted question-answering (Q/A) protocol. We curate a dataset of 300 egocentric videos and design 1,000 sound-focused questions to probe model outputs. To characterize hallucinations, we propose a grounded taxonomy that distinguishes between foreground action sounds from the user activities and background ambient sounds. Our evaluation shows that advanced AV-LLMs, such as Qwen2.5 Omni, exhibit high hallucination rates, achieving only 27.3% and 39.5% accuracy on Q/As related to foreground and background sounds, respectively. With this work, we highlight the need to measure the reliability of multimodal responses, emphasizing that robust evaluation of hallucinations is essential to develop reliable AV-LLMs.

AI.tldr: This paper reveals that large audio-visual language models (AV-LLMs) suffer from audio hallucinations in egocentric videos, where they infer sounds from visual cues that are not actually audible. The authors create a dataset of 300 egocentric videos with 1,000 sound-focused Q/As and a taxonomy distinguishing foreground vs. background sounds, finding that models like Qwen2.5 Omni achieve only 27.3% and 39.5% accuracy on these tasks.
AI.motivation: Audio hallucination in AV-LLMs is understudied, especially in egocentric videos where sound is crucial due to unstable visual information. The authors want to systematically measure and highlight this problem.
AI.method: The authors propose a systematic evaluation framework using a targeted Q/A protocol. They curate 300 egocentric videos, design 1,000 sound-focused questions, and develop a grounded taxonomy distinguishing foreground action sounds and background ambient sounds to characterize hallucinations.
AI.result: Advanced AV-LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5 Omni) achieve only 27.3% accuracy on foreground sound Q/As and 39.5% on background sound Q/As, indicating high hallucination rates.
AI.conclusion: Robust evaluation of audio hallucinations is essential for developing reliable AV-LLMs, and current models are far from trustworthy in egocentric video understanding.

title: SAGE: Sparse Adaptive Guidance for Dependency-Aware Tabular Data Generation
summary: Generating high-fidelity synthetic tabular data remains a critical challenge for enhancing data availability in privacy-sensitive and low-resource domains. Recent approaches leverage LLMs by representing table rows as sequences, yet suffer from two fundamental limitations: (1) they model feature dependencies densely, introducing spurious correlations; and (2) they assume static relationships between features, ignoring how these dependencies vary with feature values. To overcome these limitations, we introduce SAGE (Sparse Adaptive Guidance), a novel LLM-based generation framework that enforces sparse and dynamic dependency guidance. SAGE discretizes features into value-aware pseudo-features and constructs a mutual information-based sparse dependency graph. This graph adaptively guides generation through explicit context selection or implicit logit correction, enabling LLMs to focus on truly relevant information during synthesis. Our extensive experiments across six datasets and multiple tasks reveal that SAGE not only improves data fidelity and downstream utility, boosting F1 scores by 10% compared to previous LLM-based methods, but also reduces policy violations by one point. These results highlight the importance of adaptive structure in tabular data generation and provide new insights into context-sensitive control of LLMs.

AI.tldr: SAGE is a framework that improves LLM-based synthetic tabular data generation by enforcing sparse and adaptive dependency relationships between features, boosting F1 scores by 10% over prior methods while reducing policy violations.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based tabular data generation methods model feature dependencies densely (introducing spurious correlations) and assume static relationships between features, ignoring how dependencies vary with feature values. These limitations reduce data fidelity.
AI.method: SAGE discretizes features into value-aware pseudo-features, constructs a mutual information-based sparse dependency graph, and uses this graph to adaptively guide LLM generation through explicit context selection or implicit logit correction.
AI.result: Across six datasets and multiple tasks, SAGE improves F1 scores by 10% over previous LLM-based methods and reduces policy violations by one point, demonstrating better data fidelity and downstream utility.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, sparse structure is critical for high-quality tabular data generation, and SAGE provides a promising approach for context-sensitive control of LLMs in this domain.

title: Empirical Ablation and Ensemble Optimization of a Convolutional Neural Network for CIFAR-10 Classification
summary: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) remain a central approach in image classification, but their performance depends strongly on architectural and training choices. This paper presents an empirical ablation-based study of CNN optimization for the CIFAR-10 benchmark. The study evaluates 17 progressive modifications involving training duration, learning-rate scheduling, dropout configuration, pooling strategy, network depth, filter arrangement, and dense-layer design. The goal is to identify which changes improve generalization and which increase complexity without improving performance. The baseline model achieved 79.5\% test accuracy. Extending training duration improved performance steadily, whereas several structural redesigns reduced accuracy despite greater architectural variation. Based on the strongest individual configurations, a weighted ensemble was constructed, achieving 86.38\% accuracy in the reduced-data setting and 89.23\% when trained using the full CIFAR-10 dataset. These results suggest that performance gains in CNN-based classification depend less on indiscriminate increases in depth or parameter count than on careful empirical selection of training and architectural modifications. The study therefore highlights the practical value of ablation-oriented optimization and ensemble learning for small-image classification.

AI.tldr: This paper conducts an empirical ablation study on CNN optimization for CIFAR-10, evaluating 17 progressive modifications. The best results (86.38% with reduced data, 89.23% with full data) were achieved through a weighted ensemble of the strongest configurations, showing that careful selection of modifications matters more than simply increasing depth or parameters.
AI.motivation: To systematically identify which CNN architectural and training modifications genuinely improve generalization on CIFAR-10 versus those that increase complexity without performance gains.
AI.method: Empirical ablation-based study evaluating 17 progressive modifications including training duration, learning-rate scheduling, dropout, pooling strategy, network depth, filter arrangement, and dense-layer design, followed by weighted ensemble construction.
AI.result: Baseline: 79.5% accuracy. Best ensemble: 86.38% (reduced data) and 89.23% (full CIFAR-10). Extended training improved performance; several structural redesigns reduced accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Performance gains depend more on careful empirical selection of modifications than on indiscriminate increases in depth or parameter count. Ablation-oriented optimization and ensemble learning are valuable for small-image classification.

title: PathMoG: A Pathway-Centric Modular Graph Neural Network for Multi-Omics Survival Prediction
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables. Source code available at https://github.com/wangzoyou/pathmog
summary: Cancer survival prediction from multi-omics data remains challenging because prognostic signals are high-dimensional, heterogeneous, and distributed across interacting genes and pathways. We propose PathMoG, a pathway-centric modular graph neural network for multi-omics survival prediction. PathMoG reorganizes genome-scale inputs into 354 KEGG-informed pathway modules, introduces a Hierarchical Omics Modulation module to condition gene-expression representations on mutation, copy number variation, pathway, and clinical context, and uses dual-level attention to capture both intra-pathway driver signals and inter-pathway clinical relevance. We evaluated PathMoG on 5,650 patients across 10 TCGA cancer types and observed consistent improvements over representative survival baselines. The framework further provides gene-level, pathway-level, and patient-level interpretability, supporting biologically grounded and clinically relevant risk stratification.

AI.tldr: PathMoG is a pathway-centric modular graph neural network that improves multi-omics cancer survival prediction by reorganizing data into 354 KEGG pathway modules, using hierarchical conditioning and dual-level attention, achieving consistent gains across 5,650 patients and 10 cancer types with multi-level interpretability.
AI.motivation: Cancer survival prediction from multi-omics data is challenging due to high-dimensional, heterogeneous, and distributed prognostic signals across interacting genes and pathways.
AI.method: PathMoG reorganizes genome-scale inputs into 354 KEGG-informed pathway modules, uses a Hierarchical Omics Modulation module to condition gene-expression on mutation, CNV, pathway, and clinical context, and employs dual-level attention for intra-pathway and inter-pathway modeling.
AI.result: Evaluated on 5,650 patients across 10 TCGA cancer types, showing consistent improvements over representative survival baselines.
AI.conclusion: PathMoG provides gene-level, pathway-level, and patient-level interpretability, supporting biologically grounded and clinically relevant risk stratification.

title: Risk-Aware Robust Learning: Reducing Clinical Risk under Label Noise in Medical Image Classification
summary: Noisy labels are a pervasive challenge in medical image classification, where annotation errors arise from inter-observer variability and diagnostic ambiguity. Although several noise-robust learning methods have been proposed, their evaluation predominantly relies on accuracy-oriented metrics, overlooking the clinical implications of asymmetric error costs. In medical diagnosis, a false negative (missed disease) carries substantially higher consequences than a false positive (false alarm), as delayed treatment can directly impact patient outcomes. In this work, we investigate whether noise-robust training methods preserve clinical safety under label noise. We conduct a systematic risk-aware evaluation of the state-of-the-art noise-robust methods Coteaching, DivideMix, UNICON, and a GMM-based filtering approach on binarized DermaMNIST and PathMNIST datasets under clean and label noise rates of 20%, and 40%. Beyond balanced accuracy, we adopt a cost-sensitive Global Risk formulation that explicitly penalizes false negatives. Our analysis reveals that the robustness of state-of-the-art methods does not guarantee clinical safety. Furthermore, we demonstrate that integrating cost-sensitive optimization into noise-robust training significantly reduces clinical risk, while mantaining model utility. These findings demonstrate that noise-robust learning must be evaluated through a clinical risk lens, and that combining robust training with cost-sensitive optimization can meaningfully reduce risk in noisy-label medical imaging scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that state-of-the-art noise-robust training methods (Coteaching, DivideMix, UNICON, GMM filtering) do not guarantee clinical safety under label noise, and integrating cost-sensitive optimization significantly reduces clinical risk by penalizing false negatives more heavily than false positives.
AI.motivation: Noisy labels are common in medical image classification, but existing noise-robust methods are evaluated with accuracy-oriented metrics that ignore the asymmetric costs of errors—especially the high cost of false negatives in clinical settings.
AI.method: Systematic risk-aware evaluation of Coteaching, DivideMix, UNICON, and GMM-based filtering on binarized DermaMNIST and PathMNIST under clean labels and 20%/40% label noise. Used cost-sensitive Global Risk formulation to explicitly penalize false negatives.
AI.result: Noise-robust methods do not guarantee clinical safety; integrating cost-sensitive optimization into noise-robust training significantly reduces clinical risk while maintaining model utility.
AI.conclusion: Noise-robust learning must be evaluated through a clinical risk lens, and combining robust training with cost-sensitive optimization can meaningfully reduce risk in noisy-label medical imaging.

title: Complexity of Linear Regions in Self-supervised Deep ReLU Networks
comment: Accepted for publication in 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition - Findings Track (CVPRF)
summary: There has been growing interest in studying the complexity of Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) based activation networks. Recent work investigates the evolution of the number of piecewise-linear partitions (linear regions) that are formed during training. However, current research is limited to examining the complexity of models trained in a supervised way. Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) differs in that it directly optimises the representation space using a loss function to enhance the model's performance across multiple downstream tasks. This study investigates the local distribution of linear regions produced by SSL models. We demonstrate that the evolution of linear regions correlates with the representation quality by utilising SplineCam to extract two-dimensional polytopes near the data distribution. We track the number, area, eccentricity, and boundaries of regions throughout training. The study compares supervised, contrastive, and self-distillation methods over two standard benchmark datasets, MNIST and FashionMNIST. The analysis of the experimental results shows that self-supervised methods create substantially fewer regions to achieve comparable accuracy to supervised models. Contrastive methods rapidly expand regions over time, whereas self-distillation methods tend to consolidate by merging neighbouring regions. Lastly, we can detect representation collapse early within the geometric space of linear regions. Our analysis suggests that polytopal metrics can serve as reliable indicators of representation quality and model performance.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how self-supervised learning (SSL) models evolve their piecewise-linear regions (ReLU partitions) during training, showing that SSL models create fewer regions than supervised ones, contrastive methods expand regions rapidly, self-distillation merges neighboring regions, and polytopal metrics can detect representation collapse early.
AI.motivation: Prior work on ReLU network complexity (linear regions) focused on supervised learning; this paper extends analysis to self-supervised learning (SSL) to understand how representation quality relates to the geometric partitioning of the input space.
AI.method: Using SplineCam, the authors extract 2D polytopes near the data distribution and track region count, area, eccentricity, and boundaries across training. They compare supervised, contrastive (SimCLR), and self-distillation (BYOL) methods on MNIST and FashionMNIST.
AI.result: SSL models produce substantially fewer linear regions than supervised models at comparable accuracy. Contrastive methods rapidly expand regions; self-distillation merges neighboring regions. Representation collapse can be detected early via geometric region metrics.
AI.conclusion: Polytopal metrics (region count, area, eccentricity) serve as reliable indicators of representation quality and model performance in SSL, enabling early detection of representation collapse.

title: Mammographic Lesion Segmentation with Lightweight Models: A Comparative Study
summary: Breast cancer is a leading cause of cancer-related mortality among women worldwide, with mammography as the primary screening tool. While deep learning models have shown strong performance in lesion segmentation, most rely on computationally intensive architectures that limit their use in resource-constrained environments. This study evaluates the performance and efficiency of lightweight models for mammographic lesion segmentation. Architectures including MobileNetV2, EfficientNet Lite, ENet, and Fast-SCNN were compared against a U-Net baseline using the INbreast dataset with 5-fold cross-validation. Performance was assessed using Dice score, Intersection over Union (IoU), and Recall, alongside model complexity. MobileNetV2 with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SCSE) achieved the best performance, with a Dice score of 0.5766 while using approximately 75\% fewer parameters than U-Net. Cross-dataset evaluation on the DMID dataset showed reduced accuracy due to domain shift but preserved recall. These results demonstrate that lightweight architectures offer a practical balance between performance and efficiency for deployable CAD systems.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates lightweight deep learning models (MobileNetV2, EfficientNet Lite, ENet, Fast-SCNN) against U-Net for mammographic lesion segmentation. MobileNetV2 with SCSE achieved the best Dice score (0.5766) with 75% fewer parameters than U-Net, showing practical balance between performance and efficiency for deployable CAD systems.
AI.motivation: Breast cancer screening via mammography is critical, but existing deep learning segmentation models are computationally intensive and unsuitable for resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: Compared lightweight architectures (MobileNetV2, EfficientNet Lite, ENet, Fast-SCNN) against U-Net baseline on INbreast dataset using 5-fold cross-validation. Metrics: Dice score, IoU, Recall, and model complexity. Cross-dataset evaluation on DMID dataset.
AI.result: MobileNetV2 with SCSE achieved Dice score of 0.5766 using ~75% fewer parameters than U-Net. Cross-dataset evaluation showed reduced accuracy due to domain shift but preserved recall.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight architectures offer a practical balance between performance and efficiency, making them suitable for deployable computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) systems in resource-constrained settings.

title: AMAVA: Adaptive Motion-Aware Video-to-Audio Framework for Visually-Impaired Assistance
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures. Published in the Proceedings of the 15th International Conference on Pattern Recognition Applications and Methods (ICPRAM 2026), pages 282--289
summary: Navigational aids for blind and low vision individuals struggle conveying dynamic real-world environments, leading to cognitive overload from continuous, undifferentiated feedback. We present AMAVA, a novel real-time video-to-audio framework that converts mobile device video into contextually relevant sound effects or text-to-speech descriptions. We propose a motion-aware pipeline using a lightweight AI classification model to distinguish between low and high-movement scenes followed by a real-time text-to-audio synthesis pipeline to enhance environmental perception more efficiently. In static environments, AMAVA generates spoken audio scene descriptions for situational awareness. In high-movement situations, it prioritizes safety by delivering sound cues, such as spoken hazard alerts and environmental sound effects. These audio outputs are produced by a decoder-only transformer-based vision-language model with mixture-of-experts and cross-modal attention for visual understanding, in conjunction with neural text-to-speech and natural sound synthesis networks. The proposed framework uses prompt-based caching and category-specific throttling to avoid auditory clutter and minimize latency. We present a comprehensive evaluation of the system, including a real-time navigation study comparing a white cane alone versus with AMAVA, that shows a significant increase in user confidence and perceived safety.

AI.tldr: AMAVA is a real-time video-to-audio framework that converts mobile video into context-aware sound effects or speech descriptions, using a motion-aware pipeline to reduce cognitive overload for blind and low vision individuals during navigation.
AI.motivation: Existing navigational aids for blind/low vision individuals struggle with dynamic environments, causing cognitive overload from continuous, undifferentiated feedback.
AI.method: A motion-aware pipeline with a lightweight AI classifier distinguishes low vs. high-movement scenes; uses a decoder-only transformer VLM with MoE and cross-modal attention, plus neural TTS and sound synthesis; employs prompt-based caching and category-specific throttling to reduce clutter and latency.
AI.result: A real-time navigation study comparing white cane alone vs. with AMAVA showed significant increase in user confidence and perceived safety.
AI.conclusion: AMAVA effectively enhances environmental perception for blind/low vision users by dynamically adapting audio feedback based on movement context, reducing cognitive load while improving safety and confidence.

title: Advancing Ligand-based Virtual Screening and Molecular Generation with Pretrained Molecular Embedding Distance
summary: Molecular similarity plays a central role in ligand-based drug discovery, such as virtual screening, analog searching, and goal-directed molecular generation. However, traditional similarity measures, ranging from fingerprint-based Tanimoto coefficients to 3D shape overlays, are often computationally expensive at scale or rely on hand-crafted molecular descriptors. Meanwhile, many deep learning approaches to similarity-aware design still depend on similarity-specific supervision or costly data curation, limiting their generality across targets. In this work, we propose pretrained embedding distance (PED) as an effective alternative, computed directly from pretrained molecular models without task-specific training. Experimental results show that PED exhibits distinct correlations with traditional similarity metrics, and performs effectively in both ranking molecules for virtual screening and guiding molecular generation via reward design. These findings suggest that pretrained molecular embeddings capture rich structural information and can serve as a promising and scalable similarity measurement for modern AI-aided drug discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using pretrained embedding distance (PED) from deep learning models as a scalable, task-free molecular similarity metric that correlates with traditional measures and performs well in virtual screening and goal-directed molecular generation.
AI.motivation: Traditional similarity measures (fingerprint Tanimoto, 3D shape overlays) are computationally expensive or rely on hand-crafted descriptors, and deep learning approaches often require similarity-specific supervision or costly data curation, limiting generality across targets.
AI.method: The authors propose pretrained embedding distance (PED), computed directly from pretrained molecular models without task-specific training, as an alternative similarity metric.
AI.result: PED shows distinct correlations with traditional similarity metrics and performs effectively in ranking molecules for virtual screening and guiding molecular generation via reward design.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained molecular embeddings capture rich structural information and can serve as a promising, scalable similarity measurement for AI-aided drug discovery.

title: 2nd of the 5th PVUW MeViS-Audio Track: ASR-SaSaSa2VA
summary: Audio-based video object segmentation aims to locate and segment objects in videos conditioned on audio cues, requiring precise understanding of both appearance and motion. Recent audio-driven video segmentation methods extend MLLMs by fusing audio and visual features for end-to-end localization. Despite their promise, these approaches are computationally intensive, struggle with aligning temporal audio cues to dynamic video content, and depend on large paired audio-video datasets. To address these challenges, we present ASR-SaSaSa2VA, a resource-efficient framework for audio-guided video segmentation. The key idea is to convert audio inputs into textual motion descriptions via automatic speech recognition (ASR) models and then leverage pre-trained text-based referring video segmentation models (e.g., SaSaSa2VA) for pixel-level predictions. To further enhance robustness, we incorporate a no-target expression detection module, implemented by a fine-tuned audio-based MLLM, which filters out audio clips that do not refer to any target object. This design allows the system to exploit strong pre-trained models while effectively handling ambiguous or irrelevant audio inputs. Our approach achieves a final score of 80.7 in the 5th PVUW Challenge (MeViS-v2-Audio track), earning the second-place ranking.

AI.tldr: ASR-SaSaSa2VA converts audio to text via ASR, then uses text-based video segmentation models for audio-guided segmentation, achieving 80.7 score and 2nd place in MeViS-v2-Audio track.
AI.motivation: Existing audio-driven video segmentation methods are computationally intensive, struggle with aligning temporal audio cues to dynamic video, and depend on large paired audio-video datasets.
AI.method: (1) Convert audio to textual motion descriptions using ASR models. (2) Leverage pre-trained text-based referring video segmentation models (SaSaSa2VA) for pixel-level predictions. (3) Incorporate a no-target expression detection module via fine-tuned audio-based MLLM to filter irrelevant audio clips.
AI.result: Achieved a final score of 80.7 in the 5th PVUW Challenge (MeViS-v2-Audio track), earning second place.
AI.conclusion: The proposed resource-efficient framework effectively exploits strong pre-trained models while handling ambiguous/irrelevant audio inputs, demonstrating competitive performance.

title: SceneSelect: Selective Learning for Trajectory Scene Classification and Expert Scheduling
comment: This paper has been accepted by ICIC 2026
summary: Accurate trajectory prediction is fundamentally challenging due to high scene heterogeneity - the severe variance in motion velocity, spatial density, and interaction patterns across different real-world environments. However, most existing approaches typically train a single unified model, expecting a fixed-capacity architecture to generalize universally across all possible scenarios. This conventional model-centric paradigm is fundamentally flawed when confronting such extreme heterogeneity, inevitably leading to a severe generalization gap, degraded accuracy, and massive computational waste. To overcome this bottleneck, rather than refining restricted model-centric architectures, we propose selective learning, a novel scene-centric paradigm. It explicitly analyzes the characteristics of the underlying scene to dynamically route inputs to the most appropriate expert models. As a concrete implementation of this paradigm, we introduce SceneSelect. Specifically, SceneSelect utilizes unsupervised clustering on interpretable geometric and kinematic features to discover a latent scene taxonomy. A highly decoupled classification module is then trained to assign real-time inputs to these scene categories, and a highly extensible, plug-and-play scheduling policy automatically dispatches the trajectory sequence to the optimal expert predictor. Crucially, this decoupled design ensures excellent generalization capabilities, allowing seamless integration with different off-the-shelf models and robust adaptation across new datasets without requiring computationally expensive joint retraining. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks (ETH-UCY, SDD, and NBA) demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms strong single-model and ensemble baselines, achieving an average improvement of 10.5%, showcasing the effectiveness of scene-aware selective learning.

AI.tldr: SceneSelect proposes a scene-centric selective learning paradigm that dynamically routes trajectory inputs to specialized expert models based on scene characteristics, outperforming unified single-model approaches by 10.5% on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing trajectory prediction models use a single unified model to handle all scenarios, but high scene heterogeneity (varying velocity, density, and interaction patterns) causes generalization gaps, accuracy degradation, and computational waste.
AI.method: SceneSelect uses unsupervised clustering on geometric and kinematic features to discover a latent scene taxonomy, trains a decoupled classification module for real-time scene assignment, and employs a plug-and-play scheduling policy to dispatch inputs to the optimal expert predictor.
AI.result: Achieves consistent 10.5% average improvement over strong single-model and ensemble baselines on ETH-UCY, SDD, and NBA benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The decoupled, scene-centric design enables excellent generalization, seamless integration with off-the-shelf models, and robust cross-dataset adaptation without joint retraining.

title: GoClick: Lightweight Element Grounding Model for Autonomous GUI Interaction
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) element grounding (precisely locating elements on screenshots based on natural language instructions) is fundamental for agents interacting with GUIs. Deploying this capability directly on resource-constrained devices like mobile phones is increasingly critical for GUI agents requiring low latency. However, this goal faces a significant challenge, as current visual grounding methods typically employ large vision-language model (VLM) (more than 2.5B parameters), making them impractical for on-device execution due to memory and computational constraints. To address this, this paper introduces GoClick, a lightweight GUI element grounding VLM with only 230M parameters that achieves excellent visual grounding accuracy, even on par with significantly larger models. Simply downsizing existing decoder-only VLMs is a straightforward way to design a lightweight model, but our experiments reveal that this approach yields suboptimal results. Instead, we select an encoder-decoder architecture, which outperforms decoder-only alternatives at small parameter scales for GUI grounding tasks. Additionally, the limited capacity of small VLMs encourages us to develop a Progressive Data Refinement pipeline that utilizes task type filtering and data ratio adjustment to extract a high-quality 3.8M-sample core set from a 10.8M raw dataset. Training GoClick using this core set brings notable grounding accuracy gains. Our experiments show that GoClick excels on multiple GUI element grounding benchmarks while maintaining a small size and high inference speed.
