In the second stage, an iterative training-and-refinement loop progressively improves both the segmentation model and the pseudo-labels over multiple rounds, where each round trains the model from scratch on current pseudo-labels and generates updated predictions with voxel-wise uncertainty estimates. An uncertainty-guided refinement step further leverages the foundation model's global feature space to correct high-uncertainty regions by aggregating labels from their most similar confident neighbors, establishing a virtuous cycle of mutual improvement. Extensive experiments on a wide range of segmentation tasks across different modalities and anatomical targets demonstrate that SemiSAM-O1 significantly narrows the performance gap between one-label semi-supervised learning and full supervision, while significantly reducing the computational overhead of online foundation model inference.

AI.tldr: SemiSAM-O1 is a semi-supervised learning framework for medical image segmentation that uses only one annotated template image. It leverages foundation model features (beyond prompting) in two stages: first generating coarse pseudo-labels via feature similarity propagation, then iteratively refining both the segmentation model and pseudo-labels through uncertainty-guided correction using the foundation model's global feature space. It narrows the gap to full supervision while reducing computational overhead.
AI.motivation: To alleviate the annotation burden in medical image segmentation, especially in complex imaging modalities where existing foundation model-driven SSL methods fail to maintain competitive performance under extremely limited annotation scenarios.
AI.method: SemiSAM-O1 extends specialist-generalist collaborative learning to one-label setting. Stage 1: Foundation model's encoder extracts dense features; class prototypes from the single annotated template propagate to unlabeled pool via feature similarity to generate coarse initial pseudo-labels. Stage 2: Iterative training-and-refinement loop progressively improves the segmentation model and pseudo-labels over multiple rounds, with uncertainty-guided refinement using the foundation model's global feature space to correct high-uncertainty regions by aggregating labels from confident neighbors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across various segmentation tasks, modalities, and anatomical targets show SemiSAM-O1 significantly narrows the performance gap between one-label SSL and full supervision, while substantially reducing computational overhead of online foundation model inference.
AI.conclusion: SemiSAM-O1 effectively achieves competitive segmentation performance with only one annotated template, demonstrating the feasibility of extreme annotation-efficient medical image segmentation through strategic use of foundation model representations beyond their prompting interfaces.

title: Conflict-Aware Harmonized Rotational Gradient for Multiscale Kinetic Regimes
summary:
In this paper, we propose a harmonized rotational gradient method, termed HRGrad, for simultaneously tackling multiscale time-dependent kinetic problems with varying small parameters.
  These parameters exhibit asymptotic transitions from microscopic to macroscopic physics, making it a challenging multi-task problem to solve over all ranges simultaneously.
  Solving tasks in different asymptotic regions often encounter gradient conflicts, which can lead to the failure of multi-task learning.
  To address this challenge, we explicitly encode a hidden representation of these parameters, ensuring that the corresponding solving tasks are serialized for simultaneous training.
  Furthermore, to mitigate gradient conflicts, we segment the prediction results to construct task losses and introduce a novel gradient alignment metric to ensure a positive dot product between the final update and each loss-specific gradient.
  This metric maintains consistent optimization rates for all task losses and dynamically adjusts gradient magnitudes based on conflict levels.
  Moreover, we provide a mathematical proof demonstrating the convergence of the HRGrad method, which is evaluated across a range of challenging asymptotic-preserving neural networks (APNNs) scenarios.
  We conduct an extensive set of experiments encompassing the Bhatnagar-Gross-Krook (BGK) equation and the linear transport equation in all ranges of Knudsen number.
  Our results indicate that HRGrad effectively overcomes the `failure modes' of APNNs in these problems.

AI.tldr: HRGrad is a harmonized rotational gradient method that resolves gradient conflicts in multiscale time-dependent kinetic problems by encoding hidden representations of small parameters and using a gradient alignment metric to ensure consistent optimization, successfully addressing failure modes in asymptotic-preserving neural networks.
AI.motivation: Multiscale kinetic problems with varying small parameters exhibit asymptotic transitions from microscopic to macroscopic physics. Solving tasks across different asymptotic regions simultaneously leads to gradient conflicts, causing multi-task learning failure in APNNs.
AI.method: HRGrad encodes hidden representations of parameters to serialize solving tasks, segments prediction results for task losses, and introduces a gradient alignment metric ensuring positive dot products between the final update and each loss-specific gradient, with dynamic magnitude adjustment based on conflict levels.
AI.result: Experiments on BGK and linear transport equations across all Knudsen number ranges show HRGrad effectively overcomes failure modes of APNNs, with mathematical proof of convergence provided.
AI.conclusion: HRGrad provides an effective solution for multi-task training in asymptotic-preserving neural networks, mitigating gradient conflicts and enabling simultaneous solving of multiscale kinetic problems.

title: TopoHR: Hierarchical Centerline Representation for Cyclic Topology Reasoning in Driving Scenes with Point-to-Instance Relations
summary: Topology reasoning is crucial for autonomous driving. Current methods primarily focus on instance-level learning for centerline detection, followed by a sequential module for topology reasoning that relies on simplified MLP layers. Moreover, they often neglect the importance of \textit{point-to-instance} (P2I) relationships in topology reasoning. To address these limitations, we present TopoHR (Topological Hierarchical Representation), a novel end-to-end framework that establishes cyclic interaction between centerline detection and topology reasoning, allowing them to iteratively enhance each other. Specifically, we introduce a hierarchical centerline representation including point queries, instance queries, and semantic representations. These multi-level features are seamlessly integrated and fused within a hierarchical centerline decoder. Furthermore, we design a hierarchical topology reasoning module that captures both fine-grained P2I relationships and global instance-to-instance (I2I) connections within a unified architecture. With these novel components, TopoHR ensures accurate and robust topology reasoning. On the OpenLane-V2 benchmark, TopoHR refreshes state-of-the-art performance with significant improvements. Notably, compared with previous best results, TopoHR achieves +3.8 in $\mathrm{DET}_{\text{l}}$, +5.4 in $\mathrm{TOP}_{\text{ll}}$ on $\text{subset_A}$ and +11.0 in $\mathrm{DET}_{\text{l}}$, +7.9 in $\mathrm{TOP}_{\text{ll}}$ on $\text{subset_B}$, validating the effectiveness of the proposed components. The code will be shared publicly at https://github.com/Yifeng-Bai/TopoHR.git.

AI.tldr: TopoHR is an end-to-end framework for autonomous driving that uses a hierarchical representation to jointly optimize centerline detection and topology reasoning through cyclic interaction, achieving state-of-the-art results on OpenLane-V2.
AI.motivation: Current topology reasoning methods rely on sequential MLP-based modules after centerline detection and neglect point-to-instance (P2I) relationships. TopoHR addresses these limitations by establishing cyclic interaction between detection and reasoning.
AI.method: A hierarchical centerline representation (point queries, instance queries, semantic representations) fused in a hierarchical decoder, plus a unified topology reasoning module capturing both fine-grained P2I and global instance-to-instance (I2I) relationships.
AI.result: On OpenLane-V2, TopoHR achieves +3.8 DET_l, +5.4 TOP_ll on subset_A and +11.0 DET_l, +7.9 TOP_ll on subset_B over previous SOTA.
AI.conclusion: TopoHR validates that cyclic interaction between detection and reasoning, along with multi-level hierarchical representation, significantly improves topology reasoning accuracy and robustness for autonomous driving.

title: The Optimal Sample Complexity of Multiclass and List Learning
summary:
While the optimal sample complexity of binary classification in terms of the VC dimension is well-established, determining the optimal sample complexity of multiclass classification has remained open. The appropriate complexity parameter for multiclass classification is the DS dimension, and despite significant efforts, a gap of $\sqrt{\text{DS}}$ has persisted between the upper and lower bounds on sample complexity.
  Recent work by Hanneke et al. (2026) shows a novel algebraic characterization of multiclass hypothesis classes in terms of their DS dimension. Building up on this, we show that the maximum hypergraph density of any multiclass hypothesis class is upper-bounded by its DS dimension. This proves a longstanding conjecture of Daniely and Shalev-Shwartz (2014). As a consequence, we determine the optimal dependence of the sample complexity on the DS dimension for multiclass as well as list learning.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the Daniely-Shalev-Shwartz conjecture by showing that the maximum hypergraph density of any multiclass hypothesis class is bounded by its DS dimension, thereby closing the gap between upper and lower bounds on sample complexity for multiclass classification.
AI.motivation: The optimal sample complexity of multiclass classification in terms of the DS dimension has remained open, with a persistent √DS gap between existing upper and lower bounds.
AI.method: Building on recent algebraic characterization of multiclass hypothesis classes (Hanneke et al., 2026), the authors prove that the maximum hypergraph density is upper-bounded by the DS dimension, which resolves a conjecture by Daniely and Shalev-Shwartz (2014).
AI.result: The DS dimension fully determines the sample complexity of multiclass classification (and list learning), closing the √DS gap.
AI.conclusion: The optimal dependence of sample complexity on DS dimension is now established for both multiclass classification and list learning.

title: FDIM: A Feature-distance-based Generic Video Quality Metric for Versatile Codecs
summary: Video technology is advancing toward Ultra High Definition (UHD) and High Dynamic Range (HDR), which intensifies the need for higher compression efficiency for these high-specification videos. Beyond advances in traditional codecs, neural video codecs (NVCs) have attracted significant research attention and have evolved rapidly over the past few years. The coding artifacts of NVCs often exhibit content-varying and generative characteristics, which differ from those of conventional codecs and are challenging for traditional video quality assessment (VQA) methods to capture. Therefore, VQA metrics are required to generalize across different codecs, content types, and dynamic ranges to better support video codec research and evaluation. In this paper, we propose FDIM, a feature-distance-based generic video quality metric for both traditional and neural video codecs across SDR and HDR formats. FDIM employs a hybrid architecture that integrates deep and hand-crafted features. The deep feature component learns multi-scale representations to capture distortions ranging from structural and textural fidelity degradation to high-level semantic deviations, while the hand-crafted feature component provides stable complementary cues to improve overall generalization. We trained FDIM on a large-scale subjective quality assessment dataset (DCVQA) consisting of over 16k video sequences encoded by traditional block-based hybrid video codecs and end-to-end perceptually optimized neural video codecs. Extensive experiments on ten SDR/HDR VQA datasets containing diverse, previously unseen codecs demonstrate that FDIM achieves strong generalization and high correlation with subjective assessment. The source code for FDIM and the DCVQA validation set will be released at https://github.com/MCL-ZJU/FDIM.

AI.tldr: FDIM is a hybrid feature-distance video quality metric that uses both deep and hand-crafted features to effectively assess videos compressed by traditional and neural codecs across SDR and HDR formats, achieving strong generalization on diverse unseen codecs.
AI.motivation: As video technology advances toward UHD and HDR, higher compression efficiency is needed. Neural video codecs (NVCs) produce content-varying, generative artifacts different from traditional codecs, making existing VQA methods inadequate. A generalized VQA metric is needed to support codec research across different codecs, content types, and dynamic ranges.
AI.method: FDIM uses a hybrid architecture combining deep features (learning multi-scale representations for structural, textural, and semantic distortions) with hand-crafted features (providing stable complementary cues). It was trained on DCVQA, a large-scale dataset with 16k+ sequences encoded by both traditional block-based hybrid codecs and end-to-end neural video codecs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ten SDR/HDR VQA datasets containing diverse, previously unseen codecs demonstrate that FDIM achieves strong generalization and high correlation with subjective assessment.
AI.conclusion: FDIM is an effective generic video quality metric that generalizes well across both traditional and neural video codecs in SDR and HDR formats, outperforming existing methods on unseen codecs.

title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation Network Integrating Object-Level Label and Scene-Level Semantic Features for Multimodal Remote Sensing Images
summary: Semantic segmentation of multi-modal remote sensing imagery plays a pivotal role in land use/land cover (LULC) mapping, environmental monitoring, and precision earth observation. Current multi-modal approaches mainly focus on integrating complementary visual modalities, yet neglect the incorporating of non-visual textual data - a rich source of knowledge that can bridge semantic gaps between visual patterns and real-world concepts. To address this limitation, we propose TSMNet, a text supervised multi-modal open vocabulary semantic segmentation network that synergistically integrates textual supervision with visual representation for open-vocabulary semantic segmentation. Unlike conventional multi-modal segmentation frameworks, TSMNet introduces a dual-branch text encoder to extract both scene-level semantic and object-level label information from various textual data, enabling dynamic cross-modal fusion. These text-derived features dynamically interact with visual embeddings through the proposed text-guided visual semantic fusion module, enabling domain-aware feature refinement and human-interpretable decision-making. To verify our method, we innovatively construct two new multi-modal datasets, and carry out extensive experiments to make a comprehensive comparison between the proposed method and other state-of-the-art (SOTA) semantic segmentation models. Results demonstrate that TSMNet achieves superior segmentation accuracy while exhibiting robust generalization capabilities across diverse geographical and sensor-specific scenarios. This work establishes a new paradigm for explainable remote sensing analysis, demonstrating that textual knowledge integration significantly enhances model generalizability. The source code will be available at https://github.com/yeyuanxin110/TSMNet

AI.tldr: TSMNet is a text supervised multi-modal open vocabulary semantic segmentation network that integrates textual supervision (scene-level and label information) with visual data via a dual-branch text encoder and text-guided visual semantic fusion module. It achieves superior segmentation accuracy, robust generalization across diverse scenarios, and introduces two new multi-modal datasets.
AI.motivation: Current multi-modal segmentation approaches focus on integrating visual modalities but neglect non-visual textual data, which can bridge semantic gaps between visual patterns and real-world concepts.
AI.method: TSMNet uses a dual-branch text encoder to extract scene-level semantic and object-level label information from textual data, combined with a text-guided visual semantic fusion module for dynamic cross-modal interaction and feature refinement.
AI.result: TSMNet achieves superior segmentation accuracy with robust generalization capabilities across diverse geographical and sensor-specific scenarios, outperforming SOTA models.
AI.conclusion: Textual knowledge integration significantly enhances model generalizability, establishing a new paradigm for explainable remote sensing analysis.

title: EXACT: an explainable anomaly-aware vision foundation model for analysis of 3D chest CT
summary: Chest computed tomography (CT) is central to the detection and management of thoracic disease, yet the growing scale and complexity of volumetric imaging increasingly exceed what can be addressed by scan-level prediction alone. Clinically useful AI for CT must not only recognize disease across the whole volume, but also localize abnormalities and provide interpretable visual evidence. Existing vision-language foundation models typically compress scans and reports into global image-text representations, limiting their ability to preserve spatial evidence and support clinically meaningful interpretation. Here we developed EXACT, an explainable anomaly-aware foundation model for three-dimensional chest CT that learns spatially resolved representations from paired clinical scans and radiology reports. EXACT was pre-trained on 25,692 CT-reports pairs using anatomy-aware weak supervision, jointly learning organ segmentation and multi-instance anomaly localization without manual voxel-level annotations. The resulting organ-specific anomaly-aware maps assign each voxel a disease-specific anomaly score confined to its corresponding anatomy, jointly encoding lesion extent and organ-level context. In retrospective multinational and multi-center evaluations, EXACT showed broad and consistent improvements across clinically relevant CT tasks, spanning multi-disease diagnosis, zero-shot anomaly localization, downstream adaptation, and visually grounded report generation, outperforming existing three-dimensional medical foundation models. By transforming routine clinical CT scans and free-text reports into explainable voxel-level representations, EXACT establishes a scalable paradigm for trustworthy volumetric medical AI.

AI.tldr: EXACT is an explainable anomaly-aware foundation model for 3D chest CT that learns spatially resolved representations from 25,692 CT-report pairs using anatomy-aware weak supervision, achieving superior performance across multi-disease diagnosis, zero-shot anomaly localization, downstream adaptation, and visually grounded report generation.
AI.motivation: Chest CT is critical for thoracic disease management, but existing vision-language models compress scans and reports into global representations, losing spatial evidence needed for clinically meaningful interpretation and localization.
AI.method: EXACT is pre-trained on 25,692 CT-report pairs using anatomy-aware weak supervision, jointly learning organ segmentation and multi-instance anomaly localization without manual voxel annotations, producing organ-specific anomaly-aware maps with voxel-level disease scores.
AI.result: In retrospective multinational and multi-center evaluations, EXACT showed broad and consistent improvements across multi-disease diagnosis, zero-shot anomaly localization, downstream adaptation, and visually grounded report generation, outperforming existing 3D medical foundation models.
AI.conclusion: By transforming routine clinical CT scans and free-text reports into explainable voxel-level representations, EXACT establishes a scalable paradigm for trustworthy volumetric medical AI.

title: 6thGrid-Net: Unified Remote Sensing Image Dehazing Based on Color Restoration and Edge-Preserving
summary: Remote sensing images are frequently degraded by adverse weather conditions, particularly clouds and haze, which severely impair downstream applications. Existing restoration methods typically rely on computationally heavy architectures or sequential pipelines (e.g., detail enhancement followed by color rendition) that suffer from mutual interference and artifact accumulation. Furthermore, recent unified grid-based approaches utilize fixed, isotropic interpolation kernels, neglecting the intrinsic low-dimensional manifold of natural images and inevitably causing edge blur. To address these limitations, we propose 6th Grid-Net, a highly efficient and unified remote sensing image restoration framework tailored for resource-constrained edge devices. Specifically, we construct a novel six-dimensional fusion tensor that seamlessly integrates the color rendition capabilities of 3D LUTs with the spatial-luminance detail preservation of bilateral grids. To overcome the drawbacks of standard trilinear interpolation, we introduce a manifold-adaptive high-dimensional sampling mechanism. This mechanism dynamically adjusts the interpolation kernel based on local edge orientation, texture strength, and color similarity, enabling simultaneous global color stylization and local edge refinement in a single forward pass. Additionally, an edge-aware grid smoothing constraint and dynamic quantization are incorporated to suppress ghosting artifacts and significantly compress the model size. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that 6th Grid-Net achieves state-of-the-art restoration quality across various degradation scenarios.

AI.tldr: 6th Grid-Net is a unified, efficient remote sensing image restoration framework that uses a six-dimensional fusion tensor combining 3D LUTs and bilateral grids, with manifold-adaptive sampling for edge-preserving restoration on resource-constrained devices.
AI.motivation: Existing restoration methods suffer from computational heaviness, sequential pipeline artifacts, and fixed isotropic interpolation that blurs edges. There is a need for efficient, unified restoration suitable for edge devices.
AI.method: Proposes a six-dimensional fusion tensor integrating 3D LUT color rendition with bilateral grid spatial-luminance detail preservation, plus a manifold-adaptive high-dimensional sampling mechanism that adjusts interpolation kernels based on local edge orientation, texture strength, and color similarity.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art restoration quality across multiple benchmark datasets in various degradation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses cloud/haze degradation with high efficiency, suitable for resource-constrained edge devices while avoiding artifacts like ghosting and edge blur.

title: Robust Deepfake Detection, NTIRE 2026 Challenge: Report
summary: Robustness is a long-overlooked problem in deepfake detection. However, detection performance is nearly worthless in the real world if it suffers under exposure to even slight image degradation. In addition to weaker degradations that can accidentally occur in the image processing pipeline, there is another risk of malicious deepfakes that specifically introduce degradations, purposefully exploiting the detector's weaknesses in that regard. Here, we present an overview of the NTIRE 2026 Robust Deepfake Detection Challenge, which specifically addresses that problem. Participants were tasked with building a detector that would later be tested on an unknown test-set, which included both common and uncommon degradations of various strengths. With a total number of 337 participants and 57 submissions to the final leaderboard, the first edition of the challenge was well received. To ensure the reliability of the results, participants were given only 24h to complete the test run with no labels provided, limiting the possibility of training on the test data. Furthermore, the top solutions were scored on a private test-set to detect any such overfitting. This report presents the competition setting, dataset preparation, as well as details and performance of methods. Top methods rely on large foundation models, ensembles, and degradation training to combine generality and robustness.

AI.tldr: The NTIRE 2026 Robust Deepfake Detection Challenge addressed the overlooked problem of robustness in deepfake detection, requiring participants to build detectors resilient to various image degradations. With 337 participants and 57 final submissions, top solutions used foundation models, ensembles, and degradation training. A 24-hour blind test and private test-set ensured result reliability.
AI.motivation: Robustness in deepfake detection has been long overlooked, yet detection performance becomes nearly worthless in real-world scenarios when exposed to even slight image degradation. There is also risk of malicious deepfakes purposely introducing degradations to exploit detector weaknesses.
AI.method: A challenge was organized (NTIRE 2026) where participants built detectors tested on an unknown test-set with common/uncommon degradations of various strengths. Reliability was ensured via a 24-hour blind test run (no labels provided) and a private test-set to detect overfitting. Top methods used large foundation models, ensembles, and degradation training.
AI.result: 337 participants total, 57 final submissions to the leaderboard. The challenge was well received. Top solutions relied on large foundation models, ensembles, and degradation training to achieve generality and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The challenge successfully addressed robustness in deepfake detection and demonstrated that combining large foundation models, ensembles, and degradation training yields effective robust detection. The competition setting and dataset preparation are detailed in the report.

title: PEPS: Positional Encoding Projected Sampling -- Extended
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) are increasingly being used as tools to map coordinates to signals, encompassing applications from neural fields to texture compression, shape representations, and beyond. Most INR methods are based on using high-dimensional projections of the initial coordinates through encoders such as grid or positional encoding. Nevertheless, positional encoding is often insufficient and grids, as we show in this paper, require high resolution for being able to learn. In this paper, we demonstrate that positional encoding can be used not only as a high-dimensional embedding but also decomposed as a series of meaningful points. We propose the Positional Encoding Projected Sampling, where we treat the projection of the original coordinate at each frequency as a point of interest. We describe the motion of each point with respect to the frequencies and show that it follows a unique pattern. Finally, we use the unique motion of each point as a basis decomposition for doing learned positional encoding using grids. We prove, using three competitive applications; image representation, texture compression, and signed distance function; that the proposed approach outperforms the current state of the art methods, and often requires 25\% less parameters for equivalent reconstruction error or rendering.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Positional Encoding Projected Sampling (PEPS), a method that treats positional encoding projections as meaningful points with unique motion patterns, using them as basis decomposition for grid-based learned positional encoding, achieving SOTA results with 25% fewer parameters in image representation, texture compression, and signed distance functions.
AI.motivation: Existing INR methods rely on high-dimensional projections via positional encoding or grids, but positional encoding is often insufficient, and grids require high resolution. The authors aim to bridge this gap by decomposing positional encoding into meaningful projected points.
AI.method: The authors propose Positional Encoding Projected Sampling (PEPS), which treats each coordinate's projection at each frequency as a point of interest, analyzes its unique motion pattern across frequencies, and uses this motion as a basis decomposition for learned positional encoding with grids.
AI.result: PEPS outperforms current SOTA methods in three applications: image representation, texture compression, and signed distance functions. It requires 25% fewer parameters for equivalent reconstruction error or rendering quality.
AI.conclusion: Positional encoding can be decomposed into meaningful points with unique motion patterns, which when used as basis decomposition for grid-based learning, yields superior efficiency and performance across multiple INR tasks.

title: PointTransformerX:Portable and Efficient 3D Point Cloud Processing without Sparse Algorithms
comment: This paper has been accepted at IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshops (CVPRW), 2026
summary: 3D point cloud perception remains tightly coupled to custom CUDA operators for spatial operations, limiting portability and efficiency on non-NVIDIA, AMD, and embedded hardware. We introduce PointTransformerX (PTX), a fully PyTorch-native vision transformer backbone for 3D point clouds, removing all custom CUDA operators and external libraries while retaining competitive accuracy. PTX introduces 3D-GS-RoPE, a rotary positional embedding that encodes 3D spatial relationships directly in self-attention without neighborhood construction, and further replaces sparse convolutional patch embedding with a linear projection. PTX explores inference-time scaling of attention windows to improve accuracy without retraining. With a redesigned feed-forward network, PTX achieves 98.7\% of PointTransformer V3's accuracy on ScanNet with 79.2\% fewer parameters and executing 1.6\times faster while requiring just 253 MB memory. PTX runs natively on NVIDIA GPUs, AMD GPUs (ROCm), and CPUs, providing an efficient and portable foundation for point cloud perception.

AI.tldr: PTX is a fully PyTorch-native 3D point cloud backbone that removes all custom CUDA operators, using 3D-GS-RoPE positional embeddings and linear patch projection. It achieves 98.7% of PointTransformer V3's accuracy on ScanNet with 79.2% fewer parameters, 1.6× faster inference, and runs natively on NVIDIA, AMD, and CPU hardware.
AI.motivation: 3D point cloud perception relies on custom CUDA operators, limiting portability to non-NVIDIA hardware (AMD, embedded devices). The authors aim to create a fully portable, efficient backbone without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: (1) 3D-GS-RoPE: rotary positional embedding encoding 3D spatial relations directly in self-attention, eliminating neighborhood construction. (2) Linear projection replacing sparse convolutional patch embedding. (3) Inference-time scaling of attention windows for accuracy gains. (4) Redesigned feed-forward network for efficiency.
AI.result: 98.7% of PointTransformer V3's accuracy on ScanNet, 79.2% fewer parameters, 1.6× faster execution, 253 MB memory usage.
AI.conclusion: PTX provides an efficient, portable, CUDA-free foundation for 3D point cloud perception that runs natively on NVIDIA, AMD (ROCm), and CPUs, democratizing point cloud research across diverse hardware.

title: POCA: Pareto-Optimal Curriculum Alignment for Visual Text Generation
summary: Current visual text generation models struggle with the trade-off between text accuracy and overall image coherence. We find that achieving high text accuracy can reduce aesthetic quality and instruction-following capability. Although reinforcement learning approaches can alleviate the problem through aligning with multiple rewards, they are often unstable for text generation, as existing approaches normally optimize multiple rewards in a weighted-sum way. In addition, it is difficult to balance the weight of each reward. Moreover, reinforcement learning requires a set of training instructions. A large number of prompts require more training time and computing resources, while a small set leads to poor performance. Hence, how to select the prompts for efficient training is an unsolved problem. In this study, we propose Pareto-Optimal Curriculum Alignment (POCA), a framework that addresses this issue as a multi-objective problem by: 1) identifying the Pareto-optimal set to avoid simple scalarization and 2) designing an adaptive curriculum alignment strategy to manage a learning sequence of a multi-reward dataset using automatic difficulty assessment, which is crucial for optimal convergence as RL methods explore in a limited data environment. In synergy, POCA finds the Pareto-optimal set in a unified reward space, which eliminates inconsistent signals to find the best trade-off solution from different rewards under an easy-to-hard optimization landscape. The experimental results show that POCA significantly improves all metrics such as CLIP, HPS scores and sentence accuracy.

AI.tldr: POCA is a new framework using Pareto-optimal curriculum alignment to improve visual text generation by balancing text accuracy and image coherence through multi-reward optimization and adaptive learning sequencing.
AI.motivation: Existing visual text generation models face a trade-off between text accuracy and image coherence. Reinforcement learning approaches are unstable due to weighted-sum multi-reward optimization, and prompt selection for efficient training remains unsolved.
AI.method: POCA treats the problem as multi-objective optimization by: 1) identifying Pareto-optimal sets to avoid simple scalarization, and 2) using adaptive curriculum alignment with automatic difficulty assessment to manage learning sequences of multi-reward datasets efficiently.
AI.result: POCA significantly improves all metrics, including CLIP scores, HPS scores, and sentence accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Pareto-optimal curriculum alignment effectively resolves the trade-off between text accuracy and image coherence, achieving better convergence in limited data environments compared to traditional weighted-sum RL approaches.

title: Multivariate Gaussian NeRF for Wide Field-of-View Ultrasound Reconstruction
summary: Wide Field-of-View (WFoV) reconstruction enhances 3D ultrasound imaging by providing valuable anatomical context for segmentation models and visualization. Clinical ultrasound volumes are predominantly acquired using convex probes, which generate expanding, diverging acoustic beams to maximize anatomical coverage. Stitching these sweeps together traditionally introduces significant compounding artifacts and aliasing due to depth-dependent resolution changes. Here, we introduce Ultra-Wide-NeRF, a Multivariate 3D Gaussian (MVG) NeRF-based method for WFoV ultrasound reconstruction. By explicitly modeling the complex beam geometry using distance-dependent convex volumetric sampling and anisotropic 3D Gaussians, our method inherently mitigates these compounding artifacts and provides anti-aliasing. Beyond simply reconstructing a static 3D grid, our NeRF-based approach yields a continuous neural representation of the tissue, enabling the synthesis of high-fidelity novel views from arbitrary virtual trajectories. We validate Ultra-Wide-NeRF for intracardiac echocardiography on phantom and porcine datasets, demonstrating that our method expands the spatial context important in intraoperative navigation. Code will be open-sourced upon publication.

AI.tldr: Ultra-Wide-NeRF is a NeRF-based method using Multivariate 3D Gaussians and convex volumetric sampling to reconstruct wide field-of-view 3D ultrasound volumes from convex probe sweeps, reducing compounding artifacts and enabling novel view synthesis for intracardiac echocardiography.
AI.motivation: Traditional stitching of convex probe ultrasound sweeps introduces compounding artifacts and aliasing due to depth-dependent resolution changes, limiting the quality of WFoV 3D reconstruction for clinical applications like intraoperative navigation.
AI.method: A Multivariate 3D Gaussian (MVG) NeRF-based approach that models complex beam geometry via distance-dependent convex volumetric sampling and anisotropic 3D Gaussians to mitigate artifacts and enable anti-aliasing.
AI.result: Validated on phantom and porcine datasets for intracardiac echocardiography, showing expanded spatial context useful for intraoperative navigation.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a continuous neural representation for WFoV ultrasound reconstruction, enabling high-fidelity novel view synthesis from arbitrary virtual trajectories while reducing traditional compounding artifacts.

title: Omni-o3: Deep Nested Omnimodal Deduction for Deliberative Audio-Visual Reasoning
summary: Omnimodal understanding entails a massive, highly redundant search space of cross-modal interactions, demanding focused and deliberative reasoning. Current reasoning paradigms rely on either sequential step-by-step generation or parallel sample-by-sample rollouts, leading to isolated reasoning trajectories. This inability to share promising intermediate paths severely limits exploration efficiency and causes compounding errors in complex audio-visual tasks. To break this bottleneck, we introduce Omni-o3, a novel framework driven by a deep nested deduction policy. By formulating reasoning as a dynamic recursive search, Omni-o3 inherently shares reasoning prefixes across branches, enabling the iterative execution of four atomic cognitive actions: expansion, selection, simulation, and backpropagation. To empower this framework, we propose a robust two-stage training paradigm: (1) cold-start supervised fine-tuning on 101K high-quality, long-chain trajectories distilled from 3.5M diverse omnimodal samples, enabling necessary recursive search patterns; and (2) nested group rollout-driven exploratory reinforcement learning on 18K complex multi-turn samples, explicitly guided by a novel multi-step reward model to stimulate deep nested reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Omni-o3 achieves competitive performance across 11 benchmarks, unlocking advanced capabilities in comprehensive audio-visual, visual-centric, and audio-centric reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Omni-o3 introduces a deep nested deduction policy for omnimodal understanding, formulating reasoning as a dynamic recursive search that shares reasoning prefixes across branches, enabled by a two-stage training paradigm (cold-start SFT + reinforcement learning) and achieving competitive results across 11 multimodal benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning paradigms rely on isolated sequential or parallel trajectories, which fail to share promising intermediate paths, severely limiting exploration efficiency and causing compounding errors in complex audio-visual tasks.
AI.method: (1) A deep nested deduction policy that formulates reasoning as dynamic recursive search with four atomic cognitive actions (expansion, selection, simulation, backpropagation). (2) Cold-start SFT on 101K high-quality trajectories distilled from 3.5M samples. (3) Nested group rollout-driven exploratory RL on 18K complex samples guided by a multi-step reward model.
AI.result: Competitive performance across 11 benchmarks in audio-visual, visual-centric, and audio-centric reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Omni-o3 unlocks advanced capabilities in omnimodal reasoning by enabling shared reasoning prefixes through recursive search, outperforming existing isolated trajectory approaches.

title: Computer Vision-Based Early Detection of Container Loss at Sea
comment: Accepted and Presented at SMRC x ICMASS/MTEC 2026
summary: Containerised shipping underpins global trade, yet container loss at sea remains a persistent safety, environmental, and economic challenge. Despite compliance with Cargo Securing Manuals, dynamic maritime conditions such as vessel motion, wind loading, and severe sea states can progressively destabilise container stacks, leading to overboard losses. With the new International Maritime Organisation's (IMO) mandatory reporting requirements for lost containers, there is an urgent need for a reliable, evidence-based early detection solution for destabilised containers. This study showcases a low-cost, retrofittable computer vision-based system for early detection of destabilised containers using existing onboard cameras. The framework integrates object segmentation to isolate container stacks, temporal object tracking using optical flow and individual objects' residual motion extraction to quantify relative movement. Experimental evaluation on real onboard ship footage demonstrates that the proposed pipeline effectively isolates container-level motion under challenging conditions of varying sea states and visibility conditions. By enabling early alerts for crew intervention and navigational adjustment, the proposed approach enhances cargo safety, operational resilience, and regulatory compliance.

AI.tldr: A low-cost computer vision system using existing onboard cameras is proposed for early detection of destabilised shipping containers, integrating object segmentation, optical flow tracking, and residual motion extraction to detect relative movement in real ship footage.
AI.motivation: Container loss at sea poses persistent safety, environmental, and economic challenges. With new IMO mandatory reporting requirements for lost containers, there is an urgent need for a reliable, evidence-based early detection solution for destabilised containers.
AI.method: The framework integrates object segmentation to isolate container stacks, temporal object tracking using optical flow, and individual objects' residual motion extraction to quantify relative movement. It uses existing onboard cameras for a low-cost, retrofittable solution.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on real onboard ship footage demonstrates that the proposed pipeline effectively isolates container-level motion under challenging conditions of varying sea states and visibility conditions.
AI.conclusion: By enabling early alerts for crew intervention and navigational adjustment, the proposed approach enhances cargo safety, operational resilience, and regulatory compliance.

title: Radiomics- and Clinical Feature-Driven Prediction of Volumetric Response in Skull-Base Meningioma after CyberKnife Radiosurgery
summary: Skull-base meningiomas are often characterized by favorable long-term prognosis, yet their anatomical complexity and proximity to critical neurovascular structures make treatment selection challenging. Stereotactic radiosurgery with CyberKnife represents an effective therapeutic option when surgical resection is not feasible; however, not all patients benefit equally from this treatment. Early identification of patients likely to respond to radiosurgery remains an open clinical problem. In this study, we propose a radiomics- and clinical feature-driven framework for predicting volumetric response in skull-base meningiomas treated with CyberKnife. Unlike most existing approaches that focus on progression-free survival or recurrence, our method targets volumetric response as an indicator of treatment efficacy. Pre-treatment MRI images from 104 patients were processed to extract radiomic features, which were combined with clinical variables and analyzed using six models. To ensure methodological rigor, the entire modeling process was implemented within a nested cross-validation scheme. Among the evaluated models, TabPFN achieved the best overall performance, with an AUC of 0.81 and consistently favorable classification metrics. These results suggest that advanced machine learning architectures, when combined with robust validation strategies, can effectively capture patterns associated with treatment response even in small-sample, high-dimensional settings.

AI.tldr: A radiomics and clinical feature-driven framework using TabPFN predicts volumetric response in skull-base meningiomas treated with CyberKnife, achieving AUC 0.81 via nested cross-validation on 104 patients.
AI.motivation: Early identification of patients likely to respond to CyberKnife radiosurgery remains an open clinical problem, as not all patients benefit equally and existing approaches focus on survival/recurrence rather than volumetric response.
AI.method: Pre-treatment MRI from 104 patients; radiomic features extracted and combined with clinical variables; six machine learning models evaluated with nested cross-validation; TabPFN performed best.
AI.result: TabPFN achieved AUC 0.81 with consistently favorable classification metrics, outperforming other models in this small-sample, high-dimensional setting.
AI.conclusion: Advanced ML architectures with robust validation can effectively capture treatment response patterns in small-sample, high-dimensional settings for skull-base meningioma radiosurgery.

title: Graph-augmented Segmentation of Complex Shapes in Laser Powder bed Fusion for Enhanced In Situ Inspection
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering (T-ASE)
summary: The technological maturity of in situ inspection and monitoring methods in additive manufacturing is steadily increasing, enabling more efficient and practical qualification procedures. In this context, image segmentation of powder bed images in Laser Powder Bed Fusion (L-PBF) has been investigated by various authors, leveraging both edge detection and machine learning approaches to identify deviations from nominal geometry. Despite these developments, several challenges remain, including the sensitivity of segmentation performance to industrial illumination conditions and layer-to-layer variability in pixel intensity patterns. The study addresses these limitations by proposing a graph-augmented segmentation approach. The underlying principle consists of preserving the geometrical information at a global level rather than at pixel-wise level, modeling dependencies and relational information among spatial regions with a Graph Neural Network bottleneck embedded into a U-Net architecture. This allows enhancing the consistency and accuracy of the geometry reconstruction in the presence of spatial and layer-wise photometric variability systematically faced in real data. The method is evaluated against benchmark techniques for the in situ reconstruction of lattice structures produced by L-PBF, demonstrating its potential as a scalable solution for robust in situ inspection and geometric verification in industrial environments.

AI.tldr: A graph-augmented segmentation approach combining Graph Neural Networks with U-Net architecture is proposed to improve in situ inspection of L-PBF powder bed images, handling illumination variability and layer-to-layer intensity changes for robust geometric reconstruction of lattice structures.
AI.motivation: Existing image segmentation methods for powder bed images in L-PBF suffer from sensitivity to industrial illumination conditions and layer-to-layer pixel intensity variability, limiting their practical reliability.
AI.method: The authors propose a graph-augmented segmentation approach that preserves geometrical information at a global level using a Graph Neural Network bottleneck embedded into a U-Net architecture to model dependencies and relational information among spatial regions.
AI.result: The method outperforms benchmark techniques for in situ reconstruction of lattice structures produced by L-PBF, demonstrating improved consistency and accuracy under spatial and layer-wise photometric variability.
AI.conclusion: The graph-augmented approach shows potential as a scalable, robust solution for in situ inspection and geometric verification in industrial additive manufacturing environments.

title: Touchless Intraoperative Image Access System Based on Vision-Based Hand Tracking
summary: Touchless interaction with medical images is becoming increasingly important in the surgical field, where sterility and continuity of the operational workflow are essential requirements. This work presents a vision-based system for intraoperative navigation of medical images through hand gestures acquired using a single RGB camera. Unlike many existing solutions, the system does not require additional hardware or user-specific training. Hand tracking is performed in real time using MediaPipe Hands, which provides a 2.5D estimation of hand landmarks. Simple and intuitive gestures are then mapped into translation, rotation, and zoom commands, enabling continuous and natural interaction with the image viewer. The system architecture is independent from the visualization software and, for implementation simplicity, in this study it was integrated with PyVista. Performance was evaluated through frame-level logging and quantitative analysis of latency, stability, and interaction robustness metrics. Experimental results highlight real-time behavior, with reduced latencies and stable control, in line with the requirements of fluid interaction. The system demonstrates the feasibility of a low-cost touchless solution for intraoperative access to medical images, laying the groundwork for future clinical evaluations.

AI.tldr: A vision-based touchless system using a single RGB camera and MediaPipe Hands enables real-time hand gesture control (translation, rotation, zoom) for intraoperative medical image navigation, achieving low latency and stable interaction without additional hardware or user training.
AI.motivation: Touchless interaction is crucial in surgical settings to maintain sterility and workflow continuity. Existing solutions often require extra hardware or user-specific training, limiting practicality.
AI.method: Uses MediaPipe Hands for real-time 2.5D hand landmark estimation from a single RGB camera, maps simple gestures to translation, rotation, and zoom commands, integrated with PyVista for visualization.
AI.result: Real-time performance with low latency, stable control, and robust interaction, meeting fluid interaction requirements for intraoperative use.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of a low-cost, hardware-free touchless system for intraoperative medical image navigation, setting the stage for future clinical evaluations.

title: Instance Awareness of Multi-class Semantic Segmentation Loss Functions
comment: 8 pages, 4 Figures, Accepted as Poster at CV4CLINIC workshop at CVPR
summary: Instance-sensitive losses for semantic segmentation such as blob loss and CC loss were designed to address instance imbalance, ensuring small lesions generate the same gradient as large ones, but operate only on single-class segmentation. In multi-class settings, class imbalance poses an additional problem: rare classes with few instances receive a disproportionately small share of the training signal. We show that extending instance-sensitive losses to multi-class segmentation via a one-vs-rest class decomposition repurposes them to also address class imbalance, as uniform averaging over classes ensures each class contributes equally regardless of frequency. We further show that inverse-size weighting, which destabilizes training when applied globally due to weight imbalances across rare and common classes, becomes effective when integrated within the per-component loss, confining the reweighting to each component's spatial context. On the BraTS-METS 2025 dataset (260 test cases), multi-class CC loss improves foreground Dice (0.64 +/- 0.26 vs. 0.59 +/- 0.27 baseline) and rare-class Dice, while maintaining Panoptic Quality at DSC threshold 0.5. Multi-class blob loss achieves the best Panoptic Quality at threshold 0.5 (0.40 +/- 0.24 vs. 0.38 +/- 0.25 baseline) and recognition quality (0.53 +/- 0.29 vs. 0.49 +/- 0.30). Integrating inverse-size weighting within the per-component loss increases rare-class Dice to 0.44 +/- 0.36 at the cost of reduced detection quality.

AI.tldr: This paper extends instance-sensitive segmentation losses (blob loss, CC loss) from single-class to multi-class settings via one-vs-rest decomposition, effectively addressing both instance and class imbalance. On BraTS-METS 2025, multi-class CC loss improves foreground and rare-class Dice, while multi-class blob loss achieves best Panoptic Quality. Inverse-size weighting within per-component loss boosts rare-class Dice but reduces detection quality.
AI.motivation: Instance-sensitive losses (blob loss, CC loss) address instance imbalance in single-class segmentation but fail in multi-class settings where rare classes receive disproportionately small training signals due to class imbalance.
AI.method: Extends instance-sensitive losses to multi-class segmentation using one-vs-rest class decomposition, ensuring uniform class contribution. Integrates inverse-size weighting within per-component loss to stabilize training.
AI.result: On BraTS-METS 2025 (260 test cases): multi-class CC loss improves foreground Dice (0.64 vs. 0.59) and rare-class Dice; multi-class blob loss achieves best Panoptic Quality (0.40 vs. 0.38) and recognition quality (0.53 vs. 0.49); per-component inverse-size weighting boosts rare-class Dice to 0.44 but reduces detection quality.
AI.conclusion: One-vs-rest decomposition repurposes instance-sensitive losses to handle class imbalance in multi-class segmentation, and localized inverse-size weighting improves rare-class performance with a trade-off in detection quality.

title: ReVSI: Rebuilding Visual Spatial Intelligence Evaluation for Accurate Assessment of VLM 3D Reasoning
summary: Current evaluations of spatial intelligence can be systematically invalid under modern vision-language model (VLM) settings. First, many benchmarks derive question-answer (QA) pairs from point-cloud-based 3D annotations originally curated for traditional 3D perception. When such annotations are treated as ground truth for video-based evaluation, reconstruction and annotation artifacts can miss objects that are clearly visible in the video, mislabel object identities, or corrupt geometry-dependent answers (e.g., size), yielding incorrect or ambiguous QA pairs. Second, evaluations often assume full-scene access, while many VLMs operate on sparsely sampled frames (e.g., 16-64), making many questions effectively unanswerable under the actual model inputs. We improve evaluation validity by introducing ReVSI, a benchmark and protocol that ensures each QA pair is answerable and correct under the model's actual inputs. To this end, we re-annotate objects and geometry across 381 scenes from 5 datasets to improve data quality, and regenerate all QA pairs with rigorous bias mitigation and human verification using professional 3D annotation tools. We further enhance evaluation controllability by providing variants across multiple frame budgets (16/32/64/all) and fine-grained object visibility metadata, enabling controlled diagnostic analyses. Evaluations of general and domain-specific VLMs on ReVSI reveal systematic failure modes that are obscured by prior benchmarks, yielding a more reliable and diagnostic assessment of spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: ReVSI is a new benchmark for evaluating spatial intelligence in vision-language models, fixing issues where prior benchmarks use invalid QA pairs (from 3D annotations mismatched to video, or assuming full-scene access). It re-annotates 381 scenes, regenerates QA pairs with human verification, and provides frame-budget variants for controlled, valid evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current spatial intelligence evaluations are systematically invalid: (1) QA pairs derived from point-cloud annotations don't match video content (missing objects, mislabeled identities, corrupted geometry answers); (2) evaluations assume full-scene access while VLMs operate on sparse frames, making many questions unanswerable.
AI.method: ReVSI re-annotates objects and geometry across 381 scenes from 5 datasets using professional 3D annotation tools, regenerates all QA pairs with bias mitigation and human verification, and provides variants across frame budgets (16/32/64/all) with fine-grained visibility metadata for controlled diagnostics.
AI.result: Evaluations of general and domain-specific VLMs on ReVSI reveal systematic failure modes that were obscured by prior benchmarks, yielding a more reliable and diagnostic assessment.
AI.conclusion: ReVSI provides a more valid and controllable benchmark for evaluating spatial intelligence in VLMs by ensuring answerable and correct QA pairs under actual model inputs, and exposing previously hidden failure modes.

title: BIMStruct3D: A Fully Automated Hybrid Learning Scan-to-BIM Pipeline with Integrated Topology Refinement
summary: Automatic generation of Building Information Models (BIM) from building scans is a key challenge in architecture and construction. We present a modular pipeline for generating IFC-compliant BIM from 3D point clouds. The hybrid approach combines learning-based semantic segmentation with topology-aware geometric reconstruction to model structural elements accurately. We propose vIoU, adapting voxel-based overlap evaluation to Scan-to-BIM by enabling holistic, instance-matching-free comparison of reconstructed and ground-truth models. We release the German Hospital dataset (DeKH), including high-resolution point clouds, ground truth BIMs, and semantic annotations. Experiments on DeKH and CV4AEC datasets show significant improvements over a RANSAC-based baseline, demonstrating robustness and scalability.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a modular pipeline for generating IFC-compliant BIM from 3D point clouds using hybrid learning-based segmentation and topology-aware reconstruction, introduces the vIoU metric for evaluation, and releases the German Hospital dataset (DeKH).
AI.motivation: Automatic generation of BIM from building scans is a key challenge in architecture and construction.
AI.method: A modular pipeline combining learning-based semantic segmentation with topology-aware geometric reconstruction, plus a novel vIoU evaluation metric.
AI.result: Significant improvements over a RANSAC-based baseline on DeKH and CV4AEC datasets, demonstrating robustness and scalability.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach effectively generates IFC-compliant BIM from point clouds, outperforming traditional methods.

title: Unconstrained Multi-view Human Pose Estimation with Algebraic Priors
summary: Recovering 3D human pose from multi-view imagery typically relies on precise camera calibration, which is often unavailable in real-world scenarios, thereby severely limiting the applicability of existing methods. To overcome this challenge, we propose an unconstrained framework that synergizes deep neural networks, algebraic priors, and temporal dynamics for uncalibrated multi-view human pose estimation. First, we introduce the Triangulation with Transformer Regressor (TTR), which reformulates classical triangulation into a data-driven token fusion process to bypass the dependency on explicit camera parameters. Second, to explicitly embed the inherent algebraic relations of the multi-view variety into the learning process, we propose the Gröbner basis Corrector (GC). This pioneering loss formulation enforces constraints derived from the multi-view variety to ensure the neural predictions strictly adhere to the laws of projective geometry. Finally, we devise the Temporal Equivariant Rectifier (TER), which exploits the equivariance property of human motion to impose temporal coherence and structural consistency, effectively mitigating scale ambiguity in uncalibrated settings. Extensive evaluations on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our framework establishes a new state-of-the-art for uncalibrated multi-view human pose estimation. Notably, our approach significantly closes the performance gap between calibration-free methods and fully calibrated oracles.

AI.tldr: Proposes an uncalibrated multi-view 3D human pose estimation framework using TTR (transformer-based triangulation), Gröbner basis Corrector (projective geometry constraints), and Temporal Equivariant Rectifier (temporal consistency), achieving SOTA results without camera calibration.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-view 3D human pose estimation methods require precise camera calibration, which is often unavailable in real-world settings, limiting practical applicability.
AI.method: Three components: (1) TTR - reformulates classical triangulation as a data-driven token fusion process using transformers; (2) Gröbner basis Corrector (GC) - enforces multi-view projective geometry constraints via a novel loss; (3) Temporal Equivariant Rectifier (TER) - uses human motion equivariance for temporal coherence and scale disambiguation.
AI.result: Establishes new state-of-the-art for uncalibrated multi-view human pose estimation on standard benchmarks; significantly closes the performance gap between calibration-free methods and fully calibrated oracles.
AI.conclusion: Synergizing deep learning (TTR), algebraic priors (GC), and temporal dynamics (TER) effectively solves uncalibrated multi-view human pose estimation, removing the dependency on camera calibration while maintaining high accuracy.

title: Don't Pause! Every prediction matters in a streaming video
summary: Streaming video models should respond the moment an event unfolds, not after the moment has passed. Yet existing online VideoQA benchmarks remain largely retrospective. They pause the video at fixed timestamps, pose questions about current or past events, and score models only at those moments. This protocol leaves streaming predictions untested. To close this gap, we introduce SPOT-Bench, featuring multi-turn proactive queries that evaluate general streaming perception and assistive capabilities required by an always-on, real-time assistant. SPOT-Bench comes with Timeliness-F1, a consolidated metric that measures streaming predictions by their temporal precision and balanced coverage across the entire video. Our benchmark reveals: (i) offline models detect events reliably but spam predictions unprompted; (ii) post-training for silence reduces spamming but induces unresponsiveness; (iii) half of the streaming video expects no response, which we term dead-time - compute spent here does not affect response latency. These findings motivate AsynKV, a training-free streaming adaptation of offline models, that retains their event perception while improving their streaming behavior. AsynKV features a long-short term memory, utilized efficiently by scaling compute during dead-time. It serves as a strong baseline on SPOT-Bench, outperforming existing streaming models, and achieves state-of-the-art on retrospective benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SPOT-Bench evaluates streaming video models on proactive, multi-turn queries with Timeliness-F1 metric. Findings reveal offline models spam predictions, post-training reduces spamming but causes unresponsiveness, and half of video is "dead-time" needing no response. AsynKV is proposed as a training-free streaming adaptation that excels on both SPOT-Bench and retrospective benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing online VideoQA benchmarks are retrospective—they pause at fixed timestamps and ask about past/current events, leaving streaming (real-time, proactive) predictions untested. There is a need to evaluate models as always-on, real-time assistants that respond as events unfold.
AI.method: (1) SPOT-Bench: multi-turn proactive queries for streaming video, with Timeliness-F1 metric measuring temporal precision and balanced coverage. (2) AsynKV: training-free streaming adaptation using long-short term memory with efficient compute scaling during dead-time (periods requiring no response).
AI.result: (i) Offline models detect events reliably but spam predictions unprompted. (ii) Post-training for silence reduces spamming but induces unresponsiveness. (iii) ~50% of streaming video is dead-time. AsynKV outperforms existing streaming models on SPOT-Bench and achieves SOTA on retrospective benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SPOT-Bench reveals fundamental trade-offs in streaming video understanding. AsynKV provides a strong, training-free baseline for streaming adaptation, balancing event perception and appropriate response timing.

title: Monocular Depth Estimation via Neural Network with Learnable Algebraic Group and Ring Structures
summary: Monocular depth estimation (MDE) has witnessed remarkable progress driven by Convolutional Neural Networks and transformer-based architectures. However, these approaches typically treat the problem as a generic image-to-image regression on Euclidean grids, thereby overlooking the intrinsic algebraic and geometric structures induced by perspective projection. To address this limitation, we propose LAGRNet, a novel framework that fundamentally grounds MDE in algebraic geometry by explicitly embedding learnable group, ring, and sheaf structures into the deep learning pipeline. Modeling feature maps as sections of a sheaf over an approximated image manifold, our method first establishes a Group-defined Feature Manifold (GFM) parameterized by a learned algebraic group action to enforce projective equivariance and robustness against view changes. To facilitate algebraically consistent cross-scale interactions, we subsequently introduce a Ring Convolution Layer (RCL) that formulates feature fusion as a graded ring homomorphism. Furthermore, to ensure global topological consistency, a Sheaf-based Module (SM) aggregates local depth cues via Čech nerve on the image topology. Extensive zero-shot evaluations across the KITTI, NYU-Depth V2, and ETH3D benchmarks demonstrate that LAGRNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and generalization capabilities.

AI.tldr: LAGRNet improves monocular depth estimation by embedding algebraic geometry structures (group, ring, sheaf) into deep learning, outperforming SOTA on KITTI, NYU-Depth V2, and ETH3D.
AI.motivation: Existing MDE methods treat depth estimation as generic image-to-image regression on Euclidean grids, ignoring the intrinsic algebraic and geometric structures from perspective projection.
AI.method: LAGRNet introduces three components: (1) Group-defined Feature Manifold (GFM) for projective equivariance, (2) Ring Convolution Layer (RCL) for algebraically consistent cross-scale fusion, (3) Sheaf-based Module (SM) for global topological consistency via Čech nerve.
AI.result: Zero-shot evaluations on KITTI, NYU-Depth V2, and ETH3D show LAGRNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Embedding algebraic geometry structures (group, ring, sheaf) into deep learning is a promising direction for grounding MDE in projective geometry, leading to better performance and robustness.

title: An Affordable,Wearable Stereo-Eye-Tracking Platform
summary: Research on video-based eye-tracking has long explored stereo and glint-based methods, yet existing wearable eye trackers - both commercial and open-source - offer limited flexibility for algorithm development and comparative evaluation. We present an affordable, wearable stereo eye-tracking platform built from off-the-shelf and 3D-printable components that explicitly targets this gap. The system combines four infrared eye cameras, infrared illumination, an optional scene camera, and software support for calibration and synchronized data acquisition. By design, the platform supports multiple eye-tracking paradigms, including stereo, glint-based, and binocular approaches, within a single hardware configuration. Rather than optimizing for end-user robustness, the platform prioritizes modularity and extensibility for research use. This paper focuses on the hardware architecture and calibration pipeline and demonstrates the feasibility of the approach using a prototype implementation. All hardware designs and documentation are made openly available.

AI.tldr: An affordable, open-source wearable stereo eye-tracking platform built from off-the-shelf and 3D-printable components, supporting multiple tracking paradigms (stereo, glint-based, binocular) for research flexibility.
AI.motivation: Existing wearable eye trackers lack flexibility for algorithm development and comparative evaluation; researchers need a modular, extensible platform.
AI.method: Four infrared eye cameras + IR illumination + optional scene camera; supports stereo, glint-based, and binocular paradigms in one hardware config; open-source hardware and calibration pipeline.
AI.result: Demonstrated feasibility via prototype; platform prioritizes modularity over end-user robustness.
AI.conclusion: The platform fills a gap by providing an affordable, flexible, and openly-available wearable eye-tracking solution for research communities.

title: See Further, Think Deeper: Advancing VLM's Reasoning Ability with Low-level Visual Cues and Reflection
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have benefited from Reinforcement Learning (RL) for enhanced reasoning. However, existing methods still face critical limitations, including the lack of low-level visual information and effective visual feedback. To address these problems, this paper proposes a unified multimodal interleaved reasoning framework \textbf{ForeSight}, which enables VLMs to \textbf{See Further} with low-level visual cues and \textbf{Think Deeper} with effective visual feedback. First, it introduces a set of low-level visual tools to integrate essential visual information into the reasoning chain, mitigating the neglect of fine-grained visual features. Second, a mask-based visual feedback mechanism is elaborated to incorporate visual reflection into the thinking process, enabling the model to dynamically re-examine and update its answers. Driven by RL, ForeSight learns to autonomously decide on tool invocation and answer verification, with the final answer accuracy as the reward signal. To evaluate the performance of the proposed framework, we construct a new dataset, Character and Grounding SalBench (CG-SalBench), based on the SalBench dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that the ForeSight-7B model significantly outperforms other models with the same parameter scale, and even surpasses the current SOTA closed-source models on certain metrics.

AI.tldr: ForeSight is a unified multimodal reasoning framework that uses Reinforcement Learning to help Vision-Language Models "see further" with low-level visual tools and "think deeper" with mask-based visual feedback, achieving SOTA results on the new CG-SalBench dataset.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs lack low-level visual information and effective visual feedback, limiting their reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: (1) Low-level visual tools for integrating fine-grained visual features into reasoning chains. (2) Mask-based visual feedback mechanism for visual reflection and dynamic answer revision. (3) RL-driven training with final answer accuracy as reward signal for autonomous tool invocation and verification.
AI.result: ForeSight-7B significantly outperforms same-scale models and surpasses current SOTA closed-source models on certain metrics on the CG-SalBench dataset.
AI.conclusion: Integrating low-level visual cues and visual feedback via RL effectively enhances VLM reasoning capabilities.

title: SycoPhantasy: Quantifying Sycophancy and Hallucination in Small Open Weight VLMs for Vision-Language Scoring of Fantasy Characters
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed as evaluators in tasks requiring nuanced image understanding, yet their reliability in scoring alignment between images and text descriptions remains underexplored. We investigate whether small, open-weight VLMs exhibit \emph{sycophantic} behavior when evaluating image-text alignment: assigning high scores without grounding their judgments in visual evidence. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the \emph{Bluffing Coefficient} (\bc), a metric that measures the mismatch between a model's score and its evidence recall. We evaluate six open-weight VLMs ranging from 450M to 8B parameters on a benchmark of 173,810 AI-generated character portraits paired with detailed textual descriptions. Our analysis reveals a significant inverse correlation between model size and sycophancy rate ($r = -0.96$, $p = 0.002$), with smaller models exhibiting substantially higher rates of unjustified high scores. The smallest model tested (LFM2-VL, 450M) produced sycophantic evaluations in 22.3\% of cases, compared to 6.0\% for the largest (LLaVA-1.6, 7B). These findings have direct implications for the deployment of small, open-weight VLMs as automated evaluators within attribute-rich, synthetic image evaluation tasks, where the gap between assigned scores and cited visual evidence is both measurable and consequential.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates sycophantic behavior in small, open-weight VLMs when evaluating image-text alignment, introducing a Bluffing Coefficient metric. Testing on 173,810 AI-generated character portraits, they find smaller models (e.g., 450M) produce unjustified high scores in 22.3% of cases versus 6.0% for larger models (7B), with a strong inverse correlation (r=-0.96) between model size and sycophancy rate.
AI.motivation: While VLMs are increasingly used as automated evaluators for image-text alignment, their reliability—especially whether they assign high scores without actual visual grounding (sycophantic behavior)—remains underexplored. The authors aim to quantify this gap.
AI.method: The authors propose the Bluffing Coefficient (BC) to measure mismatch between a model's assigned score and its ability to recall visual evidence. They evaluate six open-weight VLMs (450M to 8B parameters) on 173,810 AI-generated character portraits with detailed text descriptions.
AI.result: A significant inverse correlation (r=-0.96, p=0.002) exists between model size and sycophancy rate. The smallest model (450M) shows 22.3% sycophantic evaluations, while the largest (7B) shows only 6.0%.
AI.conclusion: Small, open-weight VLMs are unreliable as automated evaluators in attribute-rich synthetic image tasks due to their tendency to produce unjustified high scores ungrounded in visual evidence, which has practical implications for their deployment.

title: Multispectral airborne laser scanning dataset for tree species classification: MS-ALS-SPECIES
summary: The shift from stand-level to individual-tree-level forest assessments supports improved biodiversity mapping, particularly in boreal ecosystems where tree species like aspen (Populus tremula L.) play a keystone role. While airborne laser scanning (ALS) is the standard for such inventories, a major limitation is the small number of publicly available ALS datasets containing high-quality, field-validated reference data. Furthermore, open multispectral ALS datasets with high-quality field reference data are completely lacking despite the potential of multispectral ALS data for tree species classification. This paper presents and details an open multispectral ALS dataset used in a recent international benchmarking study of machine learning and deep learning methods for tree species classification by Taher et al. (2026). The dataset comprises 6326 segment-level point clouds of individual trees representing nine species in Southern Finland. The point cloud data has been acquired using two multispectral laser scanning systems each operating at three laser wavelengths: a helicopter-borne system (HeliALS) with a point density exceeding 1000 points/m$^2$ and an Optech Titan system with approximately 35 points/m$^2$. We provide a detailed description of field data collection techniques developed in the study to facilitate the collection of high-quality ground truth data in an efficient and scalable manner. Additionally, our article presents new analyses on species classification using multispectral data building upon the initial findings of Taher et al. (2026). Furthermore, we study the relation between classification accuracy and tree height to highlight the versatility of the open dataset and to demonstrate the advantage of the point transformer model for small trees and minority species.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an open multispectral ALS dataset with 6326 individual-tree point clouds (9 species) from two systems (HeliALS >1000 pts/m², Optech Titan ~35 pts/m²) in Southern Finland, including field data collection methods, new classification analyses beyond a prior benchmark study, and analysis of accuracy vs. tree height using point transformer models.
AI.motivation: The shift to individual-tree-level forest assessments improves biodiversity mapping, but there is a lack of open multispectral ALS datasets with high-quality field reference data, especially for tree species classification in boreal ecosystems where species like aspen play a keystone role.
AI.method: The dataset includes 6326 segment-level point clouds of 9 tree species collected from two multispectral ALS systems (HeliALS and Optech Titan) in Southern Finland, with detailed field data collection techniques for efficient and scalable ground truth acquisition.
AI.result: The paper presents new analyses on species classification using multispectral data, extending findings from Taher et al. (2026), and demonstrates the relationship between classification accuracy and tree height, showing point transformer models' advantage for small trees and minority species.
AI.conclusion: This open dataset fills a critical gap in publicly available multispectral ALS reference data and enables further research on tree species classification, with point transformers showing particular promise for challenging cases like small trees and rare species.

title: Global Context or Local Detail? Adaptive Visual Grounding for Hallucination Mitigation
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are frequently undermined by object hallucination--generating content that contradicts visual reality--due to an over-reliance on linguistic priors. We introduce Positive-and-Negative Decoding (PND), a training-free inference framework that intervenes directly in the decoding process to enforce visual fidelity. PND is motivated by our key finding of a critical attention deficit in VLMs, where visual features are empirically under-weighted. Our framework corrects this via a dual-path contrast: The positive path amplifies salient visual evidence using multi-layer attention to encourage faithful descriptions, directly counteracting the attention deficit. Simultaneously, the negative path identifies and degrades the core object's features to create a strong counterfactual, which penalizes ungrounded, prior-dominant generation. By contrasting the model's outputs from these two perspectives at each step, PND steers generation towards text that is not just linguistically probable, but visually factual. Extensive experiments on benchmarks like POPE, MME, and CHAIR show that PND achieves state-of-the-art performance with up to 6.5% accuracy improvement, substantially reducing object hallucination while also enhancing descriptive detail--all without requiring any model retraining.
